**Step 7:** Final boxed result:
$$
\boxed{\,F_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{N \varepsilon_0 t V^2}{g} = \frac{n \varepsilon_0 t V^2}{g}\,}
$$
**(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**(i) Total capacitance**

$$
C = \frac{120 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
C = \frac{120 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
C = \frac{1.06248 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.3124 \times 10^{-14} \, F \approx 53.12 \, fF
$$

**(1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer ≈ 53.1 fF)**

**(ii) Actuation force**

$$
F_e = \frac{120 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 30^2}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
F_e = \frac{120 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 900}{4 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
F_e = \frac{4.78116 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.195 \times 10^{-6} \, N \approx 1.20 \, \mu N
$$

**(1 mark for setup, 1 mark for ≈ 1.20 μN)**

**(iii) Static equilibrium displacement**

$$
x_{eq} = \frac{F_e}{k} = \frac{1.195 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2} = 9.96 \times 10^{-7} \, m \approx 0.996 \, \mu m
$$

**(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for ≈ 1.0 μm)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout for Question A:** A common mistake is writing `N = n` instead of `N = 2n`. Since each movable finger has *two* sidewalls (left and right), the number of effective gaps is **twice** the number of movable fingers. Losing this factor of 2 costs you **2 full marks** in KTU valuation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Conceptual + Application

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of an electrostatic comb drive. Discuss why it exhibits a constant-force characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare comb drives with parallel-plate electrostatic actuators. Explain why parallel-plate actuators suffer from pull-in instability while comb drives do not, in the ideal case. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A comb drive consists of two sets of interdigitated finger electrodes fabricated typically from polysilicon (or single-crystal silicon in SOI processes). **(1 mark)**
- The stator is anchored to the substrate, while the rotor is suspended by folded-beam or crab-leg flexures that permit lateral motion. **(1 mark)**
- The fingers have typical dimensions: length 10–100 μm, width 1–5 μm, gap 1–5 μm, thickness 2–25 μm, and number of fingers ranging from a few dozen to several hundred. **(1 mark)**

**Working (2 marks):**
- A voltage `V` applied between stator and rotor sets up a uniform electric field `E = V/g` in each inter-finger gap. **(1 mark)**
- The lateral electrostatic force `F = ½ V² (dC/dx)` pulls the rotor sideways; the rotor is held in equilibrium when this force balances the spring restoring force `kx`. **(1 mark)**

**Constant-Force Explanation (2 marks):**
- Capacitance `C ∝ L` (overlap), and `L = L₀ + x`, so `C` is linear in `x`. **(1 mark)**
- Therefore `dC/dx = constant`, and `F = ½ V² × (constant)` is independent of `x`. **(1 mark)**

**Schematic expected:** A top-view drawing showing stator fingers (hatched/anchored) interleaved with rotor fingers (suspended on flexures), with labels `g`, `L`, `t`, `x`, and `N`.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Comb Drive | Parallel-Plate |
|---|---|---|
| Capacitance variation | Linear in `x` (overlap) | Inverse in gap |
| Force vs. displacement | Constant | Increases with `x` |
| Stable operating range | Full mechanical travel | 0 to `g/3` |
| Pull-in | No (ideal) | Yes, at `x = g₀/3` |

**Pull-in explanation — Parallel-Plate (2 marks):**
- For a parallel-plate, `C(x) = ε₀ A / (g₀ - x)`. **(0.5 mark)**
- The electrostatic force `F = ½ V² ε₀ A / (g₀ - x)²` grows as the gap shrinks. **(0.5 mark)**
- The electrical stiffness `k_e = -dF/dx` becomes more negative as `x → g₀/3`, and the net stiffness `k - 2k_e` vanishes at `x = g₀/3`, causing the plates to snap together (pull-in). **(1 mark)**

**Pull-in explanation — Comb Drive (2 marks):**
- For a comb drive, `C(x) = (N ε₀ t / g) x`, so `F = ½ V² N ε₀ t / g` is **constant**. **(0.5 mark)**
- The electrical stiffness `k_e = -dF/dx = 0` in the ideal case. **(0.5 mark)**
- Hence the net stiffness remains positive (equal to mechanical `k`) for all `x`, and **no pull-in occurs** in the ideal case. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout for Question B:** Students often claim comb drives have *no* pull-in at all. The correct statement is: in the **ideal** comb drive with no fringing fields and no sidewall variations, pull-in does not occur. In real devices, fringing fields at the finger tips cause a mild effective electrical stiffness, and some pull-in may occur near the extreme of travel. State this nuance to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Comb Drives**

- ✅ A comb drive is an **in-plane (lateral) electrostatic actuator** with interdigitated stator and rotor fingers.
- ✅ Total capacitance: **`C = N·ε₀·t·L/g`** where **`N = 2n`** (factor of 2 is *mandatory*).
- ✅ Electrostatic force: **`F = ½ N·ε₀·t·V²/g`** — the single most important formula in this topic.
- ✅ Force is **independent of displacement** because `dC/dx = N·ε₀·t/g` is a *geometric constant*.
- ✅ Stored energy: **`U = ½ C V²`**; co-energy method gives `F = ½ V² (dC/dx)`.
- ✅ Equilibrium displacement: **`x_eq = F/k = (N·ε₀·t·V²) / (2g·k)`**.
- ✅ No pull-in instability (in the ideal case) because **electrical stiffness `k_e = 0`**.
- ✅ Applications: micromirrors (DLP), accelerometers (ADXL), RF MEMS, micro-grippers, tunable capacitors.
- ✅ **Dimensional check:** Always verify force is in Newtons: `[ε₀][t][V²/g] = (F/m)(m)(V²/m) = (F·V²)/m²`... use `½ ε₀ E²` (energy density) approach for cross-check.
- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Forgetting `N = 2n`, or mixing up `L` (overlap) and `g` (gap) in the force formula.
- ✅ **Vacuum permittivity:** `ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m` — memorize to 3 significant figures.
- ✅ Comb drives are typically **capacitively-sensed** as well as **voltage-actuated** — they are *transducers* in both directions (reversible).
- ✅ Real devices use **differential** comb configurations to suppress common-mode feedthrough and linearize the response.
- ✅ **Levitation combs** (slanted fingers) produce out-of-plane force — useful for vertical actuators without pull-in.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, *always* include: definition, schematic, derivation, numerical evaluation, and a comparison table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Grippers - Micro Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Grippers - Micro Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Micro Grippers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro Grippers** are microscale end-effectors in MEMS that grasp, hold, transport, and release objects in the **1 μm to 1 mm** size range, typically actuated by electrostatic, piezoelectric, thermal, or shape-memory mechanisms.

They are the "fingers" of micro-assembly workstations, micro-robots, and biomedical micromanipulation systems. The fundamental engineering challenge is to produce sufficient gripping force (typically **μN to mN** range) without damaging fragile specimens like biological cells, optical fibers, or MEMS sub-components.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a micro gripper as **tweezers controlled by a tiny electric hand**. When you switch on the voltage, the "fingers" (jaws) close in on a tiny object — like picking up a single grain of sand or a living cell under a microscope. The "muscles" doing the work are invisible electric fields, tiny thermal expansions, or smart alloy phase changes — all engineered at the chip scale using the same lithography used to make computer processors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Micro grippers are assessed in the domain of *microactuators* and their evaluation typically centers on **force-displacement trade-offs**, **response time**, and **fabrication compatibility** with standard silicon processes.

---

## 1.2 Micro Motors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro Motors** are MEMS devices that convert electrical energy into controlled rotational mechanical motion at the microscale, with rotor diameters typically between **100 μm and 5 mm**, used in applications such as microfluidic pumping, optical switching, and watch mechanisms.

Micromotors are broadly classified by their energy conversion mechanism:
- **Electrostatic (variable-capacitance) motors** — most common in polysilicon MEMS
- **Electromagnetic motors** — high torque, requires magnetic materials
- **Piezoelectric (ultrasonic) motors** — traveling-wave or standing-wave type
- **Combustion/chemical micromotors** — research-grade, high power density

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **hard-disk platter shrunk to the size of a sesame seed** with copper-colored electrodes radiating like the spokes of a bicycle wheel from its center. When you energize the electrodes in a precise sequence (like flicking switches around the spokes), a **whisper of static electricity** pushes the wheel around — but with enough force to spin a micro-gear or pump fluid through a lab-on-a-chip. The entire motor sits comfortably under a single strand of human hair.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** Both micro grippers and micro motors belong to Module-1 classification of *microscale actuators*. The Electrostatic motor (wobble / side-drive) is the canonical KTU Module-1 exam topic.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gripping force | **1 μN – 100 mN** | N |
| Jaw displacement | **0.1 – 500 μm** | μm |
| Operating voltage | **5 – 200 V** | V |
| Motor rotor diameter | **100 μm – 5 mm** | mm |
| Motor speed | **100 – 100,000 rpm** | rpm |
| Motor torque | **1 pN·m – 1 μN·m** | N·m |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Micro Grippers — Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Electrostatic Micro Grippers

The jaw is a compliant mechanical structure (flexure) anchored at one end and fitted with capacitor plates at the gripping tips. A voltage V applied across the plates generates an attractive force:

$$F_{elec} = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_0\,A\,\frac{V^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_0$ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (permittivity of free space)
- $A$ = overlap area of the capacitor plates
- $d$ = gap between plates
- $V$ = applied voltage

The displacement of the jaw (x) is governed by the balance between the electrostatic force and the elastic restoring force of the suspension flexure, characterized by stiffness k:

$$x = \frac{F_{elec}}{k} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 k d^{2}}$$

### 2.1.2 Piezoelectric Micro Grippers

A piezo stack (typically PZT) elongates when voltage is applied. The elongation is:

$$\Delta L = d_{33}\,V\,\frac{L}{t}$$

where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (≈ **3 × 10⁻¹⁰ m/V** for PZT). The gripper jaw is mechanically amplified using a lever or compliant structure.

### 2.1.3 Thermal / Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Micro Grippers

Two metal arms with different coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) bend when heated. The tip displacement is:

$$\Delta x = L^{2}\,\Delta\alpha\,\frac{\Delta T}{2t}$$

where $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2$ is the CTE mismatch, L is the arm length, t is the thickness. For SMAs (Nitinol), phase change (martensite ↔ austenite) produces strains of up to **8%**.

### 2.1.4 Why and How — Step Logic

1. **Actuation signal applied** (V or ΔT) → energy input stored in actuator
2. **Energy converted to mechanical strain** → jaw begins to move
3. **Compliant flexure stores elastic energy** → resists motion
4. **Equilibrium reached** when actuator force = elastic restoring force
5. **Object grasped** with force = actuator force at equilibrium displacement

---

## 2.2 Micro Motors — Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Variable-Capacitance (Electrostatic) Micromotors

The rotor and stator are concentric cylindrical polysilicon structures with radial electrodes. Voltage applied between rotor and stator creates an electric field that produces tangential force on the rotor. The torque is derived from the change in capacitance with angular displacement:

$$T = \frac{1}{2}\,V^{2}\,\frac{dC}{d\theta}$$

Energy stored in the motor capacitance is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2}\,C(\theta)\,V^{2}$$

The motor self-starts when the field configuration is asymmetric — achieved by biasing stator electrodes in a phased sequence.

### 2.2.2 Synchronous Operation

For an N-phase motor driven at switching frequency f, the synchronous mechanical speed is:

$$\omega_{sync} = \frac{2\pi f}{N_{p}}$$

where $N_p$ is the number of pole pairs.

### 2.2.3 Side-Drive vs Wobble Motor

| Feature | Side-Drive Motor | Wobble Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Symmetric planar rotor | Conical/ball rotor |
| Friction role | Low | Critical (drives rotation) |
| Torque | Higher | Lower |
| Speed | Higher | Lower |
| Fabrication | More complex | Simpler |
| Bearing | Air/electrostatic | Ball/contact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked:** Why does the wobble motor rotate if the rotor never completes a full revolution? **Answer:** The rotor *precesses* (wobbles) around the stator; the contact friction with the underlying surface translates this precession into continuous rotation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains every equation you need for Part A and Part B questions on micro grippers and micro motors.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrostatic jaw force | $F = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_0\,A\,\frac{V^{2}}{d^{2}}$ | Parallel plate approximation |
| 2 | Jaw displacement | $x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 k d^{2}}$ | k = flexure stiffness |
| 3 | Piezo strain | $\Delta L = d_{33}\,V\,L/t$ | $d_{33}$ is piezo coefficient |
| 4 | Thermal tip displacement | $\Delta x = \frac{L^{2}\,\Delta\alpha\,\Delta T}{2t}$ | Bimorph cantilever |
| 5 | SMA recovery strain | $\varepsilon_{SMA} \le 0.08$ | Phase change Nitinol |
| 6 | Motor torque | $T = \frac{1}{2}\,V^{2}\,\frac{dC}{d\theta}$ | Variable-capacitance |
| 7 | Motor stored energy | $U = \frac{1}{2}\,C(\theta)\,V^{2}$ | Electrostatic |
| 8 | Synchronous speed | $\omega_{sync} = \frac{2\pi f}{N_{p}}$ | N-phase motor |
| 9 | Output power | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output |
| 10 | Wobble motor precession | $\theta_{rot} = \theta_{precess} \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | φ = cone half-angle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Electrostatic micro grippers are used in IVF (In-Vitro Fertilization) clinics for sperm/egg manipulation. Micro motors power the autofocus mechanism in cell-phone cameras, lab-on-a-chip fluidic pumps, and micro-air-vehicle propulsion testbeds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Electrostatic Gripping Force for a Comb-Drive Micro Gripper

We derive the force generated by a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator used as the driving element of a micro gripper jaw.

### Step 1 — Define the System

A movable plate of area A is suspended by a spring of stiffness k, located at distance d from a fixed plate. Voltage V is applied across the gap.

### Step 2 — Capacitance of the Parallel-Plate Configuration

The capacitance between the two plates is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0\,A}{d}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Stored in the Capacitor

The energy stored in the capacitor at applied voltage V is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2}\,C\,V^{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d}$$

### Step 4 — Force from the Energy Gradient

The electrostatic force acting on the movable plate is the negative gradient of stored energy with respect to gap:

$$F = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial d}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate

Applying the differentiation (treating A, V, $\varepsilon_0$ as constants):

$$F = -\left(-\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d^{2}}\right) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d^{2}}$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$\boxed{\,F_{elec} = \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{d^{2}}\,}$$

The force scales **quadratically with voltage** and **inversely with the square of the gap**. This quadratic dependence is a key reason why electrostatic micro grippers require either high voltages (50–200 V) or very small gaps (1–5 μm).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Synchronous Speed of an N-Phase Variable-Capacitance Micromotor

### Step 1 — Define Pole Structure

An N-phase electrostatic motor has $N_p$ pole pairs. Each pole pair corresponds to one electrical cycle (360° electrical = 360°/N_p mechanical).

### Step 2 — Electrical Switching

The stator phases are switched at frequency f (Hz). In one electrical period T = 1/f, the rotor advances by **one pole pair** in mechanical angle.

### Step 3 — Mechanical Angle per Electrical Cycle

Mechanical angle traversed per switching cycle:

$$\Delta\theta_{mech} = \frac{2\pi}{N_{p}} \quad \text{(radians per cycle)}$$

### Step 4 — Angular Velocity

Multiplying by the number of cycles per second:

$$\omega = \Delta\theta_{mech} \times f = \frac{2\pi f}{N_{p}}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to RPM

$$N_{rpm} = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{60\,f}{N_{p}}$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$\boxed{\,\omega_{sync} = \frac{2\pi f}{N_{p}}\;\text{rad/s}\quad \text{or}\quad N_{rpm} = \frac{60\,f}{N_{p}}\,}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** A 3-phase electrostatic motor with $N_p = 6$ pole pairs driven at f = 1 kHz has:

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{60 \times 1000}{6} = 10{,}000 \text{ rpm}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Actuator

| Design Need | Best Actuator | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Large jaw displacement (>100 μm) | SMA / Thermal | SMA strain up to 8% |
| Fast response (<1 ms) | Electrostatic | No thermal time constant |
| Low voltage (<10 V) | Piezoelectric | High energy density |
| High force (>1 mN) | SMA | Large stress output |
| Standard CMOS-compatible fab | Electrostatic | Polysilicon friendly |
| Biocompatibility requirement | Piezo (PZT) | Non-thermal, sterile |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Electrostatic Micro Gripper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Voltage Source V] --> B[Comb-Drive Actuator]
    B --> C[Compliant Flexure Linkage]
    C --> D[Movable Jaw]
    D --> E[Micro Object]
    B -.capacitive force.-> C
    C -.elastic restoring force.-> B
    D --> F[Force Sensor optional]
    F --> G[Closed-Loop Controller]
    G -.feedback V.-> A
    classDef power fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33
    classDef mech fill:#e2ecfd,stroke:#36c
    classDef sense fill:#e2fde2,stroke:#3a3
    class A power
    class B,C,D,E,F mech
    class G sense
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- Red node: electrical energy domain
- Blue nodes: mechanical motion domain
- Green node: sensing / control domain
- The dotted lines represent *physical coupling* (force balance), not signal flow.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Variable-Capacitance Micromotor (Side-Drive)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Source] --> B[DC-to-AC Inverter]
    B --> C[Phase Sequencer]
    C --> P1[Phase 1 Stator Electrodes]
    C --> P2[Phase 2 Stator Electrodes]
    C --> P3[Phase 3 Stator Electrodes]
    P1 --> R[Rotor Disc]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    R --> S[Output Shaft]
    S --> M[Micro Load: pump gear valve]
    classDef src fill:#fff3c2,stroke:#a80
    classDef stat fill:#dde2ff,stroke:#339
    classDef rotor fill:#ffd6f0,stroke:#939
    class A,B,C src
    class P1,P2,P3 stat
    class R,S,M rotor
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The inverter creates the multiphase AC excitation required for synchronous operation.
- Stator phases are energized in sequence: 1 → 2 → 3 → 1 …
- The rotor experiences a rotating electric field that drags it via tangential electrostatic force.

---

## 4.3 Wobble Motor — Working Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotor Cone precessing] --> B[Contact point with substrate]
    B --> C[Friction force tangential to contact]
    C --> D[Translates precession into rotation]
    D --> E[Continuous slow rotation]
    classDef proc fill:#f9e2fd,stroke:#939
    classDef force fill:#fde2c2,stroke:#a83
    classDef out fill:#c2fde2,stroke:#383
    class A proc
    class B,C force
    class D,E out
```

**Conceptual meaning:** The wobble motor's *rotor* does NOT spin freely about its own axis. Instead, it traces a small circular path (precession) at the tip while the friction with the base substrate converts this wobble into a slow continuous rotation of the entire rotor body about the central vertical axis.

---

## 4.4 Micro Gripper — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Target object under microscope] --> B[Position the gripper using micro-positioning stage]
    B --> C[Apply actuation voltage to jaws]
    C --> D[Jaws close and apply gripping force Fg]
    D --> E{Object held successfully?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Translate to target location]
    E -- No --> G[Adjust force or position and retry]
    G --> C
    F --> H[Release by reducing voltage to zero]
    H --> I[Withdraw gripper]
    classDef input fill:#e2f0ff,stroke:#039
    classDef action fill:#fff3e2,stroke:#a80
    classDef decision fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#c33
    classDef out fill:#e2ffe2,stroke:#3a3
    class A,B input
    class C,D,F,H,I action
    class E,G decision
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a micro gripper. List any two actuation mechanisms used in micro grippers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **micro gripper** is a MEMS device used to grasp, hold, and manipulate objects in the size range of 1 μm to 1 mm.

Two actuation mechanisms:
1. **Electrostatic actuation** — uses attractive force between charged capacitor plates to close the jaws.
2. **Thermal / Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) actuation** — uses differential thermal expansion or martensite-to-austenite phase change to produce jaw motion.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two actuation mechanisms.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a wobble motor? How does it produce rotation despite the rotor not completing a full revolution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **wobble motor** is a type of variable-capacitance micromotor in which the rotor (typically conical or spherical) does not spin about its own symmetry axis.

The rotor **precesses** (wobbles) around the stator when the stator phases are excited in sequence. The contact between the rotor tip and the substrate produces a **tangential friction force** that converts the precessional motion into a slow, continuous rotation of the entire rotor body.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for precession, 1 mark for friction-based rotation.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each)

> **MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — (14 marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of an **electrostatic micro gripper**. Derive the expression for the gripping force.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **side-drive variable-capacitance micromotor** with its construction, working principle, and derive the expression for the **synchronous speed**.  **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Construction (2 marks):**
An electrostatic micro gripper consists of:
- Two opposing **flexure-suspended jaws** made of polysilicon
- **Comb-drive or parallel-plate capacitors** integrated at the jaw bases
- **Electrical contact pads** for voltage application
- A central **supporting anchor** fixed to the substrate

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
1. A voltage V is applied between the stator and rotor of the comb-drive.
2. Capacitive attraction produces force $F = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$.
3. The compliant flexure converts this force into a controlled jaw displacement.
4. The jaws close on the micro-object with a predictable force.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
- Capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 A / d$
- Energy $U = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / (2d)$
- Force $F = -\partial U / \partial d = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / (2d^2)$
- Hence $F_{elec} = \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Construction (2 marks):**
A side-drive motor has:
- A **planar rotor disc** suspended above the substrate by **journal air bearings**
- A **stator** with radial electrodes surrounding the rotor
- **3 or more phases** of electrodes energized in sequence

**Working (2 marks):**
- Voltage applied to stator phases in sequence produces a **rotating electric field**.
- The tangential component of the electrostatic force on the rotor produces **torque**.
- The rotor rotates synchronously with the field.

**Derivation of synchronous speed (3 marks):**

$$\omega_{sync} = \frac{2\pi f}{N_p} \quad \text{or} \quad N_{rpm} = \frac{60\,f}{N_p}$$

where $f$ = switching frequency, $N_p$ = number of pole pairs.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — (14 marks)

**(a)** Compare the **electrostatic** and **piezoelectric** actuation mechanisms for micro grippers. Mention two merits and two demerits of each.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **wobble motor** with suitable diagram. How is continuous rotation achieved?  **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Electrostatic | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| Force generation | Capacitive attraction | Crystal lattice strain |
| Voltage | High (50–200 V) | Low (5–50 V) |
| Displacement | Moderate | Small (μm) |
| Response time | Microseconds | Milliseconds |
| Power consumption | Very low | Moderate |
| CMOS compatible | Excellent | Requires PZT layer |

**Electrostatic** — Merits: fast, low power, CMOS compatible. Demerits: high voltage, force ∝ V² (non-linear).
**Piezoelectric** — Merits: high force density, low voltage, linear response. Demerits: small stroke, hysteresis, requires PZT integration.

*(Award 1 mark for each correctly stated merit/demerit.)*

**Conclusion (3 marks):** Choose electrostatic for high-speed, low-force biomedical tasks and piezo for precision assembly where small but strong gripping force is needed.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Construction (2 marks):**
- A **stator** with multiple phase electrodes
- A **conical/ball-shaped rotor** that sits on the stator
- The rotor does not have a true central shaft — it rests with its tip touching the substrate

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Stator phases are excited sequentially.
2. The rotor is pulled toward the energized electrode.
3. Since the rotor is conical, it tilts (precesses) toward that electrode.
4. As the next phase is energized, the rotor tilts toward it, completing a circular precession.
5. The rotor **never rotates about its own axis** — it wobbles.

**Continuous rotation mechanism (2 marks):**
- The precession causes the rotor tip to slide along the substrate.
- **Friction** between the tip and substrate translates the sliding motion into rotation of the entire rotor body about the central vertical axis.
- This rotation is slow (10–500 rpm) but continuous.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing precession with rotation** — Students often wrongly state that the rotor spins on its own axis. It does NOT. The rotor only precesses; rotation is achieved indirectly through friction. (**−2 marks** if not clarified.)
> 2. **Forgetting the quadratic V dependence** — Many students write $F \propto V$ instead of $F \propto V^2$ for electrostatic force. This is a **−1 mark** deduction in Part A derivations.
> 3. **Mixing up jaw force and jaw displacement** — Force is governed by electrostatics; displacement is governed by flexure stiffness. State both separately.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — Torque in $\mu N\cdot m$ not $N\cdot m$ for micromotors; rpm vs rad/s confusion in synchronous speed questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1: Micro Grippers — Micro Motors)**

- ✅ **Micro gripper** = microscale end-effector for grasping 1 μm – 1 mm objects.
- ✅ Four major actuation mechanisms: **electrostatic, piezoelectric, thermal, SMA**.
- ✅ Key formula: $F = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$ (electrostatic force).
- ✅ SMA can deliver up to **8% strain** — largest among MEMS actuators.
- ✅ **Micro motor** = microscale rotational actuator; main types: variable-capacitance (electrostatic), electromagnetic, piezoelectric.
- ✅ **Wobble motor** rotates via friction-mediated precession; rotor never completes a full revolution about its own axis.
- ✅ **Side-drive motor** uses a planar rotor in journal air bearings; rotor experiences direct tangential force.
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $\omega_{sync} = 2\pi f / N_p$ rad/s.
- ✅ Output power: $P = T \cdot \omega$.
- ✅ All electrostatic MEMS actuators exhibit **quadratic voltage dependence** — a critical point frequently tested.
- ✅ Air bearings and friction play distinct roles: bearings isolate rotor (side-drive); friction is the *driving mechanism* (wobble).
- ✅ Standard KTU 14-mark question pattern: (a) 7-mark design/derivation + (b) 7-mark comparison/explanation.
- ✅ Always state **units** explicitly in numerical answers (N, μm, rpm, N·m).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Micro Valves # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Micro Valve** is a Micro-Electro-Mechanical System (MEMS) device that precisely regulates, controls, or directs the flow of fluids (liquids or gases) within micro-scale channels, typically featuring characteristic dimensions in the range of **1 μm to 1000 μm**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for OEAET722 (MEMS), micro valves are classified as **active fluidic control components** that integrate mechanical microstructures with actuation mechanisms to achieve flow modulation, isolation, or proportional distribution in microfluidic and biomedical microsystems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 - Microfluidics):** Micro valves are categorized as a foundational active microfluidic element. They are graded for their ability to convert an input signal (electrical, thermal, magnetic, or pneumatic) into a mechanical displacement that alters fluid flow paths. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **household water tap** connected to a municipal pipe. When you rotate the handle, a rubber washer (called a *gasket*) moves up and down, either opening or closing the passage for water. The amount of rotation determines the flow rate — a little twist gives a trickle, a full turn gives a gush. A **micro valve does exactly the same thing**, but at a scale **1000 times smaller** than a grain of sand: - The "pipe" is a **microchannel** etched into silicon, glass, or polymer (PDMS). - The "handle" is an **actuator** (a tiny motor made from electrostatic, piezoelectric, magnetic, or thermal materials). - The "rubber washer" is a **flexible diaphragm** or a **poppet** (a small disc/ball) that seals the orifice. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Definition:** A micro valve is a microscopic switch for fluids. Instead of letting electrons flow (like a transistor), it lets liquids or gases flow — and like a transistor, it can turn the flow ON, OFF, or modulate it proportionally. ## 1.3 Classification Overview Micro valves are broadly bifurcated into two master categories, and KTU questions frequently test this distinction: | Category | Definition | Typical Actuation | Response Type | |----------|------------|-------------------|---------------| | **Active Valves** | Require external energy input to operate | Electrostatic, Piezoelectric, Electromagnetic, SMA, Thermal bimetallic | **Externally controlled** | | **Passive Valves** | Operate using inherent fluid or system properties (e.g., pressure, capillary action) | No external control; rely on flow dynamics | **Self-regulating** (check valves, no-return valves) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metric to Remember:** A widely used dimensionless figure-of-merit for valves is the **Cv (Valve Flow Coefficient)**, representing the gallons per minute of water that flows through the valve with a 1 psi pressure drop. In micro-scale systems, the equivalent metric is **hydraulic resistance R_h**, typically measured in **Pa·s/m³**. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics are essential for micro valve design and are recurrent in KTU numerical problems: - **Dynamic Viscosity of Water (at 20°C):** μ = **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** P_atm = **101,325 Pa** - **Surface Tension of Water (at 20°C):** γ = **0.0728 N/m** - **Typical Microchannel Width Range:** **10 μm to 500 μm** - **Typical Diaphragm Thickness Range:** **1 μm to 50 μm** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A micro valve operates on the principle of **geometric modulation of a fluidic path**. The operational cycle is broken into three structured steps: ### Step 1: Signal Reception An external stimulus (voltage, current, pressure, temperature, or magnetic field) is applied to the actuator. For an **electrostatic valve**, this is a DC or AC voltage; for a **piezoelectric valve**, it is also voltage but with reverse electromechanical coupling. ### Step 2: Mechanical Displacement Translation The actuator converts the input energy into a **mechanical displacement (δ)** of a flexible element. This element is most commonly: - A **circular or rectangular diaphragm** (clamped at edges) - A **poppet** (a small plunger) - A **cantilever beam** The displacement is governed by the actuator's constitutive law. For example, for an electrostatic actuator: $$F_{elec} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A \cdot V^2}{g^2}$$ where: - $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the dielectric - $A$ = overlap area - $V$ = applied voltage - $g$ = air gap ### Step 3: Flow Path Modulation The mechanical displacement either: 1. **Closes** the valve seat (cutting off flow — bistable or normally-closed). 2. **Opens** a bypass channel (initiating flow — normally-open). 3. **Proportionally throttles** the flow (servo or proportional valve). > [!NOTE] > **"Why" Explanation:** A micro valve is essentially a *transducer* — it transforms an electrical (or thermal/magnetic) input into a fluidic output. The "transduction chain" is: **Energy → Mechanical Strain → Geometric Change → Flow Change**. ## 2.2 Major Actuation Mechanisms — Comparative Analysis KTU 2024 expects students to compare at least 2–3 actuation principles. Below is the high-yield comparative matrix: | Actuation Principle | Driving Force Equation | Typical Displacement | Response Time | Key Advantage | Key Limitation | |---------------------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------|---------------|----------------| | **Electrostatic** | $F = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 g^2}$ | 1 – 10 μm | μs range | Low power, fast | High voltage (50–200 V) | | **Piezoelectric** | $\delta = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot n$ | 1 – 20 μm | μs range | High force, precise | Requires multilayer stack | | **Electromagnetic** | $F = BIL$ | 50 – 500 μm | ms range | Large stroke | Needs magnetic materials | | **Thermal (Bimetallic)** | $\delta = \frac{\alpha \Delta T L^2}{2t}$ | 10 – 100 μm | 10–100 ms | Simple, large stroke | High power, slow | | **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Phase change at A_s temperature | Up to 8% strain | Seconds | Very large force | High current, fatigue | | **Pneumatic** | $F = P \cdot A$ | 10 – 1000 μm | 10–100 ms | Large force, simple | Needs external pump | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **cheat sheet** that consolidates every critical equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the Micro Valves topic. All units are SI unless otherwise specified. | # | Formula | Symbol Description | Unit | Application | |---|---------|-------------------|------|-------------| | 1 | $Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R_h}$ | Volumetric flow rate; Pressure drop; Hydraulic resistance | m³/s; Pa; Pa·s/m³ | Fundamental flow relation | | 2 | $R_h = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$ (cylindrical) | Viscosity, length, radius | Pa·s/m³ | Hagen-Poiseuille resistance | | 3 | $R_h = \frac{12 \mu L}{w h^3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{h}{w}\right)^{-1}$ (rect.) | Width, height of channel | Pa·s/m³ | Rectangular microchannel | | 4 | $\delta_{max} = \frac{P \cdot a^4}{16 D}$ (circular clamped) | Pressure, radius, flexural rigidity | m | Diaphragm deflection | | 5 | $D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Young's modulus, thickness, Poisson's ratio | N·m | Flexural rigidity | | 6 | $F_{elec} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 g^2}$ | Electrostatic force | N | Electrostatic actuator | | 7 | $Re = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | Flow regime check | | 8 | $\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Laplace pressure (capillary) | Pa | Capillary valve action | | 9 | $C_v = Q \sqrt{\frac{SG}{\Delta P}}$ | Valve flow coefficient | gpm/psi^½ | Sizing metric | | 10 | $f_{nat} = \frac{10.21}{2\pi a^2} \sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho t}}$ | Natural frequency of clamped circular plate | Hz | Dynamic response | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Isolation Rule:** Every `$$` block above is separated by one blank line above and below to comply with the markdown rendering protocol. Additionally, notice that absolute-value bars have been replaced with `\vert` or descriptive text to prevent markdown table breakage. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications Micro valves are **mission-critical** in the following industries: 1. **Biomedical Microdevices:** Lab-on-chip (LoC) platforms for drug delivery, DNA sequencing, and point-of-care diagnostics. The **ABI 7500 PCR system** uses micro valves for nanoliter reagent dispensing. 2. **Inkjet Printing:** Hewlett-Packard's **Thermal InkJet (TIJ)** cartridges use thin-film resistors to nucleate vapor bubbles that act as passive micro valves. 3. **Automotive MEMS:** Bosch and Infineon manufacture **micro valves for fuel injection** in direct-injection engines — pressures reach 2000 bar. 4. **Analytical Chemistry:** Gas chromatography (GC) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) systems use micro valves for sample injection loops. 5. **Spacecraft Propulsion:** NASA's **Digital Micro Propulsion System** uses an array of micro valves for precise thrust control in CubeSats. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When explaining the engineering utility in exams, always mention the *trade-off* between response time, power consumption, and stroke length. This shows the examiner that you understand the design optimization process, not just the theory. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydraulic Resistance of a Rectangular Microchannel This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU MEMS examinations. It forms the basis for sizing micro valves and computing pressure drops. ### Given: A rectangular microchannel of width $w$, height $h$, and length $L$ carries an incompressible Newtonian fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu$ under a pressure gradient $\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$. ### Assumptions: 1. Steady-state, laminar flow (since $Re \ll 2300$ in microchannels). 2. No-slip condition at the walls. 3. Fully developed flow (entrance effects neglected). 4. Incompressible fluid. ### Starting Point — Navier-Stokes Equation (x-direction): For fully developed laminar flow, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to: $$\mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$ **Step 1: Recognize that** $\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$ is a **constant** (since flow is fully developed, pressure gradient is linear in x). **Step 2: Define** the pressure gradient constant as: $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$ This is a negative number (pressure drops in flow direction), so we write it as $-\frac{\Delta P}{L}$. **Step 3: Apply boundary conditions:** The no-slip condition requires the velocity to be zero at all four walls: - $u = 0$ at $y = 0$ and $y = h$ - $u = 0$ at $z = 0$ and $z = w$ **Step 4: Solve using the separable approach** (for $h \ll w$, assume infinite parallel plates and solve 1D): For a wide, thin channel where $w \gg h$, the velocity profile is parabolic in $y$: $$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot y(h-y)$$ **Step 5: Integrate to find volumetric flow rate:** $$Q = \int_0^h \int_0^w u(y) \, dz \, dy$$ $$Q = w \int_0^h \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot y(h-y) \, dy$$ **Step 6: Evaluate the integral:** $$\int_0^h y(h-y) \, dy = \left[ \frac{h y^2}{2} - \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_0^h = \frac{h^3}{2} - \frac{h^3}{3} = \frac{h^3}{6}$$ **Step 7: Substitute and simplify:** $$Q = w \cdot \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \frac{h^3}{6}$$ $$Q = \frac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$ **Step 8: Rearrange into hydraulic resistance form:** $$Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R_h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_h = \frac{12 \mu L}{w h^3}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer (Wide Channel Approximation):** The hydraulic resistance of a wide rectangular microchannel is: > $$R_h = \frac{12 \mu L}{w h^3}$$ > For a non-wide channel (arbitrary aspect ratio), the correction factor is: > $$R_h = \frac{12 \mu L}{w h^3} \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{h}{w} \right]^{-1}$$ > (valid for $h/w < 0.5$). ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Deflection of a Clamped Circular Diaphragm This derivation is essential for designing **diaphragm-actuated micro valves**. ### Given: A circular diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $t$ is clamped at its edges. It is subjected to a uniform differential pressure $P$ across its surface. ### Starting Point — Plate Theory Equation: For small deflections ($w \ll t$), the governing differential equation is: $$D \nabla^4 w = P$$ where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity**: $$D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$ **Step 1: Recognize the symmetry** — Use polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$: $$\nabla^4 w = \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\}$$ **Step 2: Apply boundary conditions** for clamped edges: - At $r = a$ (edge): $w = 0$ and $\frac{dw}{dr} = 0$ - At $r = 0$ (center): $w$ is finite and $\frac{dw}{dr} = 0$ **Step 3: Standard result from plate theory** (Boresi & Schmidt): The maximum deflection occurs at the center ($r = 0$): $$\delta_{max} = \frac{P a^4}{64 D}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Many students incorrectly write $\frac{P a^4}{16 D}$ — that formula is for a *simply-supported* diaphragm, not a clamped one. The factor is **64**, not 16, for clamped edges. Examiners will deduct **2 marks** for this error. **Step 4: Substitute the flexural rigidity:** $$\delta_{max} = \frac{P a^4 \cdot 12(1-\nu^2)}{64 E t^3}$$ $$\delta_{max} = \frac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Design Insight:** The deflection scales with $a^4$ (radius to the fourth power). Doubling the diaphragm radius **increases the deflection by 16×**. This is why micro valves favor small diaphragms for tight control. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrostatic Actuation Force This is the fundamental equation for the **most common actuation mechanism** in MEMS micro valves. ### Given: Two parallel plates of area $A$ separated by gap $g$, with a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ filling the gap. Voltage $V$ is applied. ### Derivation: **Step 1: Energy stored in the capacitor:** $$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g} \cdot V^2$$ **Step 2: Force is the negative gradient of energy with respect to gap:** $$F = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial g} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial g} \left[ \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 g} \right]$$ **Step 3: Perform the differentiation:** $$F = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{g^2} \right)$$ **Step 4: Final expression:** $$F = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 g^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** The force scales with $V^2$ (quadratic). To double the force, you must **increase the voltage by √2 ≈ 1.41 times**, not 2 times. Also, the force is **always attractive** for electrostatic actuators (pull-in instability occurs when $g$ reduces to 1/3 of initial value). ## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A microfluidic normally-closed micro valve uses an electrostatic actuator. The diaphragm is circular with $a = 500 \, \mu m$, $t = 10 \, \mu m$, and is made of silicon (Young's modulus $E = 170 \, GPa$, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.28$). The valve is required to deflect by $\delta = 5 \, \mu m$ to fully open the orifice. Calculate the required pressure difference across the diaphragm. ### Solution: **Step 1: Calculate flexural rigidity:** $$D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{(170 \times 10^9)(10 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12(1 - 0.28^2)}$$ $$D = \frac{(170 \times 10^9)(10^{-15})}{12(1 - 0.0784)} = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-4}}{12 \times 0.9216}$$ $$D = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-4}}{11.0592} = 1.537 \times 10^{-5} \, N \cdot m$$ **Step 2: Rearrange the deflection formula to solve for P:** $$\delta_{max} = \frac{P a^4}{64 D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac{64 D \delta_{max}}{a^4}$$ **Step 3: Substitute values:** $$P = \frac{64 \times (1.537 \times 10^{-5}) \times (5 \times 10^{-6})}{(500 \times 10^{-6})^4}$$ **Step 4: Calculate numerator:** $$64 \times 1.537 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.918 \times 10^{-9}$$ **Step 5: Calculate denominator:** $$(500 \times 10^{-6})^4 = (5 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 625 \times 10^{-16} = 6.25 \times 10^{-14}$$ **Step 6: Final pressure:** $$P = \frac{4.918 \times 10^{-9}}{6.25 \times 10^{-14}} = 78,688 \, Pa \approx 78.7 \, kPa$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** A pressure difference of approximately **78.7 kPa** (about 0.78 atm) is required to actuate the valve. This is well within the operating range of standard laboratory syringe pumps. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Micro Valve Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[External Stimulus
Voltage, Pressure, Magnetic Field] B[Actuator Mechanism
Electrostatic, Piezoelectric, SMA, Pneumatic] C[Mechanical Coupling
Lever, Diaphragm, Cantilever] D[Seal Element
Poppet, Gasket, O-ring] E[Fluid Inlet Port
Upstream Microchannel] F[Fluid Outlet Port
Downstream Microchannel] G[Flow Path Modulation
Open, Closed, Throttled] H[Output Flow Condition
Q, P, Re Controlled] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> G E --> G G --> F G --> H I[Sensor Feedback
Pressure, Flow, Position] --> B I -.Optional.-> B ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD MV[Micro Valves] AV[Active Valves] PV[Passive Valves] EL[Electrostatic] PZ[Piezoelectric] EM[Electromagnetic] TH[Thermal Bimetallic] SM[SMA Based] PN[Pneumatic] CK[Check Valves] CP[Capillary Valves] BV[Burstable Valves] SV[Squeeze Valves] MV --> AV MV --> PV AV --> EL AV --> PZ AV --> EM AV --> TH AV --> SM AV --> PN PV --> CK PV --> CP PV --> BV PV --> SV ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operating State Transition ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Closed Closed --> Opening: Actuator Energized Opening --> Open: Diaphragm Deflects Beyond Threshold Open --> Throttling: Partial Signal Applied Throttling --> Open: Full Signal Restored Open --> Closing: Actuator De-energized Closing --> Closed: Diaphragm Returns to Rest Closed --> [*] note right of Closed: Normally Closed State note right of Open: Fully Open State note right of Throttling: Proportional Control State ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Description — Typical Electrostatic Diaphragm Micro Valve > [!NOTE] > The following textual schematic is a **block-level functional architecture flow** describing the cross-section of a typical normally-closed electrostatic diaphragm micro valve. This representation is used in lieu of physical CAD drawings, which cannot be rendered in this format. **Layer Stack (from bottom to top):** - **Layer 1 — Substrate:** Silicon wafer (thickness = 500 μm). Contains the **fluid inlet** and **fluid outlet** ports etched from the backside using deep reactive ion etching (DRIE). - **Layer 2 — Lower Electrode:** A thin gold or aluminum film (200 nm) patterned on the substrate. Serves as the **fixed electrode** of the electrostatic actuator. - **Layer 3 — Spacer / Dielectric:** A patterned photoresist or silicon dioxide layer (thickness = 2 μm) that defines the **air gap** between the electrodes. - **Layer 4 — Diaphragm:** A thin silicon or polymer membrane (thickness = 5–20 μm) bonded on top. This is the **movable electrode** and the **seal element**. - **Layer 5 — Cover:** A glass or Pyrex cap with etched microchannels that form the **upstream and downstream fluidic paths**. **Operation Cycle:** 1. **No voltage applied:** The diaphragm rests on the orifice (seat) — the valve is **normally closed**. 2. **Voltage applied (e.g., 80 V):** Electrostatic attraction pulls the diaphragm downward, lifting it off the seat — fluid flows from inlet to outlet. 3. **Voltage reduced:** Elastic restoring force of the diaphragm returns it to the seat — the valve **closes**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1] [3 Marks]** > **Q1.** Define a micro valve. Differentiate between **active** and **passive** micro valves with one example each. **Model Answer:** A **micro valve** is a MEMS device that regulates the flow of fluids (gases or liquids) in micro-scale channels, typically fabricated using microfabrication techniques with characteristic dimensions in the range of 1 μm to 1000 μm. | Aspect | Active Valve | Passive Valve | |--------|--------------|---------------| | **Energy Source** | Requires external input (electrical, thermal, magnetic) | Self-actuated; uses fluid/flow properties | | **Control** | Externally controllable | No external control | | **Example** | Electrostatic diaphragm valve | Check valve (no-return valve) | > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2] [3 Marks]** > **Q2.** List any **three actuation mechanisms** used in active micro valves. State the governing equation for the force/displacement produced by each. **Model Answer:** 1. **Electrostatic:** $F = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 g^2}$ 2. **Piezoelectric:** $\delta = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V$ 3. **Electromagnetic:** $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern] [14 Marks]** > > ### **Question A:** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the working of an **electrostatic diaphragm micro valve**. Derive the expression for the electrostatic force and the maximum diaphragm deflection. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A rectangular microchannel of width $w = 200 \, \mu m$, height $h = 50 \, \mu m$, and length $L = 10 \, mm$ carries water ($\mu = 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$). Calculate the **(i)** hydraulic resistance, and **(ii)** volumetric flow rate for a pressure drop of $\Delta P = 1 \, kPa$. ### Model Solution — Question A: #### Part (a) Solution: **Schematic Description:** An electrostatic diaphragm micro valve consists of: - A **fixed bottom electrode** (gold/chrome) on a silicon substrate. - A **spacer layer** defining an air gap (1–5 μm). - A **flexible diaphragm** (silicon, polysilicon, or polymer) acting as the movable top electrode. - A **glass cover** with fluidic inlet and outlet ports. - The **valve seat** is a precisely etched orifice in the substrate. **Working Principle:** In the **resting (no-voltage)** state, the diaphragm lies flat against the seat due to its own elastic restoring force, blocking fluid flow (normally-closed configuration). When a **DC voltage (typically 50–200 V)** is applied between the diaphragm and the bottom electrode, **electrostatic attraction** pulls the diaphragm downward toward the substrate. The diaphragm deflects by an amount $\delta$, lifting off the seat and **opening the fluidic path**. When the voltage is removed, the elastic strain energy stored in the deformed diaphragm restores it to its original position, **closing the valve**. **Electrostatic Force Derivation:** Energy stored in the parallel-plate capacitor: $$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 g}$$ Force is the negative gradient of energy with respect to the air gap $g$: $$F = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial g} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 g^2}$$ **Diaphragm Deflection Derivation:** For a circular diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $t$ under uniform pressure $P$: $$\delta_{max} = \frac{P a^4}{64 D} \quad \text{where} \quad D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$ #### Part (b) Solution: **Given:** $w = 200 \, \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, m$, $h = 50 \, \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, m$, $L = 10 \, mm = 0.01 \, m$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$, $\Delta P = 10^3 \, Pa$. **(i) Hydraulic Resistance:** Since $w \gg h$, we use the wide-channel approximation: $$R_h = \frac{12 \mu L}{w h^3}$$ $$R_h = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.01}{(2 \times 10^{-4}) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})^3}$$ $$R_h = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{(2 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1.25 \times 10^{-13})}$$ $$R_h = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-17}} = 4.8 \times 10^{12} \, Pa \cdot s / m^3$$ **(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate:** $$Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R_h} = \frac{10^3}{4.8 \times 10^{12}} = 2.08 \times 10^{-10} \, m^3/s$$ Converting to μL/min: $$Q = 2.08 \times 10^{-10} \times 10^9 \times 60 = 12.5 \, \mu L / min$$ > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern] [14 Marks]** > > ### **Question B:** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **piezoelectric, electromagnetic, and thermal bimetallic** actuation mechanisms for micro valves. Discuss the merits and demerits of each in at least **three** comparison parameters. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A **capillary (passive) micro valve** has a microchannel of radius $r = 100 \, \mu m$. The fluid is water with surface tension $\gamma = 0.0728 \, N/m$ and contact angle $\theta = 30°$. Calculate the **Laplace pressure** required to initiate flow. ### Model Solution — Question B: #### Part (a) Solution: | Parameter | Piezoelectric | Electromagnetic | Thermal Bimetallic | |-----------|---------------|-----------------|--------------------| | **Working Principle** | Reverse piezoelectric effect: voltage causes strain in crystals like PZT | Lorentz force: $F = BIL$ on current-carrying conductor in magnetic field | Differential thermal expansion of two bonded metals with different $\alpha$ | | **Stroke / Displacement** | 1 – 20 μm (limited) | 50 – 500 μm (large) | 10 – 100 μm (moderate) | | **Response Time** | μs (very fast) | ms (moderate) | 10 – 100 ms (slow) | | **Force Output** | Very high (up to N range) | Moderate | Low | | **Operating Voltage** | 10 – 200 V | 1 – 24 V (low) | 1 – 5 V (very low) | | **Power Consumption** | Low (capacitive) | Moderate | High (continuous heating) | | **Key Advantage** | High precision and force | Large stroke, low voltage | Simple fabrication, large stroke | | **Key Limitation** | Small stroke, hysteresis | Requires magnetic materials, bulky | High power, slow thermal response | #### Part (b) Solution: **Given:** $r = 100 \, \mu m = 10^{-4} \, m$, $\gamma = 0.0728 \, N/m$, $\theta = 30°$. **Laplace Pressure Equation:** $$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$ **Substituting values:** $$\cos(30°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$ $$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 0.866}{10^{-4}}$$ $$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{0.1261}{10^{-4}} = 1261 \, Pa \approx 1.26 \, kPa$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Units Mistake:** Forgetting to convert μm to m is the most common error (deduct **1 mark**). > 2. **Angle in Degrees vs. Radians:** Scientific calculators default to degrees — ensure your calculator is in the correct mode (deduct **1 mark** if not). > 3. **Sign of $\cos\theta$:** If $\theta > 90°$, $\cos\theta$ becomes negative, indicating **repulsive capillary action** — the fluid will not spontaneously flow (deduct **2 marks** if this physics is missed). > 4. **Confusing Clamped vs. Simply-Supported Diaphragms:** Use $a^4/64D$ for clamped, $a^4/16D$ for simply-supported (deduct **2 marks**). ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU OEAET722 Module 1: Micro Valves** - ✅ **Definition:** Micro valves are MEMS devices that regulate fluid flow in microchannels (1–1000 μm characteristic dimensions). - ✅ **Two Master Categories:** Active (externally controlled) and Passive (self-regulating). - ✅ **Common Active Types:** Electrostatic, Piezoelectric, Electromagnetic, SMA, Thermal Bimetallic, Pneumatic. - ✅ **Common Passive Types:** Check valves, Capillary valves, Burstable valves, Squeeze valves. - ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille for Circular Channels:** $R_h = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$. - ✅ **Rectangular Microchannel (Wide):** $R_h = \frac{12 \mu L}{w h^3}$. - ✅ **Clamped Diaphragm Deflection:** $\delta_{max} = \frac{P a^4}{64 D}$ (NOT $a^4/16D$). - ✅ **Flexural Rigidity:** $D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$. - ✅ **Electrostatic Force:** $F = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 g^2}$ (quadratic in V, attractive only). - ✅ **Laplace Pressure (Capillary):** $\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$. - ✅ **Reynolds Number Check:** Microchannel flow is almost always **laminar** ($Re < 2300$). - ✅ **Pull-in Instability:** Electrostatic actuators become unstable when $g$ reduces to **1/3** of initial value. - ✅ **Most Common Fabrication:** Silicon micromachining (DRIE, KOH etching), PDMS soft lithography, glass/silicon anodic bonding. - ✅ **Real-World Apps:** Lab-on-chip, inkjet printing, fuel injection, drug delivery, analytical chemistry (GC/HPLC), spacecraft propulsion. - ✅ **Trade-offs to Always Discuss:** Stroke vs. force, response time vs. power, voltage vs. displacement. - ✅ **Material Constants:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, F/m$, water $\mu \approx 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$, silicon $E = 170 \, GPa$.

topics.title: Micro Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Micro Pump** is a microscale fluidic actuator (typically fabricated using MEMS-compatible processes such as silicon bulk micromachining, surface micromachining, or polymer soft lithography) that converts an input energy form — electrical, mechanical, piezoelectric, electrostatic, electromagnetic, or chemical — into a controlled, directional displacement or flow of a working fluid, generally in the volumetric range of **nL/min to mL/min**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A micro pump is formally classified as a MEMS *microactuator* whose output variable is *fluidic energy* (pressure × flow), as opposed to mechanical force, motion, or thermal output.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, micro pumps sit at the intersection of **Microfluidics** and **Microactuation** under Module 1. They are essential building blocks of any complete *Lab-on-a-Chip* (LoC) or *Micro Total Analysis System* (μTAS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **medical syringe** held in your hand. You press the plunger, and liquid shoots out the nozzle. Now shrink that syringe to the size of a *grain of rice*, drive the plunger not with your thumb but with a **piezoelectric crystal that bends when you apply 10 V**, and etch two tiny *one-way swing doors* (check valves) at the inlet and outlet. That, in essence, is a **micropump**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A micro pump = a micro-scale reciprocating piston + a method to wiggle the piston + two micro-valves that ensure the fluid only flows in *one* direction.

- A standard **macroscopic pump** moves liters per minute (e.g., ~**10⁻³ m³/s**).
- A **micro pump** moves microliters per minute (~**10⁻¹¹ m³/s**).
- This represents a **reduction in volumetric throughput of 8 orders of magnitude**.

> [!TIP]
> The crucial engineering point is that *scaling laws* change. Surface tension (∝ L¹), viscous forces (∝ L²), and inertia (∝ L⁴) scale differently — making fluid dynamics at the micro-scale *fundamentally* different from the macro world.

### 1.3 Why Micro Pumps Matter — KTU Application Context

| Application Domain | Function of Micro Pump |
|---|---|
| **Drug Delivery Implants** | Continuous insulin / chemotherapy infusion |
| **Lab-on-Chip Diagnostics** | Reagent mixing & sample transport |
| **Microelectronic Cooling** | Forced convection of dielectric coolant |
| **Biomedical Wearables** | Sweat sampling & analysis |
| **Chemical Micro-reactors** | Controlled reactant dosing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Whenever an exam question asks *"Why is a micro pump required when a syringe pump can do the same job?"*, the answer must always mention **portability, integration, low dead-volume (< 1 μL), low power (< 100 mW), and biocompatibility** — these are the *four pillars* of micro pump superiority.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification Taxonomy (KTU 2024 Module 1)

```
MICRO PUMPS
├── MECHANICAL / DISPLACEMENT PUMPS
│   ├── Reciprocating (Diaphragm) Pumps
│   │   ├── Piezoelectric actuation
│   │   ├── Electrostatic actuation
│   │   └── Electromagnetic actuation
│   ├── Peristaltic Pumps
│   └── Check-valve / Valveless (Diffuser–Nozzle) Pumps
└── NON-MECHANICAL / DYNAMIC PUMPS
    ├── Electroosmotic Pumps
    ├── Electromagnetic (Magneto-hydrodynamic) Pumps
    ├── Electrohydrodynamic Pumps
    ├── Surface-tension (Capillary) Pumps
    ├── Acoustic (Ultrasonic) Pumps
    └── Centrifugal Pumps
```

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

#### A. Reciprocating Diaphragm Pump (Most KTU-Examined Variant)

1. **Diaphragm at rest** — fluid surrounds the pump chamber.
2. **Actuator energised** — diaphragm deflects *downward*, increasing chamber pressure.
3. **Inlet check valve CLOSES** (high pressure side); **outlet check valve OPENS**.
4. **Fluid displaced** out through the outlet.
5. **Actuator de-energised** — diaphragm returns; chamber pressure drops below inlet pressure.
6. **Inlet check valve OPENS**; **outlet check valve CLOSES**.
7. **Fresh fluid drawn in** — completing one cycle (stroke).

#### B. Diffuser–Nozzle (Valveless) Pump

1. The diffuser element has an **asymmetric fluidic resistance** depending on flow direction.
2. Flow entering from the *narrow* end experiences a low resistance (R_low).
3. Flow entering from the *wide* end experiences a high resistance (R_high).
4. With a *reciprocating* diaphragm, fluid prefers to *exit* through the diffuser and *enter* through the nozzle.
5. Net DC flow is produced **without any moving valve element**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Valveless pumps are preferred in **biomedical applications** because mechanical check valves suffer from *fatigue*, *clogging*, and *dead volume*. Diffuser–nozzle pumps solve all three issues simultaneously.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization Block** — the following equations are the **5 most-asked formulas** in KTU MEMS papers. All must be derived from first principles in the derivations section below.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diaphragm centre deflection (uniform pressure, edge-clamped circular) | $w_{max} = \dfrac{P \cdot a^4}{64 \, D}$ | $P$ = pressure (Pa); $a$ = diaphragm radius (m); $D$ = flexural rigidity (N·m) |
| 2 | Flexural Rigidity of Diaphragm | $D = \dfrac{E \, t^3}{12 \, (1 - \nu^2)}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa); $t$ = diaphragm thickness (m); $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 3 | Displacement Volume per Stroke | $\Delta V = \int_0^{a} 2\pi r \, w(r) \, dr$ | $r$ = radial coordinate; $w(r)$ = local deflection |
| 4 | Volumetric Flow Rate of Reciprocating Pump | $Q = \Delta V \cdot f \cdot \eta_v$ | $f$ = actuation frequency (Hz); $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency (0.5–0.9) |
| 5 | Electroosmotic Flow Rate (Smoluchowski) | $Q = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, \zeta \, A \, V}{\eta \, L}$ | $\varepsilon$ = permittivity (F/m); $\zeta$ = zeta potential (V); $A$ = cross-section (m²); $V$ = voltage (V); $\eta$ = viscosity (Pa·s); $L$ = channel length (m) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Equations (1) and (2) govern *all* diaphragm-based micropump design — from insulin pumps (Medtronic MiniMed) to inkjet printheads (HP Thermal Inkjet).

### 2.4 The Pressure–Flow Characteristic Curve

Every micro pump has a *characteristic line* on a **Pressure (ΔP) vs. Flow (Q)** plot:

$$
Q = Q_0 - K_p \, \Delta P
$$

- $Q_0$ = *zero-pressure* flow rate (maximum theoretical throughput).
- $K_p$ = *fluidic conductance* coefficient (slope of the line).
- $\Delta P$ = back pressure the pump must overcome.

> [!NOTE]
> At the **shut-off head** ($\Delta P_{max}$), the flow becomes zero: $\Delta P_{max} = Q_0 / K_p$. This is a standard KTU numerical problem type.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Diaphragm Centre Deflection for a Circular Edge-Clamped Plate

**Starting Principle:** A thin circular diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $t$, edge-clamped along its periphery, and subjected to a uniform pressure $P$ across its surface, deflects according to the small-deflection plate-bending equation.

**Step 1 — The Plate Bending Equation (axisymmetric form):**

The classical Kirchhoff–Love plate theory gives, for axisymmetric loading:

$$
D \, \nabla^4 w(r) = P
$$

where $\nabla^4$ is the *biharmonic operator* and $D$ is the flexural rigidity (Equation 2 from the formula sheet).

**Step 2 — In polar coordinates, the biharmonic operator simplifies to:**

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left\{ r \frac{d}{dr}\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dw}{dr}\right)\right] \right\} = \frac{P}{D}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate once (with respect to $r$):**

$$
r \frac{d}{dr}\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dw}{dr}\right)\right] = \frac{P \, r^2}{2D} + C_1
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $r$ and integrate again:**

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dw}{dr}\right) = \frac{P \, r^2}{4D} + C_1 \ln(r) + C_2
$$

**Step 5 — Since the deflection is finite at $r=0$, the singularity term $C_1 \ln(r)$ must vanish; hence $C_1 = 0$. Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate once more:**

$$
r \frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{P \, r^4}{16 D} + C_2 \, \frac{r^2}{2} + C_3
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate a final time to get the deflection profile:**

$$
w(r) = \frac{P \, r^4}{64 D} + C_2 \, \frac{r^2}{4} + C_3 \ln(r) + C_4
$$

**Step 7 — Apply boundary conditions:**

- **(i) Finite at centre:** $C_3 = 0$.
- **(ii) Edge-clamped — zero deflection at the rim:** $w(a) = 0$.

$$
0 = \frac{P \, a^4}{64 D} + C_2 \, \frac{a^2}{4} + C_4
$$

- **(iii) Edge-clamped — zero slope at the rim:** $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=a} = 0$.

$$
0 = \frac{P \, a^3}{16 D} + C_2 \, \frac{a}{2}
$$

From (iii):

$$
C_2 = -\frac{P \, a^2}{8 D}
$$

Substitute $C_2$ into (ii) to obtain $C_4$:

$$
C_4 = -\frac{P \, a^4}{64 D} + \frac{P \, a^4}{32 D} = \frac{P \, a^4}{64 D}
$$

**Step 8 — The full deflection profile:**

$$
w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D} \left( a^2 - r^2 \right)^2
$$

**Step 9 — Evaluate at the centre ($r=0$) to obtain the maximum deflection:**

$$
\boxed{\,w_{max} = \frac{P \, a^4}{64 D}\,}
$$

This is **Equation 1** of the formula sheet. The derivation is complete. Notice the fourth-power dependence on the radius — a *doubling* of the diaphragm radius causes a **16×** increase in centre deflection. This is the geometric lever that allows MEMS devices to deliver significant stroke volume from very small pressures.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Displacement Volume per Stroke

**Objective:** Find the total volume of fluid displaced when the diaphragm deflects by the profile $w(r)$.

**Step 1 — Set up the volume integral.** A differential annular ring at radius $r$ of width $dr$ has area $2\pi r \, dr$. Multiplied by the local deflection $w(r)$, it gives the differential displaced volume:

$$
dV_d = 2\pi r \, w(r) \, dr
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from centre ($r=0$) to the rim ($r=a$):**

$$
V_d = \int_0^{a} 2\pi r \cdot \frac{P}{64 D} \left( a^2 - r^2 \right)^2 dr
$$

**Step 3 — Pull out the constants:**

$$
V_d = \frac{\pi P}{32 D} \int_0^{a} r \, \left( a^2 - r^2 \right)^2 dr
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the square inside the integral:**

$$
\left( a^2 - r^2 \right)^2 = a^4 - 2a^2 r^2 + r^4
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and integrate term by term:**

$$
\int_0^{a} r \left( a^4 - 2a^2 r^2 + r^4 \right) dr = \int_0^{a} \left( a^4 r - 2 a^2 r^3 + r^5 \right) dr
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate each term:**

$$
\left[ \frac{a^4 r^2}{2} - \frac{2 a^2 r^4}{4} + \frac{r^6}{6} \right]_0^{a} = \frac{a^6}{2} - \frac{a^6}{2} + \frac{a^6}{6} = \frac{a^6}{6}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_d = \frac{\pi P \, a^6}{192 \, D}\,}
$$

This is the *fundamental MEMS design equation* for any diaphragm-driven micropump. Notice the **sixth-power dependence** on the diaphragm radius — geometric scaling is *very* powerful at the micro scale.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Flow Rate of a Reciprocating Pump

**Step 1 — Each full electrical cycle produces one full mechanical cycle (one suction + one discharge stroke).**

**Step 2 — In a *lossless* pump, the average flow rate equals the displaced volume times the actuation frequency:**

$$
Q_{ideal} = V_d \cdot f
$$

**Step 3 — In a *real* pump, leakage, dead volume, and valve back-flow losses reduce the effective throughput. We model this with a volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q = V_d \cdot f \cdot \eta_v\,}
$$

This is **Equation 4** of the formula sheet.

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Electroosmotic Flow Rate (Smoluchowski Equation)

**Step 1 — Setting up the problem.** A microchannel of length $L$ and cross-section $A$ is filled with an electrolyte. An electric field $E = V/L$ is applied along the channel.

**Step 2 — The surface of the channel carries a *zeta potential* $\zeta$ (typically $\vert\zeta\vert \approx$ **25–100 mV** for glass or PDMS).**

**Step 3 — The electric field exerts a force on the *excess counter-ions* in the Electric Double Layer (EDL). These ions drag the bulk fluid via viscous coupling, producing a *plug-like* flow profile (not Poiseuille parabolic).

**Step 4 — The Helmholtz–Smoluchowski slip velocity at the wall is:**

$$
u_s = \frac{\varepsilon \, \zeta \, E}{\eta} = \frac{\varepsilon \, \zeta \, V}{\eta \, L}
$$

**Step 5 — Since the flow is plug-like, the average velocity equals the slip velocity. Therefore, the volumetric flow rate is:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q = u_s \cdot A = \frac{\varepsilon \, \zeta \, A \, V}{\eta \, L}\,}
$$

This is **Equation 5** of the formula sheet. It is *the* foundational equation of all electrically-driven microfluidic transport.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A silicon diaphragm micro pump has a circular diaphragm of radius $a = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, thickness $t = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m, with $E = 170$ GPa, $\nu = 0.28$. During actuation, a uniform pressure of $P = 5$ kPa is applied. If the actuation frequency is $f = 100$ Hz and volumetric efficiency is $\eta_v = 0.75$, calculate the average flow rate in μL/min.

**Solution:**

$$
D = \frac{E \, t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{(170 \times 10^9) \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12 (1 - 0.28^2)}
$$

$$
D = \frac{170 \times 10^9 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-13}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{0.02125}{11.059} = 1.922 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
w_{max} = \frac{P \, a^4}{64 D} = \frac{(5000) \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^4}{64 \times 1.922 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
w_{max} = \frac{5000 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-11}}{0.1230} = 6.504 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.6504 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{\pi P a^6}{192 D} = \frac{\pi \times 5000 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^6}{192 \times 1.922 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{15708 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-17}}{0.3690} = 2.724 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^3 = 2.724 \text{ nL}
$$

$$
Q = V_d \cdot f \cdot \eta_v = 2.724 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 0.75
$$

$$
Q = 2.043 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 12.26 \text{ μL/min}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The entire numerical problem reduces to *plug-and-chug* into the two boxed formulas: $V_d = \pi P a^6 / (192 D)$ and $Q = V_d f \eta_v$. This is the **exact pattern** of KTU 14-mark numerical questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Diaphragm Micro Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwr[DC Power Supply] --> drv[Driver Circuit]
    drv --> act[Micro Actuator]
    act --> dia[Silicon Diaphragm]
    dia --> chm[Pump Chamber]
    chm --> in1[Inlet Check Valve]
    chm --> out1[Outlet Check Valve]
    in1 --> src[Reservoir or Inlet Channel]
    out1 --> dst[Outlet Channel or Biofluidic Target]
    dia -.feedback.-> ctl[Closed Loop Pressure Sensor]
    ctl -.control signal.-> drv
    style pwr fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style act fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
    style dia fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style chm fill:#833c0c,color:#ffffff
    style ctl fill:#2e5395,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Operation Sequence of a Diaphragm Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    state1[State 1: Diaphragm at Rest Position]
    state2[State 2: Diaphragm Deflects Downward]
    state3[State 3: Outward Stroke Chamber Volume Decreases]
    state4[State 4: Diaphragm Returns to Rest Position]
    state5[State 5: Inward Stroke Chamber Volume Increases]
    state1 --> state2 --> state3 --> state4 --> state5 --> state1
    style state1 fill:#999999,color:#ffffff
    style state2 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    state3_Note[/Outlet valve OPEN, Inlet valve CLOSED/] -.linked.-> state3
    state4_Note[/Pressure drops below inlet line pressure/] -.linked.-> state4
    state5_Note[/Outlet valve CLOSED, Inlet valve OPEN/] -.linked.-> state5
```

### 4.3 Comparative Decision Map — Selecting the Right Micro Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Application Requires Micro Pump] --> q1{Is fluid conductive?}
    q1 -- YES --> q2{High voltage available?}
    q1 -- NO --> q3{Need moving parts?}
    q2 -- YES --> eo[Electroosmotic Pump]
    q2 -- NO --> ehd[Electrohydrodynamic Pump]
    q3 -- YES --> q4{High pressure needed?}
    q3 -- NO --> cap[Capillary or Surface Tension Pump]
    q4 -- YES --> piezo[Piezoelectric Diaphragm Pump]
    q4 -- NO --> peri[Peristaltic or Diffuser Nozzle Pump]
    style start fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style eo fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style ehd fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
    style cap fill:#833c0c,color:#ffffff
    style piezo fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style peri fill:#833c0c,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Diffuser–Nozzle Element (Valveless Pump Building Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Flow Direction A: Nozzle Mode]
    inB[Flow Direction B: Diffuser Mode]
    element[Asymmetric Wedge Geometry]
    inA --> element
    element --> inB
    element -- High Resistance Path --> rHigh[R_d high when flow enters wide end]
    element -- Low Resistance Path --> rLow[R_d low when flow enters narrow end]
    style inA fill:#2e5395,color:#ffffff
    style inB fill:#833c0c,color:#ffffff
    style element fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]  
**Define a micro pump. List any two actuation principles used in mechanical micro pumps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **micro pump** is a microscale fluidic device that delivers controlled volumes of fluid, typically in the range of **nL/min to mL/min**, by converting an input energy form (electrical, mechanical, chemical, or thermal) into a directional fluid flow. It is fabricated using MEMS-compatible processes such as silicon bulk micromachining, surface micromachining, or polymer replication.

Two actuation principles used in mechanical micro pumps are:

1. **Piezoelectric actuation** — A piezo disc bonded to the diaphragm bends when a voltage is applied, producing a reciprocating stroke.
2. **Electrostatic actuation** — Parallel-plate electrodes attract each other when energised, deflecting the diaphragm.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Electromagnetic, Thermopneumatic, Shape-memory-alloy.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**Distinguish between a check-valve micro pump and a valveless (diffuser–nozzle) micro pump. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Check-Valve Pump | Valveless (Diffuser–Nozzle) Pump |
|---|---|---|
| **Moving valve elements** | Yes (suspended flaps or balls) | No |
| **Dead volume** | Higher (~μL scale) | Negligible |
| **Resistance to particle clogging** | Poor (valves jam) | Excellent (no moving parts) |
| **Biocompatibility for cell handling** | Limited | Excellent |
| **Maximum back pressure** | Higher | Lower |

**Advantage of check-valve pump:** Higher achievable back pressure, suitable for high-resistance loads such as needle injection.  
**Advantage of valveless pump:** No mechanical fatigue, ideal for long-term implantable biomedical applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Piezoelectric Diaphragm Micro Pump Design

**Q3(a).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]  
**(7 marks)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the working of a **piezoelectric diaphragm micro pump with two check valves**. Discuss the role of the piezo actuator in determining the pump flow rate.

**Q3(b).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]  
**(7 marks)** A silicon diaphragm micro pump has the following specifications:

- Diaphragm radius: $a = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Diaphragm thickness: $t = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Young's modulus of silicon: $E = 170$ GPa
- Poisson's ratio: $\nu = 0.28$
- Applied uniform pressure: $P = 8$ kPa
- Actuation frequency: $f = 200$ Hz
- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_v = 0.80$

**Calculate:**  
(i) The flexural rigidity $D$ of the diaphragm.  
(ii) The maximum centre deflection $w_{max}$.  
(iii) The displaced volume per stroke $V_d$.  
(iv) The average flow rate $Q$ in μL/min.

---

### **Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 marks**

**Working (5 marks):**
