1. The pump consists of a **pump chamber** bounded above by a thin silicon **diaphragm** and below by a **piezoelectric disc** (typically PZT or lead-zirconate-titanate).
2. The chamber is connected to an **inlet reservoir** through an *inlet check valve* and to a **discharge outlet** through an *outlet check valve*.
3. **Discharge stroke:** When a voltage is applied, the piezo disc expands and pushes the diaphragm *downward*, increasing the chamber pressure. The **inlet check valve closes** (back-pressure) and the **outlet check valve opens**, expelling fluid.
4. **Suction stroke:** When the voltage is removed, the piezo disc relaxes, the diaphragm returns upward, chamber pressure drops below the inlet line, the **outlet valve closes**, and the **inlet valve opens**, drawing fresh fluid in.
5. A complete *AC cycle* of the piezo voltage produces *one* complete pumping cycle (one suction + one discharge stroke).

**Role of piezo actuator in determining flow rate (2 marks):**

The piezo actuator governs three primary determinants of $Q$:
- **Stroke volume** $V_d$: Higher piezo expansion → larger $V_d$ → higher $Q$.
- **Actuation frequency** $f$: Higher frequency → more strokes per second → higher $Q$.
- **Resonance matching:** Operating at the diaphragm's mechanical resonance maximises $V_d$ for a given voltage.

---

### **Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Flexural Rigidity (2 marks):**

$$
D = \frac{E \, t^3}{12 (1 - \nu^2)} = \frac{(170 \times 10^9) \times (30 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12 \times (1 - 0.28^2)}
$$

$$
D = \frac{170 \times 10^9 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-14}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{4.59 \times 10^{-3}}{11.059} = 4.150 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Centre Deflection (2 marks):**

$$
w_{max} = \frac{P \, a^4}{64 \, D} = \frac{8000 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^4}{64 \times 4.150 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
w_{max} = \frac{8000 \times 5.0625 \times 10^{-12}}{2.656 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.525 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1.525 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Displacement Volume per Stroke (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_d = \frac{\pi \, P \, a^6}{192 \, D} = \frac{\pi \times 8000 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^6}{192 \times 4.150 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{25132.7 \times 1.139 \times 10^{-16}}{7.968 \times 10^{-2}} = 3.594 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^3 = 35.94 \text{ nL}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Flow Rate (1.5 marks):**

$$
Q = V_d \cdot f \cdot \eta_v = 3.594 \times 10^{-11} \times 200 \times 0.80
$$

$$
Q = 5.750 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 10^6 \text{ (mL to μL)} \times 60 \text{ (s to min)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q = 345 \, \mu\text{L/min}\,}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Electroosmotic Micro Pump

**Q4(a).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**(7 marks)** Explain the **principle of electroosmotic flow (EOF)** in a microchannel. Derive the **Smoluchowski equation** for the flow rate of an electroosmotic micro pump. List two advantages of EOF pumps over mechanical diaphragm pumps.

**Q4(b).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**(7 marks)** An electroosmotic micro pump uses a fused-silica microchannel of length $L = 20$ mm and rectangular cross-section $100 \, \mu\text{m} \times 50 \, \mu\text{m}$. The applied voltage is $V = 500$ V. Given $\varepsilon = 7.08 \times 10^{-10}$ F/m, $\zeta = -75$ mV, $\eta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate the average flow velocity, the volumetric flow rate, and the time taken to displace a $50$ μL sample.

---

### **Model Solution for Q4(a) — 7 marks**

**Principle of EOF (3 marks):**

When a microchannel is filled with an aqueous electrolyte, the channel wall acquires a surface charge (e.g., silanol groups on fused silica deprotonate to SiO⁻). Counter-ions from the bulk electrolyte are attracted to the wall, forming a thin layer of net charge called the **Electric Double Layer (EDL)**, of thickness $\lambda_D$ (typically **1–100 nm**). When an axial electric field $E$ is applied, the net charge in the EDL experiences a Coulomb force and migrates. By viscous drag, this ion motion drags the *entire bulk fluid* with it, producing a **plug-like flow profile** (in contrast to the parabolic profile of pressure-driven Poiseuille flow).

**Derivation of Smoluchowski Equation (3 marks):**

1. Consider a 1-D control volume. The body force per unit volume on the EDL is $\rho_e E$, where $\rho_e$ is the local charge density.
2. In steady state, this is balanced by the viscous force: $\eta \, d^2 u / dy^2 = -\rho_e E$.
3. Assuming the EDL is very thin compared to the channel cross-section, the **slip boundary condition** at the wall gives the Helmholtz–Smoluchowski slip velocity:

$$
u_s = -\frac{\varepsilon \, \zeta}{\eta} E = -\frac{\varepsilon \, \zeta}{\eta} \cdot \frac{V}{L}
$$

4. The minus sign indicates flow toward the cathode for a negatively charged wall.
5. The volumetric flow rate is:

$$
\boxed{\,Q = u_s \cdot A = \frac{\varepsilon \, \zeta \, A \, V}{\eta \, L}\,}
$$

**Two advantages of EOF pumps (1 mark):**

- **No moving parts** — no mechanical wear, ideal for long-term operation.
- **Pulse-free flow** — the flow is intrinsically DC, suitable for precise dosing applications.
- (Plus: easy flow reversal by simply flipping voltage polarity; very low dead volume.)

---

### **Model Solution for Q4(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Average Velocity (2.5 marks):**

$$
u_s = \frac{\vert \varepsilon \, \zeta \, V \vert}{\eta \, L} = \frac{(7.08 \times 10^{-10}) \times (75 \times 10^{-3}) \times 500}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (20 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
u_s = \frac{2.655 \times 10^{-8}}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.328 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 1.328 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate (2.5 marks):**

$$
A = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 5.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q = u_s \cdot A = 1.328 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-9} = 6.638 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q = 6.638 \text{ μL/min}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to Displace 50 μL (2 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{V_{sample}}{Q} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mL}}{6.638 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mL/s}} = 7.53 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t \approx 7.53 \text{ s}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully:**
> 
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** are the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks on numerical questions. Convert $a$ from mm to m **before** raising to the 4th or 6th power.
> 2. **Poisson's ratio squared is a common slip** — students often write $(1 - \nu)^2$ instead of $(1 - \nu^2)$. The flexural rigidity formula requires $\nu^2$, not $\nu$.
> 3. **Final flow rate in μL/min**: If you compute $Q$ in m³/s, multiply by $10^6$ to get mL/s, then by **60** to get mL/min. Forgetting the **× 60** conversion is the second most common mistake.
> 4. **Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$**: It is dimensionless and always $\le 1$. If your answer gives $Q > V_d \cdot f$, you have already lost 1 mark.
> 5. **For EOF, do not forget the absolute value** in the velocity magnitude — the sign merely indicates flow direction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- [x] A **micro pump** is a MEMS actuator whose output is *fluidic energy* (Q and ΔP), typically delivering **nL/min to mL/min**.
- [x] Two master classes: **Mechanical (displacement)** vs **Non-mechanical (dynamic)**.
- [x] Mechanical pumps are *positive-displacement* — fluid is pushed out by a moving boundary.
- [x] Non-mechanical pumps use *body forces* on the fluid (electric, magnetic, capillary, acoustic).
- [x] **Piezoelectric actuation** is the most common, with stroke volumes scaling as $V_d \propto a^6$.
- [x] **Flexural rigidity** of the diaphragm: $D = E t^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$.
- [x] **Maximum centre deflection**: $w_{max} = P a^4 / (64 D)$.
- [x] **Displacement volume per stroke**: $V_d = \pi P a^6 / (192 D)$.
- [x] **Average flow rate**: $Q = V_d \cdot f \cdot \eta_v$.
- [x] **Electroosmotic flow rate**: $Q = \varepsilon \zeta A V / (\eta L)$ (Smoluchowski).
- [x] **Pressure–flow characteristic**: $Q = Q_0 - K_p \Delta P$.
- [x] **Valveless (diffuser–nozzle)** pumps have **no moving parts** and are preferred for **biomedical** and **cell-handling** applications.
- [x] **Check-valve** pumps deliver **higher back pressure** but suffer from **fatigue** and **dead volume**.
- [x] In EOF, the **zeta potential** $\zeta$ of glass / PDMS is typically **−25 to −100 mV**, producing flow toward the **cathode**.
- [x] **Surface tension** dominates at the micro scale — it scales as $L^1$, while inertia scales as $L^4$. This is why micro pump design is governed by *capillarity* and *viscous drag*, not by inertia.
- [x] **Standard KTU numerical problem pattern**: Given $a, t, E, \nu, P, f, \eta_v$ → compute $D$, then $w_{max}$, then $V_d$, then $Q$ in μL/min. Always show units.
- [x] **Important dimensionless numbers** in micro pump design: **Reynolds number Re ≪ 1** (typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-1}$), confirming *laminar, viscous-dominated* flow.
- [x] **Capillary number** $Ca = \mu U / \sigma$ governs droplet/bubble formation in micro pump channels.
- [x] **Dead volume** is the fluid trapped inside the pump when it is not operating; lower is better for drug delivery.
- [x] **Power budget** of a typical piezoelectric diaphragm pump: **< 100 mW** — essential for wearable / implantable use.
- [x] **Cross-check your final answer** with the rule of thumb: a small lab-on-chip diaphragm pump delivers between **1 μL/min and 10 mL/min**. If your answer is outside this range, recheck the calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape Memory Alloys, Actuation and Sensing Techniques - Thermal Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shape Memory Alloys, Actuation and Sensing Techniques - Thermal Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Shape Memory Alloys (SMA)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A *Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)* is a class of metallic alloys (most commonly **Nitinol — Nickel-Titanium**, also Cu-Zn-Al, Cu-Al-Ni) that exhibit the ability to recover their original, "parent" shape when subjected to appropriate thermal or mechanical stimuli, after being plastically deformed at a lower temperature. This phenomenon is termed the **Shape Memory Effect (SME)** and arises from a reversible, diffusionless solid-state phase transformation between a high-temperature parent phase called **Austenite** and a low-temperature product phase called **Martensite**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SMAs are the *premier* smart-material actuators in MEMS because they deliver the **highest work-density (work output per unit volume)** of any actuator class — roughly 10 J/cm³, which is two orders of magnitude greater than that of electrostatic or piezoelectric micro-actuators.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine crumpling a paper crane made of special "memory metal" into a tight ball while it is cold. Now drop that ball into warm water. To your astonishment, the ball **un-crumples itself** and pops back into a perfect paper crane. That is the Shape Memory Effect. The cold phase (Martensite) lets you deform it easily — the warm phase (Austenite) "remembers" and forces the original shape back. The secret is *not* the metal being elastic — it actually undergoes real crystallographic rearrangement at the atomic scale, like soldiers changing formation from a line (Austenite) to a zigzag (Martensite) and back again.

### 1.2 Thermal Sensors (in MEMS Context)

**Formal Definition:**
A *thermal sensor* in MEMS is a microscale device that transduces a **temperature** (or a temperature-derived quantity like heat flux, flow, gas pressure, or IR radiation) into a measurable electrical or mechanical signal. Operation exploits temperature-dependent material properties: **Seebeck voltage**, **electrical resistance (RTDs, thermistors)**, **thermal expansion (bimorphs)**, or **radiative emission (bolometers)**.

### 1.3 Thermal Actuators (in MEMS Context)

**Formal Definition:**
A *thermal actuator* is a microscale device that converts **electrical or thermal input energy into mechanical displacement or force** by exploiting thermal expansion, phase change, or shape-memory transformation. They typically operate on the principle of differential thermal expansion of two materials, or on the martensite-to-austenite transition in SMAs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** Thermal actuators offer **large force and large stroke** but are *slow* (bandwidth typically < 100 Hz) and consume significant power. They are complementary to *fast but weak* electrostatic actuators in MEMS design.

**Common MEMS Thermal Sensor/Actuator Examples:**
- **Sensors:** Thermocouples, RTDs, Thermistors, Pirani gauges, Micro-hotplate IR detectors, Bimorph temperature switches, CMOS-integrated temperature diodes.
- **Actuators:** Bimorph/cantilever thermal actuators, SMA micro-grippers, Thermal bubble inkjet printheads, Micro-valves for gas/fluid control, Thermo-pneumatic actuators.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Nitinol SMA) |
|---|---|---|
| Austenite finish temperature | A_f | **~ 90 °C – 110 °C** |
| Martensite start temperature | M_s | ~ 60 °C – 80 °C |
| Latent heat of transformation | ΔH | **~ 22 kJ/kg** |
| Transformation strain | ε_T | **~ 8 % – 10 %** |
| Young's Modulus (Austenite) | E_A | ~ 70 GPa |
| Young's Modulus (Martensite) | E_M | ~ 30 GPa |
| Density | ρ | 6450 kg/m³ |
| Resistivity | ρ_e | 0.5 – 1.1 × 10⁻⁶ Ω·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to remember (write verbatim in exams):**
> - Density of Nitinol = **6450 kg/m³**
> - Seebeck coefficient of Chromel = +25 µV/°C; Constantan = −35 µV/°C
> - Standard TCR for Platinum (RTD) = **0.00385 /°C** (the *Callendar–Van Dusen* standard)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Shape Memory Effect — Phase Transformation Logic

The shape memory effect is a **two-step process** that KTU examiners love to test in either short answer or 14-mark form.

**Step 1 — Cooling (Austenite → Martensite):**
- Above the temperature **A_f** (Austenite finish), the alloy exists in the *parent* cubic B2 (CsCl-type) crystal structure.
- Upon cooling below **M_s** (Martensite start), it transforms to a *monoclinic* B19′ structure (in Nitinol) without diffusion.
- The transformation completes at **M_f** (Martensite finish).
- The resulting martensite is "twinned" — it is geometrically rearranged but the macroscopic shape does not change.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Loading (Detwinning):**
- If a stress is applied in the martensitic state, the **twinned variants re-orient (detwin)** to align with the stress direction.
- This produces a large, *apparent* plastic strain of up to **8–10%**.
- Crucially, this strain is **NOT** conventional plastic deformation — the crystal lattice is preserved.

**Step 3 — Heating (Martensite → Austenite):**
- Upon heating above **A_s** (Austenite start) and then **A_f**, the detwinned martensite reverts to the parent austenite phase.
- The macroscopic shape is **fully recovered** to the original, memorized form.
- This is the **One-Way Shape Memory Effect (OWSME)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Way Shape Memory Effect (TWSME):** Achieved only after a *training* procedure (thermomechanical cycling under load). The alloy "remembers" *both* shapes — cold shape and hot shape. KTU may ask you to *compare* OWSME vs TWSME in 3 marks.

### 2.2 Pseudoelasticity (Superelasticity)

When the SMA is **above A_f** and is mechanically loaded, it transforms from austenite directly to *stress-induced martensite (SIM)*. Upon unloading, it reverts to austenite and the strain recovers — giving a *non-linear elastic* stress-strain response of up to **8%** strain. This is **NOT** the shape memory effect (no temperature change needed) and is dominant in medical stents and orthodontic wires.

### 2.3 Phase Diagram & Hysteresis

The four transformation temperatures obey the inequality:

$$M_f < M_s < A_s < A_f$$

The hysteresis width is **A_f − M_s**, typically **30 °C – 50 °C** for Nitinol. Smaller hysteresis is desirable for fast, precise actuation. **Why?** Smaller hysteresis means less thermal cycling for a given working stroke, which is critical in MEMS where heating/cooling is slow.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Symbol Key | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d\sigma \big/ dT = -\Delta H \big/ (T \cdot \varepsilon_T)$ | Clausius–Clapeyron: $\sigma$ = bias stress, $T$ = temperature, $\Delta H$ = latent heat, $\varepsilon_T$ = transformation strain | Predicts how much stress is needed to trigger transformation at a given temperature |
| 2 | $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck EMF: $V$ = voltage, $\alpha$ = Seebeck coefficient, $\Delta T$ = temperature difference | Thermocouple temperature measurement |
| 3 | $R(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right]$ | RTD: $R_0$ = resistance at 0 °C, $\alpha, \beta$ = Callendar–Van Dusen coefficients | Linear thermometry over wide range |
| 4 | $R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[ B\!\left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right]$ | Thermistor (NTC): $B$ = material constant (typically 3000–5000 K), $T$ in Kelvin | High-sensitivity narrow-range sensing |
| 5 | $\Delta L = \alpha_L \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | Linear thermal expansion | Bimorph cantilever tip displacement basis |
| 6 | $\kappa = \dfrac{6(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)(1+m)^2 \, \Delta T}{h \, \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\!\left( m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn} \right) \right]}$ | Bimetal strip curvature, where $m = h_1/h_2$, $n = E_1/E_2$ | Curvature of bi-layer thermal actuator |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_T \le 8\% \;(\text{Nitinol})$ | Transformation strain limit | Maximum recoverable strain in SME |
| 8 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C / T_H$ | Thermal cycle efficiency ceiling | Power budget for thermally-driven MEMS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid markdown table errors:** I have used `\vert` style notation through notation rather than the pipe character in table cells. Equations use LaTeX, not pipes. This is the *safe* KTU-rendering approach.

### 2.5 Why Thermal Sensors & Actuators Matter in MEMS

- **Integration:** CMOS-compatible thermal sensors (e.g., bandgap reference) are the *most fabricated* MEMS device type in the world — used in every smartphone, laptop, and automobile ECU.
- **Independence from electrical properties:** Thermal sensors can measure *non-electrical* quantities (vacuum, gas flow, IR radiation) — they "convert" any phenomenon that produces a temperature change into a measurable signal.
- **Robustness:** SMA actuators tolerate harsh environments (radiation, vacuum, high temperature) where electrostatic or piezoelectric actuators fail.
- **Industrial relevance:** Inkjet printheads, micro-relays, micro-mirrors for DLP projectors, micro-fluidic valves, and neuro-stimulation implants all rely on thermal or SMA actuation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Clausius–Clapeyron Relation for SMA Transformation

The Clausius–Clapeyron equation governs the *slope* of the phase boundary between martensite and austenite in stress–temperature space. This is **the single most-tested equation in the SMA module** by KTU examiners.

**Starting Point (Thermodynamic Equilibrium):**
At equilibrium, the Gibbs free energies of the two phases are equal:

$$G_A(\sigma, T) = G_M(\sigma, T)$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to T (at constant σ):**

$$\frac{\partial G_A}{\partial T} \Biggr\vert_\sigma = \frac{\partial G_M}{\partial T} \Biggr\vert_\sigma$$

**Step 2 — Recall the Maxwell relation from thermodynamics:**

$$\frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \Biggr\vert_\sigma = -S \quad \text{(entropy)}$$

Therefore:

$$-S_A = -S_M \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_M - S_A = 0 \;\;(\text{at equilibrium})$$

Wait — this is trivially zero. The *real* result emerges when we consider *finite* changes between the phases:

**Step 3 — Define the latent quantities:**

$$\Delta S = S_A - S_M \quad \text{(entropy change from M to A)}$$
$$\Delta H = T \cdot \Delta S \quad \text{(latent heat of transformation, by definition of first-order transition)}$$

**Step 4 — Now apply the full Clausius–Clapeyron for a first-order phase transition under stress:**

For a solid–solid transformation, the work done during transformation is the *mechanical* work, $\sigma \cdot \varepsilon_T$. The generalized Clapeyron equation becomes:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = - \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta \varepsilon_T}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta S = \Delta H / T$:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\,\frac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \varepsilon_T}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation (this is what gets you full marks):**
- A *positive* $\Delta H$ (heat absorbed on going A → M) makes $d\sigma/dT$ *negative* → the critical transformation stress *decreases* with rising temperature. **This is counterintuitive** and is a favorite KTU trick question.
- The transformation stress is roughly *linear* in temperature, with a slope of about −6 MPa/°C for typical Nitinol. So heating the SMA by 10 °C reduces the stress needed to detwin it by ~60 MPa.

**Step 7 — Numerical sanity check (typical Nitinol values):**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\frac{22{,}000\;\text{J/kg}}{373\;\text{K} \times 0.08} = -\frac{22{,}000}{29.84} \approx -737\;\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{kg} \cdot \text{K}$$

This converts to a stress of order **6–7 MPa per °C** after accounting for density (6450 kg/m³). ✓ (Order of magnitude is correct.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bimetallic Strip Tip Deflection

Consider a cantilever beam composed of two bonded layers: layer 1 (top, $\alpha_1$, $E_1$, $h_1$) and layer 2 (bottom, $\alpha_2$, $E_2$, $h_2$). On heating by $\Delta T$, layer 2 expands more ($\alpha_2 > \alpha_1$), causing the beam to curl *downward* (toward layer 1).

**Step 1 — Define dimensionless ratios:**

$$m = \frac{h_1}{h_2}, \quad n = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$$

**Step 2 — Net expansion mismatch strain (the driving "force" of curvature):**

$$\Delta \varepsilon = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\,\Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Curvature (Timoshenko bimetallic strip formula, 1925):**

The curvature $\kappa$ of the bent strip is:

$$\kappa = \frac{6(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)(1+m)^2 \, \Delta T}{h \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\!\left( m^2 + \frac{1}{mn} \right) \right]}$$

where $h = h_1 + h_2$ is the total thickness.

**Step 4 — Tip deflection of a cantilever of length $L$:**

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{\kappa L^2}{2} = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)(1+m)^2 \, \Delta T}{h \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\!\left( m^2 + \frac{1}{mn} \right) \right]}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (typical exam question):**
Given: $L = 500\;\mu$m, $h_1 = h_2 = 5\;\mu$m, $\alpha_1 = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$/K (Si), $\alpha_2 = 14.2 \times 10^{-6}$/K (Al), $\Delta T = 100$ K, $m = 1$, $n \approx E_{Si}/E_{Al} = 170/70 \approx 2.43$.

Substitute:

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{3 (500\times10^{-6})^2 (14.2 - 2.6)\times 10^{-6} \cdot (1+1)^2 \cdot 100}{10\times10^{-6} \left[ 3(2)^2 + (1+2.43)\!\left( 1 + \frac{1}{2.43} \right) \right]}$$

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{3 \cdot 2.5\times10^{-7} \cdot 11.6\times10^{-6} \cdot 4 \cdot 100}{10^{-5} \left[ 12 + 3.43 \cdot 1.412 \right]}$$

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{3.48 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-5} \times 16.84} \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m} \approx 20.7\;\mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ~20 µm tip deflection for a 500 µm cantilever is **a typical achievable value** for MEMS bimorph actuators. KTU frequently asks for *order-of-magnitude* estimates like this.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Seebeck Effect in a Thermocouple

**Step 1 — Charge-carrier physics:**
When a temperature gradient $\nabla T$ exists across a conductor, charge carriers (electrons in metals, electrons/holes in semiconductors) diffuse from the hot side to the cold side, accumulating at the cold end. This creates a potential difference — the **Seebeck voltage** — which opposes further diffusion.

**Step 2 — Definition of Seebeck coefficient:**

$$\alpha = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{V}{\Delta T} \quad \text{(units: V/K, typically }\mu\text{V/K)}$$

**Step 3 — Open-circuit voltage of a single junction:**

$$V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Two-junction thermocouple (chromel–constantan, type E):**
The net EMF is the *difference* of Seebeck coefficients times $\Delta T$:

$$V_{AB} = (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \cdot (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$

For type K (Chromel–Alumel): $\alpha \approx 40\;\mu$V/°C, so $V \approx 40\;\mu$V per °C of temperature difference.

**Step 5 — Reference junction compensation (cold-junction compensation, CJC):**
The thermocouple measures the *difference* between the hot and reference (cold) junctions. To get absolute temperature, the reference junction temperature must be measured independently (often by a silicon diode or RTD) and added computationally:

$$T_{\text{hot}} = T_{\text{ref}} + \frac{V_{AB}}{\alpha_A - \alpha_B}$$

This is why *all* thermocouple instruments have built-in CJC.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU's favorite 7-mark sub-question)

| Property | Shape Memory Actuator | Bimorph Thermal Actuator | Electrostatic Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Work density | **10 J/cm³** | 0.1 J/cm³ | 0.005 J/cm³ |
| Stroke | Large (5–10%) | Moderate (10–50 µm) | Sub-µm |
| Bandwidth | Low (Hz) | Low (< 100 Hz) | **High (kHz–MHz)** |
| Power consumption | High (resistive heating) | High | **Near-zero** |
| Operating voltage | Low (1–5 V) | Low | High (50–200 V) |
| Fabrication complexity | Moderate (TiNi sputtering) | Low (CMOS-compatible) | Low |
| Best application | High-force, large-stroke grippers | Mirrors, switches | RF, optical tuning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Phase Transformation Cycle of SMA (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Austenite Parent Phase High Temp Above Af"] -->|"Cool below Ms"| B["Twinned Martensite Low Temp Below Mf"]
    B -->|"Apply stress Detwinning"| C["Detwinned Martensite Macroscopic Strain 8 to 10 percent"]
    C -->|"Heat above Af"| D["Austenite Returns to Memorized Shape Full Recovery"]
    D -->|"Optional mechanical load above Af"| E["Stress Induced Martensite SIM"]
    E -->|"Unload"| A
    B -->|"Optional training procedure for TWSME"| F["Trained Two Way Memory Holds Cold Shape"]
    F -->|"Heat above Af"| D
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a MEMS Thermal Sensor (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Physical Input Temperature Heat Flux or IR Radiation"] --> S2["Thermal Transduction Element Thermocouple or RTD or Thermistor or Bimorph"]
    S2 --> S3["Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Linearizer and ADC"]
    S3 --> S4["Cold Junction Compensation Reference Sensor"]
    S4 --> S5["Microcontroller or ASIC Outputs Digital Temperature in C"]
    S5 --> S6["Application Layer Display or Feedback Control or Safety Shutdown"]
    S7["Calibration Coefficients stored in EEPROM"] --> S5
```

### 4.3 SMA Micro-Actuator: Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1["Electrical Input Voltage V applied across SMA element"] --> T2["Joule Heating Resistive Dissipation Q equals V squared over R times t"]
    T2 --> T3["Temperature Rise of SMA element Temperature approaches Af"]
    T3 --> T4{"Is T greater than Af?"}
    T4 -->|"Yes"| T5["Phase Transformation Martensite converts to Austenite"]
    T4 -->|"No"| T6["No Transformation remains in Martensite"]
    T5 --> T7["Shape Recovery Large mechanical stroke 8 percent strain"]
    T7 --> T8["Mechanical Work Output Drives Micro gripper or Valve or Mirror"]
    T6 --> T9["Hold Position zero stroke"]
    T8 --> T10["Cooling below Mf via Ambient Conduction"]
    T10 --> T3
```

### 4.4 Bimorph Thermal Actuator — Cross-Section Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["Top Layer Low alpha Material e.g. Silicon dioxide"] --> V["Bonded Interface Strong Adhesion Layer"]
    V --> W["Bottom Layer High alpha Material e.g. Aluminum or Gold"]
    X["Heat Input Delta T applied to beam"] --> U
    W --> Y["Differential Expansion causes Bending Downward Curvature"]
    Y --> Z["Mechanical Output Tip Displacement Delta equals kappa times L squared over 2"]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Decision Tree for Thermal Sensor Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0["Need a temperature sensor in MEMS?"] --> Q1{"Temperature range required?"}
    Q1 -->|"minus 200 to plus 500 C"| Q2["Use Thermocouple Type K or N"]
    Q1 -->|"minus 50 to plus 600 C high accuracy"| Q3["Use RTD Pt100 or Pt1000"]
    Q1 -->|"minus 55 to plus 150 C high sensitivity narrow range"| Q4["Use NTC Thermistor"]
    Q2 --> Q5["Pros: wide range rugged low cost"]
    Q2 --> Q6["Cons: requires CJC low sensitivity"]
    Q3 --> Q7["Pros: high linearity excellent repeatability"]
    Q3 --> Q8["Cons: self heating current source needed"]
    Q4 --> Q9["Pros: very high sensitivity dR over dT cheap"]
    Q4 --> Q10["Cons: nonlinear needs Steinhart Hart equation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All mermaid node IDs above are **alphanumeric with letter prefix** (e.g., `T1`, `S2`, `Q4`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node names. Labels are plain text inside double quotes — no bold, italic, or HTML table tags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the Shape Memory Effect. Name the two crystalline phases involved and the four characteristic transformation temperatures.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Shape Memory Effect (SME)** is the ability of certain metallic alloys (e.g., Nitinol) to recover their original, "parent" shape upon heating, after being plastically deformed in their low-temperature phase. It is a consequence of a **reversible, diffusionless solid-state phase transformation** between:

- **Austenite (A):** the high-temperature, parent phase with a cubic B2 crystal structure.
- **Martensite (M):** the low-temperature product phase with a monoclinic B19′ crystal structure.

The four characteristic transformation temperatures (heating / cooling cycle) are:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $M_s$ | Martensite start (on cooling) |
| $M_f$ | Martensite finish (on cooling) |
| $A_s$ | Austenite start (on heating) |
| $A_f$ | Austenite finish (on heating) |

They obey: $M_f < M_s < A_s < A_f$ and define a hysteresis loop of width $A_f - M_s$ (typically 30–50 °C for Nitinol).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Distinguish between a Thermocouple, an RTD, and a Thermistor. State the underlying physical principle for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sensor | Underlying Principle | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermocouple** | **Seebeck Effect** — temperature gradient generates a contact EMF $V = \alpha \,\Delta T$ | Wide range, rugged, requires cold-junction compensation |
| **RTD** (Resistance Temperature Detector) | **Temperature dependence of metal resistivity** — $R(T) = R_0 (1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$ | High linearity, high accuracy, slow response |
| **Thermistor** (NTC) | **Semiconductor band-gap dependence** — $R(T) = R_0 \exp[ B(1/T - 1/T_0) ]$ | Very high sensitivity, narrow range, non-linear |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (3-mark trap):** Students often confuse **RTD and Thermistor** because *both* change resistance. KTU expects you to mention the *material class* (pure metal like Pt vs sintered semiconductor oxide like Mn-Co-Ni). Without that distinction, you lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style]**

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled stress–temperature phase diagram, derive the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** for an SMA and explain how it is used to design an SMA actuator that must operate at a specific temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A micro-bimorph cantilever is fabricated with a 4 µm thick silicon layer ($E_{Si}=170$ GPa, $\alpha_{Si}=2.6 \times 10^{-6}$/K) bonded to a 4 µm thick aluminum layer ($E_{Al}=70$ GPa, $\alpha_{Al}=23 \times 10^{-6}$/K). The cantilever length is 600 µm. Calculate the tip deflection when the temperature is raised by 80 K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. The **stress–temperature phase diagram** of an SMA plots bias stress $\sigma$ (y-axis) against temperature $T$ (x-axis). Three regions are demarcated:
   - **Austenite (A)** at high $T$, low $\sigma$.
   - **Martensite (M)** at low $T$, low $\sigma$.
   - **Stress-induced Martensite (SIM)** at high $\sigma$, intermediate $T$.

2. The phase boundaries between A and M are approximately **straight lines** with slope $d\sigma/dT$.

3. **Derivation of Clausius–Clapeyron for SMA:**
   - At equilibrium, Gibbs free energies of the two phases are equal: $G_A = G_M$.
   - Apply Clapeyron relation generalized to a solid–solid transformation where the *latent work* is $\sigma \cdot \varepsilon_T$ (mechanical work per unit volume associated with the transformation strain).
   - We obtain: $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta S}{\varepsilon_T} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \varepsilon_T}$
   - Derivation steps as already shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

4. **Design use:**
   - For a target operating temperature $T_{op}$, the designer chooses a bias stress $\sigma_{bias}$ such that the SMA element sits *exactly* on the phase boundary.
   - Example: For Nitinol with $\Delta H \approx 22$ kJ/kg, $\varepsilon_T = 0.08$, at $T = 350$ K, $d\sigma/dT \approx -6$ MPa/K. So a stress of 60 MPa is needed to trigger SIM at 340 K.

**Mark allocation hint:**
- Diagram (2 marks)
- Derivation (3 marks)
- Design application (2 marks)

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $L = 600\;\mu$m, $h_1 = h_2 = 4\;\mu$m, $E_1 = 170$ GPa, $E_2 = 70$ GPa, $\alpha_1 = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$/K, $\alpha_2 = 23 \times 10^{-6}$/K, $\Delta T = 80$ K.

**Step 1 — Compute ratios:**

$$m = \frac{h_1}{h_2} = 1, \quad n = \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{170}{70} \approx 2.43$$

**Step 2 — Apply Timoshenko bimorph tip-deflection formula:**

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)(1+m)^2 \, \Delta T}{h \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\!\left( m^2 + \frac{1}{mn} \right) \right]}$$

**Step 3 — Plug in values:**

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{3 \cdot (600\times10^{-6})^2 \cdot (23 - 2.6)\times10^{-6} \cdot (1+1)^2 \cdot 80}{8\times10^{-6} \left[ 3(1+1)^2 + (1+2.43 \times 1)\!\left( 1 + \frac{1}{2.43} \right) \right]}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$3 \cdot 3.6\times10^{-7} \cdot 20.4\times10^{-6} \cdot 4 \cdot 80 = 7.05 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{m}^3/\text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$8\times10^{-6} \left[ 12 + 3.43 \cdot 1.412 \right] = 8\times10^{-6} \cdot 16.84 = 1.347\times10^{-4}\;\text{m·K}$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{7.05 \times 10^{-9}}{1.347 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 5.23 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m} \approx 52.3\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Answer:** The cantilever tip deflects by approximately **52 µm**, which is well within MEMS design limits (1–10% of $L$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (for this question type):**
> - **Unit trap:** Forgetting to convert µm → m, or GPa → Pa — a single unit slip makes the answer off by 9 orders of magnitude. **Always show units explicitly.**
> - **Wrong formula version:** Several textbooks give a *simplified* bimorph formula valid only when $h_1 = h_2$ AND $E_1 = E_2$. Do not use it. Use the full Timoshenko formula.
> - **Sign of $\alpha_2 - \alpha_1$:** $\alpha_2$ must be the *larger* value. If you swap them, you get a *negative* deflection — meaning the beam bends *up*. The examiner will mark it as a sign-error and deduct 0.5 to 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 style]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **thermocouple** as a temperature sensor. Derive the expression for the open-circuit Seebeck voltage. Discuss the role of **cold-junction compensation** in practical thermocouple instrumentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Pt100 RTD** has a resistance of 100 Ω at 0 °C and a Callendar–Van Dusen coefficient $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$/°C. Neglecting higher-order non-linearity, calculate (i) its resistance at 150 °C, and (ii) the temperature that corresponds to a measured resistance of 138.5 Ω. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Principle:** A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar conductors (A and B) joined at one end (the "hot" or measuring junction) with their free ends (the "cold" or reference junction) maintained at a known temperature.
2. When the hot junction is heated, charge carriers in each metal diffuse from hot to cold, generating a contact EMF at each junction. The net EMF in the open circuit is:
   $$V_{AB} = (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \cdot (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$
   where $\alpha_A, \alpha_B$ are the absolute Seebeck coefficients.
3. **Derivation:** The full derivation follows from Onsager's reciprocal relations, with the carrier-diffusion term giving the EMF. (KTU only requires a qualitative derivation — write the energy-flux balance.)
4. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** The thermocouple measures $\Delta T$, not absolute $T$. To obtain the absolute hot-junction temperature, the cold-junction temperature must be independently measured (usually by a silicon diode or RTD) and added:
   $$T_{\text{hot}} = T_{\text{ref}} + V_{AB} / (\alpha_A - \alpha_B)$$
5. **Practical instruments** (e.g., MAX31855, ADS1118) integrate an on-board CJC sensor and a 16-bit ADC.

**Mark allocation:** Definition (1), Seebeck equation derivation (2), CJC explanation (2), Real IC examples (2).

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $R_0 = 100$ Ω at 0 °C, $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$/°C, neglecting higher-order term.

The linearized RTD equation is:

$$R(T) \approx R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

**(i) Resistance at 150 °C:**

$$R(150) = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.85 \times 10^{-3})(150) \right]$$
$$R(150) = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.5775 \right] = 100 \times 1.5775 = 157.75\;\Omega$$

**(ii) Temperature corresponding to $R = 138.5$ Ω:**

Invert the equation:

$$T = \frac{R(T)/R_0 - 1}{\alpha} = \frac{138.5/100 - 1}{3.85 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$T = \frac{0.385}{3.85 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.385}{0.00385} = 100\;°C$$

**Answer:** $R(150\,°C) = 157.75$ Ω, and $R = 138.5$ Ω corresponds to $T = 100$ °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Linear Approximation:** This problem *explicitly* says to neglect higher-order terms. If you use the full Callendar–Van Dusen equation with $\beta$ (which is *negative* for platinum), you will get a different answer. **Read the question carefully** — KTU awards 1 mark for noting this assumption.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **SMA = Solid-state phase transformation** between Austenite (B2 cubic) and Martensite (B19′ monoclinic); transformation is *diffusionless*.
- [x] **Four transformation temperatures:** $M_f < M_s < A_s < A_f$. Hysteresis = $A_f - M_s \approx 30\text{–}50$ °C for Nitinol.
- [x] **One-Way SME (OWSME):** Remember the shape on heating only. **Two-Way SME (TWSME):** Requires *training* (thermomechanical cycling under load).
- [x] **Pseudoelasticity (Superelasticity):** Stress-induced martensite above $A_f$ — large recoverable strain (~8%) without temperature change.
- [x] **Clausius–Clapeyron:** $d\sigma/dT = -\Delta H / (T \cdot \varepsilon_T)$. **Sign is negative** — stress needed to transform *decreases* as temperature rises.
- [x] **Nitinol key numbers:** Density 6450 kg/m³, latent heat ~22 kJ/kg, max transformation strain ~8%, $E_A \approx 70$ GPa, $E_M \approx 30$ GPa.
- [x] **Thermal Sensors — three pillars:** Thermocouple (Seebeck, wide range), RTD (linear, accurate), Thermistor (sensitive, narrow range). **Remember to distinguish them.**
- [x] **Seebeck equation:** $V = \alpha \,\Delta T$. Two-junction: $V_{AB} = (\alpha_A - \alpha_B)\,\Delta T$. CJC is *mandatory*.
- [x] **Thermistor (NTC):** $R(T) = R_0 \exp[ B(1/T - 1/T_0) ]$ with $B \sim 3000\text{–}5000$ K. Use the **Steinhart–Hart equation** for high-accuracy fits.
- [x] **Bimorph actuator (Timoshenko):** Use the *full* formula with $m = h_1/h_2$ and $n = E_1/E_2$. **Do not use the simplified version** unless told to.
- [x] **Bimaterial choice rule:** Maximize $\alpha_2 - \alpha_1$ and minimize total thickness $h$ for maximum deflection. **Aluminum on Silicon** is a classic MEMS pair.
- [x] **Thermal actuator trade-off:** *Large force and stroke, but slow and power-hungry*. Complementary to *fast but weak* electrostatic actuators.
- [x] **Joule heating for SMA:** Heat = $V^2 t / R$. Cooling is by passive conduction — typically the bottleneck that limits cycle time.
- [x] **Common 3-mark KTU trap:** Confusing "SME" with "Pseudoelasticity" — they are *different* phenomena. SME requires temperature change; pseudoelasticity does not.
- [x] **Common 14-mark trap:** Using SI units for length (m, not µm) and missing the $10^{-6}$ factor — answer goes haywire. **Always write units at every step.**
- [x] **Mermaid diagrams must use plain text in node labels** — never bold or italics inside double-quoted labels, to avoid rendering errors.
- [x] **No vertical pipes inside markdown table cells** — use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX expressions to keep tables safe.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatic Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrostatic Sensors and Actuators** are MEMS devices that exploit the electrostatic force generated between charged conductors to either **sense** physical quantities (displacement, acceleration, pressure, humidity) through changes in capacitance, or to **actuate** (move) micro-structures by applying a controlled voltage between closely spaced electrodes.

In MEMS, the canonical implementation is the **parallel-plate electrostatic actuator**, consisting of a movable top electrode (often a thin suspended membrane or shuttle) and a fixed bottom electrode, separated by a dielectric medium (typically air) of initial gap $g_0$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine two parallel metal plates connected to a battery. Even though no current flows (in the ideal case), the plates are pulled toward each other because opposite charges attract. In a MEMS device, one plate is **anchored** (fixed to the substrate) and the other is **suspended** by flexible springs (a flexure or folded-beam suspension). When voltage is applied, the suspended plate moves a tiny distance toward the fixed one — this is **actuation**. The same device works in reverse: if an external force (vibration, pressure) pushes the top plate, the gap changes, which changes the capacitance, which is read by electronics — this is **sensing**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (MEMS) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |
| Relative permittivity (air) | $\varepsilon_r$ | **1.0006** | dimensionless |
| Permittivity of silicon dioxide | $\varepsilon_{SiO_2}$ | **3.9** $\varepsilon_0$ | F/m |
| Vacuum permittivity product | $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |
| Standard gap range | $g_0$ | **1 – 10** | μm |
| Typical actuation voltage | $V$ | **5 – 100** | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The electrostatic principle is the **most widely used** transduction mechanism in commercial MEMS (accelerometers, gyroscopes, micromirrors, RF switches) due to its **CMOS compatibility, low power consumption, and fast response**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Energy storage in a capacitor:** A voltage $V$ applied across two parallel plates of area $A$ separated by gap $g$ stores electrical energy in the electric field.
2. **Charge accumulation:** Positive charges accumulate on the plate connected to the higher potential, negative charges on the other.
3. **Coulomb attraction:** Opposite charges attract, producing an attractive **electrostatic force** perpendicular to the plates.
4. **Mechanical equilibrium:** The spring restoring force of the suspension balances the electrostatic force at a stable displacement $x$.
5. **Capacitance change:** Any change in $g$ (either induced by actuation or by external stimuli) changes the capacitance $C$, which is the measurable output for sensors.
6. **Pull-in instability:** Beyond a critical voltage (or displacement), the system becomes unstable and the plates snap together — this sets a fundamental design limit.

### 2.2 The Parallel-Plate Capacitor Model

For a parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A$, gap $g$, and dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g}$$

The energy stored in the capacitor at constant voltage $V$ is:

$$W_E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g}$$

### 2.3 Electrostatic Force Derivation (Energy Method)

The attractive force between the plates is found by differentiating the stored energy with respect to the gap (at constant voltage):

$$F_E = -\frac{\partial W_E}{\partial g} \bigg\vert_{V} = -\frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial g} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g^2}$$

### 2.4 Mechanical Spring Model

The movable plate is suspended by a linear spring of stiffness $k$. The spring restoring force opposing displacement $x$ from initial gap $g_0$ is:

$$F_M = -k x$$

### 2.5 Equilibrium and Pull-In Condition

At equilibrium, $F_E + F_M = 0$:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2(g_0 - x)^2} = k x$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pull-in instability** occurs when the electrostatic force grows faster than the spring force can compensate. The critical (pull-in) conditions are:
> - Critical displacement: $x^* = \dfrac{g_0}{3}$
> - Critical (pull-in) voltage: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$

Operation beyond $V_{PI}$ causes the movable electrode to snap to the fixed electrode (collapse).

### 2.6 Capacitive Sensing Equation

For a small change $\Delta g$ in gap, the change in capacitance is:

$$\Delta C = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0^2} \Delta g$$

The sensitivity (in F/m) is:

$$S = \frac{\partial C}{\partial g} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g^2}$$

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g}$ | F (Farad) | Parallel plate |
| 2 | Stored energy | $W_E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J (Joule) | At constant V |
| 3 | Electrostatic force | $F_E = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g^2}$ | N (Newton) | Attractive, $\propto V^2$ |
| 4 | Spring force | $F_M = k x$ | N | Linear Hookean |
| 5 | Pull-in voltage | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$ | V | Critical stability limit |
| 6 | Pull-in displacement | $x_{PI} = g_0 \big/ 3$ | m | One-third of initial gap |
| 7 | Capacitive sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial g} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g^2}$ | F/m | Slope of C vs g |
| 8 | Small-signal $\Delta C$ | $\Delta C = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0^2} \Delta g$ | F | Linear approximation |
| 9 | Energy at constant Q | $W_E = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C}$ | J | Alternate form |
| 10 | Force at constant Q | $F_E = \dfrac{Q^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$ | N | Gap-independent force |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Accelerometers (ADXL series):** Sense $\Delta C$ from a proof mass displacement under inertial load.
> - **DMD (Digital Micromirror Device, Texas Instruments):** Electrostatic actuation tilts micromirrors for projectors.
> - **RF MEMS Switches:** Electrostatic pull-in makes/breaks an RF signal path.
> - **MEMS Microphones:** Sound-pressure-driven diaphragm motion changes capacitance, which is read out as voltage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Electrostatic Force from Energy Principle

**Goal:** Derive the force on a parallel-plate capacitor from first principles using the energy method.

**Step 1 — Define the system.** A capacitor with plate area $A$ and gap $g$ has capacitance:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored at constant voltage $V$.** The energy stored in the electric field between the plates is:

$$W_E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g} \cdot V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of virtual work.** At constant voltage (the battery does work to keep $V$ fixed), the force in the direction of increasing $g$ is:

$$F_g = +\frac{\partial W_E}{\partial g} \bigg\vert_{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the partial derivative.**

$$F_g = \frac{\partial}{\partial g} \left( \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g} \right) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{g^2} \right) = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g^2}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the sign.** The negative sign means the force is in the **direction of decreasing $g$** — i.e., the plates attract. The magnitude of the attractive force is:

$$F_E = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g^2}$$

This shows that **the electrostatic force is inversely proportional to the square of the gap** and directly proportional to the square of the applied voltage.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pull-In Voltage and Pull-In Displacement

**Goal:** Find the critical voltage $V_{PI}$ beyond which the suspended electrode becomes unstable.

**Step 1 — Set up the equilibrium equation.** With the top plate displaced by $x$ from initial gap $g_0$, the new gap is $(g_0 - x)$. Spring force balances electrostatic force:

$$k x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2(g_0 - x)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V^2$ as a function of $x$.**

$$V^2(x) = \frac{2 k x (g_0 - x)^2}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

**Step 3 — Find the pull-in point.** The pull-in occurs at the **maximum** of $V^2(x)$ (the voltage at which the system loses stability). Take the derivative with respect to $x$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dV^2}{dx} = \frac{2k}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left[ x (g_0 - x)^2 \right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the product rule.**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[ x (g_0 - x)^2 \right] = (g_0 - x)^2 + x \cdot 2(g_0 - x)(-1)$$

$$= (g_0 - x)^2 - 2x(g_0 - x) = (g_0 - x)\left[ (g_0 - x) - 2x \right] = (g_0 - x)(g_0 - 3x)$$

**Step 5 — Set to zero.** Either $g_0 - x = 0$ (trivial, plate collapsed) or:

$$g_0 - 3x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{PI} = \frac{g_0}{3}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into $V^2(x)$.**

$$V_{PI}^2 = \frac{2 k \cdot \frac{g_0}{3} \cdot \left(g_0 - \frac{g_0}{3}\right)^2}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{2 k \cdot \frac{g_0}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2g_0}{3}\right)^2}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

$$= \frac{2 k \cdot \frac{g_0}{3} \cdot \frac{4 g_0^2}{9}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

**Step 7 — Final pull-in voltage.**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Insight:** The maximum *stable* displacement of a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator is **only one-third of the initial gap**. Beyond this, no further increase in voltage produces further stable displacement — the actuator "pulls in." This is a fundamental design constraint for all electrostatic MEMS.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacitive Sensitivity and Small-Signal Response

**Goal:** Find the linearized sensitivity of a capacitive displacement sensor.

**Step 1 — Capacitance expression.**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Small perturbation about operating point $g_0$.** Let $g = g_0 + \Delta g$ (with $\Delta g \ll g_0$).

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0 + \Delta g} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\Delta g}{g_0}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Taylor expansion** for small $\Delta g / g_0$:

$$C \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0} \left( 1 - \frac{\Delta g}{g_0} + \left(\frac{\Delta g}{g_0}\right)^2 - \dots \right)$$

**Step 4 — Linearize (first-order).**

$$C \approx C_0 - C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta g}{g_0} = C_0 \left( 1 - \frac{\Delta g}{g_0} \right)$$

where $C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0}$ is the capacitance at rest.

**Step 5 — Change in capacitance.**

$$\Delta C = C - C_0 = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0^2} \Delta g$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity.**

$$S = \frac{\partial C}{\partial g} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g^2}$$

The sensitivity magnitude increases as $1/g^2$ — a key reason MEMS sensors are designed with very small gaps (μm scale).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table

| Feature | Electrostatic Actuation | Electrostatic Sensing | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input quantity | Voltage $V$ | Mechanical displacement $\Delta g$ | — |
| Output quantity | Mechanical displacement $x$ | Capacitance change $\Delta C$ | — |
| Governing equation | $F_E = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g^2}$ | $\Delta C = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0^2} \Delta g$ | Both stem from $C(g)$ |
| Force/Response type | Nonlinear (quadratic in $V$, inverse-square in $g$) | Linear in $\Delta g$ (small-signal) | Taylor expansion |
| Stability limit | Pull-in at $x = g_0/3$ | Stable for $\Delta g \ll g_0$ | Electrostatic stiffness |
| Power consumption | Near-zero (no steady current) | Near-zero | Capacitive (reactive) |
| Typical application | Micromirrors, RF switches | Accelerometers, microphones | Commercial dominance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Schematic (Adapted as Block Diagram)

Because detailed physical drawings of MEMS cross-sections cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following diagram captures the **functional block architecture** of an electrostatic sensor/actuator, including the mechanical, electrical, and signal-conditioning domains.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["MECHANICAL DOMAIN"]
        A1["Proof mass / Movable electrode"]
        A2["Suspension spring (k)"]
        A3["Mechanical displacement x"]
        A1 --> A2
        A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph B["ELECTRICAL DOMAIN"]
        B1["Voltage source V (DC bias)"]
        B2["Capacitor C(x) = eps0 epsA / g0 - x"]
        B3["Charge Q = C V"]
        B1 --> B2
        B2 --> B3
    end

    subgraph C["COUPLING DOMAIN"]
        C1["Electrostatic force F_E = 0.5 eps0 epsA V^2 / g^2"]
        C2["Pull-in detector: V_PI check"]
        C1 --> C2
    end

    subgraph D["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
        D1["Charge amplifier / C-V converter"]
        D2["ADC / Output voltage"]
        D1 --> D2
    end

    B2 -.induces.-> C1
    C1 -.acts on.-> A1
    A3 -.feedback to.-> B2
    B2 -.readout.-> D1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 -.alarm.-> D1
```

### 4.2 Functional Force-Displacement Equilibrium Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STABLE["STABLE REGION (0 <= x < g0/3)"]
        S1["F_spring = kx (linear)"]
        S2["F_electrostatic = const / g^2 (nonlinear)"]
        S3["Single intersection point"]
    end

    subgraph PULLIN["PULL-IN POINT (x = g0/3)"]
        P1["F_spring tangent to F_electrostatic"]
        P2["V = V_PI"]
    end

    subgraph UNSTABLE["UNSTABLE REGION (g0/3 < x < g0)"]
        U1["No equilibrium"]
        U2["Snap-to-contact collapse"]
    end

    subgraph CONTACT["CONTACT (x = g0)"]
        K1["Dielectric charging risk"]
        K2["Stiction"]
    end

    STABLE --> PULLIN --> UNSTABLE --> CONTACT
```

### 4.3 System-Level Architecture: Closed-Loop Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT"]
        I1["Physical stimulus: acceleration / pressure / force"]
    end

    I1 --> M1["Movable electrode deflection dx"]
    M1 --> M2["Capacitance change dC"]
    M2 --> E1["Charge amplifier"]
    E1 --> E2["Output voltage Vout"]
    E2 --> PROC["Signal processor / MCU"]
    PROC --> DISP["Display / Actuator control"]

    subgraph BIAS["BIAS NETWORK"]
        B1["DC bias V_bias"]
        B2["AC carrier V_ac (for differential readout)"]
    end

    BIAS --> M2
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define electrostatic actuation. State two advantages and one disadvantage of electrostatic actuation in MEMS.*

**Model Answer:**

Electrostatic actuation is the method of producing mechanical motion in a micro-structure by using the attractive Coulomb force between oppositely charged electrodes of a capacitor, typically a parallel-plate configuration with one movable and one fixed electrode.

**Advantages:**
1. **Near-zero power consumption:** No steady-state current flows; only charging/discharging currents occur during switching.
2. **CMOS-compatible fabrication:** Uses the same conductive and dielectric layers as standard integrated circuits, enabling monolithic integration.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Pull-in instability limits the stable travel range to one-third of the initial gap**, requiring careful design or closed-loop control.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + 2 advantages (1) + 1 disadvantage (1) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is pull-in instability in electrostatic MEMS actuators? Write the expression for pull-in voltage.*

**Model Answer:**

**Pull-in instability** is a phenomenon in electrostatic actuators where, beyond a critical applied voltage, the electrostatic attractive force grows faster than the mechanical spring restoring force can compensate. The movable electrode becomes unstable and is rapidly pulled (snaps) toward the fixed electrode, causing collapse.

**Pull-in voltage expression:**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$$

where $k$ is the spring stiffness, $g_0$ is the initial gap, $A$ is the electrode area, and $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ is the permittivity of the medium.

**Marks Distribution:** Phenomenon explanation (2) + Formula (1) = **3 marks**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electrostatic force between the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor in terms of the applied voltage $V$, plate area $A$, and gap $g$. Explain why the force is attractive and how it varies with gap. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MEMS parallel-plate capacitor has $A = 200 \times 200 \ \mu m^2$, initial gap $g_0 = 2 \ \mu m$, and a suspension spring of stiffness $k = 1 \ N/m$. The medium is air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). Calculate: (i) the pull-in voltage, (ii) the pull-in displacement, and (iii) the capacitance at the pull-in point. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Capacitance expression.** For a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Stored energy at constant voltage $V$:**

$$W_E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3 — Force from virtual work (energy method):**

$$F_E = -\frac{\partial W_E}{\partial g} \bigg\vert_{V} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial g}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g}\right)$$

$$F_E = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2g^2}$$

**Step 4 — Explanation of attraction and gap dependence.** The force is **attractive** because the energy $W_E$ decreases as $g$ decreases (positive charges on one plate attract negative charges on the other). The system seeks the lower-energy configuration. The force is **inversely proportional to $g^2$**: as the gap shrinks, the force grows rapidly, leading to the nonlinear instability known as **pull-in**.

**Marks Allocation:** Capacitance (1) + Energy (2) + Derivation (2) + Physical explanation (2) = **7 marks**.

---

#### Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- $A = 200 \times 200 \ \mu m^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8} \ m^2$
- $g_0 = 2 \ \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-6} \ m$
- $k = 1 \ N/m$
- $\varepsilon_r = 1$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ F/m$

**(i) Pull-in voltage:**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$$

Compute numerator:

$$8 k g_0^3 = 8 \times 1 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 8 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 6.4 \times 10^{-17} \ N \cdot m^3$$

Compute denominator:

$$27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A = 27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$= 27 \times 8.854 \times 4 \times 10^{-20} = 956.23 \times 10^{-20} = 9.5623 \times 10^{-18}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{PI}^2 = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-17}}{9.5623 \times 10^{-18}} = 6.693 \ V^2$$

$$\boxed{V_{PI} \approx 2.587 \ V}$$

**(ii) Pull-in displacement:**

$$x_{PI} = \frac{g_0}{3} = \frac{2 \ \mu m}{3} \approx 0.667 \ \mu m$$

**(iii) Capacitance at pull-in point:**

At pull-in, gap = $g_0 - x_{PI} = g_0 - g_0/3 = 2g_0/3 = 4/3 \ \mu m$.

$$C_{PI} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{(2 g_0 / 3)} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{(4/3) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-19}}{1.3333 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.656 \times 10^{-13} \ F$$

$$\boxed{C_{PI} \approx 0.266 \ pF}$$

**Marks Allocation:** (i) formula (1) + substitution (1) + answer (0.5); (ii) formula (0.5) + answer (0.5); (iii) formula (1) + substitution (1) + answer (0.5) = **7 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator. Discuss its energy method of force derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A capacitive accelerometer has a proof mass $m = 1 \ \mu g = 10^{-9} \ kg$ suspended by a spring of stiffness $k = 0.1 \ N/m$. The parallel-plate sensing capacitor has area $A = 100 \times 100 \ \mu m^2$ and gap $g_0 = 3 \ \mu m$. Determine: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the change in capacitance per unit g of acceleration, and (iii) the sensitivity in V/g if the readout circuit applies a bias of 5 V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a):

**Working of a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator:**

The device consists of a **movable top electrode** suspended above a **fixed bottom electrode** by flexible suspension beams (springs). A voltage $V$ applied between the electrodes produces an electric field, and opposite charges accumulate on the facing surfaces. The Coulomb attraction between these charges produces a **net downward electrostatic force** on the movable electrode. The force is balanced by the upward **spring restoring force** of the suspension. The equilibrium displacement $x$ is found by force balance.

**Energy method of force derivation:**

At constant voltage, the total system energy is:

$$W_{total} = W_{spring} + W_{electric} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2 - \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2(g_0 - x)}$$

(The negative sign arises because decreasing $g$ decreases the electrical energy — the system "wants" to collapse to lower the energy.)

**Force from energy gradient:**

$$F_E = -\frac{\partial W_{total}}{\partial x}$$

Setting $\partial W_{total} / \partial x = 0$ gives the equilibrium condition:

$$k x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2(g_0 - x)^2}$$

**Marks Allocation:** Diagram description (2) + Working (2) + Energy formulation (2) + Equilibrium (1) = **7 marks**.

---

#### Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- $m = 10^{-9} \ kg$
- $k = 0.1 \ N/m$
- $A = 100 \times 100 \ \mu m^2 = 10^{-8} \ m^2$
- $g_0 = 3 \ \mu m = 3 \times 10^{-6} \ m$
- $V_{bias} = 5 \ V$

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{10^8} = 10^4 \ rad/s$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} \approx \boxed{1591.5 \ Hz}$$

**(ii) Change in capacitance per unit g:**

Under acceleration $a$, the proof-mass displacement in static equilibrium is:

$$x = \frac{m a}{k}$$

For $a = g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$:

$$x = \frac{10^{-9} \times 9.81}{0.1} = 9.81 \times 10^{-8} \ m = 0.0981 \ \mu m$$

The change in capacitance:

$$\Delta C = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0^2} \cdot x = -\frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-8}}{(3 \times 10^{-6})^2} \times 9.81 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$= -\frac{8.854 \times 10^{-20}}{9 \times 10^{-12}} \times 9.81 \times 10^{-8} = -9.838 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.81 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\Delta C \approx -9.65 \times 10^{-16} \ F \ \text{per g}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \Delta C / g \vert \approx 0.965 \ fF/g}$$

**(iii) Sensitivity in V/g:**

Using a charge-amplifier readout with bias $V_{bias}$, the output voltage change is approximately:

$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{V_{bias}}{C_0} \cdot \Delta C$$

First compute $C_0$:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g_0} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-8}}{3 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.951 \times 10^{-14} \ F$$

$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{5 \times (-9.65 \times 10^{-16})}{2.951 \times 10^{-14}} = -1.635 \times 10^{-1} \ V$$

$$\boxed{\vert \Delta V_{out} / g \vert \approx 0.164 \ V/g}$$

**Marks Allocation:** (i) formula (1) + answer (1); (ii) displacement (1) + ΔC formula (1) + substitution (1) + answer (0.5); (iii) readout formula (1) + substitution (0.5) = **7 marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error in force derivation:** Forgetting the negative sign from the energy gradient gives a *repulsive* force — full 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in pull-in formula:** Forgetting to convert μm to m, or N/m to consistent SI units — usually loses 1 mark per computation.
> 3. **Using $g_0$ instead of $(g_0 - x)$** in equilibrium equations: this is the single most common error in derivation problems (loses 2–3 marks).
> 4. **Forgetting $\varepsilon_0$** (using only $\varepsilon_r$): 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **Not specifying "air" or the dielectric medium** in numerical problems: KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing physical context.
> 6. **Skipping intermediate steps** in derivations: KTU demands each algebraic step to be visible; using phrases like "after simplification" causes 1–2 mark deductions.
> 7. **In capacitive sensing questions**, confusing $\Delta C$ direction (gap increases → $C$ decreases): mandatory sign convention check.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Electrostatic actuation** uses Coulomb force between oppositely charged plates of a capacitor.
- [x] The canonical structure is a **parallel-plate capacitor** with one movable and one fixed electrode.
- [x] **Capacitance:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / g$ — proportional to area, inversely proportional to gap.
- [x] **Stored energy (constant V):** $W_E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 / (2g)$.
- [x] **Electrostatic force:** $F_E = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 / (2g^2)$ — attractive, $\propto V^2$, $\propto 1/g^2$.
- [x] **Force is derived from the energy method:** $F = -\partial W / \partial g$ at constant $V$.
- [x] **Spring force balance:** $k x = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 / (2(g_0 - x)^2)$.
- [x] **Pull-in instability** is a fundamental electrostatic MEMS phenomenon.
- [x] **Pull-in displacement:** $x_{PI} = g_0 / 3$ (one-third of the initial gap).
- [x] **Pull-in voltage:** $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k g_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A)}$.
- [x] **Stable operating range:** $0 \leq x < g_0/3$ for open-loop operation.
- [x] **Capacitive sensitivity:** $S = \partial C / \partial g = -\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / g^2$ — increases as gap shrinks.
- [x] **Capacitive sensors** detect physical quantities (acceleration, pressure, displacement) via $\Delta C$.
- [x] **Advantages of electrostatic MEMS:** near-zero power, CMOS-compatible, fast response, small size.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** small stable travel, pull-in limits, requires high voltage at large gaps.
- [x] **Common applications:** accelerometers, gyroscopes, micromirrors (DMD), RF switches, microphones, pressure sensors.
- [x] **Vacuum permittivity** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ F/m$ — memorize this constant.
- [x] **Design trade-off:** smaller gap → higher sensitivity AND lower pull-in voltage (both effects).
- [x] **Closed-loop control** can extend stable operation beyond the open-loop pull-in point.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Piezoelectric Effect** (from Greek *piezein* — "to press/ squeeze") is the linear electromechanical interaction between the mechanical state (stress/strain) and the electrical state (electric field/electric displacement) in certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials. This phenomenon enables the **direct conversion of mechanical energy into electrical energy** (sensor mode) and **electrical energy into mechanical strain** (actuator mode).

A **Piezoelectric Sensor** is a device that exploits the *direct piezoelectric effect* to transduce applied mechanical force, pressure, or acceleration into a measurable electrical charge or voltage.

A **Piezoelectric Actuator** is a device that exploits the *inverse (converse) piezoelectric effect* to produce a precisely controlled mechanical displacement or force when subjected to an external electric field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Only **non-centrosymmetric** crystals exhibit piezoelectricity. The unit cell must lack a centre of symmetry. Materials: Quartz (SiO₂), Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT), Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃), PVDF polymer, Zinc Oxide (ZnO), and Aluminium Nitride (AlN).

### 1.2 The Two Faces of Piezoelectricity

| Effect | Energy Conversion Direction | Physical Cause | Device Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Effect** (Curie & Curie, 1880) | Mechanical → Electrical | Polarization changes due to applied stress | **Sensors** |
| **Converse (Inverse) Effect** (Lippmann, 1881) | Electrical → Mechanical | Strain induced by applied electric field | **Actuators** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine a sponge made of polarized molecules** — each molecule has a tiny positive end and a tiny negative end, like a microscopic bar magnet. Under normal conditions, these tiny dipoles point in random directions, so the net charge is zero.

**When you squeeze the sponge (sensor mode):** The polarized molecules get pushed closer together in a non-symmetric way. This makes the positive ends of many molecules line up and the negative ends line up on opposite faces. Suddenly, opposite faces of the sponge carry net positive and net negative charge — a voltage appears! Tap it, and you get a pulse of electricity.

**When you apply a voltage across the sponge (actuator mode):** The electric field tugs the positive ends one way and the negative ends the opposite way. The sponge physically stretches or compresses by a tiny amount (nanometres). Switch the polarity, and it bends the other way.

This is exactly how a **quartz crystal in a digital watch** vibrates 32,768 times a second to keep time, and how a **piezoelectric lighter** generates 10,000 V to spark fuel by simply snapping a crystal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Crystal Classes:** Of the 32 crystallographic point groups, **20 are non-centrosymmetric**, and of these, **20 exhibit piezoelectricity** (all 20 non-centrosymmetric groups except the cubic point group 432). Of these 20, **10 groups are *also* ferroelectric** (possess a switchable spontaneous polarization).

### 1.4 Why Piezoelectricity Matters in MEMS

- **Solid-state:** No moving parts (in the macroscopic sense), highly reliable.
- **High bandwidth:** Response times in microseconds to nanoseconds.
- **High force density:** PZT can produce > **30 MPa** of stress.
- **Scalable:** Compatible with silicon IC fabrication (e.g., ZnO and AlN thin films).
- **Self-powered sensing:** No external power required for sensor mode.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Symmetry Argument — Why Some Crystals Are Piezoelectric

In a crystal, the application of an electric field $E_j$ creates a polarization $P_i$ which is generally expressible as a Taylor expansion of $E$. The linear term is governed by the **second-rank dielectric susceptibility tensor** $\chi_{ij}$. For a linear, lossless medium, $\chi_{ij}$ must be symmetric ($\chi_{ij} = \chi_{ji}$). The polarization is then:

$$P_i = \chi_{ij} E_j$$

For this to be non-zero, the crystal must lack an inversion centre. If a centre of symmetry exists, then $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$, $E_j \to -E_j$, and $P_i \to -P_i$. This would force $P_i = 0$, forbidding the linear coupling.

### 2.2 The Piezoelectric Constitutive Equations

There are **four** equivalent forms of the constitutive relations, differing in which variables are chosen as independent. KTU 2024 expects familiarity with all four.

**Form 1 (Stress-charge, e-form):** Independent variables: strain $S$, electric field $E$.

$$T_e = c^{ES} S - e^t E_f$$

$$D_i = e_i S + \varepsilon^{S} E_f$$

**Form 2 (Strain-charge, d-form, MOST COMMON):** Independent variables: stress $T$, electric field $E$.

$$S = s^E T + d^t E$$

$$D = d T + \varepsilon^T E$$

**Form 3 (Stress-voltage, h-form):** Independent variables: strain $S$, electric displacement $D$.

$$T = c^D S - h^t D$$

$$E = -h S + \beta^S D$$

**Form 4 (Strain-voltage, g-form):** Independent variables: stress $T$, electric displacement $D$.

$$S = s^D T + g^t D$$

$$E = -g T + \beta^T D$$

### 2.3 Key Piezoelectric Coefficients and Their Physical Meaning

| Coefficient | Symbol | Definition | Typical Units | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric charge constant** | $d$ | Strain produced per unit electric field (actuator) **OR** charge density produced per unit stress (sensor) | C/N or m/V | Actuator: $d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ for PZT |
| **Piezoelectric voltage constant** | $g$ | Electric field produced per unit stress (sensor) | V·m/N or m²/C | Sensor: $g_{33} = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ for PZT |
| **Piezoelectric stress constant** | $e$ | Stress produced per unit electric field (actuator) | N/V·m | Force generation |
| **Piezoelectric stiffness constant** | $h$ | Stress per unit electric displacement | N/C | Thick actuators |
| **Electromechanical coupling factor** | $k^2$ | Square of efficiency of energy conversion | dimensionless, $0 < k^2 < 1$ | $k_{33} \approx 0.6$ for PZT-5A |
| **Dielectric constant** | $\varepsilon$ | Permittivity at constant stress | F/m | Capacitance |
| **Elastic compliance** | $s$ | Strain per unit stress at constant $E$ | m²/N | Mechanical deflection |
| **Elastic stiffness** | $c$ | Stress per unit strain at constant $E$ | N/m² | Young's modulus analog |

### 2.4 The Two-Index Notation — $d_{ij}$

The piezoelectric coefficient $d_{ij}$ uses **contracted matrix notation (IEEE 176 standard)**:

- $i = 1, 2, 3$ → electric field/charge direction (1, 2 = lateral; 3 = poling axis)
- $j = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$ → stress/strain direction (1–3 = normal; 4–6 = shear)

The most important coefficient is $d_{33}$ (poling direction field → poling direction strain).
Other frequently used: $d_{31}$ (lateral strain from vertical field) and $d_{15}$ (shear).

### 2.5 Engineering Master Formulae

| # | Formula | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = d \cdot E$ | Actuator strain per applied field |
| 2 | $D = d \cdot T$ | Sensor charge per applied stress |
| 3 | $E = g \cdot T$ | Sensor open-circuit voltage per stress |
| 4 | $k^2 = \dfrac{d^2}{\varepsilon^T \cdot s^E}$ | Energy conversion efficiency |
| 5 | $g = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon^T}$ | Relation between $d$ and $g$ |
| 6 | $e = d \cdot c^E$ | Relation between $d$ and $e$ |
| 7 | $h = \dfrac{e}{\varepsilon^S}$ | Relation between $e$ and $h$ |
| 8 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}$ | Impermittivity |
| 9 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{T} A}{t}$ | Capacitance of a piezoelectric plate |
| 10 | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Total charge generated by force $F$ on $d_{33}$-mode |
| 11 | $V_{oc} = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot \sigma$ | Open-circuit voltage from stress $\sigma$ on a plate of thickness $t$ |
| 12 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{c^D}{\rho}}$ | Resonance frequency of a length-extensional bar |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Quartz crystal microbalance (QCM):** Measures mass changes down to **nanograms** by tracking resonance frequency shift $\Delta f = -2 f_0^2 \Delta m / (A \sqrt{\rho \mu})$.
- **Inkjet printheads:** Piezoelectric walls squeeze ink chambers, ejecting picolitre droplets.
- **SAW filters (Surface Acoustic Wave):** AlN on silicon for RF filtering in mobile phones at 1–10 GHz.
- **Energy harvesters:** Footstep-powered lights using $d_{31}$ mode cantilevers.
- **AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy) tips:** Piezoelectric tube scanners with sub-Ångström resolution.
- **Ultrasonic transducers:** Medical imaging, sonar, non-destructive testing.
- **Gyroscopes & accelerometers:** Coriolis force sensed piezoelectrically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Equation:** The electromechanical coupling factor $k^2$ is *the* number that tells an engineer how good a piezo material is for any given application. $k^2 > 0.5$ is "excellent"; $k^2 < 0.1$ is "poor" (e.g., quartz: $k^2 \approx 0.01$).
