> ✅ **Bending Stress:** $\sigma = My/I$ — maximum at extreme fiber ($y = c$); $I = bh^3/12$ for rectangles; maximum moment at the support of cantilevers and at the midspan of fixed-fixed beams under uniform load.
>
> ✅ **Thermal Stress:** $\sigma_{\text{th}} = E\alpha\Delta T$ — critical in packaged MEMS and multi-layer structures; mismatched CTE between layers is a common failure trigger.
>
> ✅ **Stress Transformation (2D):** $\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$.
>
> ✅ **Principal Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ — plug into **every** Part B question.
>
> ✅ **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{\max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$ — occurs on planes at 45° to principal planes.
>
> ✅ **Shear Modulus Relation:** $G = E / [2(1 + \nu)]$ — memorize this **3-mark** favorite.
>
> ✅ **MEMS-Specific Applications to Mention in Answers:**
> - Residual stress in LPCVD polysilicon (causes beam curling)
> - Thermal stress in anodic bonding (Pyrex-to-silicon)
> - Stress concentrations at comb-finger corners
> - Stress in pressure-sensor diaphragms (bending)
> - Stress in gyroscope proof masses (centrifugal + bending)
>
> ✅ **Numerical Hygiene:** Convert everything to **SI (m, N, Pa)** before calculation. Always show step-by-step substitutions.
>
> ✅ **Examiner's Eye:** When asked to "explain," always include: **definition + formula + physical meaning + MEMS example**. A 3-mark answer that hits all four is virtually guaranteed full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Strain

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)**, **strain** is formally defined as a dimensionless measure of the *normalized deformation* experienced by a material element under the action of applied mechanical forces, thermal loads, or residual fabrication stresses. Mathematically, it represents the **change in length per unit original length** along a specified spatial direction, and it is a *tensor quantity* of rank two in rigorous continuum mechanics.

In MEMS Module 2, the concept of strain is the fundamental kinematic descriptor that quantifies *how much* a suspended micro-beam, a thin-film membrane, or a proof mass deflects relative to its original geometry, before any consideration of the *forces* (stress) producing such deformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** For MEMS, strain is the most critical kinematic parameter because it directly determines the *sensitivity* of piezoresistive pressure sensors, the *gauge factor* of MEMS accelerometers, the *pull-in voltage* of capacitive transducers, and the *fatigue life* of RF MEMS switches. Mastering strain in this course means understanding it as both a *macroscopic* engineering variable and a *local field* at the micro-scale.

## 1.2 Types of Strain — The Three Fundamental Modes

Strain in a 3D deformable body is fully characterized by **three independent normal strain components** and **three independent shear strain components**, forming the **strain tensor** $\varepsilon_{ij}$.

| Mode | Symbol | Description | MEMS Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal (Axial) Strain** | $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$ | Length change along an axis | Beam bending under acceleration |
| **Shear Strain** | $\gamma_{xy}, \gamma_{yz}, \gamma_{zx}$ | Angular distortion of an element | Torsional mirror in optical MEMS |
| **Volumetric (Bulk) Strain** | $\varepsilon_v$ | Relative change in total volume | Packaged die under thermal cycling |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Band" Intuition

Imagine you have a **rubber band** of original length $L_0 = 10 \text{ cm}$ marked with two dots. When you stretch the band to a new length $L = 12 \text{ cm}$:

- The dots have moved **further apart** by $\Delta L = 2 \text{ cm}$.
- The **strain** is simply this displacement *normalized* by the original length: $\varepsilon = 2/10 = 0.2$ (or **20%**).
- If instead you **squeezed** the band to $L = 9 \text{ cm}$, $\varepsilon = -1/10 = -0.1$ (**compressive** strain).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dimensionless?** Because we divide a length by a length, the units cancel. This is what makes strain a *pure number* — easy to use in calculations and universally applicable from nanometer-scale resonators to meter-scale civil structures.

**Real-world MEMS analogy:** The same principle applies to a **silicon cantilever beam** in an atomic force microscope (AFM). When the AFM tip pushes down on the beam's free end, the beam bends. The bending is described by a *gradient of normal strain* along the beam's thickness — the top fiber is in tension, the bottom fiber is in compression, and the neutral axis (mid-plane) experiences zero strain.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**S**train is **S**hape change" — it's *geometric* (kinematic). Don't confuse it with **stress**, which is *force per unit area* (dynamic). Stress causes strain; strain measures the result.

## 1.4 Engineering Strain vs. True Strain

Two distinct definitions of normal strain are used in MEMS design:

**Engineering Strain (Nominal Strain):**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**True (Logarithmic / Natural) Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL'}{L'} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$$

> [!WARNING]
> For MEMS applications involving *small deflections* (sub-micron), $\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} \approx \varepsilon_{\text{true}}$ to within 0.1% error. However, for *large-deflection* devices such as microfluidic membranes or large-stroke actuators, true strain must be used. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you use engineering strain for a problem clearly involving large deformation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Normal Strain — Component-Wise Derivation

Consider an infinitesimal material element in a deformable body. Let the original position of a point be $\vec{X} = (X, Y, Z)$ and the deformed position be $\vec{x} = (x, y, z)$. The displacement field is:

$$\vec{u} = \vec{x} - \vec{X} = (u, v, w)$$

For *small deformations* (the standard MEMS assumption), the **Cauchy infinitesimal strain tensor** components are:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

$$\varepsilon_{xy} = \varepsilon_{yx} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{yz} = \varepsilon_{zy} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{zx} = \varepsilon_{xz} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor of ½ for shear?** The shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ is the *total* change in angle between two originally perpendicular line elements. The off-diagonal tensor component $\varepsilon_{xy}$ is the *symmetric half* of this. Many MEMS textbooks write $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$ — both are correct, but you must be consistent with notation.

## 2.2 Volumetric (Dilatational) Strain

The volumetric strain measures the *fractional change in volume* of a small element:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} = \text{tr}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})$$

In **isotropic materials** under hydrostatic pressure, $\varepsilon_{xx} = \varepsilon_{yy} = \varepsilon_{zz} = \varepsilon$, giving $\varepsilon_v = 3\varepsilon$.

## 2.3 Poisson's Effect — Transverse Strain Coupling

When a material is stretched in one direction, it *contracts* in the perpendicular directions. This is the **Poisson effect**, and the dimensionless ratio is **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{transverse}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$$

**KTU High-Yield Values for MEMS Materials:**

| Material | Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Crystal Silicon (Si ⟨100⟩)** | **130** | **0.28** | 2330 |
| Polysilicon | 160 | 0.22 | 2320 |
| Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) | 70 | 0.17 | 2200 |
| Aluminum (thin film) | 70 | 0.35 | 2700 |
| Gold (Au) | 78 | 0.44 | 19300 |
| SU-8 Photoresist | 4.0 | 0.33 | 1200 |
| PDMS (elastomer) | 0.001–0.01 | 0.49 | 970 |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — MEMORIZE THIS TABLE.** Every formula below appears directly in KTU Module 2 questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering normal strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | Beam deflection |
| 2 | True (logarithmic) strain | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L/L_0)$ | dimensionless | Large-deflection membranes |
| 3 | Shear strain (small angle) | $\gamma \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ | radians | Torsional micromirrors |
| 4 | Volumetric strain | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | dimensionless | Packaged die stress |
| 5 | Poisson's ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\perp}/\varepsilon_{\parallel}$ | dimensionless | Biaxial thin-film stress |
| 6 | Hooke's law (uniaxial) | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Pa | Piezoresistive sensors |
| 7 | Hooke's law (shear) | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Pa | Shear-mode gyroscopes |
| 8 | Bulk modulus relation | $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Pa | Hydrostatic pressure sensors |
| 9 | Biaxial strain (thin film) | $\varepsilon_{\text{film}} = (\sigma_f)(1-\nu_f)/E_f$ | dimensionless | Residual stress analysis |
| 10 | Thermal strain | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$ | dimensionless | Thermal mismatch in packaging |
| 11 | Generalized Hooke's law | $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl}\varepsilon_{kl}$ | Pa | Full anisotropic case |
| 12 | Piezoresistive gauge factor | $GF = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Strain gauge design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Caution in KTU Answer Scripts:** When writing `|x|` in handwritten or typed answers, ensure the vertical bars are clearly distinct from the letter "I" or the number "1". Examiners routinely misread poorly drawn absolute value bars as the digit 1, leading to lost marks.

## 2.5 Real-World MEMS Engineering Utility

Strain analysis in MEMS is not merely theoretical — it directly drives the following design decisions:

- **Piezoresistive Pressure Sensors:** The sensitivity $S = GF \times E \times \pi$ depends linearly on the strain produced by the diaphragm bending. Higher strain → higher sensitivity.
- **Accelerometer Proof Mass:** The strain at the anchor of a cantilever is used to size the piezoresistor for maximum signal-to-noise ratio.
- **RF MEMS Switches:** Cycle life is determined by the *maximum cyclic strain amplitude* in the flexure — typically limited to $<0.5\%$ to avoid fatigue.
- **Microfluidic Membranes:** The strain gradient controls the *pumping volume per cycle* in peristaltic pumps.
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs):** Cross-axis coupling is governed by the *off-diagonal shear strain* components — minimizing these improves device accuracy.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Engineering Strain from Displacement Field

We will derive the engineering normal strain in the $x$-direction for a 1D bar stretched along its axis.

**Starting Premise:** Consider a uniform bar of original length $L_0$ lying along the $x$-axis. A point at original coordinate $X$ displaces to $x = X + u(X)$ after loading, where $u(X)$ is the displacement field.

**Step 1 — Define a small material segment:**

A second point originally at $X + \Delta X$ moves to $X + \Delta X + u(X + \Delta X)$.

The new length of this segment is:

$$L = [X + \Delta X + u(X + \Delta X)] - [X + u(X)] = \Delta X + [u(X + \Delta X) - u(X)]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the change in length:**

$$\Delta L = L - L_0 = \Delta X + [u(X + \Delta X) - u(X)] - \Delta X = u(X + \Delta X) - u(X)$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta L = u(X + \Delta X) - u(X)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of engineering strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{u(X + \Delta X) - u(X)}{\Delta X}$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $\Delta X \to 0$:**

In the limit of an infinitesimal material segment, the engineering normal strain becomes the *spatial gradient* of the displacement field:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \lim_{\Delta X \to 0} \frac{u(X + \Delta X) - u(X)}{\Delta X} = \frac{du}{dx}$$

**Interpretation:** Strain is the *spatial derivative* of displacement. Wherever displacement varies spatially, there is strain. A rigid-body translation ($u = \text{const}$) produces *zero* strain.

## 3.2 Derivation: True Strain from Engineering Strain

**Starting Premise:** For large deformations, the engineering strain is no longer accurate because the *original* length $L_0$ keeps changing as the material extends. The rigorous definition uses an *incremental* ratio referenced to the *instantaneous* length.

**Step 1 — Write the incremental strain definition:**

$$d\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from the original length $L_0$ to the final length $L$:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL'}{L'} = \big[\ln L'\big]_{L_0}^{L} = \ln L - \ln L_0 = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of engineering strain:**

Since $L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$$

**Step 4 — Taylor series expansion for small strain:**

Using $\ln(1+x) = x - \frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x^3}{3} - \cdots$ for small $x$:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} \approx \varepsilon_{\text{eng}} - \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{eng}}^{2}}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{eng}}^{3}}{3} - \cdots$$

For $\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} \ll 1$ (true in most MEMS), the leading term suffices: $\varepsilon_{\text{true}} \approx \varepsilon_{\text{eng}}$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Strain in a MEMS Cantilever

**Problem Statement:** A silicon cantilever beam (length $L = 500 \text{ μm}$, thickness $t = 5 \text{ μm}$, width $b = 50 \text{ μm}$) is subjected to a tip force $F = 10 \text{ μN}$. The Young's modulus of silicon along the relevant crystal direction is $E = 130 \text{ GPa}$. Calculate:

(a) The maximum bending stress at the fixed end.
(b) The maximum normal strain at the top surface of the fixed end.
(c) The tip deflection.

**Given Data (extracted from the problem):**
- $L = 500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $t = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $b = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $F = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}$
- $E = 130 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Compute the second moment of area for a rectangular cross-section:**

$$I = \frac{b t^{3}}{12} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12}$$

Expanding the cube:

$$t^{3} = (5 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 125 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^{3}$$

Multiplying:

$$b \cdot t^{3} = (50 \times 10^{-6})(125 \times 10^{-18}) = 6250 \times 10^{-24} = 6.25 \times 10^{-21} \text{ m}^{4}$$

Dividing by 12:

$$I = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-21}}{12} = 5.208 \times 10^{-22} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment at the fixed end:**

For a cantilever with a tip load, the maximum moment occurs at the root:

$$M_{\max} = F \cdot L = (10 \times 10^{-6})(500 \times 10^{-6}) = 5000 \times 10^{-12} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress (at top surface, $y = t/2$):**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \cdot (t/2)}{I} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-9})(2.5 \times 10^{-6})}{5.208 \times 10^{-22}}$$

Numerator:

$$(5 \times 10^{-9})(2.5 \times 10^{-6}) = 12.5 \times 10^{-15} = 1.25 \times 10^{-14} \text{ N·m}^{2}$$

Dividing:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-14}}{5.208 \times 10^{-22}} = 2.40 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 24 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum normal strain at the top surface:**

Applying Hooke's law in uniaxial form:

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{E} = \frac{2.40 \times 10^{7}}{130 \times 10^{9}} = 1.846 \times 10^{-4} = 184.6 \text{ μstrain}$$

**Step 5 — Tip deflection using the standard cantilever formula:**

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})(500 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{3 (130 \times 10^{9})(5.208 \times 10^{-22})}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$L^{3} = (500 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 125000 \times 10^{-18} = 1.25 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = (10 \times 10^{-6})(1.25 \times 10^{-13}) = 1.25 \times 10^{-18} \text{ N·m}^{3}$$

Computing the denominator:

$$3EI = 3 \times (130 \times 10^{9}) \times (5.208 \times 10^{-22}) = 3 \times 67.7 \times 10^{-13} = 2.031 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N·m}^{2}$$

Final division:

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-18}}{2.031 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.15 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 0.0615 \text{ μm}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Max bending stress | $\sigma_{\max}$ | **24 MPa** |
| Max normal strain | $\varepsilon_{\max}$ | **184.6 μstrain** |
| Tip deflection | $\delta_{\text{tip}}$ | **0.0615 μm** |

> [!NOTE]
> The maximum strain of 184.6 μstrain is *very small* (below 0.02%) — well within the linear elastic regime of silicon. This justifies the use of Hooke's law and small-deflection theory in the analysis.
