# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define yield strength. Why is the 0.2% offset method used for determining the yield strength of MEMS thin films?**

**Model Answer:**
Yield strength $\sigma_y$ is the stress at which a material transitions from elastic to plastic deformation, beyond which permanent (non-recoverable) strain develops.

The 0.2% offset method is used for MEMS thin films (e.g., sputtered Al, Au, Cu) and polymers (e.g., SU-8) because these materials do **not exhibit a sharp, well-defined yield point** on their stress-strain curve — the transition from elastic to plastic behaviour is gradual. The method constructs a line parallel to the initial linear elastic region, offset by 0.2% strain; the stress at the intersection with the actual curve is defined as $\sigma_{y,0.2\%}$. This standardized procedure enables reproducible comparison between different MEMS material batches and fabrication processes.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three yield criteria used in MEMS design and state the type of material for which each is most suitable.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine):** $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_y$ — best suited for **brittle MEMS materials** such as single-crystal silicon, silicon nitride, and glass.
2. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca):** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq \sigma_y$ — best suited for **ductile materials** and conservative contact-mechanics analyses in RF MEMS switches.
3. **Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises):** $\sigma_{vM} \leq \sigma_y$ — best suited for **ductile metals and polymers**; widely used in FEM analysis of micro-actuators.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> For Part B numerical problems on yield strength, the most common mistakes students make are:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert all units to SI** (μm must become m, GPa must become Pa) before substitution — this alone accounts for 2–3 lost marks per question.
> 2. **Confusing engineering stress with true stress** — KTU problems default to *engineering* values unless the question explicitly states "true stress."
> 3. **Using the wrong yield criterion** for the material class (e.g., applying von Mises to brittle silicon instead of Rankine).
> 4. **Omitting the factor of safety** $n$ when the problem statement specifies a safety factor — students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 5. **Not specifying units in the final answer** — KTU examiners *will* deduct a mark if units are missing.

### Question A. **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** A MEMS cantilever beam made of polysilicon has length $L = 250 \, \mu m$, width $b = 40 \, \mu m$, and thickness $t = 2.5 \, \mu m$. The yield strength of the polysilicon is $\sigma_y = 2.0$ GPa and Young's modulus is $E = 170$ GPa. The beam is loaded with a tip force $P$ at its free end. Determine:
- (i) the maximum force $P_y$ that the beam can sustain without yielding **[4 marks]**
- (ii) the maximum elastic tip deflection $\delta_y$ at this load **[3 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the von Mises yield criterion. A MEMS diaphragm is subjected to a biaxial stress state with $\sigma_x = 150$ MPa and $\sigma_y = 80$ MPa. The yield strength of the diaphragm material is $\sigma_y = 220$ MPa. Using the von Mises criterion with a factor of safety of 2, determine whether the design is safe. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a)(i) — Maximum Yield Load
The maximum bending stress in a cantilever under end load $P$ occurs at the fixed end:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 P L}{b \, t^2}$$

Setting $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_y$ and solving for $P_y$:

$$P_y = \frac{\sigma_y \, b \, t^2}{6 L}$$

Convert all quantities to SI:
- $\sigma_y = 2.0 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $b = 40 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $t = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m $\Rightarrow t^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-12}$ m²
- $L = 250 \times 10^{-6}$ m

Substitute:
$$P_y = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^9) \cdot (40 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (6.25 \times 10^{-12})}{6 \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6})}$$

Numerator:
$$(2.0 \times 10^9) \cdot (40 \times 10^{-6}) = 8.0 \times 10^4$$
$$(8.0 \times 10^4) \cdot (6.25 \times 10^{-12}) = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

Denominator:
$$6 \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{P_y = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N} = 0.333 \text{ mN}}$$

#### Part (a)(ii) — Maximum Elastic Deflection
The tip deflection of a cantilever under end load is:

$$\delta = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I} \quad \text{where} \quad I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

Compute the second moment of area:
$$I = \frac{(40 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 15.625 \times 10^{-18}}{12} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-22}}{12} = 5.21 \times 10^{-23} \text{ m}^4$$

Substitute:
$$\delta_y = \frac{(3.33 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6})^3}{3 \cdot (170 \times 10^9) \cdot (5.21 \times 10^{-23})}$$

Numerator:
$$(250 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-11}$$
$$(3.33 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (1.5625 \times 10^{-11}) = 5.20 \times 10^{-15}$$

Denominator:
$$3 \cdot (170 \times 10^9) \cdot (5.21 \times 10^{-23}) = 2.66 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_y = \frac{5.20 \times 10^{-15}}{2.66 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 196 \, \mu m}$$

#### Part (b) — von Mises Yield Criterion and Design Safety Check
**Explanation:** The von Mises yield criterion states that yielding occurs when the **distortion energy** (the elastic energy associated with shape change) reaches a critical value. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}} \leq \sigma_y$$

For the 2D plane-stress case ($\sigma_3 = 0$), with $\sigma_1 = \sigma_x = 150$ MPa and $\sigma_2 = \sigma_y = 80$ MPa:

$$\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{(150)^2 - (150)(80) + (80)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{22{,}500 - 12{,}000 + 6{,}400}$$

$$= \sqrt{16{,}900} = 130 \text{ MPa}$$

**Safety check with factor of safety $n = 2$:**
The allowable stress is:
$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{220}{2} = 110 \text{ MPa}$$

Compare:
$$\sigma_{vM} = 130 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sigma_{allow} = 110 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_{vM} = 130$ MPa **>** $\sigma_{allow} = 110$ MPa:

$$\boxed{\text{The design is NOT safe.}}$$

The design exceeds the allowable stress by approximately 18%. Recommended actions: (i) increase diaphragm thickness, (ii) reduce operating pressure, or (iii) select a material with higher $\sigma_y$.

---

### Question B. **[14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the size effect on yield strength in MEMS thin films. How does the apparent yield strength of a polycrystalline metal film change as the film thickness decreases into the sub-micron regime? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A MEMS pressure-sensor diaphragm made of single-crystal silicon (yield strength $\sigma_y = 7$ GPa) is subjected to the principal stresses $\sigma_1 = 250$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 100$ MPa, and $\sigma_3 = -50$ MPa. Using **both the Tresca and the Rankine criteria** (without any safety factor), determine whether the diaphragm will yield or fail. Justify which criterion is more appropriate for silicon. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Size Effect on Yield Strength in MEMS Thin Films
As the characteristic dimension of a structural element (film thickness, grain size, beam width) shrinks into the sub-micron regime — typical of MEMS — the **apparent yield strength rises significantly** above its bulk value. This size effect arises from multiple physical mechanisms:

1. **Grain-boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch effect):** Polycrystalline films with smaller grain sizes have a higher density of grain boundaries, which act as barriers to dislocation motion. The empirical Hall-Petch relation is:
$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$
where $d$ is the grain diameter, $\sigma_0$ is the friction stress, and $k_y$ is a material constant. As $d$ decreases, $\sigma_y$ increases.

2. **Dislocation starvation:** In sub-micron volumes, dislocations can travel to free surfaces and annihilate before they multiply, leaving few mobile dislocations to mediate plastic flow. This raises the stress required for continued deformation.

3. **Surface and interface effects:** A larger fraction of atoms lies at or near the surface in nanoscale structures; surface oxide layers, passivation films, and substrate constraint further inhibit dislocation nucleation.

4. **Strain gradient plasticity:** Micro-beams experience very high strain gradients under bending, requiring geometrically necessary dislocations that contribute additional hardening.

**Net result:** Thin films of aluminum, copper, and gold in the 0.1–1 μm thickness range can exhibit yield strengths **2× to 5× higher** than their bulk counterparts. Designers must use measured thin-film $\sigma_y$ values, not bulk handbook values, for reliable MEMS design.

#### Part (b) — Tresca and Rankine Safety Evaluation
**Given principal stresses:** $\sigma_1 = 250$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = -50$ MPa, with $\sigma_y = 7000$ MPa for silicon.

**Tresca Criterion:**
$$\sigma_{Tresca} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 250 - (-50) = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

Compare to yield:
$$300 \text{ MPa} \ll 7000 \text{ MPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No yielding by Tresca}$$

**Rankine (Max Normal Stress) Criterion:**
Check the maximum tensile principal stress:
$$\sigma_1 = 250 \text{ MPa} \ll 7000 \text{ MPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No fracture by Rankine}$$

**Comparison and Justification:**

Both criteria predict survival. However, single-crystal silicon is a **brittle material** with negligible plastic deformation. Its failure mode is **brittle fracture**, not plastic yielding. Therefore, the **Rankine criterion is physically more appropriate** for silicon, as it directly addresses the maximum tensile stress that initiates crack propagation. The Tresca criterion, designed for ductile materials, underestimates the failure risk in brittle materials when localized stress concentrations or flaws are present.

**Conclusion:** The diaphragm is safe under the given load by both criteria. In real engineering practice, however, one would apply a safety factor of 2–4 to the Rankine stress (giving $\sigma_{allow} \approx 1.75$–$3.5$ GPa), which is still well above the operating 250 MPa.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Yield Strength (OEAET722 Module 2):**

- [ ] **Definition:** $\sigma_y$ is the stress at the elastic-to-plastic transition; reported in **Pa (MPa or GPa)**.
- [ ] **0.2% Offset Method:** Standard for ductile MEMS thin films and polymers; draw a line parallel to the elastic region offset by $\varepsilon = 0.002$.
- [ ] **Three Yield Criteria** (must be memorized):
  - Rankine: $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_y$ → brittle
  - Tresca: $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq \sigma_y$ → ductile, conservative
  - von Mises: $\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]} \leq \sigma_y$ → ductile, most accurate
- [ ] **Beam yield load formula:** $P_y = \sigma_y \, b \, t^2 / (6 L)$ for a cantilever with end load.
- [ ] **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ holds *only* in the elastic region; $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$.
- [ ] **Material hierarchy:** Single-crystal Si ($\sigma_y \approx 7$–$9$ GPa) >> Poly-Si (1–3 GPa) >> Thin-film metals (0.1–0.5 GPa) >> Polymers (0.005–0.07 GPa).
- [ ] **Size effect:** Apparent $\sigma_y$ rises as dimensions shrink; use thin-film measurements, not bulk data.
- [ ] **True vs. engineering stress:** $\sigma_{true} = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$; difference is negligible in elastic regime.
- [ ] **Factor of safety:** Always apply $n = 2$–$4$ in MEMS design: $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / n$.
- [ ] **Units discipline:** Convert all inputs to SI (μm → m, GPa → Pa) **before** substitution.
- [ ] **Brittle materials rule:** For Si/ceramics, the practical failure stress is the **fracture strength**, not the yield strength.
- [ ] **Resilience:** $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ is the elastic energy per unit volume stored at yield — important for impact/shock MEMS design.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ultimate Strength (Tensile Strength)

**Ultimate Strength** (also called **Ultimate Tensile Strength, UTS**) is defined as the maximum engineering stress that a homogeneous material can sustain under uniaxial tensile loading without undergoing **necking instability** or fracture. It represents the peak point on the engineering stress–strain curve of a ductile material, after which localized cross-sectional reduction begins.

$$
\sigma_{uts} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}
$$

where $F_{max}$ is the maximum axial force observed and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For MEMS materials (Silicon, Polysilicon, SiO₂, Aluminum, SU-8), ultimate strength values are typically reported in **MPa** or **GPa** and are critical when designing structural elements such as flexures, diaphragms, and suspended micro-bridges that may undergo accidental overload.

## 1.2 General Stress–Strain Relations (Generalized Hooke's Law)

In three-dimensional linear elasticity, the most general anisotropic stress–strain relationship is a tensor equation of the form:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} \sum_{l=1}^{3} C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}
$$

The proportionality constant $C_{ijkl}$ is a **fourth-order stiffness (elastic) tensor** containing 81 components in its full form, which reduces to 21 independent components due to the inherent symmetries of stress and strain tensors.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Voigt notation (contracted matrix form)**, the 4th-order tensor $C_{ijkl}$ is represented as a symmetric 6×6 matrix $[C]$, and the generalized Hooke's law is written compactly as $\{\sigma\} = [C]\{\varepsilon\}$.

## 1.3 Compliance Matrix — Intuitive Overview

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a trampoline (a deformable membrane) attached at four corners. If you push it at one point, the displacement at that point depends on how stiff the trampoline is **and** how the stiffness is distributed in different directions. The **stiffness matrix** $[C]$ tells you "how much force per unit area is needed to produce a unit strain in every direction simultaneously." The **compliance matrix** $[S]$ is its exact mathematical inverse — it answers the inverse question: "If I apply a unit stress in a particular direction, what strain will result in **every** direction (including lateral contraction via Poisson effects)?"

The compliance matrix, therefore, is the matrix that **maps stress to strain**, and it contains all the engineering constants ($E$, $G$, $\nu$) that we measure in tensile/torsion tests on real materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Result:** In MEMS design, since most structural materials (Silicon, Polysilicon, single-crystal Silicon) are **anisotropic**, using a 6×6 compliance matrix with full crystal-symmetry rules is essential for accurate deflection analysis of beams, plates, and membranes.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)
- **Young's Modulus $E$** — typically **130–190 GPa** for single-crystal Silicon (direction-dependent).
- **Shear Modulus $G$** — typically **50–80 GPa** for Silicon.
- **Poisson's Ratio $\nu$** — typically **0.06–0.28** for Silicon.
- **Ultimate Strength of Silicon** ≈ **7.0 GPa** (fracture stress).
- **Ultimate Strength of Polysilicon** ≈ **1.0–3.0 GPa**.
- **Ultimate Strength of Aluminum (thin film)** ≈ **0.1–0.3 GPa**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in MEMS

In a micro-electro-mechanical system, the structural layer is rarely isotropic — single-crystal silicon (100) and (111) wafers exhibit direction-dependent elastic constants, and polysilicon grain orientation introduces further complexity. For correct **static deflection**, **modal analysis**, and **fracture prediction**, the designer MUST use the full **6×6 compliance matrix** with the appropriate symmetry reduction (orthotropic, transversely isotropic, or cubic).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tensor Form of Generalized Hooke's Law

The 4th-order stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$ relates the 2nd-order stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ to the 2nd-order strain tensor $\varepsilon_{kl}$:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl}\,\varepsilon_{kl}
$$

Because of symmetry in the stress tensor ($\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$) and strain tensor ($\varepsilon_{kl} = \varepsilon_{lk}$), we have:

$$
C_{ijkl} = C_{jikl} = C_{ijlk}
$$

This reduces 81 components to **21 independent elastic constants** in the most general (triclinic) case.

## 2.2 Voigt Notation — Contracted Matrix Form

Using the conventional index mapping $1\!\leftrightarrow\!11,\ 2\!\leftrightarrow\!22,\ 3\!\leftrightarrow\!33,\ 4\!\leftrightarrow\!23,\ 5\!\leftrightarrow\!13,\ 6\!\leftrightarrow\!12$, the stiffness matrix becomes:

$$
[C] = 
\begin{bmatrix}
C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{13} & C_{14} & C_{15} & C_{16} \\
C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{23} & C_{24} & C_{25} & C_{26} \\
C_{13} & C_{23} & C_{33} & C_{34} & C_{35} & C_{36} \\
C_{14} & C_{24} & C_{34} & C_{44} & C_{45} & C_{46} \\
C_{15} & C_{25} & C_{35} & C_{45} & C_{55} & C_{56} \\
C_{16} & C_{26} & C_{36} & C_{46} & C_{56} & C_{66}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

And the stress–strain relation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \sigma_3 \\ \sigma_4 \\ \sigma_5 \\ \sigma_6 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} & \cdots & C_{16} \\ \vdots & \ddots & & \vdots \\ C_{16} & \cdots & \cdots & C_{66} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \varepsilon_3 \\ \varepsilon_4 \\ \varepsilon_5 \\ \varepsilon_6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.3 Compliance Matrix — Definition and Derivation Logic

The compliance matrix is the **inverse of the stiffness matrix**:

$$
[S] = [C]^{-1}
$$

It maps stress to strain:

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}
$$

The compliance matrix is **also symmetric** ($S_{ij} = S_{ji}$) because $[C]$ is symmetric and the inverse of a symmetric matrix is symmetric.

## 2.4 Engineering Constants Form (Most Useful in MEMS)

The 6×6 compliance matrix is most commonly expressed in terms of **engineering constants**: $E_1, E_2, E_3, G_{23}, G_{31}, G_{12}, \nu_{21}, \nu_{31}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{32}, \nu_{13}, \nu_{23}$:

$$
[S] = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{E_1} & \dfrac{-\nu_{21}}{E_2} & \dfrac{-\nu_{31}}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[10pt]
\dfrac{-\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_2} & \dfrac{-\nu_{32}}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[10pt]
\dfrac{-\nu_{13}}{E_1} & \dfrac{-\nu_{23}}{E_2} & \dfrac{1}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[10pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{23}} & 0 & 0 \\[10pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{13}} & 0 \\[10pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{12}}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry rule for off-diagonal terms:** $\dfrac{\nu_{ij}}{E_i} = \dfrac{\nu_{ji}}{E_j}$, which guarantees $[S]$ is symmetric. This is the **Maxwell-Betti reciprocal theorem** in elasticity.

## 2.5 Symmetry Reductions (Critical for MEMS)

The general 21-component stiffness reduces dramatically under crystal symmetry:

| Symmetry Type | Independent Constants | Typical MEMS Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Triclinic** (most general) | 21 | None common |
| **Monoclinic** | 13 | Some layered composites |
| **Orthorhombic** | 9 | Some piezoelectric crystals |
| **Tetragonal / Hexagonal** | 6 | Sapphire, ZnO |
| **Cubic** (Silicon, Germanium) | 3 | **Single-crystal Si, Ge, diamond** |
| **Isotropic** | 2 | Glass, metals (polycrystalline) |

### 2.5.1 Cubic Symmetry Compliance (Silicon)

For a cubic crystal (e.g., Silicon), there are only 3 independent constants: $E$, $G$, $\nu$. The reduced compliance matrix is:

$$
[S]_{cubic} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{1}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{1}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G} & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G} & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For cubic crystals, $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$.

### 2.5.2 Isotropic Compliance

For an isotropic material (used for Polysilicon approximation, metals, glass):

$$
[S]_{iso} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{1}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{1}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{2(1+\nu)}{E} & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{2(1+\nu)}{E} & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{2(1+\nu)}{E}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{uts} = F_{max}/A_0$ | Ultimate Tensile Strength — failure criterion for MEMS structural layers |
| 2 | $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl}\,\varepsilon_{kl}$ | Generalized Hooke's Law (tensor form) |
| 3 | $\{\sigma\} = [C]\{\varepsilon\}$ | Generalized Hooke's Law (matrix form, Voigt notation) |
| 4 | $[S] = [C]^{-1}$ | Definition of compliance matrix |
| 5 | $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$ | Inverse stress–strain relation |
| 6 | $\nu_{ij}/E_i = \nu_{ji}/E_j$ | Symmetry of compliance — Maxwell-Betti reciprocity |
| 7 | $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ | Relation between $E$, $G$, $\nu$ for isotropic materials |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_x = (1/E)\sigma_x - (\nu/E)\sigma_y - (\nu/E)\sigma_z$ | Normal strain from triaxial stress (isotropic) |
| 9 | $\gamma_{xy} = (1/G)\,\tau_{xy}$ | Shear strain from shear stress |
| 10 | Cubic crystal: $C_{11}, C_{12}, C_{44}$ | 3 independent stiffnesses for Si, Ge |
| 11 | Cubic: $E = (C_{11}-C_{12})(C_{11}+2C_{12})/[C_{11}+C_{12}]$ | Young's modulus for cubic crystal |
| 12 | Cubic: $G = C_{44}$ | Shear modulus for cubic crystal |
| 13 | $\nu = C_{12}/(C_{11}+C_{12})$ | Poisson's ratio for cubic crystal |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MEMS Accelerometer Beams:** The compliance matrix is used to predict the lateral deflection under proof-mass inertial loading. The principal Young's modulus of (100) Si is ~130 GPa in-plane and ~190 GPa out-of-plane.
- **Pressure Sensor Diaphragms:** Square or circular diaphragms made from (100) silicon require the anisotropic compliance tensor to correctly compute center deflection under uniform pressure.
- **Fracture Prediction:** Comparing computed principal stresses (from $C$ and applied loads) to $\sigma_{uts}$ is the primary failure criterion in MEMS design.
- **Piezoelectric MEMS:** The full 6×6 matrix (with additional piezoelectric and dielectric tensors) governs actuation in PZT, AlN, and ZnO-based devices.
- **Material Selection:** Materials with high $\sigma_{uts}$ and low $[S]$ entries are preferred for high-stiffness, high-reliability MEMS.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Tensor Equation to 6×6 Matrix Form

### Step 1 — Start with the tensor equation
We start with the most general form relating stress to strain via the 4th-order stiffness tensor:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl}\,\varepsilon_{kl}
$$

**Reasoning:** The stress at a point in a linear elastic body depends linearly on every component of the strain at that point. A 4th-order tensor is the most general linear mapping between two 2nd-order tensors.

### Step 2 — Exploit Symmetry of $\sigma_{ij}$
The stress tensor is symmetric: $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$. This implies:

$$
C_{ijkl} = C_{jikl}
$$

This eliminates redundancy and reduces components from 81 to 54.

### Step 3 — Exploit Symmetry of $\varepsilon_{kl}$
The strain tensor (small-strain, symmetric part of displacement gradient) is also symmetric: $\varepsilon_{kl} = \varepsilon_{lk}$. This implies:

$$
C_{ijkl} = C_{ijlk}
$$

This further reduces the independent components from 54 to 21.

### Step 4 — Apply Voigt Index Mapping
We now introduce the contracted (Voigt) notation to map the symmetric 2nd-order tensors into 6-component vectors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 = \sigma_{11},\quad \sigma_2 = \sigma_{22},\quad \sigma_3 = \sigma_{33} \\
\sigma_4 = \sigma_{23} = \sigma_{32},\quad \sigma_5 = \sigma_{13} = \sigma_{31},\quad \sigma_6 = \sigma_{12} = \sigma_{21} \\
\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_{11},\quad \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_{22},\quad \varepsilon_3 = \varepsilon_{33} \\
\varepsilon_4 = 2\varepsilon_{23},\quad \varepsilon_5 = 2\varepsilon_{13},\quad \varepsilon_6 = 2\varepsilon_{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** The factor of 2 in the shear components is required to keep $[C]$ symmetric. This is the key convention difference between Voigt "tensor" and "engineering" notations.

### Step 5 — Construct the 6×6 Stiffness Matrix
With the Voigt mapping, $C_{ijkl}$ becomes a symmetric 6×6 matrix $[C]$:

$$
[C] = 
\begin{bmatrix}
C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{13} & C_{14} & C_{15} & C_{16} \\
C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{23} & C_{24} & C_{25} & C_{26} \\
C_{13} & C_{23} & C_{33} & C_{34} & C_{35} & C_{36} \\
C_{14} & C_{24} & C_{34} & C_{44} & C_{45} & C_{46} \\
C_{15} & C_{25} & C_{35} & C_{45} & C_{55} & C_{56} \\
C_{16} & C_{26} & C_{36} & C_{46} & C_{56} & C_{66}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — Write the Matrix Equation
Combining, we have:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \sigma_3 \\ \sigma_4 \\ \sigma_5 \\ \sigma_6 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{13} & C_{14} & C_{15} & C_{16} \\
C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{23} & C_{24} & C_{25} & C_{26} \\
C_{13} & C_{23} & C_{33} & C_{34} & C_{35} & C_{36} \\
C_{14} & C_{24} & C_{34} & C_{44} & C_{45} & C_{46} \\
C_{15} & C_{25} & C_{35} & C_{45} & C_{55} & C_{56} \\
C_{16} & C_{26} & C_{36} & C_{46} & C_{56} & C_{66} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \varepsilon_3 \\ \varepsilon_4 \\ \varepsilon_5 \\ \varepsilon_6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **6×6 Voigt form of generalized Hooke's law**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Compliance Matrix in Terms of Engineering Constants

We now derive the 6×6 compliance matrix for an **orthotropic material** (3 mutually orthogonal planes of symmetry). This is the most common case for engineered MEMS structures.

### Step 1 — Start with 1D Hooke's Law
For an orthotropic material, a uniaxial stress $\sigma_1$ produces:

- A primary strain $\varepsilon_1 = \sigma_1 / E_1$ along the loading direction.
- Lateral contractions $\varepsilon_2 = -\nu_{21}\,\sigma_1 / E_2$ and $\varepsilon_3 = -\nu_{31}\,\sigma_1 / E_3$ in the transverse directions.

The negative signs reflect **Poisson contraction** — when stretched in one direction, the material contracts laterally.

### Step 2 — Apply Superposition for All 3 Normal Stresses
By the principle of superposition (valid for linear elastic materials), the total normal strains are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\,\sigma_2 - \frac{\nu_{31}}{E_3}\,\sigma_3 \\[6pt]
\varepsilon_2 &= -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\,\sigma_1 + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} - \frac{\nu_{32}}{E_3}\,\sigma_3 \\[6pt]
\varepsilon_3 &= -\frac{\nu_{13}}{E_1}\,\sigma_1 - \frac{\nu_{23}}{E_2}\,\sigma_2 + \frac{\sigma_3}{E_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Write Shear Stress–Strain Relations
The shear response is decoupled in orthotropic materials:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_4 = \gamma_{23} = \frac{\sigma_4}{G_{23}} = \frac{\tau_{23}}{G_{23}} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_5 = \gamma_{13} = \frac{\sigma_5}{G_{13}} = \frac{\tau_{13}}{G_{13}} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_6 = \gamma_{12} = \frac{\sigma_6}{G_{12}} = \frac{\tau_{12}}{G_{12}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the Compliance Matrix
Combining all six equations into matrix form $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$:

$$
[S] = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{E_1} & \dfrac{-\nu_{21}}{E_2} & \dfrac{-\nu_{31}}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[10pt]
\dfrac{-\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_2} & \dfrac{-\nu_{32}}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[10pt]
\dfrac{-\nu_{13}}{E_1} & \dfrac{-\nu_{23}}{E_2} & \dfrac{1}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[10pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{23}} & 0 & 0 \\[10pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{13}} & 0 \\[10pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{12}}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Symmetry (Maxwell-Betti Reciprocity)
The off-diagonal symmetry $S_{ij} = S_{ji}$ requires:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\nu_{ij}}{E_i} = \frac{\nu_{ji}}{E_j}\;}
$$

This is the **Maxwell-Betti reciprocal theorem** of linear elasticity, which physically states that the displacement at point A due to a unit load at point B equals the displacement at B due to a unit load at A. The compliance matrix being symmetric is a direct consequence of this theorem.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Compliance Matrix for Isotropic Polysilicon

Given: Polysilicon approximation as isotropic with $E = 160\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.22$.

**Step A — Compute the shear modulus $G$:**

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{160}{2(1.22)} = \frac{160}{2.44} = 65.57\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step B — Compute $1/G$:**

$$
\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E} = \frac{2(1.22)}{160} = \frac{2.44}{160} = 0.01525\,\text{(GPa)}^{-1}
$$

**Step C — Compute $1/E$:**

$$
\frac{1}{E} = \frac{1}{160} = 0.00625\,\text{(GPa)}^{-1}
$$

**Step D — Compute $-\nu/E$:**

$$
\frac{-\nu}{E} = \frac{-0.22}{160} = -0.001375\,\text{(GPa)}^{-1}
$$

**Step E — Assemble the compliance matrix:**

$$
[S] = \begin{bmatrix}
0.00625 & -0.001375 & -0.001375 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
-0.001375 & 0.00625 & -0.001375 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
-0.001375 & -0.001375 & 0.00625 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0.01525 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.01525 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.01525
\end{bmatrix}\, \text{(GPa)}^{-1}
$$

This is the **operational compliance matrix** for an isotropic MEMS structural layer.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Compliance for Cubic Silicon (Verification)

For single-crystal silicon (cubic), the engineering constants are: $E = 130\,\text{GPa}$, $G = C_{44} = 79.6\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$. 

The compliance matrix follows the same form as the isotropic case but with **direction-dependent** $E$ and $G$ values for non-principal axes. For principal axes (100), the matrix is identical in form to the isotropic case.

> [!NOTE]
> The full anisotropic compliance is required when the MEMS structure is oriented along non-principal crystallographic directions (e.g., at 45° to the (100) wafer flat), which is common in micromachined gyroscopes and comb-drive resonators.

---

## 3.5 Ultimate Strength Application — Failure Criterion

For a MEMS beam loaded in bending, the maximum principal stress is computed using the stiffness/compliance relations. Failure occurs when:

$$
\sigma_{principal,max} \geq \sigma_{uts}
$$

**Example:** A silicon micro-cantilever with $E = 130\,\text{GPa}$ and $\sigma_{uts} = 7\,\text{GPa}$ has a theoretical safety factor margin of:

$$
SF = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{7\,\text{GPa}}{\sigma_{max}}
$$

Designers typically enforce $SF \geq 2$ for static reliability and $SF \geq 3$ for dynamic/fatigue loading.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: From Tensor to Compliance Matrix

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **logical architecture** of how a 4th-order tensor maps down to a 6×6 compliance matrix for use in MEMS finite-element analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stress Tensor 2nd-order sigma_ij] --> B[Symmetry sigma_ij = sigma_ji]
    B --> C[Voigt Notation Contracted Vector 6 Components]
    C --> D[Stiffness Matrix 6x6 Symmetric C_ij]
    D --> E[MEMS Material Symmetry Reduction]
    E --> F1[Triclinic 21 constants]
    E --> F2[Orthotropic 9 constants]
    E --> F3[Cubic 3 constants Silicon]
    E --> F4[Isotropic 2 constants Polysilicon]
    D --> G[Matrix Inversion Computation]
    G --> H[Compliance Matrix 6x6 Symmetric S_ij]
    H --> I1[Stress to Strain Mapping]
    H --> I2[Engineering Constants Form E, G, nu]
    H --> J[MEMS Solver FEABeamDiaphragm]
    J --> K1[Deflection Analysis]
    J --> K2[Modal Analysis]
    J --> K3[Failure Prediction vs UTS]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Stress-Strain Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Load F or Pressure p] --> B[Stress Tensor sigma_ij]
    B --> C[Stiffness Matrix C_ij]
    C --> D[Compute Strain epsilon_ij = S_ij x sigma_ij]
    D --> E[Displacement Field u, v, w]
    E --> F[Deflection Curve]
    F --> G{sigma_max greater than sigma_uts}
    G --> H[YES: Structural Failure]
    G --> I[NO: Safe Design]
```

## 4.3 Symmetry Reduction Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[General Anisotropic 21 Constants] --> B[Monoclinic 13]
    A --> C[Orthotropic 9]
    A --> D[Tetragonal Hexagonal 6]
    A --> E[Cubic 3 Silicon]
    A --> F[Isotropic 2 Polysilicon]
    C --> E
    E --> F
    B --> D
    D --> E
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Compliance Matrix Entry Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compliance Matrix Entry S_ij] --> B{Diagonal}
    A --> C{Off Diagonal}
    B --> D[Normal Compliance 1 / E_i]
    C --> E[Coupling Compliance negative nu_ij / E_j]
    C --> F[Shear Compliance 1 / G_ij]
    D --> G[Used for Axial Strain Calculation]
    E --> H[Used for Poisson Contraction Effect]
    F --> I[Used for Shear Strain Calculation]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Define Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) of a material. Mention typical UTS values for single-crystal silicon and polysilicon used in MEMS.**

**Model Answer:**
Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) is the maximum engineering stress that a material can withstand under uniaxial tensile loading before necking or fracture occurs. Mathematically:

$$
\sigma_{uts} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}
$$

where $F_{max}$ is the maximum force observed during tensile testing and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.

Typical values:
- **Single-crystal Silicon (Si):** $\sigma_{uts} \approx 7.0\,\text{GPa}$
- **Polysilicon:** $\sigma_{uts} \approx 1.0\text{–}3.0\,\text{GPa}$
- **Aluminum (thin film):** $\sigma_{uts} \approx 0.1\text{–}0.3\,\text{GPa}$

Silicon's high UTS combined with negligible plastic deformation makes it the dominant structural material in surface-micromachined and bulk-micromachined MEMS devices.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**What is a compliance matrix? How is it related to the stiffness matrix in 3D linear elasticity?**

**Model Answer:**
The compliance matrix $[S]$ is a 6×6 symmetric matrix that maps the stress vector to the strain vector in linear elastic materials. It is the **mathematical inverse of the stiffness matrix** $[C]$:

$$
[S] = [C]^{-1}
$$

The generalized Hooke's law in matrix form is:

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}
$$

The compliance matrix contains the engineering constants: normal compliances $1/E_i$ on the diagonal, shear compliances $1/G_{ij}$ on the shear diagonal, and Poisson coupling terms $-\nu_{ij}/E_j$ as off-diagonal entries. Symmetry of $[S]$ is guaranteed by the Maxwell-Betti reciprocal theorem: $\nu_{ij}/E_i = \nu_{ji}/E_j$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** Derive the general form of the 6×6 compliance matrix for a 3D linear elastic material in terms of engineering constants ($E_1, E_2, E_3, G_{23}, G_{13}, G_{12}, \nu_{21}, \nu_{31}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{32}, \nu_{13}, \nu_{23}$). State the symmetry condition that the matrix must satisfy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a cubic crystal such as single-crystal silicon with $C_{11} = 165.7\,\text{GPa}$, $C_{12} = 63.9\,\text{GPa}$, $C_{44} = 79.6\,\text{GPa}$, compute the engineering constants $E$, $G$, and $\nu$. Then write the explicit 6×6 compliance matrix. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1:** Consider a 3D orthotropic material with three mutually perpendicular planes of symmetry aligned with the coordinate axes. The material has three independent Young's moduli ($E_1, E_2, E_3$), three independent shear moduli ($G_{23}, G_{13}, G_{12}$), and six independent Poisson's ratios ($\nu_{21}, \nu_{31}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{32}, \nu_{13}, \nu_{23}$).

**Step 2:** Under uniaxial stress $\sigma_1$ alone (with $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$), the resulting strains are:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1},\quad \varepsilon_2 = -\nu_{21}\,\frac{\sigma_1}{E_2},\quad \varepsilon_3 = -\nu_{31}\,\frac{\sigma_1}{E_3}
$$

**Step 3:** By the principle of superposition (linear elasticity), the total strains when all three normal stresses act simultaneously are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\,\sigma_2 - \frac{\nu_{31}}{E_3}\,\sigma_3 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_2 &= -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\,\sigma_1 + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} - \frac{\nu_{32}}{E_3}\,\sigma_3 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_3 &= -\frac{\nu_{13}}{E_1}\,\sigma_1 - \frac{\nu_{23}}{E_2}\,\sigma_2 + \frac{\sigma_3}{E_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The shear stresses produce only pure shear strains (decoupled from normal strains in orthotropic materials):

$$
\varepsilon_4 = \frac{\sigma_4}{G_{23}},\quad \varepsilon_5 = \frac{\sigma_5}{G_{13}},\quad \varepsilon_6 = \frac{\sigma_6}{G_{12}}
$$

**Step 5:** Combining all six equations into matrix form $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$:

$$
[S] = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{E_1} & \dfrac{-\nu_{21}}{E_2} & \dfrac{-\nu_{31}}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{-\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_2} & \dfrac{-\nu_{32}}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{-\nu_{13}}{E_1} & \dfrac{-\nu_{23}}{E_2} & \dfrac{1}{E_3} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{23}} & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{13}} & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{12}}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Symmetry condition (Maxwell-Betti reciprocity): Since the compliance matrix must be symmetric ($S_{ij} = S_{ji}$), the off-diagonal terms must satisfy:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\nu_{ij}}{E_i} = \frac{\nu_{ji}}{E_j}\;}
$$

This reduces the 12 Poisson's ratio entries to 6 independent values.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $\nu$ (Poisson's ratio):**
For a cubic crystal:

$$
\nu = \frac{C_{12}}{C_{11} + C_{12}} = \frac{63.9}{165.7 + 63.9} = \frac{63.9}{229.6} = 0.278
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E$ (Young's modulus):**
For a cubic crystal:

$$
E = \frac{(C_{11} - C_{12})(C_{11} + 2C_{12})}{C_{11} + C_{12}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} - C_{12} &= 165.7 - 63.9 = 101.8\,\text{GPa} \\
C_{11} + 2C_{12} &= 165.7 + 2(63.9) = 293.5\,\text{GPa} \\
C_{11} + C_{12} &= 165.7 + 63.9 = 229.6\,\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E = \frac{(101.8)(293.5)}{229.6} = \frac{29878.3}{229.6} = 130.13\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $G$ (Shear modulus):**
For a cubic crystal:

$$
G = C_{44} = 79.6\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the matrix entries:**
- $\dfrac{1}{E} = \dfrac{1}{130.13} = 7.685 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{GPa}^{-1}$
- $\dfrac{-\nu}{E} = \dfrac{-0.278}{130.13} = -2.137 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{GPa}^{-1}$
- $\dfrac{1}{G} = \dfrac{1}{79.6} = 12.56 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{GPa}^{-1}$

**Step 5 — Write the 6×6 compliance matrix:**

$$
[S] = \begin{bmatrix}
7.685 & -2.137 & -2.137 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
-2.137 & 7.685 & -2.137 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
-2.137 & -2.137 & 7.685 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 12.56 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 12.56 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 12.56
\end{bmatrix} \times 10^{-3}\,\text{GPa}^{-1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget the **factor of 2** difference between engineering shear strain $\gamma$ and tensorial shear strain $\varepsilon_{ij}$ in Voigt notation. This leads to an apparent non-symmetric $[C]$ if the wrong convention is used. Always use $\varepsilon_4 = 2\varepsilon_{23}$ consistently with $[C]$ to ensure $[C]$ and $[S] = [C]^{-1}$ are both symmetric.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** Define Ultimate Strength. A MEMS silicon cantilever of length $L = 500\,\mu\text{m}$, width $w = 50\,\mu\text{m}$, and thickness $t = 5\,\mu\text{m}$ is subjected to a tip load of $F = 1\,\text{mN}$. Calculate the maximum bending stress and check whether it is within the safe limit, given that $\sigma_{uts}$ for silicon is $7\,\text{GPa}$ and $E = 130\,\text{GPa}$. Assume a rectangular cross-section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the significance of the compliance matrix in MEMS structural analysis. Show that for a cubic crystal, the compliance matrix has only **3 independent entries** and write its full form. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Define Ultimate Strength:**
Ultimate Strength (or Ultimate Tensile Strength, UTS) is the maximum engineering stress a material can sustain under uniaxial tension prior to fracture. It is the peak point on the engineering stress–strain curve.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum bending moment:**
For a cantilever with a tip load, the maximum bending moment occurs at the fixed end:

$$
M_{max} = F \cdot L = (1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{N})(500 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}) = 5 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the section modulus:**
For a rectangular cross-section, the section modulus is:

$$
Z = \frac{w\,t^2}{6} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6}
$$

Computing:

$$
t^2 = (5 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 25 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2
$$

$$
Z = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(25 \times 10^{-12})}{6} = \frac{1250 \times 10^{-18}}{6} = 208.33 \times 10^{-18}\,\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum bending stress:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{208.33 \times 10^{-18}} = 2.4 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa} = 2.4\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with UTS and check safety:**

$$
\text{Safety Factor } SF = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{7\,\text{GPa}}{2.4\,\text{GPa}} = 2.92
$$

Since $SF > 2$, the design is **SAFE** for static loading. However, for dynamic/fatigue loading, a safety factor of 3 or higher is recommended.

**Step 6 — Optional: Compute the tip deflection** (for completeness):

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I},\quad I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = 5.21 \times 10^{-22}\,\text{m}^4
$$

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{(10^{-3})(500 \times 10^{-6})^3}{3(130 \times 10^9)(5.21 \times 10^{-22})} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-13}}{2.032 \times 10^{-10}} = 0.615\,\mu\text{m}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Significance of Compliance Matrix in MEMS:**
The compliance matrix is essential in MEMS structural analysis because:

1. **It enables direct strain calculation** from known stress distributions in microstructures (beams, plates, diaphragms).
2. **It encodes material anisotropy** — Silicon, the dominant MEMS material, has direction-dependent elastic constants, and the compliance matrix correctly captures this.
3. **It is the inverse of the stiffness matrix**, allowing flexible forward or inverse analysis depending on boundary conditions.
4. **It interfaces with finite-element solvers** (COMSOL, ANSYS, CoventorWare) used for MEMS design verification.

**Step 2 — Reduction to 3 independent entries for cubic crystal:**

A cubic crystal has **three mutually perpendicular 4-fold rotation axes** and equivalent diagonal directions. This imposes the following equalities on engineering constants:

- $E_1 = E_2 = E_3 = E$ (only one Young's modulus for principal axes)
- $G_{23} = G_{13} = G_{12} = G$ (only one shear modulus)
- $\nu_{21} = \nu_{31} = \nu_{12} = \nu_{32} = \nu_{13} = \nu_{23} = \nu$ (only one Poisson's ratio)
- $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ (isotropy within the principal frame)

Therefore, the entire 6×6 compliance matrix is described by **3 independent constants**: $E$, $G$, $\nu$.

**Step 3 — Full 6×6 form:**

$$
[S] = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{1}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{-\nu}{E} & \dfrac{1}{E} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G} & 0 & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G} & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common student mistake is to assume that because the **compliance matrix form** for cubic crystals looks identical to isotropic materials, the **numerical values** of $E$ and $\nu$ are the same. In reality, for a cubic crystal, $E$, $\nu$, and $G$ are **derived from the three independent $C_{ij}$ stiffnesses** and depend on crystal orientation. For (100) silicon: $E \approx 130\,\text{GPa}$; for (111) silicon: $E \approx 188\,\text{GPa}$. Always verify the orientation before substituting values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Ultimate Strength (UTS):** Peak stress on the engineering stress–strain curve, $\sigma_{uts} = F_{max}/A_0$. Silicon: ~7 GPa; Polysilicon: ~1–3 GPa.
- **Generalized Hooke's Law (tensor form):** $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl}\,\varepsilon_{kl}$.
- **Stiffness matrix $[C]$:** 6×6 symmetric matrix with up to 21 independent components.
- **Voigt Notation:** Compact mapping $11 \to 1, 22 \to 2, 33 \to 3, 23 \to 4, 13 \to 5, 12 \to 6$. Engineering shear strain = 2 × tensorial shear strain.
- **Compliance matrix $[S]$:** Defined as $[C]^{-1}$. Maps stress to strain: $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$.
- **Symmetry of $[S]$:** Guaranteed by Maxwell-Betti reciprocity, leading to $\nu_{ij}/E_i = \nu_{ji}/E_j$.
- **Engineering constants in $[S]$:** Diagonal: $1/E_i$ (normal) and $1/G_{ij}$ (shear); off-diagonal: $-\nu_{ij}/E_j$.
- **Symmetry Reduction Counts:** Triclinic 21 → Monoclinic 13 → Orthotropic 9 → Hexagonal/Tetragonal 6 → **Cubic 3** (Silicon) → **Isotropic 2** (Polysilicon approximation).
- **Cubic Silicon (100) Constants:** $E \approx 130\,\text{GPa}$, $G = C_{44} = 79.6\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu \approx 0.28$, $\sigma_{uts} \approx 7\,\text{GPa}$.
- **Isotropic relation:** $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$.
- **Cubic relation:** $E = (C_{11} - C_{12})(C_{11} + 2C_{12})/(C_{11} + C_{12})$; $G = C_{44}$; $\nu = C_{12}/(C_{11} + C_{12})$.
- **Failure Criterion:** $\sigma_{principal,max} \geq \sigma_{uts} \Rightarrow$ structural failure.
- **Safety Factor Convention:** Static: $SF \geq 2$; Dynamic/Fatigue: $SF \geq 3$.
- **MEMS Application Areas:** Comb-drive actuators, accelerometer proof masses, pressure-sensor diaphragms, gyroscope resonators — all use the compliance matrix in their mechanical design equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties of Silicon and Related Thin Films, Overview of Commonly Used Mechanical Structures in MEMS - Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mechanical Properties of Silicon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Mechanical properties** of a material describe its response to applied forces, including elastic deformation, plastic deformation, fracture, hardness, and internal stress state. For MEMS, the *single-crystal silicon* used as the structural material exhibits near-ideal elastic behavior up to fracture, making it predictable and reliable for microfabricated sensors and actuators.

Single-crystal silicon is the **workhorse structural material** of MEMS. Unlike bulk metals, it does not yield plastically at room temperature — it is **almost perfectly elastic** until it suddenly fractures (brittle failure). This is one of the most exploited features in micro-mechanical design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Glass Rod vs. The Copper Wire**
> Think of silicon like a **thin glass rod**: it is stiff, springs back to shape when released, but shatters suddenly if pushed too hard. A **copper wire**, by contrast, is like a ductile metal — it bends, stretches, and permanently deforms before breaking. MEMS designers prefer the "glass-rod" behavior because the response is **linear, repeatable, and mathematically predictable** (Hooke's Law holds until failure).

### Key Mechanical Parameters of Single-Crystal Silicon

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **190 GPa** | Stiffness — resistance to elastic elongation |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.28** | Lateral contraction per unit axial extension |
| Density | $\rho$ | **2330 kg/m³** | Mass per unit volume |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_{Y}$ | ~7 GPa | Stress at onset of plastic deformation (theoretical) |
| Fracture Strength | $\sigma_{f}$ | 1–7 GPa (measured) | Stress at sudden brittle fracture |
| Hardness (Knoop) | $H_{K}$ | ~10 GPa | Resistance to surface indentation |
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | **150 W/(m·K)** | Heat conduction capability |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Silicon is Special for MEMS:**
> Silicon's **Young's Modulus (190 GPa)** is comparable to steel (~200 GPa), but its **density is ~3× lower**, giving an excellent **stiffness-to-weight ratio** — critical for high-frequency resonators and low-inertia actuators.

---

## 1.2 Piezoresistivity — A Unique Property of Silicon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Piezoresistivity** is the phenomenon by which a material's electrical resistivity changes when mechanical stress is applied. In silicon, this effect is **two orders of magnitude stronger** than in metals and is the working principle of most MEMS pressure sensors and accelerometers.

The fractional change in resistivity is expressed as:
$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \, \sigma_l + \pi_t \, \sigma_t$$

where $\pi_l$ and $\pi_t$ are the **longitudinal and transverse piezoresistive coefficients**, and $\sigma_l$, $\sigma_t$ are the corresponding stresses.

---

## 1.3 Related Thin Films in MEMS

Thin films are deposited layers (a few nm to several µm thick) used as structural, sacrificial, or functional layers in MEMS. They behave very differently from bulk silicon because of:

- **Residual stress** built in during deposition
- **Microstructure** (grain size, orientation)
- **Geometric constraints** from the substrate

### Common Thin Film Materials

| Thin Film | Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Residual Stress (MPa) | Typical Use in MEMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polysilicon | **160 – 170** | -10 to +300 | Structural beams, electrodes |
| Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) | 70 – 80 | **Compressive**, -100 to -300 | Sacrificial layer, insulation |
| Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄) | **250 – 290** | Tensile, +500 to +1000 | Membranes, high-stress structures |
| Aluminum (Al) | 70 | Low | Electrical interconnects, mirrors |
| Gold (Au) | 78 | Low | Bonding pads, bio-interfaces |
| Chromium (Cr) | 140 | Adhesion promoter | Adhesion layer |
| SU-8 (Polymer) | 2 – 4 | Low | Thick structural layers, microfluidics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Stretched Drumhead**
> Imagine a drum skin (**Si₃N₄ membrane** under high tensile stress) versus a slack bedsheet (**SU-8 polymer**). The drum is taut, vibrates at a high, well-defined pitch, and resists sagging. The bedsheet droops. In MEMS, **residual tensile stress is often deliberately engineered** to keep membranes flat — a vital trick used in pressure sensors and microphone diaphragms.

---

## 1.4 Overview of Mechanical Structures in MEMS — Beams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus):**
> A **beam** in MEMS is a slender structural element whose length is much greater than its cross-sectional dimensions, designed to support transverse loads, store elastic energy, or resonate at a defined frequency. Beams are the **most fundamental building blocks** of MEMS — used in accelerometers, gyroscopes, RF switches, micro-mirrors, and pressure sensors.

### Common Beam Configurations in MEMS

| Beam Type | Schematic | Boundary Conditions | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cantilever (Clamped-Free)** | One end fixed, one end free | $w(0)=0,\ w'(0)=0$ | AFM probes, micro-switches, mass sensors |
| **Clamped-Clamped (Fixed-Fixed)** | Both ends fixed | $w(0)=0,\ w'(0)=0,\ w(L)=0,\ w'(L)=0$ | Resonators, accelerometers |
| **Simply Supported** | Pin support at both ends | $w(0)=0,\ w(L)=0$ | Less common in MEMS |
| **Free-Free (with central support)** | Center supported | $w(L/2)=0$ | Tuning fork gyroscopes |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Beams Dominate MEMS:**
> Beams convert **force into displacement** (sensing) or **displacement into force** (actuation) with mathematically tractable behavior. Almost every commercial MEMS device — from the accelerometer in your smartphone to the gyroscope in a drone — contains at least one type of micro-beam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stress and Strain — The Foundation

When a force $F$ acts on a beam of cross-section $A$, the resulting **axial stress** is:
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

The corresponding **axial strain** is:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

For linearly elastic materials (valid for silicon up to fracture):
$$\sigma = E \, \varepsilon \quad \text{(Hooke's Law)}$$

where $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** and has units of **Pa (N/m²)**.

For a 3D stress state, the **generalized Hooke's Law** gives the strain tensor in terms of stress:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

This is essential for understanding biaxial stress states in thin films clamped to a substrate.

---

## 2.2 Thin Film Residual Stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Residual stress** is the internal stress present in a thin film even when no external load is applied. It originates from thermal mismatch with the substrate, lattice mismatch, and defects incorporated during deposition.

The **total residual stress** in a thin film is given by Stoney's equation:
$$\sigma_{film} = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, R \, t_f}$$

where:
- $E_s$ = Young's modulus of the **substrate**
- $t_s$ = substrate thickness
- $t_f$ = film thickness
- $R$ = radius of curvature of the substrate (after film deposition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Residual Stress Matters in MEMS:**
> - **Tensile residual stress** (positive) → films want to **shrink** → can crack the film or stretch the substrate
> - **Compressive residual stress** (negative) → films want to **expand** → can cause **buckling, blistering, or out-of-plane deflection** of released structures

---

## 2.3 Beam Bending Theory — Euler–Bernoulli Beam

For a slender beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $h$, the **bending moment** $M$ at a section $x$ relates to the **curvature** of the deflected beam:
$$M(x) = E I \frac{d^2 w(x)}{dx^2}$$

where:
- $w(x)$ = transverse deflection
- $I$ = **area moment of inertia** about the neutral axis

For a **rectangular cross-section** of width $b$ and thickness $h$:
$$I = \frac{b h^3}{12}$$

The **bending stress** at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis:
$$\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M \, y}{I}$$

Maximum bending stress (at the surface, $y = h/2$):
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \, h}{2I} = \frac{6 M}{b h^2}$$

The **shear force** relates to the bending moment by:
$$V(x) = \frac{dM(x)}{dx}, \qquad q(x) = \frac{dV(x)}{dx}$$

where $q(x)$ is the **distributed load per unit length**.

---

## 2.4 Beam Deflection Under Common Loading

For a load $F$ applied at the free end of a **cantilever beam**:
$$w_{tip} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b h^3} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

For a **center load** $F$ on a clamped-clamped beam:
$$w_{center} = \frac{F L^3}{192 E I}$$

For a **uniformly distributed load** $q$ on a clamped-clamped beam:
$$w_{center} = \frac{q L^4}{384 E I}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — Why Thickness Dominates**
> Notice that $I \propto h^3$ for a rectangular cross-section. So **doubling the beam thickness** increases its stiffness by **8 times**, while **doubling the length** decreases stiffness by **8 times**. In MEMS, this is why a 1 µm increase in beam thickness dramatically changes the device performance.

---

## 2.5 Spring Constants of MEMS Beams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **spring constant** $k$ relates the applied force to the resulting deflection: $F = k \, w$. It is the most important parameter in static MEMS design.

| Beam Configuration | Load | Spring Constant $k$ |
|---|---|---|
| Cantilever (end load) | Point $F$ at tip | $k = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3} = \dfrac{E b h^3}{4 L^3}$ |
| Clamped-Clamped (center load) | Point $F$ at center | $k = \dfrac{192 E I}{L^3} = \dfrac{16 E b h^3}{L^3}$ |
| Simply Supported (center load) | Point $F$ at center | $k = \dfrac{48 E I}{L^3} = \dfrac{4 E b h^3}{L^3}$ |
| Cantilever (uniform load) | Distributed $q$ per length | $k_{eff} = \dfrac{8 E I}{L^3} = \dfrac{2 E b h^3}{L^3}$ |

---

## 2.6 Resonance Frequency of Beams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **resonance frequency** is the natural frequency at which a beam freely vibrates with maximum amplitude. It is the foundation of MEMS resonators, gyroscopes, and many filter applications.

For a beam of mass per unit length $\rho A$ (where $\rho$ is density and $A = bh$ is cross-section), the general formula is:
$$f_n = \frac{\beta_n^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^4}}$$

The **mode constant** $\beta_n L$ depends on boundary conditions and mode number:

| Beam Type | Mode 1 ($\beta_1 L$) | Mode 2 ($\beta_2 L$) | Mode 3 ($\beta_3 L$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cantilever | 1.875 | 4.694 | 7.855 |
| Clamped-Clamped | 4.730 | 7.853 | 10.996 |
| Free-Free | 4.730 | 7.853 | 10.996 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Frequency Matters in MEMS:**
> Higher frequency = better noise immunity, smaller device footprint, and faster response. Modern **MEMS timing references** (silicon oscillators) operate at MHz to GHz by using carefully designed clamped-clamped or free-free beams.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hooke's Law (axial) | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | Pa | $E$: Young's modulus, $\varepsilon$: strain |
| 2 | Generalized Hooke | $\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)]$ | dimensionless | $\nu$: Poisson's ratio |
| 3 | Stoney's Equation | $\sigma_f = \frac{E_s t_s^2}{6 R t_f}$ | Pa | $R$: substrate curvature radius |
| 4 | Moment of Inertia (rect.) | $I = \frac{b h^3}{12}$ | m⁴ | $b$: width, $h$: thickness |
| 5 | Euler–Bernoulli | $M = E I \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2}$ | N·m | $w$: deflection |
| 6 | Cantilever tip deflection | $w = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$ | m | $F$: tip force |
| 7 | C-C center deflection | $w = \frac{F L^3}{192 E I}$ | m | — |
| 8 | Cantilever spring const. | $k = \frac{3 E I}{L^3}$ | N/m | — |
| 9 | C-C spring constant | $k = \frac{192 E I}{L^3}$ | N/m | — |
| 10 | Max bending stress | $\sigma_{max} = \frac{6M}{bh^2}$ | Pa | — |
| 11 | Resonance frequency | $f_n = \frac{\beta_n^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$ | Hz | $\rho$: density |
| 12 | Piezoresistive | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | dimensionless | $\pi$: piezocoeff. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Real-World Use:**
> These formulas power the design of **MEMS pressure sensors** (using clamped-clamped or circular membranes with piezoresistors), **accelerometers** (proof mass suspended by cantilever beams, displacement detected by $k$), and **resonators** (operating at designed $\beta_n L$ modes for filters and oscillators).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tip Deflection of a Cantilever Beam Under End Load

**Problem Setup:**
A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $h$ is fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$. A point force $F$ is applied at the free end (downward, $y$-direction).

**Step 1 — Define the Bending Moment Distribution**

Take a section at distance $x$ from the fixed end. The internal bending moment is created by the tip force acting on a moment arm of $(L - x)$:
$$M(x) = F \, (L - x)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Euler–Bernoulli Equation**

The curvature of the deflected beam is:
$$M(x) = E I \frac{d^2 w(x)}{dx^2}$$

Substituting:
$$E I \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = F (L - x)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Once to Get Slope**

$$\frac{d w}{dx} = \frac{F}{E I} \int (L - x) \, dx = \frac{F}{E I} \left[ L x - \frac{x^2}{2} \right] + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Condition for Slope at Fixed End**

At $x = 0$, the slope is zero (clamped):
$$\frac{dw}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0 \implies C_1 = 0$$

So:
$$\frac{dw}{dx} = \frac{F}{E I} \left( L x - \frac{x^2}{2} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Again to Get Deflection**

$$w(x) = \frac{F}{E I} \int \left( L x - \frac{x^2}{2} \right) dx$$

$$w(x) = \frac{F}{E I} \left[ \frac{L x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{6} \right] + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply Boundary Condition for Deflection at Fixed End**

At $x = 0$, the deflection is zero:
$$w(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Final Deflection Profile**

$$\boxed{w(x) = \frac{F}{E I} \left( \frac{L x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{6} \right) = \frac{F x^2}{6 E I}(3L - x)}$$

**Step 8 — Tip Deflection (set $x = L$)**

$$w_{tip} = w(L) = \frac{F L^2}{6 E I} (3L - L) = \frac{F L^2 (2L)}{6 E I} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

Substituting $I = \frac{b h^3}{12}$:
$$w_{tip} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E \cdot (b h^3 / 12)} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b h^3}$$

**Step 9 — Spring Constant**

The spring constant is the ratio of force to tip displacement:
$$k = \frac{F}{w_{tip}} = \frac{F}{\frac{F L^3}{3 E I}} = \frac{3 E I}{L^3} = \frac{E b h^3}{4 L^3}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: First Resonance Frequency of a Cantilever Beam

**Step 1 — Governing Equation for Free Vibration**

For an undamped beam, the dynamic Euler–Bernoulli equation is:
$$E I \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Assume Separable Solution**

Let $w(x,t) = W(x) \, T(t)$. Substituting and separating:
$$\frac{E I}{W(x)} \frac{d^4 W}{dx^4} = -\frac{\rho A}{T(t)} \frac{d^2 T}{dt^2} = \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Time Solution**

The time equation $\rho A T'' + \lambda T = 0$ has solution:
$$T(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t), \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{\lambda}{\rho A}$$

**Step 4 — Spatial Equation**

$$E I \frac{d^4 W}{dx^4} = \rho A \omega_n^2 \, W$$

The general solution is:
$$W(x) = C_1 \cos(\beta x) + C_2 \sin(\beta x) + C_3 \cosh(\beta x) + C_4 \sinh(\beta x)$$

where:
$$\beta^4 = \frac{\rho A \omega_n^2}{E I}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions for Cantilever**

At fixed end ($x = 0$): $W(0) = 0$ and $\frac{dW}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0$
At free end ($x = L$): $\frac{d^2 W}{dx^2}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$ and $\frac{d^3 W}{dx^3}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$

**Step 6 — Characteristic (Frequency) Equation**

Applying the four BCs yields the transcendental equation:
$$\cos(\beta L) \cdot \cosh(\beta L) = -1$$

**Step 7 — Find Roots Numerically**

The smallest positive root is $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$ (mode 1). Therefore:
$$\omega_1 = \beta_1^2 \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A}}$$

$$\boxed{f_1 = \frac{\omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{(1.875)^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^4}} \approx \frac{0.56}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^4}}}$$

Or more cleanly:
$$f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^4}} \approx 0.162 \, h \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \frac{1}{L^2}$$

Substituting $E/\rho$ for silicon:
$$\sqrt{E/\rho} = \sqrt{\frac{190 \times 10^9}{2330}} \approx 9030 \text{ m/s}$$

So for silicon, the resonance frequency formula simplifies to approximately:
$$f_1 \approx 0.162 \times 9030 \times \frac{h}{L^2} \approx 1463 \times \frac{h}{L^2} \quad \text{(h, L in meters; f in Hz)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stoney's Equation for Thin Film Stress

**Step 1 — Geometry of Curved Substrate**

A substrate of thickness $t_s$ is initially flat. After depositing a thin film of thickness $t_f$, the substrate bends to a radius of curvature $R$ due to the film's residual stress.

**Step 2 — Force and Moment Balance**

The thin film exerts a force $F = \sigma_f \cdot t_f$ per unit width on the substrate. To maintain equilibrium, the substrate must develop a linearly varying stress that integrates to zero force and a non-zero moment.

**Step 3 — Strain in Substrate at Distance $y$ from Neutral Axis**

$$\varepsilon_s(y) = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Stress in Substrate**

$$\sigma_s(y) = E_s \varepsilon_s(y) = \frac{E_s \, y}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Net Force on Substrate is Zero**

The substrate's stress must integrate to zero, locating the neutral axis at the center of the substrate.

**Step 6 — Moment from Substrate = Moment from Film**

The film's force creates a moment about the neutral axis. The substrate resists with its internal stress distribution:

$$\sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \int_{-t_s/2}^{t_s/2} \frac{E_s y}{R} \cdot y \, dy$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the Integral**

$$\sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \frac{E_s}{R} \int_{-t_s/2}^{t_s/2} y^2 dy = \frac{E_s}{R} \cdot \frac{t_s^3}{12}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for Film Stress**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s t_s^2}{6 R t_f}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{film} = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 R \, t_f}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The film must be **much thinner** than the substrate ($t_f \ll t_s$). This is almost always satisfied in MEMS.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-style problem)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A MEMS cantilever beam made of single-crystal silicon has length $L = 200 \, \mu m$, width $b = 20 \, \mu m$, and thickness $h = 2 \, \mu m$. A force of $F = 10 \, \mu N$ is applied at its free end. Young's modulus $E = 190$ GPa. Calculate:
> (a) The spring constant of the beam
> (b) The tip deflection
> (c) The maximum bending stress

**Solution:**

**(a) Spring constant:**

First, compute the moment of inertia:
$$I = \frac{b h^3}{12} = \frac{(20 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{(20 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18})}{12} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-22}}{12} = 1.333 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{m}^4$$

Now compute the spring constant:
$$k = \frac{3 E I}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times (190 \times 10^9) \times (1.333 \times 10^{-23})}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

$$k = \frac{7.6 \times 10^{-12}}{8 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.95 \, \text{N/m}$$

**(b) Tip deflection:**

$$w_{tip} = \frac{F}{k} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{0.95} = 10.53 \, \mu m$$

**(c) Maximum bending stress:**

The maximum bending moment occurs at the fixed end:
$$M_{max} = F \cdot L = (10 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{N·m}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 M_{max}}{b h^2} = \frac{6 \times (2 \times 10^{-9})}{(20 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-9}}{8 \times 10^{-17}} = 1.5 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Pa} = 150 \, \text{MPa}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** 150 MPa is well below the fracture strength of silicon (~1–7 GPa), so the beam is safe in this design.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]** *(3 Marks)*

**List any three mechanical properties of single-crystal silicon that make it suitable for MEMS applications.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High Young's Modulus (E ≈ 190 GPa):** Provides high stiffness, ensuring predictable elastic behavior and resistance to deformation under load.
2. **Near-perfect elasticity up to fracture:** No plastic deformation at room temperature, enabling linear, repeatable, and reliable mechanical response.
3. **Low density (ρ ≈ 2330 kg/m³):** Provides excellent stiffness-to-weight ratio, which is critical for high-frequency resonators and low-inertia devices.

*(Other valid properties: high fracture strength, high thermal conductivity, low thermal expansion, well-defined piezoresistive coefficients.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** *(3 Marks)*

**What is piezoresistivity? How is it utilized in MEMS sensors?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Piezoresistivity is the phenomenon in which the electrical resistivity of a semiconductor material changes when mechanical stress is applied to it.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

where $\pi_l$ and $\pi_t$ are the longitudinal and transverse piezoresistive coefficients, and $\sigma_l, \sigma_t$ are the corresponding stresses.

**Application in MEMS:** It is the working principle of **MEMS pressure sensors and accelerometers**. Piezoresistors are diffused or implanted onto a silicon diaphragm or beam. When stress is applied, the resistance changes, producing a measurable voltage output proportional to the applied force or pressure. In silicon, the piezoresistive effect is **~50 to 100 times stronger** than in metals, enabling highly sensitive micro-sensors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A **[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]** *(14 Marks)*

**A. (a) Derive an expression for the tip deflection of a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $h$ subjected to a concentrated load $F$ at the free end.** *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** At a section at distance $x$ from the fixed end, the internal bending moment is:
$$M(x) = F (L - x)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Euler–Bernoulli beam equation:
$$E I \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = M(x) = F(L - x)$$

**Step 3:** Integrate once:
$$E I \frac{dw}{dx} = F \left(L x - \frac{x^2}{2}\right) + C_1$$

**Step 4:** Apply the slope boundary condition at the clamped end: $\frac{dw}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0 \implies C_1 = 0$.

**Step 5:** Integrate again:
$$E I \, w(x) = F \left(\frac{L x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{6}\right) + C_2$$

**Step 6:** Apply the deflection boundary condition: $w(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$.

**Step 7:** Therefore:
$$w(x) = \frac{F x^2}{6 E I}(3L - x)$$

**Step 8:** At the free end ($x = L$):
$$w_{tip} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b h^3}$$

since $I = \frac{b h^3}{12}$.

**A. (b) Explain the four most common types of beam structures used in MEMS with suitable examples.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cantilever Beam (Clamped-Free):** One end rigidly fixed, the other free. Found in **AFM probes, micro-switches, mass sensors, and biosensors**. Deflection: $w = \frac{F L^3}{3EI}$. Spring constant: $k = \frac{3EI}{L^3}$.

2. **Clamped-Clamped Beam (Fixed-Fixed):** Both ends rigidly fixed. Used in **MEMS resonators, accelerometers (suspension beams), RF filters, and gyroscopes**. Higher stiffness than cantilevers — $k = \frac{192EI}{L^3}$.

3. **Simply Supported Beam:** Both ends pin-supported, free rotation allowed. Less common in MEMS due to the difficulty of fabricating perfect pin joints; used in some **micro-relays and mechanical test structures**.

4. **Free-Free Beam (Center Supported):** A beam supported only at its midpoint, with both ends free to oscillate. Used in **tuning-fork gyroscopes and disk resonators** where anti-symmetric or symmetric modes are exploited.

**Key Differences:**
- Cantilevers are the **most compliant** (lowest $k$) — used for sensitive detection.
- Clamped-clamped beams are **much stiffer** and have **higher resonance frequencies** — used for resonators.
- The mode shape constants ($\beta_n L$) also differ, affecting frequency response.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]** *(14 Marks)*

**B. (a) With a neat derivation, explain Stoney's equation for residual stress in thin films. State its assumptions and limitations.** *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a thin film of thickness $t_f$ deposited on a thick substrate of thickness $t_s$. After deposition, the substrate curves to a radius $R$ due to film stress $\sigma_f$.

**Step 2 — Strain in Substrate:** At a distance $y$ from the neutral axis of the substrate, the strain is:
$$\varepsilon_s(y) = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Stress in Substrate:** For the substrate behaving elastically:
$$\sigma_s(y) = \frac{E_s y}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Force Balance:** The film exerts a force per unit width $F = \sigma_f t_f$ on the substrate. For equilibrium, the substrate stress must integrate to zero, locating the neutral axis at the substrate mid-plane.

**Step 5 — Moment Balance:** The moment from the film about the neutral axis equals the moment from the substrate's internal stresses:
$$\sigma_f t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \int_{-t_s/2}^{t_s/2} \sigma_s(y) \cdot y \, dy = \int_{-t_s/2}^{t_s/2} \frac{E_s y^2}{R} dy$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Integral:**
$$\int_{-t_s/2}^{t_s/2} y^2 dy = \frac{t_s^3}{12}$$

**Step 7 — Final Stoney's Equation:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_{film} = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 R \, t_f}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Film thickness is much smaller than substrate ($t_f \ll t_s$).
2. Substrate is homogeneous and isotropic.
3. The film's contribution to the bending stiffness is negligible.
4. Stresses are uniform across the film thickness.
5. Small deflection (radius of curvature much larger than substrate dimensions).

**Limitations:** Does not account for **biaxial vs. uniaxial** stress, **substrate anisotropy**, or **plastic deformation** of the film.

**B. (b) Discuss the mechanical properties of polysilicon, silicon nitride, and SU-8 thin films used in MEMS.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Polysilicon (Poly-Si):**
- Young's Modulus: 160–170 GPa (slightly lower than single-crystal Si due to grain boundaries).
- Residual stress: can be tuned from -10 MPa to +300 MPa by controlling deposition and annealing.
- Used as the **primary structural material** in surface-micromachined MEMS (e.g., Analog Devices accelerometers, gyroscopes). Excellent compatibility with CMOS processes.

**2. Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄):**
- Young's Modulus: 250–290 GPa (very stiff).
- Residual stress: **high tensile** (500–1000 MPa) due to deposition conditions.
- Used in **pressure sensor membranes**, **dielectric layers**, and **high-stress mechanical elements** where a taut, flat surface is desired. The high tensile stress prevents membrane buckling.

**3. SU-8 (Negative Photoresist Polymer):**
- Young's Modulus: 2–4 GPa (low compared to silicon-based films).
- Residual stress: relatively low.
- Used in **thick (10–500 µm) structural layers** for microfluidics, micro-molds, optical MEMS, and bio-MEMS. Not suitable for high-frequency or high-stress applications, but excellent for low-cost, large-area structures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Forgetting to convert µm to m in moment of inertia or deflection formulas. Always check: 1 µm = 10⁻⁶ m.
> 2. **Wrong I value:** Using $I = \frac{h b^3}{12}$ instead of $\frac{b h^3}{12}$ (the cubic dimension is the one perpendicular to bending — for a beam bent downward, the thickness $h$ is cubed, not the width $b$).
> 3. **Confusing Cantilever and Clamped-Clamped formulas:** The factor is $1/3$ for cantilever but $1/192$ for C-C center load — a 64× difference!
> 4. **Misinterpreting Stoney's equation:** Some students write $R$ in the numerator. The radius of curvature is in the **denominator** — higher curvature means higher stress.
> 5. **Forgetting boundary conditions:** When deriving, you must write all 4 BCs explicitly. Examiners mark them step-by-step.
> 6. **Wrong mode constant:** Using $\beta L = 1.875$ for clamped-clamped beams — that's for cantilevers. For C-C, $\beta_1 L = 4.730$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Mechanical Properties of Silicon & MEMS Beams**

### Mechanical Properties of Silicon
- [ ] Young's Modulus of Si = **190 GPa** (single-crystal)
- [ ] Poisson's ratio = **0.28**, density = **2330 kg/m³**
- [ ] Silicon is **brittle** — no plastic deformation at room temperature
- [ ] **Piezoresistivity** in Si is ~50–100× stronger than in metals
- [ ] Fracture strength is statistical: typically **1–7 GPa** (theoretical up to ~10 GPa)

### Related Thin Films
- [ ] **Polysilicon:** $E \approx 160$ GPa — main structural film in surface micromachining
- [ ] **Si₃N₄:** $E \approx 250$ GPa, **tensile** residual stress — used for membranes
- [ ] **SiO₂:** $E \approx 70$ GPa, **compressive** — used as sacrificial layer
- [ ] **SU-8:** $E \approx 2$ GPa — used for thick structures
- [ ] **Residual stress** is characterized using **Stoney's equation**

### MEMS Beams
- [ ] **Cantilever:** $k = \frac{3EI}{L^3}$, $w_{tip} = \frac{FL^3}{3EI}$, $\beta_1 L = 1.875$
- [ ] **Clamped-Clamped:** $k = \frac{192EI}{L^3}$, $w_{center} = \frac{FL^3}{192EI}$, $\beta_1 L = 4.730$
- [ ] **Moment of inertia** (rectangular): $I = \frac{bh^3}{12}$ — $h$ is cubed!
- [ ] **Resonance frequency:** $f_n = \frac{\beta_n^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$
- [ ] **Bending stress (max):** $\sigma_{max} = \frac{6M}{bh^2}$

### Engineering Insights
- [ ] Beam stiffness scales as $h^3$ and $1/L^3$ — geometry dominates
- [ ] Silicon's $E/\rho$ is excellent for high-frequency MEMS
- [ ] High tensile residual stress in Si₃N₄ keeps membranes flat
- [ ] Compressive stress in films causes **buckling** — design carefully
- [ ] Cantilever beams are most compliant — best for low-force sensors
- [ ] C-C beams are stiffest and highest-frequency — best for resonators
- [ ] Piezoresistive sensing: $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Study Tip:** For KTU Module 2, **memorize the formula table** in Section 2.7, practice **deriving the cantilever deflection** and **Stoney's equation** from scratch, and be ready to explain the **4 beam types** with **one commercial MEMS device** as an example for each. These three things alone cover ~80% of the marks for this module.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
