<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **cantilever** is a rigid structural element that extends horizontally and is supported (clamped/anchored) at only **one end**, while the other end remains **free**. In the context of **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, a cantilever is a micro-fabricated beam — typically of **single-crystal silicon (Si)**, **polysilicon**, **silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)**, or **SU-8 polymer** — fabricated using bulk or surface micromachining techniques. It functions as the fundamental mechanical transducer element in devices such as **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) probes**, **accelerometers**, **pressure sensors**, **biosensors**, **RF switches**, and **resonators**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEAET722 – Module 2):**
> *A cantilever beam is a one-dimensional micro-mechanical structure fixed at one end and free at the other. It is the simplest and most widely used compliant structure in MEMS, characterized by its bending stiffness, tip deflection, resonant frequency, and stress distribution.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Diving Board**
> Imagine a **diving board** at a swimming pool. One end is firmly bolted to a concrete platform (the **clamped/fixed support**), while the opposite end extends into open air (the **free end**). When a diver stands on the free end, the board **bends downward**, with maximum deflection at the tip and maximum bending stress at the fixed root. A MEMS cantilever behaves *exactly* like this diving board, but shrunk to micrometer dimensions and fabricated from silicon instead of wood/fiberglass. Just as a stiffer board (thicker or made of a stiffer material) bends less, a MEMS cantilever with a **larger Young's modulus**, **larger thickness**, or **shorter length** will deflect less under a given force.

---

### 1.3 Geometric & Material Parameters of a MEMS Cantilever

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical MEMS Range |
|---|---|---|
| Length | $L$ | 50 µm – 500 µm |
| Width | $w$ | 10 µm – 100 µm |
| Thickness | $t$ | 0.5 µm – 10 µm |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **130–190 GPa** (for Si) |
| Density | $\rho$ | 2330 kg/m³ (Si) |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | 0.22 – 0.28 (Si) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fixed end** is called the **anchor** or **clamped support**. The mechanical boundary condition enforced there is: **deflection = 0** and **slope = 0**. The free end imposes **zero bending moment** and **zero shear force**.

---

### 1.4 Classification of MEMS Cantilevers

1. **Rectangular Cantilever** — uniform cross-section ($w \times t$) along its length.
2. **Stepped / Tapered Cantilever** — variable width or thickness along $L$ (used in AFM for higher mode shapes).
3. **Triangular (V-shaped) Cantilever** — two beams converging at the tip; higher lateral stiffness, used in AFM.
4. **Piezoelectric Cantilever** — carries a piezoelectric layer (e.g., PZT, AlN) for actuation/sensing.
5. **Bimetallic / Bimorph Cantilever** — two layers of differing thermal expansion coefficient for thermal actuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Theory — Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory

For MEMS cantilevers where the **thickness $t$ is much smaller than the length $L$** ($t/L < 1/10$), and assuming **small deflections** (deflection $< t$), the **Euler–Bernoulli beam equation** governs the static and dynamic behavior:

$$EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = q(x,t)$$

Where:
- $w(x,t)$ = transverse deflection at position $x$ and time $t$
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (Pa)
- $I$ = Area moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- $\rho$ = material density (kg/m³)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area ($A = w \cdot t$)
- $q(x,t)$ = distributed transverse load (N/m)

> [!NOTE]
> For **purely static analysis** (no time dependence), the inertial term vanishes and the equation reduces to: $EI\,\dfrac{d^4 w}{dx^4} = q(x)$.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Cantilever Bending

- **Step 1:** A transverse force (point load, distributed load, or end moment) is applied along the beam.
- **Step 2:** The beam develops an internal **bending moment** $M(x)$ that varies along the length.
- **Step 3:** The curvature $\kappa(x)$ of the neutral axis relates to the moment via: $\kappa = M/EI$ (Euler–Bernoulli relation).
- **Step 4:** Integrating curvature twice gives the **slope** and **deflection** profiles $w(x)$.
- **Step 5:** Maximum **bending stress** occurs at the **fixed end** (root) on the top and bottom surfaces: $\sigma = M \cdot c / I$, where $c = t/2$.
- **Step 6:** For dynamic cases, the beam oscillates at **resonant frequencies** determined by its mass and stiffness distribution.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Area moment of inertia (rectangular) | $I = \dfrac{w t^3}{12}$ | m⁴ | Bending stiffness |
| 2 | Cross-sectional area | $A = w \cdot t$ | m² | Mass calculation |
| 3 | Tip deflection (point load $P$ at tip) | $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{P L^3}{3 E I}$ | m | Static sensing |
| 4 | Spring constant | $k = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3} = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | N/m | Sensor design |
| 5 | Tip deflection (uniform load $q$) | $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{q L^4}{8 E I}$ | m | Pressure sensors |
| 6 | Tip deflection (end moment $M_0$) | $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{M_0 L^2}{2 E I}$ | m | Bimorph actuators |
| 7 | Maximum bending stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 P L}{w t^2}$ | Pa | Fracture/fatigue check |
| 8 | Maximum bending moment (at root) | $M_{max} = P L$ | N·m | Stress analysis |
| 9 | Mass of cantilever | $m = \rho w t L$ | kg | Dynamic analysis |
| 10 | Effective mass (1st mode) | $m_{eff} = 0.24 \, m$ | kg | Resonator design |
| 11 | Fundamental resonant frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E I}{\rho A L^4}}$ | Hz | Dynamic operation |
| 12 | Simplified resonant frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}}$ | Hz | Quick estimation |
| 13 | Stiffness matrix element | $K_{xx} = \dfrac{12 E I}{L^3}$ | N/m | FEA modeling |
| 14 | Quality factor (vacuum) | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{E \rho}}{2 \zeta_0}$ | dimensionless | Damping analysis |
| 15 | Thermal deflection (bimorph) | $\delta = \dfrac{3 L^2 \Delta \alpha \Delta T}{2 t_{total}}$ | m | Thermal actuators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **1.875** (more precisely, $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$) is the **first root of the transcendental equation $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0$**, characteristic of a fixed-free (cantilever) boundary condition.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Force/Displacement Sensing:** The linear relationship $\delta = PL^3/(3EI)$ is exploited in AFM to map atomic-scale forces.
- **Resonant Mass Sensing:** Adsorbed mass shifts $f_0$ predictably — the basis of **chemical and biological microbalances**.
- **Filtering:** MEMS cantilevers act as **mechanical bandpass filters** in RF front-ends.
- **Energy Harvesting:** Piezoelectric cantilevers convert ambient vibration into usable electrical energy.
- **Actuation:** Electrostatic, piezoelectric, or thermal actuation of cantilevers produces controlled micro-motion (e.g., micromirrors, microvalves).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tip Deflection Under a Point Load $P$ at the Free End

**Setup:** A cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, with Young's modulus $E$ and moment of inertia $I$ is fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$. A transverse point load $P$ is applied at the free end in the $-z$ direction.

**Step 1: Determine the internal bending moment $M(x)$.**

Cut the beam at an arbitrary position $x$ (between 0 and $L$). The free-body diagram of the right portion (from $x$ to $L$) shows only the load $P$ acting at the tip.

$$M(x) = -P (L - x)$$

The negative sign indicates a sagging moment (standard sign convention in mechanics of materials).

**Step 2: Apply the Euler–Bernoulli moment–curvature relation.**

The deflection $w(x)$ relates to bending moment through:

$$EI \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = M(x) = -P (L - x)$$

**Step 3: First integration — obtain the slope $\theta(x) = dw/dx$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \frac{dw}{dx} &= \int -P (L - x)\, dx \\
&= -P \left( L x - \frac{x^2}{2} \right) + C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the boundary condition at the clamped end ($x = 0$, slope = 0): $\theta(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.

**Step 4: Second integration — obtain the deflection $w(x)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \, w(x) &= \int \left( -P L x + \frac{P x^2}{2} \right) dx \\
&= -\frac{P L x^2}{2} + \frac{P x^3}{6} + C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the boundary condition at the clamped end ($x = 0$, deflection = 0): $w(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

**Step 5: Evaluate the tip deflection at $x = L$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \, w(L) &= -\frac{P L \cdot L^2}{2} + \frac{P L^3}{6} \\
&= -\frac{P L^3}{2} + \frac{P L^3}{6} \\
&= -P L^3 \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{6} \right) \\
&= -P L^3 \cdot \frac{3 - 1}{6} \\
&= -\frac{P L^3}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the magnitude of the tip deflection is:

$$\boxed{\delta_{tip} = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I}}$$

For a rectangular cross-section, substituting $I = wt^3/12$:

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{4 P L^3}{E w t^3}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spring Constant (Stiffness) of a Cantilever

By Hooke's Law, the spring constant $k$ is the ratio of applied force to resulting tip deflection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{P}{\delta_{tip}} \\
&= \frac{P}{\dfrac{P L^3}{3 E I}} \\
&= \frac{3 E I}{L^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $I = wt^3/12$:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{3 E \cdot (w t^3 / 12)}{L^3} = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** The spring constant depends on the **cube of thickness $t$** and is **inversely proportional to the cube of length $L$**. Hence, halving the beam length increases stiffness by a factor of **8**, while halving the thickness reduces stiffness by a factor of **8**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Bending Stress at the Fixed End

The maximum bending moment occurs at the clamped end:

$$M_{max} = P L \quad \text{(at } x = 0\text{)}$$

The maximum bending stress for a rectangular cross-section, using $\sigma = Mc/I$ with $c = t/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{(P L) \cdot (t/2)}{w t^3 / 12} \\
&= \frac{P L \cdot t / 2 \cdot 12}{w t^3} \\
&= \frac{6 P L}{w t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 P L}{w t^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This stress must remain **below the yield strength** of the material (e.g., for single-crystal Si, yield stress ≈ **7 GPa** in bending). Exceeding it causes **fracture** of the cantilever — a critical design constraint for AFM probes and high-acceleration sensors.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fundamental Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever

**Step 1: The eigenvalue problem.**

For free vibration of an undamped cantilever, the governing equation is:

$$EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

Assume a harmonic solution: $w(x,t) = \phi(x) e^{j \omega t}$, which leads to the spatial ODE:

$$EI \frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4} - \rho A \omega^2 \phi = 0$$

Let $\beta^4 = \dfrac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$. The general solution is:

$$\phi(x) = A_1 \cos(\beta x) + A_2 \sin(\beta x) + A_3 \cosh(\beta x) + A_4 \sinh(\beta x)$$

**Step 2: Apply boundary conditions for a cantilever (fixed at $x=0$, free at $x=L$).**

- At $x = 0$: $\phi(0) = 0$ and $\phi'(0) = 0$.
- At $x = L$: $\phi''(L) = 0$ (zero bending moment) and $\phi'''(L) = 0$ (zero shear force).

**Step 3: Resulting transcendental equation (characteristic equation).**

$$\cos(\beta L) \cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0$$

**Step 4: The roots of this equation are the eigenvalues $\beta_n L$:**

- $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$ (1st mode)
- $\beta_2 L = 4.6941$ (2nd mode)
- $\beta_3 L = 7.8548$ (3rd mode)

**Step 5: Solve for the natural angular frequency of mode $n$:**

$$\omega_n = (\beta_n L)^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$$

**Step 6: Convert to natural frequency in Hz.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_n &= \frac{\omega_n}{2 \pi} = \frac{(\beta_n L)^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **fundamental mode** ($n = 1$), using $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$:

$$\boxed{f_1 = \frac{(1.8751)^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}} \approx 0.5615 \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cantilever vs. Other MEMS Beams

| Property | Cantilever (Fixed–Free) | Fixed–Fixed Beam | Doubly-Clamped |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spring constant $k$ | $\dfrac{3EI}{L^3}$ | $\dfrac{12EI}{L^3}$ | $\dfrac{16EI}{L^3}$ |
| Tip deflection (point load) | $\dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ | $\dfrac{PL^3}{192EI}$ | $\dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ |
| Fundamental $\beta_1 L$ | 1.8751 | 4.7300 | 4.7300 |
| $f_1$ scaling | $\sim t/L^2$ | $\sim t/L^2$ | $\sim t/L^2$ |
| Compliance | Highest | Lowest | Intermediate |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define a MEMS cantilever beam. List any four applications of cantilevers in microsystems.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
A MEMS cantilever is a micro-fabricated beam, anchored at one end (fixed/clamped) and free at the other, typically made of silicon, polysilicon, or silicon nitride with typical dimensions in the range of 50–500 µm length and 0.5–10 µm thickness.
Applications: (1) **AFM probes** for nanoscale imaging, (2) **accelerometers** for inertial sensing, (3) **biosensors / chemical sensors** for mass detection, (4) **RF MEMS switches** for signal routing, (5) **piezoelectric energy harvesters**, (6) **micromirrors** in optical MEMS.

---

**Q2. State the boundary conditions satisfied by a cantilever beam of length $L$ fixed at $x = 0$.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
For a cantilever clamped at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$:

- At $x = 0$ (fixed end): Deflection $w(0) = 0$ and Slope $\left.\dfrac{dw}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0$
- At $x = L$ (free end): Bending moment $M(L) = EI \left.\dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2}\right|_{x=L} = 0$ and Shear force $V(L) = EI \left.\dfrac{d^3 w}{dx^3}\right|_{x=L} = 0$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **static tip deflection** of a rectangular MEMS cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$ when a transverse point load $P$ is applied at its free end. Use the Euler–Bernoulli beam theory. Hence, obtain the **spring constant** of the cantilever. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Internal bending moment.**
Cutting the beam at a section $x$ from the fixed end and analyzing the free-body of the right portion:

$$M(x) = -P(L - x)$$

**Step 2: Euler–Bernoulli moment–curvature relation.**

$$EI \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = -P(L - x)$$

**Step 3: First integration (slope).**

$$EI \frac{dw}{dx} = -P L x + \frac{P x^2}{2} + C_1$$

Applying $\left.\dfrac{dw}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0$ ⟹ $C_1 = 0$.

**Step 4: Second integration (deflection).**

$$EI \, w(x) = -\frac{P L x^2}{2} + \frac{P x^3}{6} + C_2$$

Applying $w(0) = 0$ ⟹ $C_2 = 0$.

**Step 5: Tip deflection at $x = L$.**

$$EI \, w(L) = -\frac{P L^3}{2} + \frac{P L^3}{6} = -\frac{P L^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{tip} = \frac{P L^3}{3EI}}$$

**Step 6: Spring constant.**

$$\boxed{k = \frac{P}{\delta_{tip}} = \frac{3EI}{L^3} = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}}$$

---

**(b)** A silicon MEMS cantilever has the following specifications: Length $L = 200 \, \mu m$, width $w = 40 \, \mu m$, thickness $t = 2 \, \mu m$, Young's modulus $E = 169$ GPa. Calculate: (i) the **spring constant**, (ii) the **tip deflection** under a point load $P = 10 \, \mu N$, and (iii) the **maximum bending stress** at the fixed end. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $w = 40 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $t = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $E = 169 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $P = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ N = $10^{-5}$ N

**Step 1: Moment of inertia.**

$$I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(40 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{(40 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18})}{12} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-22}}{12} = 2.667 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{m}^4$$

**Step 2: (i) Spring constant.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{3 E I}{L^3} \\
&= \frac{3 \times (169 \times 10^9) \times (2.667 \times 10^{-23})}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^3} \\
&= \frac{1.352 \times 10^{-11}}{8 \times 10^{-15}} \\
&= 1.690 \times 10^{3} \, \text{N/m} \\
&\approx \boxed{1.69 \, \text{N/m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: (ii) Tip deflection.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{tip} &= \frac{P}{k} = \frac{10^{-5}}{1.69 \times 10^3} \\
&= 5.92 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m} \\
&\approx \boxed{5.92 \, \text{nm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: (iii) Maximum bending stress.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{6 P L}{w t^2} \\
&= \frac{6 \times (10^{-5}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6})}{(40 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^2} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-8}}{1.6 \times 10^{-16}} \\
&= 7.5 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} \\
&\approx \boxed{75 \, \text{MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 75 MPa ≪ 7 GPa (silicon yield strength), the design is **safe from fracture**.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a cantilever-based AFM probe**. Derive the expression for its **fundamental resonant frequency** and discuss how the resonant frequency shifts when an additional mass $\Delta m$ is adsorbed on the tip. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
In an AFM, a microcantilever with a sharp nanoscale tip (radius ≈ 5–30 nm) at its free end is raster-scanned across a sample surface. As the tip interacts with surface atoms, it experiences **attractive/repulsive forces** ($10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}$ N), causing the cantilever to **deflect** ($\sim$ 0.1–10 nm). This deflection is detected by:
- **Optical beam deflection** (laser + photodiode) — most common.
- **Piezoresistive** readout — integrated strain gauges.
- **Interferometric** detection — high precision.

**Derivation of Fundamental Frequency:**

The governing equation for free vibration of a uniform cantilever:

$$EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

Substituting $w(x,t) = \phi(x) e^{j\omega t}$ and applying the four boundary conditions (clamped-free) yields the transcendental equation:

$$\cos(\beta L) \cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0$$

The smallest root is $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$, giving:

$$f_1 = \frac{(1.8751)^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}} = 0.5615 \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2}$$

**Mass Loading Effect:**

When an additional mass $\Delta m$ adsorbs at the tip, the total effective mass becomes $m_{eff}' = m_{eff} + \Delta m$. The new resonant frequency is:

$$
f_1' = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff} + \Delta m}}
$$

The frequency shift is:

$$\Delta f = f_1 - f_1' = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left( \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} - \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff} + \Delta m}} \right)$$

For small $\Delta m$: $\boxed{\Delta f \approx \dfrac{f_1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}}}$

This is the basis of **cantilever-based mass sensors** capable of detecting femtogram-level mass changes.

---

**(b)** A polysilicon MEMS cantilever used as a chemical sensor has dimensions $L = 300 \, \mu m$, $w = 50 \, \mu m$, $t = 2.5 \, \mu m$. For polysilicon, $E = 160$ GPa and $\rho = 2300 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate: (i) the **fundamental resonant frequency**, and (ii) the **frequency shift** when a mass of $\Delta m = 10$ pg is adsorbed uniformly on the cantilever. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 300 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $w = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $t = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $E = 160 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\rho = 2300$ kg/m³
- $\Delta m = 10 \times 10^{-12}$ kg

**Step 1: Mass of the cantilever.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \rho \cdot w \cdot t \cdot L \\
&= 2300 \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (300 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 2300 \times 3.75 \times 10^{-14} \\
&= 8.625 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{kg} \approx 86.25 \, \text{pg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Effective mass (fundamental mode).**

$$m_{eff} = 0.24 \times m = 0.24 \times 8.625 \times 10^{-11} = 2.07 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Moment of inertia and stiffness.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(2.5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-17}}{12} = 6.51 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
k = \frac{3EI}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times 160 \times 10^9 \times 6.51 \times 10^{-23}}{(300 \times 10^{-6})^3} = \frac{3.124 \times 10^{-11}}{2.7 \times 10^{-14}} = 1157 \, \text{N/m}
$$

**Step 4: (i) Fundamental resonant frequency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1157}{2.07 \times 10^{-11}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{5.59 \times 10^{13}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 7.477 \times 10^6 \\
&\approx \boxed{1.19 \, \text{MHz}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: (ii) Frequency shift.**

New effective mass: $m_{eff}' = m_{eff} + \Delta m = 2.07 \times 10^{-11} + 1.0 \times 10^{-11} = 3.07 \times 10^{-11}$ kg

$$
f_1' = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1157}{3.07 \times 10^{-11}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 6.14 \times 10^6 \approx 0.977 \, \text{MHz}
$$

$$\Delta f = 1.19 - 0.977 = \boxed{0.213 \, \text{MHz} \; (213 \, \text{kHz})}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the moment of inertia** $I = wt^3/12$ — many students use the wrong formula or confuse $w$ and $t$ (remember: thickness is cubed!).
> 2. **Sign convention errors** in boundary conditions — slope is zero at the **fixed** end, NOT the free end.
> 3. **Mixing up** the formula for the fundamental eigenvalue: 1.875 is for **cantilever** (fixed-free), 4.73 is for **fixed-fixed** beam. **Do not interchange them.**
> 4. **Units slip-up**: Convert µm to m consistently. A $1\,\mu m$ error in thickness causes a **3× error** in stiffness (cube dependence).
> 5. **Omitting effective mass factor** 0.24 in dynamic problems — this gives nearly 4× error in resonant frequency.
> 6. **Stress formula** must use $c = t/2$, not $t$, for rectangular sections.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **cantilever** is a **fixed–free** beam, the most compliant among standard beam configurations.
- The **moment of inertia** for a rectangular cross-section is $I = \dfrac{w t^3}{12}$.
- **Spring constant:** $k = \dfrac{3EI}{L^3} = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ — scales as $t^3$ and $1/L^3$.
- **Tip deflection under point load:** $\delta = \dfrac{P L^3}{3EI}$.
- **Tip deflection under uniform load:** $\delta = \dfrac{q L^4}{8EI}$.
- **Maximum bending stress** (at root): $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 P L}{w t^2}$ — must be checked against material yield strength.
- **Fundamental resonant frequency:** $f_1 = \dfrac{(1.8751)^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$.
- **Effective mass** for fundamental mode: $m_{eff} = 0.24\,m$.
- **Mass sensing equation:** $\Delta f \approx \dfrac{f_1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}}$ for small adsorbed mass.
- **Boundary conditions** — At fixed end: $w = 0$, $w' = 0$. At free end: $M = 0$, $V = 0$.
- **First eigenvalue** of cantilever is **1.8751** (memorize this!).
- **Design trade-off:** shorter + thicker + stiffer material = higher $k$, lower $\delta$, higher $f_1$.
- **Common MEMS materials:** Single-crystal Si (E ≈ 130–190 GPa), polysilicon (E ≈ 160 GPa), Si₃N₄ (E ≈ 250–310 GPa).
- **Critical applications:** AFM probes, accelerometers, mass/bio/chemical sensors, RF switches, energy harvesters, micromirrors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Plates in MEMS

In the context of solid mechanics and Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS), a **plate** is defined as a two-dimensional, flat structural element whose thickness ($h$) is significantly smaller than its in-plane dimensions (length $a$ and width $b$), but is large enough to resist transverse loads through **bending stiffness** rather than membrane action alone.

The ratio $\frac{h}{a}$ is the critical geometric parameter. Based on this ratio, structural plates are categorized as:

| Classification | Thickness Ratio | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Thin Plates (Membranes)** | $\frac{h}{a} < \dfrac{1}{20}$ | Governed by membrane forces, negligible bending |
| **Thin Plates (Classical)** | $\dfrac{1}{100} < \dfrac{h}{a} < \dfrac{1}{20}$ | Governed by Kirchhoff-Love Plate Theory (KLPT) |
| **Thick Plates** | $\dfrac{1}{20} < \dfrac{h}{a} < \dfrac{1}{10}$ | Shear deformation becomes significant (Mindlin Theory) |
| **Very Thick Plates** | $\dfrac{h}{a} > \dfrac{1}{10}$ | 3D elasticity theory is mandatory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEAET722, Module 2):** The "Plates" topic specifically covers the mechanics of thin elastic plates used as **diaphragms** in microsensors and microactuators. Students must master **Kirchhoff-Love plate theory**, **boundary conditions**, and **stress-deflection relationships** for both rectangular and circular geometries, as these form the analytical backbone of pressure sensors, microphones, and micro-mirrors.

In MEMS devices, a plate is more commonly referred to as a **diaphragm** when it is anchored at its periphery and used to sense pressure, acoustic waves, or inertial forces. The plate is typically fabricated from **single-crystal silicon (Si)**, **polysilicon**, **silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)**, or **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** using bulk or surface micromachining techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diving Board" Intuition

Imagine pushing down on the edge of a **diving board** (cantilever) versus pushing on the **center of a trampoline** (a plate fixed at its edges):

- A diving board bends because of a **load at one edge** and an anchor at the other. Its deformation is dominated by *bending* along one axis, and it is properly modeled as a **beam**, not a plate.
- A trampoline, however, is anchored **all around its perimeter**. When you push its center, the deformation spreads radially in **two directions** (x and y), and the entire surface works together to resist the load. This is a true **plate** behavior.

In MEMS, our "trampolines" are:
- A **silicon diaphragm** in a piezoresistive pressure sensor (push = applied pressure, anchor = rigid silicon rim).
- A **polysilicon membrane** in a condenser microphone (push = sound wave pressure, anchor = perforated backplate).
- A **suspended mirror plate** in a Digital Micromirror Device (DMD) (push = electrostatic force, anchor = torsion hinges).

In all these cases, the plate **bends and deforms** until the **internal bending moments** at any cross-section balance the applied load, just as the diving board's internal stresses balance the diver's weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are plates so crucial in MEMS?** Because they are the **transduction element** in the majority of physical sensors. A small applied pressure causes a measurable deflection, and the deflection is converted to an electrical signal via piezoresistors, capacitors, or optical readouts. Mastering plate mechanics allows you to design **predictable, linear, and reliable** microsensors.

## 1.3 Fundamental Assumptions of Classical Plate Theory (Kirchhoff-Love)

The Classical Plate Theory (CPT), also known as **Kirchhoff-Love Plate Theory (KLPT)**, is the workhorse model for MEMS diaphragm analysis. It rests on the following four core assumptions:

1. **Straight Normals Remain Straight and Normal:** The line originally perpendicular to the mid-surface of the plate remains straight and perpendicular to the deformed (or deflected) mid-surface after bending. This means **transverse shear deformation is neglected**.
2. **Negligible Transverse Normal Strain:** The strain in the thickness direction (z-direction), $\varepsilon_z$, is assumed to be zero. The plate is treated as **inextensible in the thickness direction**.
3. **Negligible Transverse Normal Stress:** The stress component $\sigma_z$ is small compared to in-plane stresses $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$, and is set to zero for bending analysis.
4. **Mid-Plane Remains Inextensible:** The mid-surface of the plate does not stretch. The in-plane displacements $u$ and $v$ vary **linearly** through the thickness, but their values at the mid-plane are zero.

These assumptions lead to a dramatically simplified governing equation that captures the essential physics of plate bending for **thin plates**, which is exactly the regime in which most MEMS diaphragms operate.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Kinematics of Plate Bending (Displacement Field)

Following the KLPT assumptions, the displacement field at any point $(x, y, z)$ in the plate is fully described by the mid-plane deflection $w(x, y)$:

$$
u(x, y, z) = -z \, \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}
$$

$$
v(x, y, z) = -z \, \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}
$$

$$
w(x, y, z) = w(x, y)
$$

Where:
- $u, v, w$ are the displacement components in the $x, y, z$ directions.
- $\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}$ and $\frac{\partial w}{\partial y}$ are the **rotations** of the mid-surface normal about the $y$ and $x$ axes, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the negative sign?** By convention, $w$ is taken as positive **downward** (in the direction of applied pressure), while the rotations $\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}$ are defined as positive when the normal rotates toward the positive $x$ axis. The negative sign ensures that the in-plane displacements $u$ and $v$ are consistent with the physical bending of the plate.

## 2.2 Strain Field in the Plate

Applying the standard small-strain definitions to the displacement field above yields:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -z \, \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -z \, \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}
$$

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -2z \, \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \, \partial y}
$$

The quantities $\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}$, $\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}$, and $\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}$ are the **curvatures** and **twist** of the deflected mid-surface.

## 2.3 Stress Field and Bending Moments

Using the generalized **Hooke's Law for plane stress** (valid because $\sigma_z = 0$), the in-plane stresses are:

$$
\sigma_x = -\frac{E \, z}{1 - \nu^2} \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right)
$$

$$
\sigma_y = -\frac{E \, z}{1 - \nu^2} \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \right)
$$

$$
\tau_{xy} = -E \, z \, \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \, \partial y}
$$

The **bending moments per unit length** are obtained by integrating the stress times the moment arm $z$ through the plate thickness $h$ (from $-h/2$ to $+h/2$):

$$
M_x = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \sigma_x \, z \, dz = -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right)
$$

$$
M_y = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \sigma_y \, z \, dz = -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \right)
$$

$$
M_{xy} = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \tau_{xy} \, z \, dz = -D(1 - \nu) \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \, \partial y}
$$

Where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate, given by:

$$
D = \frac{E \, h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The factor $\frac{1}{1 - \nu^2}$ in $D$ is what differentiates **plate bending** from **beam bending**. In a beam, the flexural rigidity is simply $\frac{EI}{1}$ (or $\frac{E h^3}{12}$ for a rectangular cross-section). In a plate, the **lateral contraction** of the material is restrained by the surrounding material, effectively **stiffening the plate**. The Poisson's ratio $\nu$ accounts for this 2D constraint, and the term $1 - \nu^2$ always **reduces** the effective compliance, increasing the apparent stiffness.

## 2.4 The Governing Plate Equation (Germain-Lagrange Equation)

The fundamental governing equation of thin plate theory is the **biharmonic equation**:

$$
D \nabla^4 w(x, y) = q(x, y)
$$

Where:
- $\nabla^4 = \frac{\partial^4}{\partial x^4} + 2\frac{\partial^4}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4}{\partial y^4}$ is the **biharmonic operator**.
- $q(x, y)$ is the **transverse load per unit area** (e.g., applied pressure in Pa for MEMS diaphragms).
- $D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity** (units: N·m).

This equation must be solved for $w(x, y)$ subject to appropriate **boundary conditions** at the edges of the plate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Once $w(x, y)$ is known, the **bending stresses** at the surface ($z = \pm h/2$) can be computed from the stress equations. The **maximum stress** (at the fixed edge for a clamped plate, or at the center for a simply supported plate) governs the **design limits** of the diaphragm. Exceeding the yield strength or fatigue limit of the material causes mechanical failure.

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions for Plates

The **order** of the plate equation is **4**, so **two boundary conditions** must be prescribed at each edge. The most common types in MEMS are:

| Edge Type | Deflection BC | Rotation/Slope BC | Physical Realization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clamped (Fixed, Built-in)** | $w = 0$ | $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial n} = 0$ | Diaphragm rigidly bonded to a silicon rim (anodically bonded to glass) |
| **Simply Supported** | $w = 0$ | $M_n = 0$ (zero moment) | Diaphragm resting on a support with no rotational restraint |
| **Free Edge** | $M_n = 0$ | $V_n = 0$ (zero shear) | Unanchored cantilevered edge (rare in MEMS diaphragms) |

Where $n$ is the outward normal to the edge, $M_n$ is the bending moment normal to the edge, and $V_n$ is the effective shear force (Kirchhoff shear) normal to the edge.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Plates in MEMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery Mandate:** Memorize these equations and their units. KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on this topic will require direct application of these formulas to **circular diaphragms** (most common in pressure sensors) and **square diaphragms** (common in micromachined accelerometers).

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity of the plate | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| 2 | $D \nabla^4 w = q$ | Germain-Lagrange plate equation | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 3 | $w_{max} = \dfrac{q a^4}{64 D}$ | Max deflection, clamped **circular** plate (radius $a$) | $\text{m}$ |
| 4 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3 q a^2}{4 h^2}$ | Max stress at clamped edge, circular plate | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 5 | $w_{max} = \dfrac{3 q a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E h^3}$ | Alternate form of clamped circular max deflection | $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $w_{max} = \dfrac{0.00126 \, q a^4}{D}$ | Max deflection, clamped **square** plate (side $a$) | $\text{m}$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{0.308 \, q a^2}{h^2}$ | Max stress at center, clamped square plate | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 8 | $w_{max} = \dfrac{4 q a^4}{64 D} = \dfrac{q a^4}{16 D}$ | Max deflection, simply supported **circular** plate | $\text{m}$ |
| 9 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3(3+\nu) q a^2}{8 h^2}$ | Max stress at center, SS circular plate | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel plate (used in capacitive plates) | $\text{F}$ |
| 11 | $f_{11} = \dfrac{10.21}{4 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho h a^4}}$ | Fundamental resonant frequency, clamped circular plate | $\text{Hz}$ |
| 12 | $\rho_{2D} = \rho \, h$ | Areal mass density of the plate | $\text{kg/m}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the formula $\vert w \vert$ is so important in MEMS:** The deflection of a plate is **directly proportional to the applied load** and the **fourth power** of its in-plane dimension. This fourth-power dependence means that a 2× increase in diaphragm size yields a **16× increase in deflection** for the same pressure. Designers exploit this to build **highly sensitive** pressure sensors, but must balance it against the risk of **large nonlinear deflections** (membrane stretching) when $w_{max} > 0.3 \, h$.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Derivation of the Germain-Lagrange Plate Equation

We will derive the governing plate equation from the equilibrium of an infinitesimal plate element subjected to a transverse load $q(x, y)$.

### Step 1: Consider an Infinitesimal Plate Element

Take a small rectangular element of the plate with dimensions $dx \times dy$ and thickness $h$. The element is acted upon by:
- Bending moments $M_x$ and $M_y$ per unit length on the faces perpendicular to the $x$ and $y$ axes.
- Twisting moments $M_{xy}$ and $M_{yx}$ on the same faces.
- Effective transverse shear forces $V_x$ and $V_y$ on these faces.
- Distributed transverse load $q(x, y)$ over the area $dx \, dy$.

### Step 2: Sum of Moments About the y-axis (to find $M_x$ variation)

The moment equilibrium about the y-axis for the element gives:

$$
M_x \, dy + \frac{\partial M_x}{\partial x} dx \, dy - M_x \, dy - M_{yx} \, dx + \left( M_{yx} + \frac{\partial M_{yx}}{\partial y} dy \right) dx - V_x \, dy \, dx + q \, dx \, dy \left( \frac{dx}{2} \right) = 0
$$

Neglecting the higher-order term $\frac{q \, dx^2 \, dy}{2}$ and simplifying:

$$
\frac{\partial M_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial M_{yx}}{\partial y} - V_x = 0
$$

By symmetry of the twisting moment ($M_{xy} = M_{yx}$):

$$
V_x = \frac{\partial M_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial M_{xy}}{\partial y}
$$

### Step 3: Sum of Moments About the x-axis

By an analogous procedure:

$$
V_y = \frac{\partial M_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial M_{xy}}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 4: Sum of Forces in the z-direction

Force equilibrium in the z-direction gives:

$$
\frac{\partial V_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial V_y}{\partial y} + q = 0
$$

### Step 5: Combine to Eliminate Shear Forces

Substituting the expressions for $V_x$ and $V_y$ into the force equilibrium equation:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 M_x}{\partial x^2} + 2 \frac{\partial^2 M_{xy}}{\partial x \partial y} + \frac{\partial^2 M_y}{\partial y^2} + q = 0
$$

### Step 6: Substitute the Constitutive Relations

Substitute the previously derived expressions for $M_x, M_y, M_{xy}$ in terms of $w$:

$$
M_x = -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right)
$$

$$
M_y = -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \right)
$$

$$
M_{xy} = -D(1 - \nu) \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}
$$

Plugging in and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2} \left[ -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right) \right] &+ 2 \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x \partial y} \left[ -D(1 - \nu) \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y} \right] \\
&+ \frac{\partial^2}{\partial y^2} \left[ -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \right) \right] + q = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Factoring out $-D$ and combining the terms, the equation reduces to:

$$
D \left( \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} + 2 \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial y^4} \right) = q(x, y)
$$

In compact notation, this is the **Germain-Lagrange Plate Equation**:

$$
\boxed{D \nabla^4 w(x, y) = q(x, y)}
$$

This is one of the most celebrated results in the mechanics of structures and the foundation of all MEMS diaphragm design.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Deflection of a Clamped Circular Plate Under Uniform Pressure

**Problem Setup:** A circular plate of radius $a$ and thickness $h$ is **clamped at its periphery** ($w = 0$, $\frac{dw}{dr} = 0$ at $r = a$) and subjected to a uniform transverse pressure $q$. Find the maximum deflection at the center.

**Step 1:** In polar coordinates, the biharmonic operator for axisymmetric loading reduces to:

$$
\nabla^4 w = \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\}
$$

**Step 2:** The governing equation becomes:

$$
\frac{D}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\} = q
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate once with respect to $r$ (multiplied by $r$):

$$
r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] = \frac{q r^2}{2D} + C_1
$$

**Step 4:** Use the **finiteness condition at the center** ($r = 0$): $r \frac{d}{dr} [\ldots]$ must vanish, so $C_1 = 0$.

**Step 5:** Integrate again:

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) = \frac{q r^2}{4D} + C_2
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by $r$ and integrate a third time:

$$
r \frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{q r^4}{16 D} + \frac{C_2 r^2}{2} + C_3
$$

For finiteness at the center, $C_3 = 0$.

**Step 7:** Divide by $r$ and integrate a fourth time:

$$
w(r) = \frac{q r^4}{64 D} + \frac{C_2 r^2}{4} + C_4
$$

**Step 8:** Apply the clamped boundary condition at $r = a$: $w(a) = 0$.

$$
\frac{q a^4}{64 D} + \frac{C_2 a^2}{4} + C_4 = 0
$$

**Step 9:** Apply the slope condition at $r = a$: $\frac{dw}{dr}\big\vert_{r=a} = 0$.

$$
\frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{q r^3}{16 D} + \frac{C_2 r}{2}
$$

$$
\frac{q a^3}{16 D} + \frac{C_2 a}{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{q a^2}{8 D}
$$

**Step 10:** Substitute $C_2$ back into the deflection expression:

$$
C_4 = -\frac{q a^4}{64 D} + \frac{q a^2}{4} \cdot \frac{q a^2}{8 D} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{?}} = -\frac{q a^4}{64 D} + \frac{q a^4}{32 D} = \frac{q a^4}{64 D}
$$

Wait — let me redo this carefully. From step 8:

$$
C_4 = -\frac{q a^4}{64 D} - \frac{C_2 a^2}{4} = -\frac{q a^4}{64 D} - \left(-\frac{q a^2}{8 D}\right) \frac{a^2}{4} = -\frac{q a^4}{64 D} + \frac{q a^4}{32 D} = \frac{q a^4}{64 D}
$$

**Step 11:** Final deflection profile:

$$
w(r) = \frac{q}{64 D} \left( a^4 - r^4 \right) - \frac{q a^2}{8 D} \left( \frac{a^2}{4} - \frac{r^2}{2} \right)
$$

Wait — let me correct this by direct substitution of $C_2$ and $C_4$:

$$
w(r) = \frac{q r^4}{64 D} - \frac{q a^2 r^2}{32 D} + \frac{q a^4}{64 D} = \frac{q}{64 D} (a^2 - r^2)^2
$$

**Step 12:** Maximum deflection occurs at the **center** ($r = 0$):

$$
\boxed{w_{max} = w(0) = \frac{q a^4}{64 D} = \frac{3 q a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E h^3}}
$$

This is the **fundamental result** for a clamped circular diaphragm and is the most-tested formula in KTU MEMS exams.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Plate Geometries (At a Glance)

This matrix is the **high-density revision tool** for KTU ESE. Use it to choose the right formula based on the geometry of the diaphragm given in the question.

| Geometry | Edge Condition | $w_{max}$ Formula | $w_{max}$ Location | $\sigma_{max}$ Formula | $\sigma_{max}$ Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Circular (radius $a$) | Clamped | $\dfrac{q a^4}{64 D}$ | Center ($r=0$) | $\dfrac{3 q a^2}{4 h^2}$ | Clamped edge ($r=a$) |
| Circular (radius $a$) | Simply Supported | $\dfrac{q a^4}{16 D}$ | Center ($r=0$) | $\dfrac{3(3+\nu) q a^2}{8 h^2}$ | Center ($r=0$) |
| Square (side $a$) | Clamped | $\dfrac{0.00126 \, q a^4}{D}$ | Center | $\dfrac{0.308 \, q a^2}{h^2}$ | Midpoint of clamped edge |
| Square (side $a$) | Simply Supported | $\dfrac{0.00406 \, q a^4}{D}$ | Center | $\dfrac{0.5 \, q a^2}{h^2}$ | Center |
| Rectangular ($a \times b$) | Clamped | Use **Navier** or **Levy** solution (series) | Center | Series expansion | Edges/Center |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always **identify the geometry and edge condition first**, then look up the corresponding formula. Do not mix up **$w_{max}$ location** and **$\sigma_{max}$ location** — for a clamped circular plate, the maximum *deflection* is at the center, but the maximum *stress* is at the clamped edge. This is a classic **2-mark error** in KTU valuation.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: MEMS Plate Diaphragm Transduction Pipeline

The following mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal-flow pipeline from physical input (pressure/force/acoustic wave) to electrical output in a generic MEMS plate-based sensor:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHYSICAL INPUT<br/>Pressure / Acoustic Wave / Inertial Force] --> B[PLATE DIAPHRAGM<br/>Si, PolySi, Si3N4, SiO2]
    B --> C[DEFLECTION w r theta t<br/>Computed via Plate Equation]
    C --> D{MEASUREMENT PRINCIPLE}
    D --> E1[PIEZORESISTIVE READOUT<br/>Bridge Network on Diaphragm]
    D --> E2[CAPACITIVE READOUT<br/>Parasitic Capacitance Change]
    D --> E3[OPTICAL READOUT<br/>Interferometric or Diffractive]
    D --> E4[PIEZOELECTRIC READOUT<br/>Charge Generation in ZnO or AlN]
    E1 --> F[ANALOG SIGNAL CONDITIONING<br/>Amplifier, Filter, ADC]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[DIGITAL OUTPUT<br/>Calibrated Pressure / Acceleration / Sound Level]
```

## 4.2 Plate Geometry and Coordinate Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CIRC[ Circular Diaphragm ]
        C1[Radius a, Thickness h]
        C2[Clamped edge: w=0, dw/dr=0 at r=a]
        C3[wmax at r=0]
        C4[Sigma max at r=a]
    end
    subgraph SQ[ Square Diaphragm ]
        S1[Side a, Thickness h]
        S2[Clamped edges: w=0, grad w=0]
        S3[wmax at center x=0, y=0]
        S4[Sigma max at edge midpoint]
    end
    subgraph RECT[ Rectangular Diaphragm ]
        R1[Sides a by b, Thickness h]
        R2[Navier or Levy solution]
        R3[Series form with sine cosine terms]
        R4[Edges clamped or simply supported]
    end
    CIRC --> USE1[Used in: Pressure Sensors, Microphones]
    SQ --> USE2[Used in: Accelerometers, Microvalves]
    RECT --> USE3[Used in: Specialty Sensors, RF MEMS]
```

## 4.3 Plate Theory Hierarchy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Plate Problem] --> T{Thickness Ratio h/a}
    T -->|h/a less than 1 over 100| M1[MEMBRANE THEORY<br/>No bending, pure in-plane tension]
    T -->|1 over 100 to 1 over 20| M2[CLASSICAL PLATE THEORY<br/>KLPT, 4th order biharmonic]
    T -->|1 over 20 to 1 over 10| M3[MINDLIN PLATE THEORY<br/>Includes transverse shear]
    T -->|h/a greater than 1 over 10| M4[3D ELASTICITY<br/>Full 3D governing equations]
    M1 --> APP1[Most MEMS RF switches, suspended membranes]
    M2 --> APP2[Most MEMS pressure sensors and microphones]
    M3 --> APP3[Thick diaphragms, accelerometer proof masses]
    M4 --> APP4[Rare in MEMS, used in validation only]
```

## 4.4 Deflection Profile of a Clamped Circular Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROFILE[Deflection w r Profile for Clamped Circular Plate]
        P0[At r=0, w=wmax = qa4 over 64D]
        P1[At r=a/2, w reduces to about 80 percent of wmax]
        P2[At r=0.7a, w reduces to about 50 percent of wmax]
        P3[At r=a, w=0 clamped boundary]
    end
    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation:** The deflection $w$ of a uniformly loaded clamped circular plate varies as $(a^2 - r^2)^2$. This is a **parabolic-squared** profile, meaning the deflection is **flatter** near the center and **drops rapidly** near the clamped edge. This is why piezoresistors placed near the **edge** of a pressure-sensor diaphragm (where strain is highest) give the **maximum sensitivity**.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define flexural rigidity of a plate. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Model Answer:**

The **flexural rigidity** of a plate, denoted by $D$, is a measure of the plate's resistance to bending deformation. It is defined as the product of the **Young's modulus** $E$ and the **second moment of area per unit width** of the plate cross-section, scaled by the **plane-stress factor** $\frac{1}{1-\nu^2}$ to account for the 2D lateral constraint of the surrounding material.

$$
D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}
$$

Where $E$ is Young's modulus (Pa), $h$ is the plate thickness (m), and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio (dimensionless). The units of $D$ are **N·m** (Newton-meters). For MEMS diaphragms, typical values of $D$ range from $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ N·m, depending on the material and thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (3 marks):** Full marks require **(i) the formula, (ii) each variable defined, (iii) the units, and (iv) a brief physical interpretation** of why the $1-\nu^2$ term appears. Losing any one of these typically costs 1 mark.

### Question 2: Differentiate between a plate and a beam. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Beam | Plate |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | 1D (length >> cross-section) | 2D (length, width >> thickness) |
| Loading direction | Transverse to length | Transverse to mid-surface |
| Curvature | Single curvature (one axis) | Double curvature (two axes) |
| Bending | One direction | Two orthogonal directions (coupled) |
| Equation | $EI \dfrac{d^4 w}{dx^4} = q$ | $D \nabla^4 w = q$ |
| Flexural rigidity | $EI$ | $D = \dfrac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ |
| Poisson's effect | Not captured in 1D Euler-Bernoulli | Captured by $(1-\nu^2)$ factor |

A **beam** carries load primarily through bending in one plane, while a **plate** carries load through bending in **two orthogonal directions**, with the load-sharing between the two directions governed by the plate equation.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Rule:** Within each 14-mark question, students must answer **either** OR (the two sub-parts are independent). Choice is given at the sub-part level for many questions. Below, we provide a complete Question A and Question B set.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the governing differential equation for the bending of a thin elastic plate under transverse load, clearly stating the **Kirchhoff-Love assumptions**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A circular silicon diaphragm of radius $a = 500 \, \mu\text{m}$ and thickness $h = 20 \, \mu\text{m}$ is rigidly clamped at its periphery. The diaphragm is subjected to a uniform pressure of $q = 10 \, \text{kPa}$. For silicon, $E = 170 \, \text{GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.28$. Calculate the **maximum deflection** and the **maximum bending stress** in the diaphragm. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** State the four Kirchhoff-Love assumptions (1 mark):

1. Straight normals remain straight, long, and normal to the mid-surface after deformation.
2. Transverse normal strain $\varepsilon_z = 0$.
3. Transverse normal stress $\sigma_z = 0$.
4. Mid-surface is inextensible (no in-plane displacement at $z = 0$).

**Step 2:** Write the displacement field (1 mark):

$$
u = -z \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}, \quad v = -z \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}, \quad w = w(x, y)
$$

**Step 3:** Derive the strain field by differentiating (1 mark):

$$
\varepsilon_x = -z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}, \quad \varepsilon_y = -z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = -2z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply plane-stress Hooke's Law to get stresses (1 mark):

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} (\varepsilon_x + \nu \varepsilon_y), \quad \sigma_y = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} (\varepsilon_y + \nu \varepsilon_x), \quad \tau_{xy} = G \gamma_{xy}
$$

**Step 5:** Define bending moments per unit length (1 mark):

$$
M_x = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_x z \, dz, \quad M_y = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_y z \, dz, \quad M_{xy} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \tau_{xy} z \, dz
$$

This yields:

$$
M_x = -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right), \quad M_y = -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \right)
$$

where $D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$.

**Step 6:** Apply equilibrium of an infinitesimal plate element (forces and moments) to derive (1 mark):

$$
\frac{\partial^2 M_x}{\partial x^2} + 2 \frac{\partial^2 M_{xy}}{\partial x \partial y} + \frac{\partial^2 M_y}{\partial y^2} + q = 0
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute moment-curvature relations to get the final equation (1 mark):

$$
\boxed{D \nabla^4 w = q(x, y)}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Compute the flexural rigidity $D$ (1 mark):

$$
D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} = \frac{(170 \times 10^9) \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12 \times (1 - 0.28^2)}
$$

$$
(20 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 8 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
1 - 0.28^2 = 1 - 0.0784 = 0.9216
$$

$$
D = \frac{170 \times 10^9 \times 8 \times 10^{-15}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{1.36 \times 10^{-3}}{11.0592} = 1.2297 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the maximum deflection at the center (2 marks):

$$
w_{max} = \frac{q a^4}{64 D} = \frac{(10 \times 10^3) \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^4}{64 \times 1.2297 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
a^4 = (500 \times 10^{-6})^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^4
$$

$$
w_{max} = \frac{10^4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14}}{64 \times 1.2297 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-10}}{7.870 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.94 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m} \approx 79.4 \, \text{nm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the maximum bending stress at the clamped edge (2 marks):

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 q a^2}{4 h^2} = \frac{3 \times 10^4 \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4 \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^2}
$$

$$
a^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^2, \quad h^2 = 4 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \times 10^4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 4 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.6875 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} \approx 4.69 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Validate the small-deflection assumption (1 mark):

$$
\frac{w_{max}}{h} = \frac{79.4 \times 10^{-9}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.97 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.4\%
$$

Since $w_{max} \ll h$, the **small-deflection linear theory** is valid.

**Final Answers:**

$$
w_{max} \approx 79.4 \, \text{nm}, \quad \sigma_{max} \approx 4.69 \, \text{MPa}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **boundary conditions** for (i) clamped, (ii) simply supported, and (iii) free edges of a thin plate. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A square silicon diaphragm of side $a = 1 \, \text{mm}$ and thickness $h = 10 \, \mu\text{m}$ is clamped on all four edges and subjected to a uniform pressure of $q = 5 \, \text{kPa}$. Calculate the **maximum deflection** and **maximum stress**. Use $E = 170 \, \text{GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.28$. Compare your result with that of a circular diaphragm of the same area and thickness. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Explain the role of boundary conditions in plate theory (1 mark).

Plate theory is a **4th-order** PDE ($D \nabla^4 w = q$). Thus, **two** boundary conditions are needed at each edge (versus only two for the entire beam problem). These conditions constrain the **deflection** and the **rotation** (or moment) at the edge.

**Step 2:** Describe each boundary condition (6 marks — 2 each):

**(i) Clamped (Fixed) Edge:**
- Deflection condition: $w = 0$ at the edge.
- Slope condition: $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial n} = 0$ (no rotation).
- Physical meaning: The edge is built into a rigid wall, preventing both translation and rotation. This is the **most common** boundary condition in MEMS diaphragms (silicon rim anchors the plate).

**(ii) Simply Supported Edge:**
- Deflection condition: $w = 0$ at the edge.
- Moment condition: $M_n = 0$ at the edge (zero bending moment; the edge can rotate freely).
- For example, on the edge $x = 0$: $w = 0$ and $M_x = -D \left( \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right) = 0$.

**(iii) Free Edge:**
- Moment condition: $M_n = 0$ at the edge.
- Effective shear condition: $V_n = Q_n + \dfrac{\partial M_{ns}}{\partial s} = 0$, where $Q_n$ is the transverse shear force and $M_{ns}$ is the twisting moment.
- Physical meaning: No constraints on either deflection or rotation (e.g., a cantilever plate's free end).

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**
```
   Clamped (CC)        Simply Supported (SS)      Free (F)
   |=========|          |---------|                 o-----o
   w=0, dw/dn=0         w=0, M_n=0                 M_n=0, V_n=0
   (rigid wall)         (knife-edge support)        (no support)
```

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Calculate flexural rigidity (1 mark):

$$
D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} = \frac{170 \times 10^9 \times (10 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{170 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-15}}{11.0592} = 1.537 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 2:** Maximum deflection of clamped **square** plate (1.5 marks):

$$
w_{max,sq} = \frac{0.00126 \, q a^4}{D} = \frac{0.00126 \times 5000 \times (10^{-3})^4}{1.537 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
a^4 = 10^{-12} \, \text{m}^4
$$

$$
w_{max,sq} = \frac{0.00126 \times 5000 \times 10^{-12}}{1.537 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{6.3 \times 10^{-12}}{1.537 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.10 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} = 410 \, \text{nm}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum stress of clamped square plate (1.5 marks):

$$
\sigma_{max,sq} = \frac{0.308 \, q a^2}{h^2} = \frac{0.308 \times 5000 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1.54 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-10}} = 1.54 \times 10^7 \, \text{Pa} = 15.4 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with a circular plate of **same area** (1.5 marks):

For the same area: $\pi a_c^2 = a_{sq}^2 = 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2$.

$$
a_c = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-6}}{\pi}} = 5.64 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 564 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

For the **circular** clamped plate:

$$
w_{max,c} = \frac{q a_c^4}{64 D} = \frac{5000 \times (5.64 \times 10^{-4})^4}{64 \times 1.537 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
a_c^4 = (5.64 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 1.012 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{m}^4
$$

$$
w_{max,c} = \frac{5000 \times 1.012 \times 10^{-13}}{64 \times 1.537 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{5.06 \times 10^{-10}}{9.84 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.14 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} = 514 \, \text{nm}
$$

**Step 5:** Comparison and comment (1.5 marks):

| Quantity | Square Plate | Circular Plate |
|---|---|---|
| Side/Radius | $a_{sq} = 1 \, \text{mm}$ | $a_c = 0.564 \, \text{mm}$ |
| $w_{max}$ | $410 \, \text{nm}$ | $514 \, \text{nm}$ |
| $\sigma_{max}$ | $15.4 \, \text{MPa}$ | $\dfrac{3 \times 5000 \times (5.64\times 10^{-4})^2}{4 \times 10^{-10}} = 11.9 \, \text{MPa}$ |

**Comment:** For the **same cross-sectional area**, a circular diaphragm gives **higher deflection** but **lower stress** than a square diaphragm. The square plate has **stress concentrations at the midpoint of each clamped edge** (corners are even more stressed), making it more prone to fracture. Circular diaphragms are therefore preferred in **high-reliability** MEMS pressure sensors.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1: Mixing up locations of $w_{max}$ and $\sigma_{max}$.** For a *clamped* circular plate, $w_{max}$ is at the **center** ($r = 0$) but $\sigma_{max}$ is at the **clamped edge** ($r = a$). Many students write both as occurring at the center. **Cost: 1 to 2 marks lost per question.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2: Forgetting the $(1 - \nu^2)$ factor in $D$.** Students often write $D = \frac{Eh^3}{12}$ (the beam formula) instead of $D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$. This gives a **deflection that is too small by a factor of 8%** (when $\nu = 0.28$). In numerical problems, this is worth **1 mark**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3: Using the wrong formula for a square plate.** The numerical coefficients (0.00126 for $w_{max}$, 0.308 for $\sigma_{max}$) are **not** the same as for a circular plate. Memorizing these coefficients incorrectly is a **fatal error** in 7-mark numerical problems. **Always state which geometry and boundary condition you are using.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4: Failing to validate the small-deflection assumption.** A $w_{max} > 0.3 h$ indicates **membrane stretching** dominates and the linear theory breaks down. The full von Karman nonlinear plate theory must then be used. The examiner expects you to **comment on this** in long-answer questions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam!**

- [ ] **Plate vs Beam:** A plate bends in **two** directions; a beam bends in **one**. The flexural rigidity of a plate is $D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$; for a beam, it is $EI = \frac{Eh^3}{12}$.
- [ ] **KLPT Assumptions:** Four assumptions — (1) normals stay normal, (2) $\varepsilon_z = 0$, (3) $\sigma_z = 0$, (4) inextensible mid-plane.
- [ ] **Governing Equation:** $D \nabla^4 w = q$ (Germain-Lagrange). This is a **4th-order** PDE; therefore, **two** BCs are needed at each edge.
- [ ] **Clamped Circular Plate (MOST TESTED):**
  - $w_{max} = \frac{qa^4}{64D}$ at $r = 0$.
  - $\sigma_{max} = \frac{3qa^2}{4h^2}$ at $r = a$.
- [ ] **Simply Supported Circular Plate:**
  - $w_{max} = \frac{qa^4}{16D}$ at $r = 0$.
  - $\sigma_{max} = \frac{3(3+\nu)qa^2}{8h^2}$ at $r = 0$.
- [ ] **Clamped Square Plate:** $w_{max} = \frac{0.00126 \, qa^4}{D}$, $\sigma_{max} = \frac{0.308 \, qa^2}{h^2}$.
- [ ] **Boundary Conditions:** Clamped = ($w=0, \partial w/\partial n = 0$). SS = ($w = 0, M_n = 0$). Free = ($M_n = 0, V_n = 0$).
- [ ] **MEMS Applications:** Pressure sensors, microphones, accelerometers, RF MEMS switches, micromirrors — all use **plate/diaphragm mechanics** as their transduction core.
- [ ] **Small-Deflection Check:** If $w_{max} > 0.3 h$, the linear theory is invalid; use **von Karman nonlinear plate theory**.
- [ ] **Common Materials:** Si (single crystal), PolySi, Si₃N₄, SiO₂. Typical dimensions: thickness 1–50 µm, lateral 100 µm – 5 mm.
- [ ] **Units Check (KTU favorite trap):** $D$ in **N·m**, $w$ in **m**, $q$ in **Pa (N/m²)**, $\sigma$ in **Pa**. Always **convert µm to m** before plugging in.
- [ ] **Resonance Frequency (for dynamic MEMS):** $f_{11} = \frac{10.21}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho h a^4}}$ for the fundamental mode of a clamped circular plate.
- [ ] **Capacitive Readout (bonus):** $\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - w} - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 A w}{d^2}$ for small $w$. This links **plate mechanics** to **electrical readout** — a favorite KTU 14-mark question topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Scheme Students:** The "Plates" topic in **OEAET722 MEMS, Module 2** is the **analytical foundation** for nearly every subsequent module (pressure sensors, accelerometers, microphones, and even RF MEMS). Mastery of the clamped circular plate formulas ($\frac{qa^4}{64D}$ and $\frac{3qa^2}{4h^2}$) is **non-negotiable** for scoring above 80% in the ESE. Always **draw a free-body diagram** of the diaphragm, **label the boundary conditions**, and **check units** before submitting your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diaphragms - Typical Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diaphragms - Typical Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Microelectromechanical Systems (MEMS)**, a **diaphragm** is a thin, planar, mechanically compliant membrane — typically of micrometer-scale thickness and millimeter or sub-millimeter lateral dimensions — that is anchored at its periphery to a rigid supporting structure (the substrate or a bossed frame). When subjected to a differential pressure, an acoustic pressure wave, a fluidic load, or an inertial force, the diaphragm undergoes elastic out-of-plane deflection. This deflection is then transduced into an electrical signal through one of several mechanisms: **piezoresistive**, **capacitive**, **piezoelectric**, **optical**, or **resonant frequency shift**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> A diaphragm is the *most fundamental and reusable mechanical building block* in MEMS sensor and actuator design. The same physical structure, when paired with different transduction principles, can realize a pressure sensor, a microphone, a micropump, or a microvalve. Mastery of diaphragm mechanics is therefore considered a **high-weightage topic** in KTU university examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Drum Head" Model

Imagine the circular skin stretched across the top of a tabla or a bongo drum. When you press down on the skin, it bends inward without tearing; when you speak into it, it vibrates in response to the sound pressure. A MEMS diaphragm behaves **identically**, but at a scale roughly **1000× smaller** and made of silicon instead of animal hide.

- **Drum frame (rigid mount)** ↔ **Silicon substrate / bossed rim**
- **Drum skin (flexible membrane)** ↔ **Thin Si / Si₃N₄ / Polysilicon film**
- **Air column beneath** ↔ **Sealed reference cavity (vacuum or back-side etch pit)**
- **Your finger pressure** ↔ **Process pressure, acoustic wave, or inertial load**

The genius of MEMS lies in the fact that this single mechanical "drum-skin" element can be wired to **four piezoresistors** (yielding a pressure sensor), to **two capacitor plates** (yielding a microphone), or to an **electrostatic actuator** (yielding a microvalve).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used in Diaphragm Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants are used universally across all diaphragm derivations in the KTU syllabus. Memorize them.

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's modulus of Silicon (⟨100⟩) | $E_{Si}$ | **170 GPa** | N/m² |
| Young's modulus of Si₃N₄ | $E_{SiN}$ | **310 GPa** | N/m² |
| Young's modulus of Polysilicon | $E_{poly}$ | **160 GPa** | N/m² |
| Poisson's ratio of Silicon | $\nu$ | **0.28** | dimensionless |
| Density of Silicon | $\rho_{Si}$ | **2330 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | Pa |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |

## 1.4 Primary Diaphragm Geometries in MEMS

A KTU examiner will almost certainly expect you to recognize and contrast the three dominant planar geometries:

1. **Circular Diaphragm** — The most analytically tractable geometry; used in absolute pressure sensors and microphones. Exhibits **rotational symmetry**.
2. **Square Diaphragm** — Easier to fabricate using anisotropic KOH/TMAH wet etching along ⟨100⟩ silicon planes; common in commercial piezoresistive pressure sensors (e.g., Motorola MPX series).
3. **Rectangular Diaphragm** — Used in flow sensors, microfluidic channels, and some microphone arrays where elongated form factors are required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Physics — How a Diaphragm "Works"

The diaphragm operates on the classical **thin-plate theory** of Kirchhoff-Love. The governing assumptions for the standard KTU analysis are:

- **Small-deflection regime:** Maximum deflection $w_{max} \leq 0.3 \times \text{thickness} (t)$. Beyond this, nonlinear (membrane) theory applies.
- **Negligible in-plane (membrane) stress** prior to deflection.
- **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the mid-surface** (Kirchhoff hypothesis).
- **Boundary condition: clamped (built-in) edge** — the most common assumption for MEMS diaphragms bonded to a rigid rim.

The core equation governing the static equilibrium of a thin plate under a uniform transverse pressure $P$ is:

$$\boxed{D \nabla^4 w(x,y) = P}$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity** (also called bending stiffness) of the diaphragm, defined as:

$$D = \frac{E \, t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of D:** It quantifies the resistance of the plate to bending. Note that $D \propto t^3$ — the diaphragm thickness is the **most powerful design lever**. Halving the thickness reduces the bending stiffness by a factor of **8**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Parameter

- **Why thickness $t$ appears cubed ($t^3$) in deflection:** A thin plate bends by *stretching* its outer fibers. The bending moment is proportional to the *second moment of area*, which scales as the cube of the thickness. This is the same reason why a sheet of paper can be bent easily but a rigid cardboard cannot.
- **Why Poisson's ratio $\nu$ appears in the denominator:** When the diaphragm bends, the central fibers try to contract laterally. The clamped edges prevent this contraction, generating an **in-plane constraint stress** that *stiffens* the diaphragm. The factor $(1-\nu^2)$ in the denominator accounts for this stiffening effect.
- **Why we use a reference vacuum cavity:** The bottom electrode in a capacitive sensor is etched from the back of the wafer and sealed. This provides a **stable reference pressure** so that the deflection is purely a function of the *applied* pressure, not of ambient drift.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** you need for a KTU university exam on diaphragms. Memorize these for a guaranteed 14 marks.

| # | Parameter | Circular Diaphragm (radius $a$) | Square Diaphragm (side $a$) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flexural Rigidity | $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | N·m |
| 2 | Max Deflection (center) | $w_{max} = \dfrac{P \, a^4}{64 \, D}$ | $w_{max} = \dfrac{0.00126 \, P \, a^4}{D}$ | m |
| 3 | Max Stress (center) | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3 P \, a^2}{4 \, t^2}$ | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{0.308 \, P \, a^2}{t^2}$ | Pa |
| 4 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g_0 - w(x,y)}$ | (same form) | F |
| 5 | Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{10.21}{2\pi a^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho h}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{35.99}{2\pi a^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho h}}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Capacitance Change (small signal) | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{w_{max}}{g_0}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{w_{max}}{g_0}$ | — |
| 7 | Piezoresistive Output | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_{44} \, \sigma_{l} \dfrac{1}{2}$ | (same form) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition used in all KTU derivations:** *Clamped (built-in) edges* — i.e., the diaphragm edge is rigidly fixed into the silicon frame. The clamped condition means: deflection $w = 0$ and slope $\partial w / \partial n = 0$ at the edge.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The diaphragm is the *workhorse* of the commercial MEMS industry. **Over 80% of all MEMS devices shipped globally** use a diaphragm as their primary mechanical transducer. The most famous application is the **piezoresistive pressure sensor** used in:
- Automotive manifold absolute pressure (MAP) sensors
- Medical blood-pressure transducers
- Altimeters in smartphones and drones
- Industrial process control
- Tire-pressure monitoring systems (TPMS)
- Barometric altimeters in wearables
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Deflection of a Clamped Circular Diaphragm

**Problem Statement:** A circular Si diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $t$ is rigidly clamped at its periphery and subjected to a uniform transverse pressure $P$. Derive the expression for the maximum (center) deflection.

### Step 1 — State the governing plate equation

The Kirchhoff plate equation for a uniformly loaded, axially symmetric, thin clamped plate in cylindrical polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\} = \frac{P}{D}$$

### Step 2 — Integrate once

Integrate both sides with respect to $r$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) = \frac{P \, r^2}{4D} + C_1$$

### Step 3 — Apply finite-deflection condition at center

By symmetry, the slope at the center ($r = 0$) must be zero: $\left. \dfrac{dw}{dr} \right|_{r=0} = 0$. This forces the integration constant $C_1 = 0$ (since the logarithmic term that would otherwise appear is singular at $r=0$).

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) = \frac{P \, r^2}{4D}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate twice more

Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate:

$$r \frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{P \, r^4}{16 D} + C_2$$

$$\frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{P \, r^3}{16 D} + \frac{C_2}{r}$$

### Step 5 — Apply clamped-edge boundary condition at $r = a$

The deflection is zero at the rim: $w(a) = 0$. Integrating $\dfrac{dw}{dr}$ from $0$ to $a$:

$$w(r) = \frac{P \, r^4}{64 D} + C_2 \ln(r) + C_3$$

To avoid logarithmic singularity at $r = 0$, we must have $C_2 = 0$. Then:

$$w(r) = \frac{P \, r^4}{64 D} + C_3$$

Applying $w(a) = 0 \Rightarrow C_3 = -\dfrac{P a^4}{64 D}$.

### Step 6 — Final deflection profile

$$\boxed{w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D} \left( a^4 - r^4 \right)}$$

### Step 7 — Maximum deflection (at center, $r = 0$)

$$\boxed{w_{max} = w(0) = \frac{P \, a^4}{64 D} = \frac{3 P \, a^4}{16 E \, t^3} (1 - \nu^2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important result** in Module 2. The second equality is obtained by substituting the explicit form of $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Bending Stress in a Circular Diaphragm

**Problem Statement:** Derive the maximum radial stress at the center of a clamped circular diaphragm.

### Step 1 — Bending moment relation

For an axially symmetric plate, the radial bending moment per unit length is:

$$M_r = -D \left( \frac{d^2 w}{dr^2} + \frac{\nu}{r} \frac{dw}{dr} \right)$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate the deflection profile

From Step 6 above, $w(r) = \dfrac{P}{64D}(a^4 - r^4)$.

$$\frac{dw}{dr} = -\frac{P \, r^3}{16 D}$$

$$\frac{d^2 w}{dr^2} = -\frac{3 P \, r^2}{16 D}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the bending moment expression

$$M_r = -D \left[ -\frac{3 P r^2}{16 D} + \frac{\nu}{r} \left( -\frac{P r^3}{16 D} \right) \right]$$

$$M_r = \frac{3 P r^2}{16} - \frac{\nu P r^2}{16} = \frac{P r^2 (3 - \nu)}{16}$$

### Step 4 — Convert bending moment to bending stress

The maximum bending stress occurs at the outer surface of the diaphragm (at $z = \pm t/2$):

$$\sigma_r = \frac{6 M_r}{t^2} = \frac{6 P r^2 (3 - \nu)}{16 t^2} = \frac{3 P r^2 (3 - \nu)}{8 t^2}$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate at the center ($r = 0$)

At $r = 0$, the bending stress vanishes — which is the classical result. The **maximum** bending stress actually occurs at the **clamped edge** ($r = a$):

$$\boxed{\sigma_{r,\max} = \frac{3 P a^2 (3 - \nu)}{8 t^2} \approx \frac{3 P a^2}{4 t^2}}$$

where the approximation uses $\nu \approx 1/3$ (close to silicon's 0.28), giving $(3 - 1/3)/2 = 4/3$, and the factor of $3/8$ becomes $1/2 \times 3/4 = $ approximately the simplified form.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacitive Readout Equation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the capacitance change for a circular diaphragm used as a parallel-plate capacitive sensor with a fixed bottom electrode at gap $g_0$.

### Step 1 — Initial (un-deflected) capacitance

The undeflected diaphragm is parallel to the bottom electrode at gap $g_0$:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \pi a^2}{g_0}$$

### Step 2 — Deflected capacitance

When the diaphragm deflects by $w(r)$, the local gap becomes $g(r) = g_0 - w(r)$, so:

$$C = \int_0^a \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, 2\pi r \, dr}{g_0 - w(r)}$$

### Step 3 — Small-signal linearization

For $w(r) \ll g_0$, we use the Taylor expansion $\dfrac{1}{g_0 - w} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_0}\left(1 + \dfrac{w}{g_0}\right)$:

$$C \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \pi a^2}{g_0} + \frac{\varepsilon_0}{g_0^2} \int_0^a w(r) \, 2\pi r \, dr$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral of $w(r)$

Substitute $w(r) = \dfrac{P}{64D}(a^4 - r^4)$:

$$\int_0^a w(r) \, 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2\pi P}{64 D} \int_0^a (a^4 r - r^5) dr$$

$$= \frac{2\pi P}{64 D} \left[ \frac{a^6}{2} - \frac{a^6}{6} \right] = \frac{2\pi P}{64 D} \cdot \frac{a^6}{3} = \frac{\pi P a^6}{96 D}$$

### Step 5 — Resulting fractional capacitance change

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{P a^4}{96 D \, g_0} = \frac{3 P a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E t^3 g_0}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **operational equation** for a capacitive MEMS pressure sensor. The fractional capacitance change is directly proportional to the applied pressure $P$ — which is why the device functions as a linear sensor.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resonant Frequency of a Circular Diaphragm

The fundamental resonant frequency of a clamped circular plate is given by the eigenfrequency of the (0,0) mode:

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{10.21}{2\pi a^2} \sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho \, t}} = \frac{10.21}{2\pi a^2} \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{12 \rho (1 - \nu^2)}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is critical for **resonant-mode sensors** (e.g., resonant pressure sensors, gravimetric chemical sensors). A shift in $f_0$ indicates a change in mass loading or stiffness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — A MEMS Capacitive Pressure Sensor

The following diagram maps the functional flow of a typical capacitive pressure-sensor system, with the diaphragm as the central element:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Pressure P applied] --> B[Thin Si Diaphragm]
    B --> C{Mechanical Deflection w r}
    C --> D[Gap g0 decreases locally]
    D --> E[Capacitance C changes]
    E --> F[Charge Amplifier C-V Converter]
    F --> G[ADC and Signal Conditioning]
    G --> H[Digital Pressure Output]
    B --> I[Reference Vacuum Sealed Cavity]
    I --> D
    H --> J[Display / Microcontroller / CAN bus]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Architecture — Piezoresistive vs. Capacitive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PZR["Piezoresistive Sensor Cross-Section"]
        P1[Si Substrate] --> P2[Bossed Rim]
        P2 --> P3[Thin Diaphragm t]
        P3 --> P4[Four Piezoresistors on top]
        P4 --> P5[Wheatstone Bridge Output]
    end
    subgraph CAP["Capacitive Sensor Cross-Section"]
        C1[Si Substrate] --> C2[Back-side Etched Cavity]
        C2 --> C3[Fixed Bottom Electrode]
        C3 --> C4[Thin Diaphragm = Top Electrode]
        C4 --> C5[Capacitance Output]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional State Machine — Diaphragm Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StaticMode
    StaticMode --> QuasiStatic: Constant P
    StaticMode --> DynamicMode: Time varying P
    QuasiStatic --> CapacitiveReadout: gap change detected
    QuasiStatic --> PiezoresistiveReadout: strain change detected
    DynamicMode --> ResonantReadout: frequency shift detected
    DynamicMode --> AcousticReadout: acoustic wave coupled
    CapacitiveReadout --> [*]
    PiezoresistiveReadout --> [*]
    ResonantReadout --> [*]
    AcousticReadout --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Application Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((MEMS Diaphragm))
    Pressure Sensor
      Automotive MAP
      Medical BP
      Altimeter
      TPMS
    Microphone
      MEMS Mic
      Hearing Aid
      Smart Speaker
    Micro Speaker
      In ear Monitor
      Haptic Feedback
    Microfluidics
      Micropump
      Microvalve
      Inkjet Head
    Accelerometer
      Inertial Mass on Diaphragm
      Crash Sensor
    Flow Sensor
      Anemometer
```
