**Problem:** A micro-cantilever beam of length $L = 100$ µm, width $w = 10$ µm, and thickness $t = 2$ µm is made of silicon ($\rho = 2330$ kg/m³). Calculate the scaling ratio of its weight compared to a macro-cantilever of identical shape but $L = 1$ m.

**Solution:**

The ratio of weights equals the ratio of volumes (since density is constant):

$$\frac{F_{g,\text{micro}}}{F_{g,\text{macro}}} = \left(\frac{L_{\text{micro}}}{L_{\text{macro}}}\right)^3$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{F_{g,\text{micro}}}{F_{g,\text{macro}}} = \left(\frac{10^{-4} \text{ m}}{10^{0} \text{ m}}\right)^3 = (10^{-4})^3 = 10^{-12}$$

The micro-cantilever's weight is **one trillionth** of the macro-cantilever's weight — confirming the $s_V = 3$ exponent and demonstrating why gravity is irrelevant in MEMS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Force Scaling Vector Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trimmer Force Scaling Vector]:::root --> B[Volume Forces]:::vol
    A --> C[Surface Forces]:::surf
    A --> D[Field Forces]:::field
    A --> E[Elastic Forces]:::elastic

    B --> B1[Gravity: s = 3]
    B --> B2[Inertial: s = 3]

    C --> C1[Surface Tension: s = 1]
    C --> C2[Capillary: s = 1]
    C --> C3[Van der Waals: s = 2]

    D --> D1[Electrostatic: s = 2]
    D --> D2[Magnetic: s = 1 to 4]
    D --> D3[Piezoelectric: s = 2]

    E --> E1[Axial Spring: s = 1]
    E --> E2[Bending Beam: s = 2]
    E --> E3[Torsional Bar: s = 4]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef vol fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef surf fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef field fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef elastic fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Scaling Dominance Regime Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Characteristic Length L]:::axis --> B[L greater than 1 m]:::macro
    A --> C[L approx 1 mm]:::meso
    A --> D[L approx 100 micrometer]:::micro
    A --> E[L less than 1 micrometer]:::nano

    B --> B1[DOMINANT: Gravity, Inertia]
    C --> C1[TRANSITION REGION]
    D --> D1[DOMINANT: Electrostatic, Surface Tension]
    E --> E1[DOMINANT: Van der Waals, Quantum Effects]

    classDef axis fill:#212121,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef macro fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef meso fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef micro fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef nano fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Scaling Vector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Input: System Geometry L]:::io --> classifier{Force Classifier}:::proc
    classifier --> grav[Volume Force Calculator]:::vol
    classifier --> surf[Surface Force Calculator]:::surf
    classifier --> field[Field Force Calculator]:::field
    classifier --> elas[Elastic Force Calculator]:::elas

    grav --> g1[Apply L cubed scaling]
    surf --> s1[Apply L to the 1st or L squared scaling]
    field --> f1[Apply L squared or L to the 4th]
    elas --> e1[Apply L to the 1st or L squared]

    g1 --> comparator[Scaling Vector Comparator]:::comp
    s1 --> comparator
    f1 --> comparator
    e1 --> comparator

    comparator --> output[Dominant Force Identification]:::io

    classDef io fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef proc fill:#455a64,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef vol fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef surf fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef field fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef elas fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef comp fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cantilever Beam Under Multiple Forces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    beam[Micro Cantilever L w t]:::device --> Fg[Gravity Force]:::vol
    beam --> Fs[Surface Tension Force]:::surf
    beam --> Fe[Electrostatic Force]:::field
    beam --> Fel[Elastic Restoring Force]:::elas

    Fg --> g1[Scales as L cubed]
    Fs --> s1[Scales as L to the 1st]
    Fe --> f1[Scales as L squared]
    Fel --> e1[Scales as L to the 1st]

    g1 --> balance{Force Balance Check}:::comp
    s1 --> balance
    f1 --> balance
    e1 --> balance

    balance -->|Fel greater than Fs| stable[Stable: No Stiction]:::ok
    balance -->|Fs greater than Fel| stuck[Unstable: Stiction Failure]:::fail

    classDef device fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef vol fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef surf fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef field fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef elas fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef comp fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fail fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define the Trimmer Force Scaling Vector. What is its physical significance in MEMS design?

**Model Answer:**

The Trimmer Force Scaling Vector $\vec{S}_F$ is a mathematical construct that captures the length-dependent scaling behavior of dominant forces in a miniaturized system. It is a vector whose components are the scaling exponents of various force categories (volume, surface, electrostatic, magnetic) as a function of the characteristic dimension $L$.

**Physical significance:**
- It identifies which forces **dominate** at a given length scale.
- It guides the **selection of actuation mechanisms** (e.g., electrostatic over magnetic at micro-scale).
- It enables **pre-design scaling analysis** to predict device behavior **before fabrication**.
- It is the foundation of **dimensional analysis** in MEMS design.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for significance with examples)**

---

### Q2. State any three dominant forces at the micro-scale and write their scaling exponents with the characteristic length $L$.

**Model Answer:**

At the micro-scale (L ≈ 100 µm), the three dominant forces and their scaling exponents are:

| Force | Equation Form | Scaling Exponent |
|---|---|---|
| Electrostatic Force | $F_e \propto L^2$ (constant V) | $s = 2$ |
| Surface Tension | $F_\gamma \propto L$ | $s = 1$ |
| Capillary Force | $F_c \propto L$ | $s = 1$ |

Gravity ($s = 3$) and inertia ($s = 3$) become negligible at this scale.

**(3 marks: 1 per force)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a)** Derive the scaling exponent for the **electrostatic force** in a parallel-plate MEMS actuator. Explain why electrostatic actuation is preferred at the micro-scale. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup the Geometry:**
Consider a parallel-plate capacitor with:
- Plate area: $A = L \times L = L^2$
- Plate separation: $d$ (often held constant in practical MEMS)
- Applied voltage: $V$ (constant)
- Dielectric medium: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$

**Step 2 — Write the Capacitance:**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L^2}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Electrostatic Force:**
The force is the negative gradient of stored energy $U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ with respect to $d$:

$$F_e = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial d} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L^2 V^2}{d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Scaling:**
For a **self-similar system** with $d \propto L$:

$$F_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r V^2 \cdot \frac{L^2}{L^2} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r V^2$$

The force becomes **independent of $L$** → scaling exponent $s_E = 0$ for ideal self-similar scaling.

**For constant gap (more practical for KTU):**
$$F_e \propto L^2 \implies s_E = 2$$

**Step 5 — Why Preferred at Micro-Scale:**
- Gravity scales as $L^3$ → negligible at micro-scale.
- Inertia scales as $L^3$ → negligible.
- Electrostatic scales as $L^2$ → dominant.
- The force is **non-contact, low-power, and fast-switching**.
- **Energy density** of electrostatic actuation $\propto L^{-1}$ → increases as devices shrink.

**(7 marks: 2 for derivation, 2 for scaling analysis, 3 for justification)**

---

**Q.A (b)** A micro-accelerometer proof mass is a silicon cube of side $L = 500$ µm. Compare its **inertial force** under an acceleration of $10g$ with a macro-cube of $L = 5$ cm under the same acceleration. State which scaling exponent governs this comparison. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Inertial Force Formula:**
$$F_i = m \cdot a = (\rho L^3) \cdot a$$

For silicon: $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³, $a = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1$ m/s².

**Step 2 — Micro-Cube Calculation:**
$$F_{i,\text{micro}} = 2330 \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 98.1$$

$$F_{i,\text{micro}} = 2330 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 98.1$$

$$F_{i,\text{micro}} = 2.857 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N} = 28.57 \text{ µN}$$

**Step 3 — Macro-Cube Calculation:**
$$F_{i,\text{macro}} = 2330 \times (0.05)^3 \times 98.1$$

$$F_{i,\text{macro}} = 2330 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 98.1$$

$$F_{i,\text{macro}} = 0.02857 \text{ N} = 28.57 \text{ mN}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio Calculation:**
$$\frac{F_{i,\text{micro}}}{F_{i,\text{macro}}} = \left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right)^3 = (10^{-2})^3 = 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5 — Governing Exponent:**
The comparison is governed by the **volume force scaling exponent** $s_V = 3$.

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
The micro-cube produces an inertial force **one millionth** of the macro-cube. This is why **micro-accelerometers require high-sensitivity readout electronics** and **high-Q mechanical amplification** (e.g., comb-finger structures) to function.

**(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for calculation, 1 for ratio, 1 for exponent, 1 for interpretation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **surface tension force** scaling with a clear derivation. Discuss its role in **stiction failure** during the wet-release process of MEMS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Physical Origin:**
Surface tension arises from cohesive molecular forces at a liquid-gas interface. It is characterized by the surface energy $\gamma$ (N/m or J/m²).

**Step 2 — Derivation of the Force:**
For a liquid meniscus of contact-line length $L_c$ in contact with a solid at contact angle $\theta$, the **vertical component** of the surface tension force pulling the structure down is:

$$F_\gamma = \gamma \cdot L_c \cdot \cos\theta$$

If the contact line scales with the characteristic dimension $L$ (i.e., $L_c \propto L$):

$$F_\gamma \propto L^1 \implies s_S = 1$$

**Step 3 — Capillary Pressure on a Parallel Plate:**
The Laplace pressure across a meniscus of radius $R$ is:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{R}$$

The total capillary **collapse force** on a plate of area $A = L^2$ is:

$$F_c = \Delta P \cdot A = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta \cdot L^2}{R}$$

For $R \propto L$ (geometric similarity), this gives $F_c \propto L$, i.e., $s_S = 1$ for the force.

**Step 4 — Stiction in Wet Release:**
During wet release (e.g., HF etching of sacrificial oxide under a polysilicon plate), the rinsing liquid is dried. As the liquid recedes, **menisci form** between the structure and the substrate. The capillary force:

$$F_c \propto L$$

must be overcome by the **elastic restoring force** of the structure's bending stiffness:

$$F_{el} = k \cdot \delta = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3} \cdot \delta \propto L^{-2} \text{ (for self-similar beams)}$$

**Step 5 — Failure Condition:**
Stiction occurs when $F_c > F_{el}$. The critical length below which stiction becomes inevitable can be derived by equating these:

$$\gamma L = \frac{E t^3 \delta}{L^2} \implies L_c = \sqrt[3]{\frac{E t^3 \delta}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 6 — Mitigation Strategies:**
- **Super-critical CO₂ drying** to eliminate meniscus formation.
- **Self-assembled monolayer (SAM) coatings** to reduce $\gamma$.
- **Dimple structures** to reduce contact area.
- **Stiffening the structure** (increasing $t$).

**(7 marks: 2 for derivation, 2 for stiction mechanism, 2 for failure criterion, 1 for mitigation)**

---

**Q.B (b)** A comb-drive actuator has 100 fingers, each of length $L = 50$ µm, width $w = 5$ µm, and gap $d = 2$ µm. Calculate the total electrostatic force at $V = 10$ V. Justify why the force does **not** depend on the finger overlap direction in the ideal case. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Force per Finger Pair:**
For a single comb-finger pair, the electrostatic force is:

$$F_{\text{pair}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot \frac{t \cdot w}{d} \cdot V^2$$

Assuming air dielectric ($\varepsilon_r = 1$) and ignoring thickness effects, the side-force is:

$$F_{\text{pair}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \frac{w \cdot V^2}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**
$$F_{\text{pair}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (10)^2}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_{\text{pair}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_{\text{pair}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 250 = 1.107 \times 10^{-9} \text{ N} = 1.107 \text{ nN}$$

**Step 3 — Total Force for 100 Fingers:**
With 100 finger pairs (200 fingers total):

$$F_{\text{total}} = 100 \times 1.107 \text{ nN} = 110.7 \text{ nN}$$

**Step 4 — Why the Force is Independent of Overlap Length:**
In the **ideal case** (no fringing fields, uniform gap), the electrostatic force on a comb finger depends only on:
- The **gap $d$** between fingers
- The **width $w$** of the finger (face area)
- The **voltage $V$**
- The **dielectric constant** $\varepsilon$

It does **not** depend on the **overlap length $L$** in the direction of motion. This is a unique feature of comb drives — they provide **constant force vs. displacement** (zero "softening"), unlike parallel-plate actuators.

This is because the capacitance $C$ varies linearly with overlap $L$, and $F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ is therefore **constant** with respect to $x$.

**Step 5 — Scaling Implication:**
The comb-drive force scales as $s_E = 1$ (linear in number of fingers and finger width) — making it highly tunable and independent of displacement, an ideal MEMS actuator property.

**(7 marks: 2 for force per pair, 2 for total, 2 for the constant-force explanation, 1 for scaling statement)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing "scaling vector" with "vector field"** → The Trimmer Force Scaling Vector is *not* a spatial vector. It is a list of scalar scaling exponents. Marks are lost if students treat it as a directional arrow.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant in power laws** → $F \propto L^n$ does not mean $F = L^n$. Always include material constants ($C$, $\gamma$, $\varepsilon$, etc.).
> 3. **Ignoring edge effects** → At micro-scale, **fringing fields** in capacitors and **edge effects in beams** become significant. The ideal-scaling formulas are approximations.
> 4. **Wrong dominant force** → At the micro-scale, the answer is **never** gravity or inertia. Examiners will deduct marks if these are claimed as dominant.
> 5. **Mixing up area and length scaling** → Surface tension along a line scales as $L^1$; surface tension on a membrane scales as $L^2$. State the geometry explicitly.
> 6. **Skipping units** → Always write N, N/m, N/m² in derivations. KTU examiners allocate 0.5–1 mark per question for unit consistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024**

- ✅ The **Trimmer Force Scaling Vector** $\vec{S}_F$ is a list of **scaling exponents**, not a physical force.
- ✅ **Volume forces** (gravity, inertia) scale as $L^3$ → vanish at micro-scale.
- ✅ **Surface tension** along a line scales as $L^1$ → dominant at micro-scale.
- ✅ **Electrostatic force** (constant $V$, fixed $d$) scales as $L^2$ → preferred actuator.
- ✅ **Magnetic forces** scale as $L^1$ to $L^4$ → lose dominance rapidly with miniaturization.
- ✅ **Viscous drag** (Stokes) scales as $L^1$ → dominates fluidic MEMS.
- ✅ **Elastic beam bending** scales as $L^{-2}$ to $L^{-3}$ → shrinks faster than capillary forces → leads to **stiction**.
- ✅ The **critical length for stiction** is derived by equating $F_c$ and $F_{el}$.
- ✅ **Comb drives** give **constant force vs. displacement** — their force scales as $L^1$ with finger count.
- ✅ **Self-similar scaling** assumption (all dimensions change by $\alpha$) is the KTU-standard approach.
- ✅ Always state the **scaling exponent** *and* the **dominant force** in a single sentence for full marks.
- ✅ The **MEMS design compass rule**: at $L < 1$ mm, **think surface tension, electrostatics, and stiction** — not gravity.
- ✅ Key constants to remember: $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 0.072$ N/m, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\rho_{\text{Si}} = 2330$ kg/m³.
- ✅ For **two marks** or more on derivations, always show: **equation → simplification → identification of exponent → physical interpretation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scaling in Electrostatic and Electromagnetic Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scaling in Electrostatic and Electromagnetic Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS), **scaling** refers to the systematic analysis of how physical quantities — such as force, energy, power, pressure, and field strength — change as the characteristic linear dimension $L$ of a device is reduced from the macro-scale to the micro-scale. The discipline is built upon the foundational work of **Edward Lee, Stephen Senturia, and others at the MIT Microsystems Technology Laboratories**, formalized in texts like Senturia's *Microsystem Design*.

For **electrostatic** and **electromagnetic** actuation, scaling is critical because these two force-generation mechanisms respond to miniaturization in fundamentally *opposite* ways:

- **Electrostatic forces** scale *favorably* (per unit volume they grow as $1/L$).
- **Electromagnetic forces** scale *unfavorably* (they shrink as $L^4$ per unit volume, and they trigger catastrophic Joule heating because power density $P/V \propto L$).

This asymmetric behavior is the *central reason* why the vast majority of commercial MEMS devices (accelerometers, gyroscopes, RF switches, micromirrors, inkjet nozzles) use **electrostatic actuation**, while electromagnetic actuation is reserved almost exclusively for specialty or macro-scale hybrid devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OEAET722 Module 3):** Students must be able to derive the scaling laws for both electrostatic and electromagnetic force-producing mechanisms, plot the dimensionless scaling exponents, and apply the comparison principle to justify the dominance of electrostatic actuation in sub-millimeter MEMS.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Muscle and the Spring

Imagine two ways to power a tiny robotic arm:

1. **Electrostatic (think: two charged plates that attract each other, like a tiny muscle made of voltage):** The smaller you make the plates, the *stronger* the pull becomes *per unit area* — almost as if the muscle became more powerful the tinier it got. This is because the electric field between the plates can be made more intense at small gaps, and the force density grows.

2. **Electromagnetic (think: a tiny copper wire coil, like a microscopic motor's muscle):** When you shrink the coil, the wire becomes thinner, its resistance *increases*, and the current-carrying capacity *drops*. The magnet tries to do the same work in a smaller package, but it overheats catastrophically — like trying to fit an entire electric motor's power into a sugar cube. The motor's "muscle" gets weaker as the device shrinks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in MEMS:** A device with $L = 1\ \mu\text{m}$ has its electromagnetic force reduced by a factor of $10^{12}$ compared to a $L = 1\ \text{cm}$ device of similar geometry, while its electrostatic counterpart loses "only" a factor of $10^6$ in absolute force but *gains* force per unit volume. This single asymmetry is the design cornerstone of micro-actuator physics.

### Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Recall)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vacuum permittivity | $\varepsilon_0$ | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}}$ | F/m |
| Vacuum permeability | $\mu_0$ | $\mathbf{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}$ | H/m |
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8}$ | m/s |
| Copper resistivity | $\rho_{\text{Cu}}$ | $\mathbf{1.68 \times 10^{-8}}$ | $\Omega \cdot$m |
| Air breakdown field | $E_{\text{break}}$ | $\approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^6}$ | V/m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electrostatic Force — The Parallel-Plate Building Block

Almost every electrostatic MEMS actuator (comb drive, parallel-plate actuator, ZIP actuator) can be reduced to the canonical parallel-plate capacitor. We begin with the geometry:

- Plate overlap area: $A = L^2$
- Gap spacing: $d = L$ (characteristic length)
- Applied voltage: $V$

**Step 1 — Capacitance:**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 L^2}{L} = \varepsilon_0 L$$

This shows the fundamental scaling $C \propto L$.

**Step 2 — Stored Electrostatic Energy:**
$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 L V^2$$

**Step 3 — The Force (negative gradient of energy w.r.t. gap):**
$$F = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d}\bigg|_{V=\text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial d} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \cdot \left(-\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}\right)$$

Substituting the dimensions:
$$F = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 V^2 \cdot \frac{L^2}{L^2} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** With voltage held *constant*, the absolute electrostatic force becomes **independent of the characteristic length $L$**. However, since area scales as $L^2$, the **force per unit area (pressure)** scales as $1/L^2$ — it *grows* dramatically at small scales.

**Step 4 — Energy Density (volume-normalized):**
$$u_e = \frac{U}{A \cdot d} = \frac{\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 L V^2}{L^3} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 L^2} \propto \frac{1}{L^2}$$

**Step 5 — Breakdown Voltage Limit:**

In practice, $V$ cannot exceed the dielectric strength of the medium. For air:
$$V_{\max} = E_{\text{break}} \cdot d = E_{\text{break}} \cdot L \propto L$$

If we let $V = \alpha L$ (where $\alpha$ is a constant field-related parameter), then:
$$F = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 (\alpha L)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \varepsilon_0 L^2 \propto L^2$$

This is the *realistic* scaling law used in MEMS design: with the breakdown constraint, electrostatic force scales as $L^2$.

### 2.2 Electromagnetic Force — The Lorentz and Coil Actuator

Electromagnetic actuation in MEMS is typically realized via one of two mechanisms: (a) the **Lorentz force** on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field, or (b) the **magnetomotive force** of a current-carrying coil. Both share the same scaling pathology.

**Lorentz Force Geometry:**
- Conductor length: $\ell = L$
- Conductor cross-section: $A_w = L^2$
- Current density: $J = I / A_w \Rightarrow I = J L^2$
- Applied magnetic flux density: $B$ (from permanent magnet or external coil)

**Step 1 — Lorentz Force Magnitude:**
$$F = B I \ell = B (J L^2)(L) = B J L^3$$

If $B$ is *constant* (permanent magnet):
$$F \propto L^3$$

**Step 2 — Self-Generated Field from a Coil (solenoid):**

For a coil of $N$ turns, length $\ell_c = L$:
$$B = \mu_0 \frac{N I}{\ell_c} = \mu_0 \frac{N \cdot J L^2}{L} = \mu_0 N J L$$

Substituting back:
$$F = B I \ell = (\mu_0 N J L)(J L^2)(L) = \mu_0 N J^2 L^4$$

So with a self-coil, $F \propto L^4$.

**Step 3 — Power Dissipation (Joule Heating):**

For the same coil, total wire length $\ell_w = N \cdot L$, wire cross-section $A_w = L^2$:
$$R = \frac{\rho \ell_w}{A_w} = \frac{\rho N L}{L^2} = \frac{\rho N}{L}$$

$$P = I^2 R = (J L^2)^2 \cdot \frac{\rho N}{L} = \rho N J^2 L^3 \propto L^3$$

**Step 4 — Power Density (Heat Removal Problem):**
$$\frac{P}{V} = \frac{\rho N J^2 L^3}{L^3} = \rho N J^2 = \text{constant}$$

But the **surface-area-normalized heat load** is:
$$\frac{P}{A_{\text{surface}}} = \frac{\rho N J^2 L^3}{L^2} = \rho N J^2 L \propto L$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Heat Removal Catastrophe:** As the device shrinks, the heat generated per unit *area* grows linearly with $1/L$. At some critical length, the device will melt or thermally fail. This is the *fundamental* reason electromagnetic MEMS is impractical below $\sim 1\ \text{mm}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities are assumed to scale with a single characteristic length $L$ unless otherwise noted.** Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ in plain text — LaTeX equivalents use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| Quantity | Symbol | Scaling Law | Notes / KTU Caveat |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitance | $C$ | $\varepsilon_0 L$ | Plate area $\propto L^2$, gap $\propto L$ |
| Electrostatic force (const. $V$) | $F_e$ | $\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V^2$ | Independent of $L$ |
| Electrostatic force (with breakdown) | $F_e$ | $\frac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \varepsilon_0 L^2$ | $\propto L^2$ — *practical MEMS case* |
| Electrostatic pressure | $P_e$ | $\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V^2 / L^2$ | $\propto 1/L^2$ — *grows* at small scale |
| Energy density (electric) | $u_e$ | $\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2 \propto 1/L^2$ | Volume normalized |
| Lorentz force (const. $B$) | $F_m$ | $B J L^3$ | $\propto L^3$ |
| Coil-generated force | $F_m$ | $\mu_0 N J^2 L^4$ | $\propto L^4$ — *worst-case scaling* |
| Coil power dissipation | $P$ | $\rho N J^2 L^3$ | $\propto L^3$ |
| Coil power per unit surface | $P/A$ | $\rho N J^2 L$ | $\propto L$ — *heat removal problem* |
| Force/Volume (electric) | $F_e/V$ | $\propto 1/L$ | Favorable scaling |
| Force/Volume (magnetic) | $F_m/V$ | $\propto L$ | Unfavorable scaling |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This scaling analysis is not merely academic — it dictates every major commercial MEMS product:

- **Analog Devices ADXL accelerometers:** Use *electrostatic* comb drives because the scaling keeps the force per unit volume useful at $\sim 10\ \mu\text{m}$ feature sizes.
- **STMicroelectronics gyroscopes:** Electrostatic drive + capacitive sense, enabled by the $1/L^2$ pressure scaling.
- **Texas Instruments DLP micromirrors:** Electrostatic tilting actuators (1 µm air gap) producing reliable torque from < 20 V.
- **Hard-disk-drive read heads:** Use *electromagnetic* actuation (voice-coil) only because the head-slider assembly operates at the macro-scale.
- **MEMS speakers and microphones (e.g., USound, xMEMS):** Purely electrostatic for the same scaling reason.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Electrostatic Force Scaling (Three Cases)

We derive the electrostatic force law for a generic parallel-plate actuator of characteristic length $L$, plate overlap area $A = L \cdot W \approx L^2$ (square plate), and gap $d = L$.

**General Expression:**
$$F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial d}\bigg|_{V=\text{const}}, \quad C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

**Computing the gradient:**
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial d} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 L^2}{L^2} = -\varepsilon_0$$

Hence:
$$F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 (-\varepsilon_0) = -\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V^2 \quad \text{(magnitude) } F = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V^2$$

#### Case A — Voltage Held Constant ($V = V_0$)

The force expression in terms of $L$:
$$F = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F \propto L^0$$

The force is **scale-invariant** in absolute terms. However, the *pressure* (force per unit area):
$$P_e = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V_0^2}{L^2} \propto L^{-2}$$

Energy stored in the device:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 L V_0^2 \propto L$$

Energy density in the gap volume $V_{\text{gap}} = A \cdot d = L^3$:
$$u_e = \frac{U}{V_{\text{gap}}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 L V_0^2}{L^3} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V_0^2}{2 L^2} \propto L^{-2}$$

#### Case B — Voltage Scales with the Breakdown Limit ($V = E_{\text{break}} \cdot d = \alpha L$)

Substituting $V = \alpha L$:
$$F = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 (\alpha L)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \varepsilon_0 L^2 \propto L^2$$

Pressure:
$$P_e = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\frac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \varepsilon_0 L^2}{L^2} = \frac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}$$

> This is the *realistic MEMS design case* — the pressure remains constant while absolute force shrinks as $L^2$.

#### Case C — Charge Held Constant ($Q = Q_0$)

The force expression in this regime is:
$$F = \frac{Q^2}{2\varepsilon_0 A} = \frac{Q_0^2}{2\varepsilon_0 L^2} \propto L^{-2}$$

Note that for the *same* device geometry, holding charge constant produces the largest force at small scales — but charge cannot be held indefinitely in a MEMS device due to leakage and breakdown, so this is the *upper theoretical bound*.

#### Summary: Electrostatic Scaling Exponents

| Mode | $F$ scaling | $P_e$ scaling | $u_e$ scaling |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V = V_0$ (const.) | $L^0$ | $L^{-2}$ | $L^{-2}$ |
| $V = \alpha L$ (breakdown) | $L^{2}$ | $L^0$ | $L^0$ |
| $Q = Q_0$ (const.) | $L^{-2}$ | $L^{-4}$ | $L^{-4}$ |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Electromagnetic Force Scaling (Coil Actuator)

We model a solenoid coil of characteristic length $L$, with conductor cross-section $A_w = L^2$ and total turn count $N$.

**Step 1 — Current:**
The current $I$ is expressed in terms of the current density $J$:
$$I = J \cdot A_w = J L^2$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic Field of the Solenoid:**
The on-axis flux density inside a long solenoid is:
$$B = \mu_0 \frac{N I}{\ell_c}$$

For a coil whose length $\ell_c$ also scales as $L$ (so that the aspect ratio is preserved):
$$B = \mu_0 \frac{N \cdot J L^2}{L} = \mu_0 N J L$$

**Step 3 — Lorentz Force on the Coil Itself (magnetomotive):**
For a current-carrying conductor of length $L$ placed in the field $B$ it itself creates (or in an external field of the same magnitude):
$$F = B I L = (\mu_0 N J L)(J L^2)(L) = \mu_0 N J^2 L^4$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{mag}} \propto L^4}$$

**Step 4 — Power Dissipation:**
The total wire length is $\ell_w = N \cdot L$. The wire resistance is:
$$R = \frac{\rho \ell_w}{A_w} = \frac{\rho N L}{L^2} = \frac{\rho N}{L}$$

The power dissipated as Joule heat is:
$$P = I^2 R = (J L^2)^2 \cdot \frac{\rho N}{L} = \rho N J^2 L^3$$

**Step 5 — Volume and Surface Area:**
- Active volume: $V = L^3$
- Cooling surface area: $A_s = 6 L^2$ (cube approximation)

**Step 6 — Power Density (Volumetric Heating):**
$$\frac{P}{V} = \frac{\rho N J^2 L^3}{L^3} = \rho N J^2 \quad \text{(constant)}$$

**Step 7 — Surface Heat Flux (Cooling Load):**
$$\frac{P}{A_s} = \frac{\rho N J^2 L^3}{6 L^2} = \frac{\rho N J^2}{6} L \propto L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling Interpretation:** As $L \to 0$, the heat generated per unit surface area grows without bound (∝ 1/L). The device's ability to shed heat through convection or conduction scales only as $L^2$, but the heat *generated* scales as $L^3$. Eventually, thermal failure occurs.

### 3.3 The Master Comparison: $F/V$ Ratio

To compare the *effectiveness* of the two mechanisms at micro-scale, we evaluate the **force per unit volume** scaling exponent:

| Mechanism | Absolute Force | Volume | Force per Unit Volume | Exponent on $L$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electrostatic ($V = \alpha L$) | $\propto L^2$ | $\propto L^3$ | $\propto 1/L$ | $L^{-1}$ |
| Electrostatic ($V$ const.) | $\propto L^0$ | $\propto L^3$ | $\propto 1/L^3$ | $L^{-3}$ |
| Electromagnetic (coil) | $\propto L^4$ | $\propto L^3$ | $\propto L$ | $L^{+1}$ |

**Ratio:**
$$\frac{(F/V)_{\text{electric}}}{(F/V)_{\text{magnetic}}} \propto \frac{1/L}{L} = \frac{1}{L^2}$$

For $L = 1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$, the ratio is $10^{12}$ in favor of electrostatic actuation. **Twelve orders of magnitude.**

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** A square parallel-plate electrostatic actuator has side length $L_1 = 1\ \text{mm}$ and gap $d_1 = 10\ \mu\text{m}$. It is scaled down to $L_2 = 100\ \mu\text{m}$ and $d_2 = 1\ \mu\text{m}$. The applied voltage is fixed at $V = 100\ \text{V}$. Compute the ratio of forces and pressures.

**Solution:**

Force is independent of $L$ (constant-voltage case):
$$F_1 = F_2 = \frac{1}{2}(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(100)^2 = 4.427 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{N} \approx 44.27\ \text{nN}$$

Pressure ratio:
$$\frac{P_{e,2}}{P_{e,1}} = \frac{F_2 / L_2^2}{F_1 / L_1^2} = \frac{L_1^2}{L_2^2} = \left(\frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-4}}\right)^2 = 100$$

So the smaller actuator produces **the same force** but at **100× the pressure** — making it far more effective per unit footprint.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Electrostatic vs Electromagnetic Scaling Decision Tree

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *decision logic* a MEMS designer uses when choosing between electrostatic and electromagnetic actuation based on characteristic length $L$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Characteristic Length L] --> B{Is L less than 1 mm?}
    B -- NO --> C[Macro Scale Device]
    C --> D[Electromagnetic Feasible]
    D --> E[Voice Coils, Solenoids, Motors]
    D --> F[Use B = const or N Turns Optimization]

    B -- YES --> G[Meso Micro Scale Device]
    G --> H{Heat Removal Possible?}
    H -- NO --> I[Electrostatic Dominates]
    I --> J[Comb Drive Actuator]
    I --> K[Parallel Plate Actuator]
    I --> L[ZIP Zipper Actuator]
    I --> M[Capacitive Sense Readout]

    H -- YES --> N[Specialty Magnetic MEMS]
    N --> O[External Magnet Required]
    N --> P[High Current Density Design]
    P --> Q[Liquid Cooling or Bulk Substrate]
    Q --> R[Loudspeaker MEMS or MRI Coil Probe]

    M --> S[Voltage Scaling V = alpha L]
    S --> T[Force Scales as L squared]
    T --> U[Pressure Scales as Constant]
    U --> V[Energy Density Scales as Constant]
    V --> W[Design Verified at 1 to 100 micron]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Scaling Exponent Map

This diagram captures the *exponent of $L$* to which each physical quantity is proportional, providing an at-a-glance visual reference for the comparison.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ELEC[Electrostatic Actuation]
        E1[Capacitance C] -->|L to the 1| E2[Energy U]
        E2 -->|L to the 0 at V const| E3[Force F]
        E2 -->|L to the 2 at V scales with L| E3
        E3 -->|L to the minus 2| E4[Pressure P]
        E3 -->|L to the 1 at breakdown| E5[Force per Volume]
    end

    subgraph MAGN[Electromagnetic Actuation]
        M1[Current I] -->|L to the 2| M2[Magnetic Field B]
        M2 -->|L to the 1| M3[Lorentz Force F]
        M3 -->|L to the 4 for coil| M4[Total Force]
        M4 -->|L to the 1| M5[Force per Volume]
        M6[Wire Resistance R] -->|L to the minus 1| M7[Joule Power P]
        M7 -->|L to the 1| M8[Power per Surface]
    end

    E5 --> COMP[Comparison Ratio]
    M5 --> COMP
    COMP -->|L to the minus 2| FINAL[Electrostatic Wins at Small L]
```

### 4.3 Force–Length Scaling Curve (Schematic)

The diagram below conceptualizes the $\log F$ vs. $\log L$ plot, with slopes representing the scaling exponents derived above. (This is a textual/visual schematic representation of the canonical scaling plot found in MEMS textbooks.)

| Log-Scale Range | Mechanism | Slope on log-log |
|---|---|---|
| $L = 1\ \text{m} \to 10^{-2}\ \text{m}$ | Electromagnetic (coil) | $+4$ |
| $L = 10^{-2}\ \text{m} \to 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$ | Electromagnetic ≈ Electrostatic | Crossing point |
| $L = 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \to 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$ | Electrostatic (breakdown-limited) | $+2$ |
| $L < 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$ | Electrostatic (charge-limited) | $0$ to $-2$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Why is electrostatic actuation preferred over electromagnetic actuation in most MEMS devices? Mention any two scaling-based reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Electrostatic actuation dominates MEMS primarily because of its favorable scaling characteristics as the characteristic device length $L$ is reduced.

1. **Force per unit volume grows as $1/L$** for electrostatic actuators (with voltage scaling at the breakdown limit), whereas for electromagnetic actuators it scales as $L$. Therefore, at small scales, electrostatic force density improves while electromagnetic force density deteriorates.

2. **No significant heat dissipation problem:** Electrostatic devices dissipate essentially zero steady-state power (current flows only during switching), whereas electromagnetic devices dissipate $P \propto L^3$ as Joule heat in the coil, and the surface heat flux $P/A \propto L$ creates an unsolvable thermal problem below $\sim 1\ \text{mm}$.

*(Award 1.5 marks for each reason; full 3 marks for clarity and use of scaling terminology.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Define "scaling" in the context of MEMS. What happens to the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor as its linear dimensions are scaled by a factor of $1/10$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Scaling** in MEMS refers to the systematic study of how physical quantities (force, energy, capacitance, power, etc.) transform as the characteristic linear dimension $L$ of a device is reduced proportionally.

For a parallel-plate capacitor with area $A = L^2$ and gap $d = L$:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 L^2}{L} = \varepsilon_0 L$$

If $L$ is reduced by a factor of $10$ (i.e., $L \to L/10$), the capacitance scales linearly with $L$, so the capacitance also reduces by a factor of $10$.

*(Award 2 marks for definition + formula; 1 mark for numerical scaling conclusion.)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Electrostatic Scaling Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) Derive the scaling law for the electrostatic force produced by a parallel-plate capacitor of characteristic length $L$ when the applied voltage is (i) held constant, and (ii) limited by the air-breakdown field. Discuss the implications for MEMS design. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We start with the parallel-plate geometry:
- Plate area: $A = L^2$
- Gap: $d = L$
- Capacitance: $C = \varepsilon_0 A / d = \varepsilon_0 L$

The electrostatic force is obtained from the energy gradient at constant voltage:
$$F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial d} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \cdot \left(-\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V^2 \cdot \frac{L^2}{L^2} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V^2$$

**Case (i) — $V$ constant ($V = V_0$):**
$$F = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F \propto L^0$$
$$P_e = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 V_0^2}{L^2} \propto L^{-2}$$

**Case (ii) — $V$ limited by breakdown ($V = E_{\text{break}} \cdot L = \alpha L$):**
$$F = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 (\alpha L)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \varepsilon_0 L^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F \propto L^2$$
$$P_e = \frac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \varepsilon_0 \quad \text{(constant)}$$

**Implications for MEMS design:**
- In case (i), the force is *scale-invariant* but pressure grows as $1/L^2$, allowing micro-actuators to deliver high force density.
- In case (ii), which is the practical case (we cannot increase voltage beyond breakdown), the force scales as $L^2$ and pressure remains constant — meaning the *device works* but produces less absolute force.
- This is why MEMS designers minimize gap $d$ to maximize $E$ while keeping $V$ moderate.

*(Award 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for both cases, 2 marks for design implications.)*

---

**(b) A square parallel-plate electrostatic actuator has $L = 500\ \mu\text{m}$ and gap $d = 5\ \mu\text{m}$, driven at $V = 50\ \text{V}$. It is scaled down by a factor of 5 (i.e., $L \to 100\ \mu\text{m}$, $d \to 1\ \mu\text{m}$). Compute the ratio of the electrostatic pressures and the ratio of stored energies in the two cases. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$
- Original: $L_1 = 500\ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, $d_1 = 5\ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$, $V = 50\ \text{V}$
- Scaled: $L_2 = 100\ \mu\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, $d_2 = 1\ \mu\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$

**Capacitances:**
$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 L_1^2}{d_1} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(5 \times 10^{-4})^2}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_1 = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2.5 \times 10^{-7})}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F} = 0.4427\ \text{pF}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 L_2^2}{d_2} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-4})^2}{10^{-6}} = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F} = 0.08854\ \text{pF}$$

**Ratio of capacitances:**
$$\frac{C_1}{C_2} = \frac{4.427 \times 10^{-13}}{8.854 \times 10^{-14}} = 5$$

This confirms $C \propto L$ (scaling by 5× reduces C by 5×).

**Electrostatic Pressure (with $V$ constant):**
$$P_e = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \frac{V^2}{d^2}$$

$$\frac{P_{e,2}}{P_{e,1}} = \frac{d_1^2}{d_2^2} = \left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}}\right)^2 = 25$$

**Numerical pressures:**
$$P_{e,1} = \frac{1}{2}(8.854 \times 10^{-12})\frac{(50)^2}{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2500)}{2 \times 10^{-11}} = 1106.75\ \text{Pa}$$

$$P_{e,2} = \frac{1}{2}(8.854 \times 10^{-12})\frac{(50)^2}{(10^{-6})^2} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2500)}{2 \times 10^{-12}} = 27668.75\ \text{Pa}$$

**Stored Energy:**
$$U_1 = \frac{1}{2} C_1 V^2 = \frac{1}{2}(4.427 \times 10^{-13})(2500) = 5.534 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{J}$$

$$U_2 = \frac{1}{2} C_2 V^2 = \frac{1}{2}(8.854 \times 10^{-14})(2500) = 1.107 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{J}$$

**Energy ratio:**
$$\frac{U_1}{U_2} = \frac{5.534 \times 10^{-10}}{1.107 \times 10^{-10}} = 5$$

**Conclusion:** Scaling by 5× reduces the stored energy by 5× (since $U \propto L$) but increases the electrostatic pressure by 25× (since $P \propto 1/d^2 \propto 1/L^2$).

*(Award 2 marks for capacitance calculation, 2 marks for pressure ratio, 2 marks for energy values, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Electromagnetic Scaling Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) For a solenoid-type electromagnetic actuator with characteristic length $L$, derive the scaling laws for: (i) the magnetic flux density, (ii) the force, and (iii) the power dissipation. Assume the current density $J$ and the number of turns per unit length are held constant. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Geometry and assumptions:**
- Solenoid length: $\ell_c = L$
- Wire cross-section: $A_w = L^2$
- Number of turns: $N$
- Current density: $J = I/A_w$, held constant
- Hence current: $I = J L^2$

**Step 1 — Magnetic Field of the Solenoid:**
$$B = \mu_0 \frac{N I}{\ell_c} = \mu_0 \frac{N (J L^2)}{L} = \mu_0 N J L$$

$$\boxed{B \propto L^1}$$

**Step 2 — Force (Lorentz/Magnetomotive):**
The force on the current-carrying conductor in its own field:
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L = (\mu_0 N J L)(J L^2)(L) = \mu_0 N J^2 L^4$$

$$\boxed{F \propto L^4}$$

**Step 3 — Power Dissipation:**
The total wire length is $\ell_w = N L$. The wire resistance:
$$R = \frac{\rho \ell_w}{A_w} = \frac{\rho N L}{L^2} = \frac{\rho N}{L}$$

Power dissipated:
$$P = I^2 R = (J L^2)^2 \cdot \frac{\rho N}{L} = \rho N J^2 L^3$$

$$\boxed{P \propto L^3}$$

**Step 4 — Force per unit volume:**
$$\frac{F}{V} = \frac{\mu_0 N J^2 L^4}{L^3} = \mu_0 N J^2 L \propto L$$

This is the *unfavorable* scaling: as $L$ decreases, force per unit volume *decreases* linearly.

*(Award 2 marks each for B, F, P derivations, 1 mark for the F/V conclusion.)*

---

**(b) Compare the scaling behavior of electrostatic and electromagnetic force per unit volume in MEMS. Why is electrostatic actuation practically preferred for devices with characteristic dimensions below $1\ \text{mm}$? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Quantity | Electrostatic (V = αL) | Electromagnetic (coil) |
|---|---|---|
| Force $F$ | $\frac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \varepsilon_0 L^2 \propto L^2$ | $\mu_0 N J^2 L^4 \propto L^4$ |
| Volume $V$ | $L^3$ | $L^3$ |
| Force/Volume | $\propto L^{-1}$ | $\propto L^{+1}$ |
| Power dissipation | $\sim 0$ (steady state) | $\rho N J^2 L^3 \propto L^3$ |
| Power per surface | $\sim 0$ | $\rho N J^2 L \propto L$ (heat problem) |

**Key observations:**

1. **Force per unit volume ratio:**
$$\frac{(F/V)_{\text{elec}}}{(F/V)_{\text{mag}}} \propto \frac{L^{-1}}{L^{+1}} = \frac{1}{L^2}$$

For $L = 100\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, the ratio is $10^8$ — i.e., electrostatic is **100 million times more effective per unit volume**.

2. **Heat dissipation:** Electrostatic devices consume essentially *zero* steady-state power. Electromagnetic coils dissipate $P \propto L^3$ and their surface heat load $P/A_s \propto L$ becomes unmanageable below 1 mm, leading to thermal failure.

3. **Fabrication compatibility:** Electrostatic actuators are made using the same lithographic processes as CMOS, allowing monolithic integration. Electromagnetic actuators require bulky coils and external magnets, defeating the purpose of miniaturization.

4. **Voltage versus current control:** Electrostatic MEMS use voltage control (low power, easy CMOS drive), while electromagnetic MEMS require high currents (heat, driver complexity).

**Conclusion:** Below $L = 1\ \text{mm}$, electrostatic actuation is preferred because its force per unit volume *grows* as devices shrink, while electromagnetic force per unit volume *shrinks*, and the heat removal problem becomes catastrophic.

*(Award 3 marks for comparison, 2 marks for heat dissipation analysis, 2 marks for the practical/engineering conclusion.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the breakdown voltage constraint:** Many students derive the $L^0$ force scaling for $V =$ const. and assume it always applies. In *practical* MEMS, $V$ scales with $L$ (the breakdown limit), so the correct scaling is $L^2$. Examiners will deduct marks for not specifying the voltage regime.
>
> 2. **Confusing absolute force scaling with pressure scaling:** Force and pressure have *different* scaling exponents. The same device can have constant force but pressure that grows as $1/L^2$. State both.
>
> 3. **Sign error in the energy-gradient force formula:** $F = -\partial U / \partial x$. Dropping the negative sign or mixing up constant-$V$ vs. constant-$Q$ regimes is a classic 1-mark penalty.
>
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical problems:** KTU examiners specifically check for unit consistency. Always write $L$ in meters, force in Newtons, pressure in Pascals, and energy in Joules.
>
> 5. **Missing the heat-removal argument in electromagnetic scaling:** Simply stating $F \propto L^4$ is incomplete. You must also discuss $P/A_s \propto L$ and why this triggers thermal failure.
>
> 6. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ (with vertical bars) inside a markdown table:** This breaks the table rendering in many viewers. Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX or write "abs(x)" in plain text.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Scaling concept:** Proportional reduction of all linear dimensions by a factor, and tracking how each physical quantity transforms.
- **Capacitance:** $C = \varepsilon_0 L$ (scales linearly with $L$).
- **Electrostatic force (V const.):** $F = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 V^2$ (scale-invariant); pressure $P_e \propto L^{-2}$.
- **Electrostatic force (breakdown-limited):** $F \propto L^2$; pressure is constant.
- **Electrostatic force (Q const.):** $F \propto L^{-2}$ (theoretical upper bound).
- **Magnetic field of a solenoid:** $B = \mu_0 N J L \propto L$.
- **Lorentz force with constant $B$:** $F \propto L^3$.
- **Coil force (self-field):** $F \propto L^4$ (worst-case).
- **Power dissipation in coil:** $P \propto L^3$.
- **Surface heat flux:** $P/A_s \propto L$ — *the killer constraint* for electromagnetic MEMS.
- **Force/volume scaling:** Electrostatic $\propto L^{-1}$ (favorable), Electromagnetic $\propto L^{+1}$ (unfavorable).
- **Ratio of force/volume:** $\propto 1/L^2$ in favor of electrostatic.
- **Practical threshold:** Below $L \approx 1\ \text{mm}$, electrostatic actuation is overwhelmingly preferred.
- **Industry examples:** ADXL accelerometers, DLP mirrors, MEMS microphones — all electrostatic.
- **Two energy regimes:** Always distinguish constant-$V$ vs. constant-$Q$ electrostatic regimes in derivations.
- **Air breakdown field:** $E_{\text{break}} \approx 3 \times 10^6\ \text{V/m}$ — sets the voltage limit.
- **Copper resistivity:** $\rho \approx 1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$ — used in coil power calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scaling in Electricity and Fluidic Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scaling in Electricity and Fluidic Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Scaling Laws in MEMS** refer to the mathematical and physical transformations that govern how various physical quantities (force, energy, power, resistance, flow rate, etc.) behave when the characteristic length scale of a system is miniaturized from the macroscopic (meter) domain to the microscopic (micrometer/nanometer) domain. The **scaling factor (S)** is defined as the ratio of the original macroscopic dimension to the miniaturized dimension:

$$S = \frac{L_{macro}}{L_{micro}} \quad \text{where} \quad S \gg 1$$

When a quantity is scaled, it follows the power-law form:

$$Q_{scaled} = Q_{original} \cdot S^{n}$$

where the exponent **n** (the **scaling exponent**) is unique to each physical phenomenon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Scaling in **electricity** deals with the behavior of voltages, currents, electric fields, electrostatic forces, and power dissipation. Scaling in **fluidic dynamics** deals with the dominance of viscous forces, the collapse of the Reynolds number, surface tension, and diffusion-driven transport at small length scales.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy 1 — The Garden Hose vs. The Straw:**
Imagine drinking water from a fire hose (large scale) versus a thin straw (small scale). In the fire hose, water gushes out in chaotic, swirling streams — this is **inertia-dominated flow** (turbulent). In the straw, water moves in smooth, parallel layers — this is **viscous-dominated flow** (laminar). The same principle applies in microfluidics: as we shrink the channel, the rules of fluid motion completely change because **inertia vanishes and viscosity takes over**.

**Real-World Analogy 2 — The Elephant vs. The Ant:**
Consider an elephant's weight (volume ∝ L³) versus the strength of its legs (cross-sectional area ∝ L²). The ant is relatively much stronger because the area-to-volume ratio increases as size decreases. Similarly, in MEMS, **surface forces** (electrostatic, surface tension, friction) scale with L², while **body forces** (gravity, inertia, magnetic body forces) scale with L³ — so surface effects dominate at the microscale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
> - Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
> - Dynamic viscosity of water (20°C): **μ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
> - Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Surface tension of water: **γ = 0.072 N/m**

### 1.3 Why Scaling Laws Matter in MEMS

The micro-world is **NOT** a scaled-down macro-world. Different physical laws dominate:

| Dominant at Macro Scale | Dominant at Micro Scale |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Gravity | Surface tension |
| Inertia | Viscosity |
| Turbulent flow | Laminar flow |
| Magnetic body forces | Electrostatic surface forces |
| Bulk conduction | Surface conduction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Scaling Rules (Foundation)

When the characteristic length **L** of a system is scaled by a factor **S**, all geometric quantities transform as:

$$L' = \frac{L}{S}, \quad A' = \frac{A}{S^{2}}, \quad V' = \frac{V}{S^{3}}$$

These three rules are the **master keys** — every other scaling relation is derived from them.

### 2.2 Scaling in Electricity

#### 2.2.1 Electric Field
The electric field between two plates is **E = V/d**. If the applied voltage scales as **V' = V/S** (constant field assumption), then:

$$E' = \frac{V'}{d'} = \frac{V/S}{L/S} = E \quad (\text{field unchanged})$$

If voltage is held constant: **E' = E·S** (field increases by factor S).

#### 2.2.2 Capacitance
For a parallel plate capacitor: **C = ε₀A/d**. Substituting scaled area and gap:

$$C' = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A/S^2}{L/S} = \frac{C}{S}$$

**Energy stored** U = ½CV²:

$$U' = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C}{S}\right)\left(\frac{V}{S}\right)^{2} = \frac{U}{S^{3}}$$

#### 2.2.3 Resistance
For a conductor of length L and cross-section A: **R = ρL/A**

$$R' = \rho\,\frac{L/S}{A/S^2} = R \cdot S$$

Resistance **increases** as size shrinks — this is why nanowires have huge resistance.

#### 2.2.4 Current and Current Density
If voltage scales as 1/S, then current **I = V/R** scales as:

$$I' = \frac{V/S}{R \cdot S} = \frac{I}{S^{2}}$$

Current density **J = I/A**:

$$J' = \frac{I/S^{2}}{A/S^{2}} = J \quad (\text{unchanged})$$

#### 2.2.5 Power Dissipation and Power Density
Power: **P = V²/R**

$$P' = \frac{(V/S)^{2}}{R \cdot S} = \frac{P}{S^{3}}$$

Power density (P per unit volume):

$$\frac{P'}{V'} = \frac{P/S^{3}}{V/S^{3}} = \frac{P}{V} \quad (\text{unchanged if voltage scales with length})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** If voltage is **NOT** scaled (held constant at macro value), then P' = V²/(R·S) = P/S, and power density becomes (P/S)/(V/S³) = P·S²/V. This means **power density increases as S²** — a major cause of **electromigration and Joule heating failures** in microelectronics.

#### 2.2.6 Electrostatic Force
For a parallel plate actuator: **F = ½ ε₀ A (V/d)²**

$$F' = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \frac{A}{S^2}\left(\frac{V}{L/S}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \frac{A}{S^2}\left(\frac{SV}{L}\right)^{2} = F$$

If voltage is held constant, the electrostatic force actually **increases by S²** — this is the key reason electrostatic actuation is preferred in MEMS.

### 2.3 Scaling in Fluidic Dynamics

#### 2.3.1 Reynolds Number (The Master Indicator)
The Reynolds number compares inertial and viscous forces:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{\text{Inertial forces}}{\text{Viscous forces}}$$

Under scaling (v' = v, L' = L/S):

$$Re' = \frac{\rho v (L/S)}{\mu} = \frac{Re}{S}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When S = 1000 (going from 1 m to 1 mm), Re drops by 1000×. Typical MEMS Re is in the range **10⁻⁶ to 100** — flow is overwhelmingly **laminar and creeping**.

#### 2.3.2 Navier–Stokes Equation
The full incompressible Navier–Stokes equation is:

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v}\right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{v} + \vec{f}_{body}$$

After scaling, the ratio of the inertia term (ρv·∇v) to the viscous term (μ∇²v) is exactly the Reynolds number. When **Re << 1**, the inertia term vanishes, and the equation reduces to the **Stokes equation (creeping flow)**:

$$0 = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{v} + \vec{f}_{body}$$

#### 2.3.3 Continuity Equation
Mass conservation for incompressible flow:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0 \quad \text{(dimensionless, scaling-invariant)}$$

#### 2.3.4 Force Scaling in Fluids
**Viscous (shear) force** scales with area:

$$F_{visc} = \mu \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} A \quad \Rightarrow \quad F'_{visc} = \frac{F_{visc}}{S^2}$$

**Inertial (pressure) force:**

$$F_{inert} \sim \rho v^{2} A \quad \Rightarrow \quad F'_{inert} = \frac{F_{inert}}{S^2}$$

**Gravity (body) force:**

$$F_{grav} = \rho g V \quad \Rightarrow \quad F'_{grav} = \frac{F_{grav}}{S^3}$$

**Surface tension force:**

$$F_{st} = \gamma L \quad \Rightarrow \quad F'_{st} = \frac{F_{st}}{S}$$

#### 2.3.5 Ratio of Surface Tension to Gravity (Capillary Length)
The **Eötvös number (Eo)** and **Capillary length** (λc) determine when surface tension dominates:

$$\lambda_c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{\rho g}}, \quad Eo = \frac{\rho g L^{2}}{\gamma}$$

For water: **λc ≈ 2.7 mm**. Below this length, **gravity becomes negligible** and surface tension rules.

#### 2.3.6 Péclet Number and Diffusion
Péclet number compares convective to diffusive transport:

$$Pe = \frac{v L}{D} = \frac{\text{Convection}}{\text{Diffusion}}$$

Diffusion time across a length L:

$$t_{diff} \sim \frac{L^{2}}{D}$$

Under scaling, t'ₐᵢff = t/S² — diffusion becomes **extremely fast** at the microscale, making mixing effortless without turbulence.

#### 2.3.7 Hagen–Poiseuille Flow
For a cylindrical microchannel of diameter D and length L, the volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^{4} \Delta p}{128 \mu L}$$

Under scaling (D' = D/S, L' = L/S):

$$Q' = \frac{\pi (D/S)^{4} \Delta p}{128 \mu (L/S)} = \frac{Q}{S^{3}}$$

The **flow resistance** (Δp/Q):

$$R_{hyd}' = \frac{128 \mu (L/S)}{\pi (D/S)^{4}} = R_{hyd} \cdot S^{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydraulic resistance scales as **S³** — microchannels are extremely resistive, which is both a challenge (pumping is hard) and an advantage (precise flow control).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Macro Formula | Scaled Form | Scaling Exponent (n) | Engineering Implication |
|---|----------|---------------|-------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Length | L | L/S | −1 | Reference |
| 2 | Area | L² | L²/S² | −2 | Surface effects |
| 3 | Volume | L³ | L³/S³ | −3 | Body effects |
| 4 | Electric Field (V const.) | E = V/d | E·S | +1 | Dielectric breakdown risk |
| 5 | Capacitance | C = ε₀A/d | C/S | −1 | Charge storage drops |
| 6 | Resistance | R = ρL/A | R·S | +1 | Nanowire heating |
| 7 | Current (V scales) | I = V/R | I/S² | −2 | Signal levels drop |
| 8 | Power (V scales) | P = V²/R | P/S³ | −3 | Less absolute power |
| 9 | Power density (V const.) | P/V | P·S²/V | +2 | Joule heating crisis |
| 10 | Electrostatic Force (V const.) | F = ½ε₀AV²/d² | F·S² | +2 | Why MEMS uses electrostatic |
| 11 | Reynolds Number | Re = ρvL/μ | Re/S | −1 | Laminar flow regime |
| 12 | Viscous Force | μvA | F/S² | −2 | Dominant friction |
| 13 | Inertial Force | ρv²A | F/S² | −2 | Negligible at micro |
| 14 | Gravity Force | ρgV | F/S³ | −3 | Micro-gravity world |
| 15 | Surface Tension | γL | F/S | −1 | Capillary dominance |
| 16 | Diffusion Time | L²/D | t/S² | −2 | Fast mixing |
| 17 | Hydraulic Resistance | Δp/Q | R·S³ | +3 | Flow control precision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the hierarchy of scaling exponents at the microscale:**
> - Positive exponents (S⁺) → quantities **grow** when shrinking → engineering challenge
> - Negative exponents (S⁻) → quantities **shrink** when shrinking → often beneficial
> - **S⁰** (zero) → scale-invariant → robust against miniaturization

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microfluidic lab-on-chip devices** exploit the dominance of laminar flow to create precise chemical gradients without turbulence.
- **Inkjet printer nozzles** rely on surface-tension-driven refill of microchannels.
- **Comb-drive actuators** in MEMS accelerometers use the S² scaling of electrostatic force.
- **Micro heat exchangers** benefit from enormous surface-to-volume ratios (S⁻¹ for area/volume).
- **DNA sequencing chips** use diffusion-driven transport (Pe ≈ 0) to separate molecules by size.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Density Scaling in Interconnects

> **Problem:** A copper interconnect in a VLSI chip is scaled down by factor S = 100. Originally, the wire length is 1 cm with cross-section 1 μm × 1 μm. The original current is 1 mA at 1 V. Find how the power density changes.

**Step 1:** Write the original resistance formula:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} = 170 \;\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the original power dissipation:

$$P = I^{2} R = (10^{-3})^{2} \cdot 170 = 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{W}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the original volume and power density:

$$V_{orig} = L \cdot A = 10^{-2} \cdot 10^{-12} = 10^{-14} \;\text{m}^{3}$$

$$\rho_{P,orig} = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-14}} = 1.7 \times 10^{10} \;\text{W/m}^{3}$$

**Step 4:** Apply scaling with voltage held constant (V' = V):

$$R' = R \cdot S = 170 \cdot 100 = 17{,}000 \;\Omega$$

$$P' = \frac{V^{2}}{R'} = \frac{1}{17{,}000} = 5.88 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{W}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the scaled volume:

$$V' = \frac{V_{orig}}{S^{3}} = \frac{10^{-14}}{10^{6}} = 10^{-20} \;\text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the new power density:

$$\rho_{P,new} = \frac{P'}{V'} = \frac{5.88 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-20}} = 5.88 \times 10^{15} \;\text{W/m}^{3}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{\rho_{P,new}}{\rho_{P,orig}} = \frac{5.88 \times 10^{15}}{1.7 \times 10^{10}} \approx 3.46 \times 10^{5} = S^{2} \cdot 2.03$$

This confirms the **S² = 10⁴** power density inflation — a primary driver of electromigration failures in nanoscale interconnects.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reynolds Number Collapse in Microchannels

> **Problem:** Water flows in a 1 cm diameter pipe at 1 m/s (typical laboratory flow). Compare with a 100 μm microchannel at the same velocity. Determine Re in each case.

**Step 1:** Macro pipe — Re calculation:

$$Re_{macro} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.01}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 9980$$

This is in the **transitional/turbulent** regime.

**Step 2:** Microchannel — Re calculation:

$$Re_{micro} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \cdot 1 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0998$$

This is **deep in the laminar/creeping** regime.

**Step 3:** Ratio confirmation:

$$\frac{Re_{micro}}{Re_{macro}} = \frac{D_{micro}}{D_{macro}} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{0.01} = 0.01 = \frac{1}{S}$$

with S = 100, confirming Re scales as 1/S.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hagen–Poiseuille Flow in a Scaled Microchannel

> **Problem:** Derive the scaling of hydraulic resistance and volume flow rate for a microchannel when diameter and length are both scaled by S.

**Step 1:** Start with Hagen–Poiseuille for a cylindrical channel:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{128 \mu L}\,\Delta p$$

**Step 2:** Substitute scaled dimensions D' = D/S, L' = L/S:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q' &= \frac{\pi (D/S)^{4}}{128 \mu (L/S)}\,\Delta p \\
&= \frac{\pi D^{4}}{128 \mu L} \cdot \frac{(1/S)^{4}}{(1/S)} \cdot \Delta p \\
&= Q \cdot \frac{1}{S^{3}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore hydraulic resistance R_hyd = Δp/Q scales as:

$$R_{hyd}' = \frac{\Delta p}{Q'} = \frac{\Delta p}{Q/S^{3}} = R_{hyd} \cdot S^{3}$$

**Step 4:** Physical interpretation: For S = 10, a microchannel of the same length has **1000× higher flow resistance** than a macro channel. This is why **electroosmotic or capillary-driven** flows are preferred over pressure-driven flows in MEMS.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Scaling of Electrostatic Force in Comb Drives

> **Problem:** A parallel plate actuator has area A = 1 mm² and gap d = 10 μm. Voltage is held at 100 V. Find how force scales when gap and area are both reduced by S = 10, but voltage is held constant.

**Step 1:** Original force:

$$F = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_0 A \left(\frac{V}{d}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-6})\left(\frac{100}{10^{-5}}\right)^{2}$$

$$F = \frac{1}{2}(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-6})(10^{7})^{2} = 4.427 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{N}$$

**Step 2:** Apply scaling (A' = A/S², d' = d/S, V' = V):

$$\begin{aligned}
F' &= \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \frac{A}{S^{2}}\left(\frac{V}{d/S}\right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \frac{A}{S^{2}}\left(\frac{SV}{d}\right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \frac{A}{S^{2}} \cdot \frac{S^{2} V^{2}}{d^{2}} \\
&= F
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Wait — this gives no scaling when voltage is held constant AND gap scales. But in real comb drives, only one dimension (the gap) is reduced; the lateral dimensions may scale differently. Let's redo it for the more common case where **gap scales but area remains**:

If only gap shrinks (d' = d/S, A unchanged, V unchanged):

$$F' = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 A \left(\frac{V}{d/S}\right)^{2} = F \cdot S^{2}$$

So the electrostatic force **increases by S²** as the gap shrinks. This is precisely why comb-drive gaps are made as small as fabrication allows.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Physical Regime | Macro-Dominant Effect | Micro-Dominant Effect | Critical Transition Length |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Electrical | Magnetic (Lorentz force) | Electrostatic (Coulomb) | ~1 mm (electromagnetic skin depth) |
| Fluidic | Inertia (turbulence) | Viscosity (laminar) | Re = 1 → L = μ/(ρv) |
| Thermal | Conduction, convection | Conduction, radiation | Knudsen number effects at ~1 μm |
| Mechanical | Gravity, inertia | Surface tension, elasticity | Capillary length ~2.7 mm (water) |
| Mass transport | Convection | Diffusion | Péclet number = 1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Scaling Effects on Dominant Physical Forces

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[MEMS Device Characteristic Length L]
    A --> B[Geometric Scaling L → L/S]
    B --> C1[Area A → A/S^2]
    B --> C2[Volume V → V/S^3]
    C1 --> D1[Surface Forces Scale as 1/S to S+2]
    C2 --> D2[Body Forces Scale as 1/S^3 to 1/S^2]
    D1 --> E1[Electrostatic Force Up by S2]
    D1 --> E2[Surface Tension Up by S]
    D1 --> E3[Viscous Drag Up by 1/S2]
    D2 --> F1[Gravity Down by 1/S3]
    D2 --> F2[Inertia Down by 1/S2]
    D2 --> F3[Magnetic Body Force Down by 1/S3]
    E1 --> G[Winner at Microscale Electrostatic]
    E2 --> H[Winner at Microscale Capillarity]
    E3 --> I[Winner at Microscale Viscous Flow]
    F1 --> J[Loser at Microscale Gravity]
    F2 --> K[Loser at Microscale Inertia]
    F3 --> L[Loser at Microscale Magnetic Body]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Reynolds Number and Flow Regime Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Characteristic Length L and Velocity v] --> B[Compute Reynolds Number]
    B --> C{Re Greater than 2300}
    C -->|Yes| D[Turbulent Flow Regime]
    C -->|No| E{Re between 1 and 2300}
    E -->|Yes| F[Transitional Laminar]
    C -->|No, Re less than 1| G[Creeping Stokes Flow]
    D --> H[Momentum Dominant Inertia Forces Win]
    F --> I[Mixed Regime Both Contribute]
    G --> J[Viscous Dominant Laminar Parallel Streamlines]
    H --> K[Use Turbulence Models]
    I --> L[Use Full Navier Stokes]
    J --> M[Use Linearized Stokes Equation]
    M --> N[Apply to Microfluidic Chip Design]
    K --> O[Apply to Macro Pipe Systems]
    L --> P[Apply to Lab on Chip and Bio MEMS]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Block Architecture: Microfluidic Scaling Effects

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Inlet Conditions]
        IN1[Pressure Drive]
        IN2[Electroosmotic Drive]
        IN3[Capillary Drive]
    end
    INPUT --> CHANNEL[Microscale Channel L in microns]
    CHANNEL --> RE{Re Calculation}
    RE --> RE1[Re very small 10 to power minus 6]
    RE1 --> LAMINAR[Pure Laminar Flow]
    LAMINAR --> DIFF[Diffusion Dominant Mixing]
    LAMINAR --> VIS[Wall Viscous Effects Dominant]
    DIFF --> MIX[Fast Homogeneous Mixing]
    VIS --> RESIST[High Hydraulic Resistance scales as S3]
    MIX --> OUT1[Output Uniform Solution]
    RESIST --> OUT2[Output Precise Flow Control]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: From Scaling Assumption to Engineering Outcome

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST1[Start Choose Scaling Assumption] --> ST2{Voltage Scaling Strategy}
    ST2 -->|V constant| ST3[Power Density Up by S2 Joule Heating]
    ST2 -->|V scales as 1/S| ST4[Power Density Constant Safe]
    ST3 --> ST5[Use Heat Sinks or Lower V]
    ST4 --> ST6[Acceptable Design]
    ST1 --> ST7{Fluid Assumption}
    ST7 -->|Re small Laminar| ST8[Use Stokes Equation]
    ST7 -->|Re large Turbulent| ST9[Use Navier Stokes with Turbulence]
    ST8 --> ST10[Microfluidic Devices]
    ST9 --> ST11[Macro Scale Fluid Systems]
```
