## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by scaling factor in MEMS? Why does electrostatic force increase when the device dimensions are reduced while voltage is held constant?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **scaling factor (S)** is the ratio of the original macro dimension to the miniaturized dimension. For a parallel plate actuator with area A and gap d, when only the gap is reduced by S (with V constant), the electrostatic force is:

$$F = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 A \left(\frac{V}{d}\right)^{2}$$

As d' = d/S, the electric field V/d' increases by S, and since force is proportional to (V/d)², force scales as **S²**. This is why electrostatic actuation dominates at the microscale — it becomes relatively stronger as devices shrink, making it the preferred actuation mechanism in MEMS accelerometers, gyroscopes, and RF switches. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for derivation, 1 mark for engineering relevance)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain why the Reynolds number is small in most microfluidic systems. What is the consequence of this on the flow regime?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Reynolds number **Re = ρvL/μ** is small in microfluidics because the characteristic length L is on the order of microns (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ m). Under scaling, Re' = Re/S. For typical flow velocities (1 mm/s to 1 m/s) in microchannels, Re lies in the range **10⁻⁶ to 100**, which is well below the laminar–turbulent transition value of 2300.

The consequence is that the flow is **laminar, deterministic, and reversible**. Streamlines are smooth and parallel, mixing occurs only by molecular diffusion (no turbulence-induced mixing), and the inertia term in the Navier–Stokes equation becomes negligible. The flow is described by the **linear Stokes equation: ∇p = μ∇²v**. (1 mark for Re scaling, 1 mark for typical value, 1 mark for consequence on flow regime)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the scaling laws for electric field, capacitance, resistance, current, and power when a MEMS device is scaled by a factor S. Discuss the implications of these scaling laws for the design of microelectronic interconnects.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A copper interconnect of length 5 mm and cross-section 2 μm × 2 μm carries a current of 0.5 mA at 3.3 V. The wire is scaled down uniformly by S = 50 while keeping the voltage constant. Calculate the new resistance, the new power dissipation, and the new power density. Comment on the result.*

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Electric Field:**
$$E = \frac{V}{d}, \quad E' = \frac{V}{d/S} = E \cdot S$$

If voltage is constant, electric field **increases by factor S** (1 mark for derivation).

**Capacitance:**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}, \quad C' = \frac{\varepsilon_0 (A/S^2)}{(L/S)} = \frac{C}{S}$$

Capacitance **decreases by factor S** (1 mark).

**Resistance:**
$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}, \quad R' = \frac{\rho (L/S)}{(A/S^2)} = R \cdot S$$

Resistance **increases by factor S** (1 mark).

**Current (V constant):**
$$I = \frac{V}{R}, \quad I' = \frac{V}{R \cdot S} = \frac{I}{S}$$

Current **decreases by factor S** (1 mark).

**Power (V constant):**
$$P = \frac{V^{2}}{R}, \quad P' = \frac{V^{2}}{R \cdot S} = \frac{P}{S}$$

Absolute power **decreases by S** (1 mark).

**Power Density (V constant):**
$$\frac{P}{V_{volume}} \to \frac{P/S}{V/S^{3}} = \frac{P}{V}\cdot S^{2}$$

**Power density increases by S²** — this is the critical issue leading to **Joule heating, electromigration, and dielectric breakdown** in nanoscale interconnects (1 mark for implication + 1 mark for correct framing).

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Given:** L = 5 mm = 5 × 10⁻³ m, A = 2 μm × 2 μm = 4 × 10⁻¹² m², I = 0.5 mA, V = 3.3 V, S = 50, ρ_Cu = 1.7 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m.

**Step 1: Original resistance**

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-12}} = 21.25 \;\Omega$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2: Original power dissipation**

$$P = \frac{V^{2}}{R} = \frac{3.3^{2}}{21.25} = 0.5124 \;\text{W}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3: Original volume and power density**

$$V_{vol} = L \cdot A = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-12} = 2 \times 10^{-14} \;\text{m}^{3}$$

$$\rho_{P,orig} = \frac{0.5124}{2 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.562 \times 10^{13} \;\text{W/m}^{3}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4: Apply scaling (V constant, S = 50)**

$$R' = R \cdot S = 21.25 \times 50 = 1062.5 \;\Omega$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5: New power dissipation**

$$P' = \frac{V^{2}}{R'} = \frac{10.89}{1062.5} = 0.01025 \;\text{W} = 10.25 \;\text{mW}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 6: New volume**

$$V'_{vol} = \frac{V_{vol}}{S^{3}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-14}}{1.25 \times 10^{5}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \;\text{m}^{3}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 7: New power density and ratio**

$$\rho_{P,new} = \frac{0.01025}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 6.4 \times 10^{16} \;\text{W/m}^{3}$$

$$\frac{\rho_{P,new}}{\rho_{P,orig}} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{16}}{2.562 \times 10^{13}} \approx 2500 = S^{2}$$

(1 mark for verification, 1 mark for comment: power density increases by S², leading to severe Joule heating and electromigration risk, requiring low-k dielectrics and copper interconnects in modern ICs)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the scaling laws for viscous, inertial, gravity, and surface tension forces in fluidic MEMS. Using these, justify why surface tension and viscous forces dominate at the microscale.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A microchannel of length 10 mm and circular cross-section of diameter 200 μm carries water at a velocity of 1 mm/s. Calculate the Reynolds number, the Péclet number (D = 2 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s), and the diffusion time for a dye molecule to traverse the channel diameter. State the dominant mixing mechanism.*

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) Solution:

Define scaling factor S = L_macro/L_micro.

**Viscous Force** (scales with area):
$$F_{visc} = \mu \frac{v}{L}A \quad \Rightarrow \quad F'_{visc} = \frac{F_{visc}}{S^{2}}$$

Scales as 1/S² (1 mark).

**Inertial Force** (scales with area and velocity²):
$$F_{inert} = \rho v^{2} A \quad \Rightarrow \quad F'_{inert} = \frac{F_{inert}}{S^{2}}$$

Scales as 1/S² (1 mark).

**Gravity Force** (scales with volume):
$$F_{grav} = \rho g V \quad \Rightarrow \quad F'_{grav} = \frac{F_{grav}}{S^{3}}$$

Scales as 1/S³ (1 mark).

**Surface Tension Force** (scales with length):
$$F_{st} = \gamma L \quad \Rightarrow \quad F'_{st} = \frac{F_{st}}{S}$$

Scales as 1/S (1 mark).

**Justification of dominance (2 marks):**

- Ratio **F_visc/F_grav** = (1/S²)/(1/S³) = S → viscous dominates over gravity for large S
- Ratio **F_st/F_grav** = (1/S)/(1/S³) = S² → surface tension dominates over gravity even more strongly
- Capillary length: λc = √(γ/ρg) ≈ 2.7 mm for water; below this, **gravity is irrelevant**
- This is why **microfluidic droplets, capillary pumps, and electrowetting devices** all exploit surface tension

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Given:** L = 10 mm, D = 200 μm = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m, v = 1 mm/s = 10⁻³ m/s, μ = 10⁻³ Pa·s, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, D_diff = 2 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s.

**Step 1: Reynolds number**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 0.2$$

(1 mark — Re is very small, **laminar flow** confirmed)

**Step 2: Péclet number**

$$Pe = \frac{v D}{D_{diff}} = \frac{10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 100$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3: Diffusion time across diameter**

$$t_{diff} = \frac{D^{2}}{D_{diff}} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4})^{2}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 20 \;\text{s}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4: Convective time across channel**

$$t_{conv} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}} = 10 \;\text{s}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5: Mixing ratio**

Since Pe = 100 > 1, convection dominates along the channel axis. However, across the diameter (transverse direction), **diffusion completes in 20 s** while convection carries fluid through in 10 s — meaning molecules spread laterally before exiting. (1 mark)

**Step 6: Dominant mechanism (1 mark) — Mixing is by molecular diffusion in the transverse direction (no turbulence). The Péclet number confirms that while longitudinal transport is convective, the lack of turbulence means **diffusion is the only cross-stream mixing mechanism**. This is why passive mixers in microfluidics use serpentine or staggered herringbone channels to stretch and fold the interface to enhance diffusion.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the S² vs. S³ distinction in power density** — many students write "power increases" instead of "power density increases by S² when V is constant." This is the most common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing up Reynolds number scaling** — Re scales as 1/S (not 1/S²). The L dependence is linear.
> 3. **Confusing viscous and inertial force scaling** — both scale as 1/S², but their **ratio** (Re) scales as 1/S.
> 4. **Ignoring the voltage scaling assumption** — every electrical scaling derivation must state whether V is constant or V scales as 1/S. Marks are lost when this is omitted.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — converting μm to m and mm/s to m/s is essential; KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per instance.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision List — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Master scaling rules:** L → L/S, A → A/S², V → V/S³
- ✅ **Electric field (V const.)** scales as **S¹**
- ✅ **Capacitance** scales as **S⁻¹**
- ✅ **Resistance** scales as **S⁺¹** (this is the #1 KTU trap)
- ✅ **Power density (V const.)** scales as **S²** → Joule heating crisis
- ✅ **Electrostatic force (V const.)** scales as **S²** → why MEMS loves electrostatic
- ✅ **Reynolds number** scales as **S⁻¹** → microfluidic Re is 10⁻⁶ to 100
- ✅ **Viscous force** scales as **S⁻²** (area-based)
- ✅ **Inertial force** scales as **S⁻²** (area-based)
- ✅ **Gravity** scales as **S⁻³** (volume-based) → negligible at micro
- ✅ **Surface tension** scales as **S⁻¹** (length-based) → dominant at micro
- ✅ **Capillary length of water ≈ 2.7 mm** — below this, surface tension wins
- ✅ **Diffusion time** scales as **S⁻²** → mixing is fast at micro
- ✅ **Hydraulic resistance** scales as **S³** → very high in microchannels
- ✅ **Stokes equation** ∇p = μ∇²v applies when **Re << 1**
- ✅ **Péclet number** Pe = vL/D compares convection to diffusion
- ✅ **Power density ratio** is the most common exam question — practice it
- ✅ **Diffusion time across channel diameter = D²/D_diff** — common numerical
- ✅ **Reynolds number threshold** for turbulence: Re > 2300
- ✅ **Always state the voltage scaling assumption** in electrical derivations

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Scaling in Electricity and Fluidic Dynamics**

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silicon (Si)** is a Group IV elemental semiconductor (atomic number **Z = 14**, atomic mass **M = 28.0855 g/mol**) that crystallises in the **diamond cubic (FCC-derived) lattice** with a lattice constant of **a = 5.431 Å** at 300 K. It is the **dominant substrate and structural material** in Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) because it uniquely combines excellent mechanical robustness, a tuneable electrical bandgap, a thermally grown native dielectric (SiO₂), and well-established batch-fabrication processes inherited from the integrated-circuit (IC) industry.

### 1.2 Intuition – The "Steel of the Micro-World"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Why Silicon and Not Steel?**
> Imagine you want to build a microscopic crane with a 50 µm-long arm. If you use steel, the grain structure is huge compared to the part — the beam will be **statistically unreliable** (some grains strong, some weak). Silicon, however, is grown as a **single, atomically perfect crystal** — every atom is in its proper place from one end of the beam to the other. This is why a tiny silicon cantilever can be **stronger per unit weight** than a steel I-beam of equivalent mass. Add to this the fact that we can **grow a perfect insulating oxide (SiO₂) by simply heating it in oxygen**, and you have the "perfect MEMS material" — strong, predictable, and processable in millions at once.

### 1.3 Why Silicon Dominates MEMS

The "silicon supremacy" in MEMS is governed by **eight engineering virtues**:

1. **Mechanical Quality** — Single-crystal yield strength ≈ **7 GPa** (≈ steel × 3 by density).
2. **Low Mechanical Loss** — Quality factor **Q > 10⁵** in vacuum, ideal for resonators.
3. **Native Oxide (SiO₂)** — Self-limiting, low-defect, used as mask, insulator, and sacrificial layer.
4. **Batch Fabrication** — 300 mm wafers hold > **70 000 chips**, drastically lowering cost.
5. **Tuneable Resistivity** — From **10⁻⁵ Ω·cm (n⁺)** to **10⁴ Ω·cm (p)** via doping.
6. **Predictable Anisotropy** — Crystal planes etch at **known, repeatable ratios**.
7. **Thermal Stability** — Melting point **1414 °C**, CTE **2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K**.
8. **CMOS Compatibility** — Direct on-chip integration of MEMS + circuits (SoC/SoP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEAET722 Module 3):** *The student must be able to explain the crystal structure, mechanical, electrical, thermal, and chemical properties of silicon, and justify its choice over other materials for MEMS devices.*

### 1.4 Classification of Silicon Used in MEMS

| Form | Crystallinity | Typical Use |
|------|---------------|-------------|
| **Single-Crystal Silicon (SCS)** | One continuous lattice | Bulk micromachined structures, wafers |
| **Polycrystalline Silicon (Polysilicon)** | Many small grains (10 nm – 1 µm) | Surface-micromachined MEMS (gears, beams) |
| **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si)** | No long-range order | Solar cells, displays, thin-film sensors |
| **Silicon-on-Insulator (SOI)** | SCS bonded to SiO₂ | High-Q resonators, precise thickness control |
| **Porous Silicon** | Nanoporous sponge | Optical sensors, biosensors, sacrificial layer |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Diamond Cubic Crystal Structure

Silicon adopts the **diamond cubic** structure, which is best understood as **two interpenetrating Face-Centred Cubic (FCC) lattices offset by (a/4, a/4, a/4)**.

**Structural facts to memorise:**

- **Basis atoms per unit cell** = 8
- **Nearest neighbour distance** = a√3/4 = **2.351 Å**
- **Coordination number** = 4 (tetrahedral bonding)
- **Atoms per unit volume** = 8/a³ = **5.00 × 10²⁸ atoms/m³**
- **Atomic packing factor (APF)** = 0.34 (very open compared to FCC 0.74)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a low APF is an advantage:** The "openness" of the diamond lattice provides the **channels and gaps** through which etchant chemicals (K⁺, OH⁻) can diffuse, giving well-behaved anisotropic etching — the very property MEMS exploits.

### 2.2 Miller Indices and Crystallographic Planes

Miller indices (hkl) are the **reciprocals of the intercepts** of a plane with the crystal axes, reduced to smallest integers.

| Plane | Intercepts | (hkl) |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| Cube face | a, ∞, ∞ | (100) |
| Body diagonal plane | a, a, ∞ | (110) |
| Body diagonal plane | a, a, a | (111) |

**Significance in MEMS:**

- **(100) wafer** → flat surface; high-etch-rate plane for KOH.
- **(111) plane** → slowest-etching plane; determines V-groove sidewalls at **54.74°** to the (100) surface.
- **(110) wafer** → produces perfectly vertical (111) sidewalls — ideal for deep trenches.

### 2.3 Mechanical, Thermal and Electrical Property Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Properties of Single-Crystal Silicon (300 K)**

| Property | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|:------:|:-----:|:----:|
| Lattice constant | a | 5.431 × 10⁻¹⁰ | m |
| Density | ρ | 2.33 × 10³ | kg/m³ |
| Young's Modulus [100] | E[100] | 130 | GPa |
| Young's Modulus [110] | E[110] | 169 | GPa |
| Young's Modulus [111] | E[111] | 188 | GPa |
| Poisson's Ratio | ν | 0.28 | – |
| Shear Modulus | G | 79.6 | GPa |
| Yield Strength | σ_y | 7.0 | GPa |
| Fracture Strength (typical) | σ_f | 1 – 3 | GPa |
| Mohs Hardness | – | 6.5 – 7 | – |
| Melting Point | T_m | 1414 | °C |
| Thermal Conductivity | k_th | 150 | W/(m·K) |
| CTE (linear) | α | 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ | /K |
| Specific Heat | c_p | 0.7 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Bandgap (indirect) | E_g | 1.12 | eV |
| Intrinsic Carrier Conc. | n_i | 1.0 × 10¹⁰ | cm⁻³ |
| Dielectric Constant | ε_r | 11.9 | – |
| Native Oxide Thickness | t_ox | ~ 1 – 100 | nm |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | d_hkl = a / √(h² + k² + l²) | Inter-planar spacing (cubic) |
| 2 | N_hkl = 2 / (a² √(h² + k² + l²)) | Atoms per unit area on (hkl), diamond cubic |
| 3 | 1/E = s₁₁ − 2(s₁₁ − s₁₂ − s₄₄/2)(l₁²l₂² + l₂²l₃² + l₃²l₁²) | Directional Young's modulus (cubic) |
| 4 | E[100] = 1 / s₁₁ | Young's modulus along [100] |
| 5 | E[110] = 4 / (s₁₁ + s₁₂ + s₄₄/2) | Young's modulus along [110] |
| 6 | E[111] = 3 / (s₁₁ + 2s₁₂ + s₄₄/3) | Young's modulus along [111] |
| 7 | tan(α) = √2 | V-groove angle 54.74° between (100) and (111) |
| 8 | R_ER = R(100) / R(111) | Etch-rate anisotropy ratio (KOH: 30 – 400) |
| 9 | σ = F / A | Mechanical stress |
| 10 | ε = ΔL / L₀ | Mechanical strain |
| 11 | E = σ / ε | Young's modulus definition (Hooke's law) |
| 12 | ν = −ε_lat / ε_axial | Poisson's ratio |
| 13 | G = E / [2(1 + ν)] | Shear modulus (isotropic) |
| 14 | ρ = m / V | Density |
| 15 | k_th_eff = k_th / [1 + (2L/a)·Kn] | Effective thermal conductivity with Knudsen correction |
| 16 | V_rms = √(3k_B T / m) | Thermal vibration of atoms |
| 17 | ΔR/R = π·ε(1 − 2ν) | Piezoresistive gauge factor model |
| 18 | f₀ = (1 / 2π)·√(k / m_eff) | Resonator natural frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The directional Young's modulus (Eq. 3–6) directly dictates the **resonant frequency** of a cantilever or comb-drive resonator. A [111]-oriented beam is ~ 45 % stiffer than a [100]-oriented one of the same dimensions — a design lever used in precision timing references.

### 2.5 Compliance Tensor Values (for derivations)

| s_ij | Value (× 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹) |
|:----:|:---------------------:|
| s₁₁ | 7.68 |
| s₁₂ | −2.14 |
| s₄₄ | 12.60 |

### 2.6 The "Why" Behind Anisotropic Etching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The (111) plane is the **densest** and the **most slowly etching** because each surface atom is **back-bonded to 3 atoms** below it (compared with only 1 back-bond for the (100) surface). The etchant must break **3 strong covalent bonds** to remove a (111) atom vs. only **1 bond** for a (100) atom. This atomistic asymmetry is the **root cause** of KOH's 100:1 etch selectivity between (100) and (111).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young's Modulus in the [110] Direction

We start from the **general directional Young's modulus** for a cubic crystal, expressed in terms of the **reduced compliances s₁₁, s₁₂, s₄₄** and the **direction cosines l₁, l₂, l₃** of the loading axis:

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[l_1 l_2 l_3]}} \;=\; s_{11} \;-\; 2\left(s_{11} - s_{12} - \frac{s_{44}}{2}\right)\left(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2\right)
$$

This is a tensor-transformation identity (Neumann's principle). For the **[110] direction**, the direction cosines are obtained by normalising the vector [1, 1, 0]:

**Step 1 — Normalise the direction vector**

$$
[l_1, l_2, l_3] = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 0\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the symmetric products**

$$
l_1^2 l_2^2 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = \tfrac{1}{4}
$$

$$
l_2^2 l_3^2 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)(0) = 0
$$

$$
l_3^2 l_1^2 = (0)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the general equation**

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = s_{11} - 2\left(s_{11} - s_{12} - \frac{s_{44}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = s_{11} - \frac{1}{2}\left(s_{11} - s_{12} - \frac{s_{44}}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Distribute and simplify**

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = s_{11} - \frac{s_{11}}{2} + \frac{s_{12}}{2} + \frac{s_{44}}{4}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = \frac{s_{11}}{2} + \frac{s_{12}}{2} + \frac{s_{44}}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognise the canonical form**

$$
\boxed{\;E_{[110]} = \dfrac{4}{s_{11} + s_{12} + \dfrac{s_{44}}{2}}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Insert numerical values**

$$
s_{11} = 7.68 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{Pa}^{-1},\;\; s_{12} = -2.14 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{Pa}^{-1},\;\; s_{44} = 12.60 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = \frac{7.68}{2} + \frac{(-2.14)}{2} + \frac{12.60}{4} \;\; \text{(all} \times 10^{-12} \text{ Pa}^{-1}\text{)}
$$

$$
= 3.84 - 1.07 + 3.15 = 5.92 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{[110]} = \frac{1}{5.92 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.69 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Pa} = 169\,\text{GPa}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** E[110] (169 GPa) lies between E[100] (130 GPa) and E[111] (188 GPa), as physically required by the anisotropy.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Young's Modulus in the [111] Direction

The direction cosines for **[111]** are:

$$
[l_1, l_2, l_3] = \left[\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Symmetric products**

$$
l_1^2 l_2^2 = l_2^2 l_3^2 = l_3^2 l_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{9}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[111]}} = s_{11} - 2\left(s_{11} - s_{12} - \frac{s_{44}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify to the canonical form**

$$
\boxed{\;E_{[111]} = \dfrac{3}{s_{11} + 2s_{12} + \dfrac{s_{44}}{3}}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Insert numbers**

$$
s_{11} + 2s_{12} + \frac{s_{44}}{3} = 7.68 + 2(-2.14) + \frac{12.60}{3}
$$

$$
= 7.68 - 4.28 + 4.20 = 7.60 \;\; (\times 10^{-12}\,\text{Pa}^{-1})
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{[111]} = \frac{3}{7.60 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.88 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Pa} = 188\,\text{GPa}\;}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Atomic Planar Density on (100), (110), (111) Planes

> [!NOTE]
> Atomic planar density N_hkl is the key driver of etching rate — denser planes etch more slowly.

**General formula for a diamond-cubic plane (hkl):**

$$
N_{hkl} = \frac{2}{a^2 \sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}\;\text{atoms/m}^2
$$

The factor of 2 arises because the (100) and (111) families in a diamond cubic lattice contain 2 atoms per (a²√(...)) primitive rectangle.

**Step 1 — (100) Plane**

$$
N_{100} = \frac{2}{(5.431 \times 10^{-10})^2 \sqrt{1}} = \frac{2}{2.950 \times 10^{-19}} = 6.78 \times 10^{18}\,\text{atoms/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — (110) Plane**

$$
N_{110} = \frac{2}{(5.431 \times 10^{-10})^2 \sqrt{2}} = \frac{6.78 \times 10^{18}}{\sqrt{2}} = 4.79 \times 10^{18}\,\text{atoms/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — (111) Plane**

$$
N_{111} = \frac{2}{(5.431 \times 10^{-10})^2 \sqrt{3}} = \frac{6.78 \times 10^{18}}{\sqrt{3}} = 3.91 \times 10^{18}\,\text{atoms/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Rank by density**

$$
\boxed{\;N_{100} > N_{110} > N_{111}\;}
$$

| Plane | Planar Density (atoms/m²) | Relative KOH Etch Rate |
|:-----:|:-------------------------:|:----------------------:|
| (100) | 6.78 × 10¹⁸ | High (≈ 1.0 µm/min) |
| (110) | 4.79 × 10¹⁸ | Medium (≈ 0.6 µm/min) |
| (111) | 3.91 × 10¹⁸ | Very Low (≈ 0.003 µm/min) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anisotropy ratio** R(100)/R(111) can be made **> 400:1** in 30 wt % KOH at 80 °C. This extreme ratio is what makes MEMS bulk micromachining of **V-grooves, pyramids, and beams with atomically flat (111) sidewalls** possible.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — V-Groove Angle (Quick Result)

A V-groove etched in a (100) wafer is bounded by four (111) planes meeting at the bottom. The angle between any (111) sidewall and the (100) surface is given by the angle between the planes' normals. Using direction cosines of [100] and [111]:

$$
\cos(\alpha) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1^2+0+0}\cdot\sqrt{1^2+1^2+1^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \alpha = \arccos(1/\sqrt{3}) = 54.7356^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha \approx 54.74^\circ\;}
$$

This is the universal **V-groove angle** seen in all bulk-micromachined silicon using alkaline etchants.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Silicon Material Taxonomy for MEMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Silicon for MEMS] --> B[Crystal Form]
    A --> C[Key Property Domains]
    A --> D[Fabrication Route]
    A --> E[Application in MEMS]

    B --> B1[Single Crystal Si]
    B --> B2[Polycrystalline Si]
    B --> B3[Amorphous Si]
    B --> B4[Porous Si]

    C --> C1[Mechanical: E, nu, sigma_y]
    C --> C2[Thermal: k th, CTE]
    C --> C3[Electrical: bandgap, doping]
    C --> C4[Chemical: native SiO2]

    D --> D1[Czochralski Wafer Pull]
    D --> D2[Float Zone Refining]
    D --> D3[LPCVD Deposition]
    D --> D4[Anodic or Stain Etching]

    E --> E1[Bulk Micromachining]
    E --> E2[Surface Micromachining]
    E --> E3[Resonators and Sensors]
    E --> E4[Biosensing Layers]

    B1 --> F1[Anisotropic KOH Etching Possible]
    B1 --> F2[High Q Mechanical Resonators]
    B2 --> F3[CMOS Compatible Structural Layer]
    B2 --> F4[Deposited Thin Films for MEMS]
    B3 --> F5[Photonic and Solar Cell Use]
    B3 --> F6[Low Temperature Processing]
    B4 --> F7[Optical Bragg Reflectors]
    B4 --> F8[Sacrificial Release Layer]

    D1 --> G1[Standard 100 mm to 300 mm Wafer]
    D2 --> G2[Ultra High Purity Resistivity]
    D3 --> G3[Thin Film Polysilicon]
    D4 --> G4[Variable Porosity Layers]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The root node `Silicon for MEMS` branches into four perspectives — *what it is* (crystal form), *what makes it useful* (property domains), *how it is made* (fabrication route) and *where it ends up* (applications). Each leaf node represents a sub-decision that an MEMS designer must make before selecting the silicon form and process.

### 4.2 Anisotropic Etching Behaviour — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[(100) Wafer in KOH] --> B[Mask Window Opening]
    B --> C[Etchant Attacks Surface Atoms]
    C --> D{Back-Bond Count}
    D -- 1 bond --> E[(100) Plane Etched Fast]
    D -- 3 bonds --> F[(111) Plane Etched Slow]
    E --> G[Sidewall Recedes]
    F --> H[Sidewall is Preserved]
    G --> I[V-Groove Formed]
    H --> I
    I --> J[54.74 Degree Sidewalls]
    J --> K[MEMS Cantilever or Diaphragm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The decision node `{Back-Bond Count}` is the **atomistic root** of macroscopic anisotropy — a beautiful example of how a quantum-mechanical detail (3 vs 1 covalent bonds) cascades into a manufacturable device geometry.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any **six** properties of silicon that make it the most suitable material for MEMS device fabrication.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. Single-crystal form has **high Young's modulus** (~ 130 – 190 GPa) and **high yield strength** (~ 7 GPa) — mechanically robust.
2. Possesses a **self-limiting native oxide (SiO₂)** usable as mask, dielectric, and sacrificial layer.
3. **Anisotropic etching** with predictable etch-rate ratios allows precise 3-D shaping.
4. **Tuneable electrical resistivity** (10⁻⁵ – 10⁴ Ω·cm) via doping — supports integrated piezoresistors.
5. **CMOS-compatible batch processing** lowers per-device cost.
6. **Low mechanical loss** (high Q-factor) in vacuum — ideal for resonators and gyroscopes.
7. (Bonus) Thermal stability (m.p. 1414 °C) and matched CTE with SiO₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 0.5 mark per correct property; the **6th** is a bonus. Always pair a property with a quantitative number.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define **Miller indices**. Why is the **(111) plane** so significant in silicon bulk micromachining?*

**Model Answer:**

- **Miller indices (hkl)** are a set of three integers that uniquely identify a crystallographic plane; they are the **smallest integer reciprocals of the intercepts** the plane makes with the three crystal axes.
- The (111) plane is the **slowest-etching** plane in alkaline etchants (KOH, TMAH, EDP) because each surface atom has **three back-bonds** to the bulk lattice, compared to only one for a (100) atom.
- Etch-rate ratio R(100)/R(111) ≈ **100 – 400 : 1**, so (111) planes **self-terminate** to form atomically flat sidewalls.
- Significance: enables **V-grooves (54.74°), pyramidal pits, smooth (111) sidewalls for beams and capillaries**, and **etch-stops** for precise thickness control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write "Miller indices are the intercepts themselves". Always clarify that they are the **reciprocals** of the intercepts, then reduced to smallest integers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **answer ANY ONE** full 14-mark question from the module. Each sub-part is 7 marks. Both alternative questions (A and B) are given below for full practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Describe the **diamond cubic crystal structure** of silicon with a neat diagram. Mention the basis atoms, lattice constant, coordination number, and atomic packing factor.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Lattice type:** Diamond cubic = 2 interpenetrating FCC lattices displaced by (a/4, a/4, a/4).
- **Lattice constant:** a = 5.431 Å at 300 K.
- **Basis atoms (8 per unit cell):** (0,0,0), (½,½,0), (½,0,½), (0,½,½) and the same shifted by (¼,¼,¼).
- **Coordination number:** 4 (tetrahedral).
- **Nearest-neighbour distance:** a√3/4 = 2.351 Å.
- **Atomic packing factor:** π√3/16 ≈ 0.34.
- **Atoms per unit volume:** 8/a³ = 5.00 × 10²⁸ /m³.
- **Bonding:** Covalent, sp³ hybridised, bond energy ≈ 226 kJ/mol.
- **Diagram:** Standard cube with 8 corner atoms + 6 face-centre atoms + 4 internal atoms at tetrahedral sites (1/4, 1/4, 1/4 etc.).

> **Mark-split:** 2 (description + basis), 2 (numerical constants), 2 (diagram), 1 (bonding).

---

**(b)** *The Young's modulus of silicon in the **[100] direction is 130 GPa**. Using the compliance tensor approach, calculate the Young's modulus in the **[110] and [111] directions**. Given s₁₁ = 7.68 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹, s₁₂ = −2.14 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹, s₄₄ = 12.60 × 10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — [110] derivation** (full steps as in Section 3.1):
Direction cosines (1/√2, 1/√2, 0) → product sum = 1/4.

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = \frac{s_{11} + s_{12} + s_{44}/2}{2} = \frac{7.68 - 2.14 + 6.30}{2} \times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
= \frac{11.84}{2} \times 10^{-12} = 5.92 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow E_{[110]} = 169\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — [111] derivation** (full steps as in Section 3.2):
Direction cosines (1/√3, 1/√3, 1/√3) → product sum = 1/3.

$$
\frac{1}{E_{[111]}} = s_{11} + 2s_{12} + \frac{s_{44}}{3} = (7.68 - 4.28 + 4.20)\times 10^{-12} = 7.60 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow E_{[111]} = 188\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Anisotropy ratio:**

$$
\frac{E_{[111]}}{E_{[100]}} = \frac{188}{130} \approx 1.45
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **45 % stiffness enhancement** along [111] is the design basis for high-frequency silicon resonators.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** *Explain **anisotropic wet etching** of silicon. Discuss the etch rates of (100), (110) and (111) planes in **KOH** and the formation of **V-grooves** with proper diagrams.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Definition:** Anisotropic etching uses etchants whose rate depends on the crystallographic orientation of the exposed plane. KOH, TMAH, EDP, CsOH are common alkaline etchants.
- **Etch rate hierarchy:** R(100) > R(110) > R(111) — driven by **back-bond density** (1, 2, 3 bonds/surface atom).
- **Typical KOH values (30 wt %, 80 °C):**
  - R(100) ≈ 1.0 – 1.4 µm/min
  - R(110) ≈ 0.6 – 0.8 µm/min
  - R(111) ≈ 0.003 – 0.02 µm/min
  - **Anisotropy ratio** ≈ 100 – 400 : 1.
- **Etching chemistry:** OH⁻ + Si → Si(OH)₄ + H₂↑ ; water complex dissolves.
- **V-groove formation:** Square mask opening on (100) wafer → fast etching on (100) bottom → (111) sidewalls self-limit at 54.74° to the surface → inverted pyramid.
- **Undercutting at convex corners** is fast (≥ R(100)); **concave corners stop at the (111) intersection line** — this is the basis of **beams, diaphragms, and sharp tips**.

> **Mark-split:** 2 (mechanism), 2 (rates and ratio), 2 (V-groove geometry + diagram), 1 (applications).

---

**(b)** *Compare **single-crystal silicon, polycrystalline silicon, and amorphous silicon** for MEMS applications in a tabular form. State at least **five comparison criteria**.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Single-Crystal Si | Poly-Si | Amorphous Si |
|-----------|-------------------|---------|--------------|
| **Crystal Order** | Perfect long-range order | Many small grains (10 nm – 1 µm) | No long-range order |
| **Fabrication** | Czochralski / Float-zone wafer | LPCVD @ 580–650 °C | LPCVD @ 400–500 °C or PECVD |
| **Young's Modulus** | Anisotropic (130 – 188 GPa) | Isotropic, ≈ 160 GPa | ≈ 80 GPa (lower) |
| **Etch Behaviour** | Anisotropic (KOH) | Isotropic in KOH | Isotropic |
| **Surface Roughness** | Atomically flat (as-grown) | Grain-boundary roughness | High dangling-bond density |
| **Residual Stress** | Negligible | Tensile/compressive (process-dependent) | High intrinsic stress |
| **CMOS Compatibility** | Limited (SOI used instead) | Excellent (standard gate material) | Moderate |
| **Typical MEMS Use** | Bulk micromachined cantilevers, gyroscopes | Surface-micromachined gears, mirrors | Photonic, solar, biosensing layers |
| **Cost** | High (wafer cost) | Moderate | Low |

> **Mark-split:** 2 (table completeness), 2 (correct numerical values), 1 (MEMS-application relevance), 1 (process remark), 1 (conclusion).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfalls for Question B:**
> 1. Forgetting to mention **residual stress** in polysilicon (this is THE critical MEMS issue — can cause beam curling and stiction).
> 2. Confusing **LPCVD deposition temperature** (≈ 625 °C poly vs 400 °C a-Si).
> 3. Stating that polysilicon is "perfectly isotropic" — its Young's modulus is **isotropic on average** but is locally **anisotropic within each grain**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Materials for MEMS: Silicon**

- ✅ Silicon has the **diamond cubic** structure with **a = 5.431 Å**, **8 atoms/cell**, **CN = 4**, **APF = 0.34**.
- ✅ The **APF of 0.34** is unusually low — this *openness* is what enables chemical etching.
- ✅ **Miller indices** are **reciprocals of intercepts**, reduced to smallest integers.
- ✅ The **(111) plane has the lowest planar atomic density** → slowest etch rate.
- ✅ **E[100] = 130 GPa; E[110] = 169 GPa; E[111] = 188 GPa** (memorise the trend, not just numbers).
- ✅ **KOH anisotropy ratio** R(100)/R(111) = **100 – 400 : 1** — single most quoted KTU fact.
- ✅ **V-groove angle = 54.74°** between (100) and (111).
- ✅ **Native SiO₂** is silicon's "secret weapon" — mask, insulator, sacrificial layer, optical window.
- ✅ **Polysilicon residual stress** is the #1 design headache in surface-micromachined MEMS.
- ✅ **Doping levels**: 10¹⁴ (intrinsic) → 10²⁰ cm⁻³ (n⁺/p⁺) for piezoresistors and heaters.
- ✅ **Young's modulus formula (cubic)**: 1/E = s₁₁ − 2(s₁₁ − s₁₂ − s₄₄/2)·(l₁²l₂² + l₂²l₃² + l₃²l₁²) — must memorise.
- ✅ **Planar density formula (diamond)**: N_hkl = 2 / [a² √(h² + k² + l²)].
- ✅ **Compliance values to memorise**: s₁₁ = 7.68, s₁₂ = −2.14, s₄₄ = 12.60 (all ×10⁻¹² Pa⁻¹).
- ✅ **Float-zone Si** has highest purity (used for power devices and high-Q resonators).
- ✅ **SOI wafers** are the modern standard for precision MEMS — eliminates substrate leakage and gives exact thickness control.
- ✅ **Amorphous silicon** is used in **MEMS displays (DMD/DLP), solar cells, and optical sensors** — not in mechanical resonators.
- ✅ **Three etch categories**: (i) Isotropic (HNA, XeF₂), (ii) Anisotropic wet (KOH, TMAH, EDP), (iii) DRIE (Bosch process — vertical sidewalls).
- ✅ **Bosch process** uses alternating SF₆ etch / C₄F₈ passivation to give **> 50:1 aspect ratio** — enables through-wafer vias.
- ✅ **Bandgap of Si = 1.12 eV (indirect)** — limits optical emission but is perfect for piezoresistive sensing.
- ✅ **Si density = 2.33 g/cm³** — three times lighter than steel, with comparable stiffness per unit mass.
- ✅ **CTE of Si ≈ 2.6 ppm/K** is closely matched to SiO₂ (0.5 ppm/K) and Si₃N₄ (3.2 ppm/K) — minimises thermal-stress failure.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Quick-Win:** If you forget everything else, remember this three-point summary:
> 1. **Diamond cubic, 8 atoms/cell, a = 5.431 Å.**
> 2. **Anisotropic E: 130 → 169 → 188 GPa for [100] → [110] → [111].**
> 3. **KOH etch ratio (100):(111) ≈ 100 – 400 : 1 → V-grooves at 54.74°.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicon Compounds - Silicon Nitride
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Silicon Compounds - Silicon Nitride

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)** is a covalently bonded ceramic compound of silicon and nitrogen, widely used in MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) and VLSI fabrication as a structural, dielectric, masking, and passivation layer. It exists in two principal crystallographic phases — **amorphous (a-Si₃N₄)** and **stoichiometric polycrystalline (α and β phases)** — though for KTU MEMS Module-3, the **amorphous, low-pressure chemically vapour-deposited (LPCVD) silicon nitride film** is the primary focus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEAET722 Module 3):** Silicon Nitride is studied as a *thin-film material* alongside Silicon Dioxide. You must know its (i) material properties, (ii) deposition techniques, (iii) stress behaviour, (iv) etch selectivity, and (v) MEMS-specific applications (sacrificial layer, structural layer, mask, passivation).

### 1.2 Empirical & Structural Snapshot

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Chemical Formula | **Si₃N₄** |
| Molecular Weight | **140.28 g/mol** |
| Density (amorphous) | **≈ 2.8 – 3.1 g/cm³** |
| Bond Type | Covalent (Si–N, partly ionic) |
| Bond Length Si–N | **≈ 1.74 Å** |
| Refractive Index | **≈ 2.0** (at 633 nm) |
| Dielectric Constant | **≈ 6 – 7** (LPCVD) |
| Band Gap | **≈ 5.0 – 5.3 eV** |
| Common Thin-Film Phase | Amorphous (a-Si₃N₄) |
| Colour (bulk ceramic) | Grey / black, polished = mirror |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Invisible Bodyguard of a Micro-Chip"

Imagine a tiny silicon chip is like a delicate **glass sculpture** in a museum. If you want to protect it from dust, scratches, water, and accidental knocks, you would coat it with a **clear, hard, chemically inert shell** that is also thin enough not to hide the sculpture. **Silicon nitride is that "shell"** in the MEMS world.

- It is **harder than glass** (so it resists scratches).
- It is **chemically stable** (so acid baths used to etch silicon dioxide barely touch it).
- It can be **stretched or compressed** during deposition to act as a mechanical beam or a stressed membrane.
- It **blocks sodium ions and water vapour** from creeping into transistors — acting as a *moat around a castle*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two nitrogens for every three silicons?**
> Each Si atom has 4 valence electrons; each N has 5. To satisfy the octet rule, 3 Si atoms share with 4 N atoms. This 3:4 ratio is what gives Si₃N₄ its **extraordinary chemical stability** — every bond is "fully used up," leaving no dangling bonds to react with water or oxygen at room temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Silicon Nitride is a "Workhorse" Material in MEMS — The 5 Pillars

1. **Chemical Inertness (Etch Selectivity Engine)**
   - Si₃N₄ etches **very slowly** in Buffered Oxide Etch (BOE / HF), while SiO₂ etches fast.
   - This makes Si₃N₄ the *perfect mask* for KOH and TMAH silicon bulk micromachining, and the *perfect structural layer* when SiO₂ is used as the sacrificial layer in surface micromachining.

2. **Mechanical Robustness**
   - High **Young's modulus (≈ 250–330 GPa)** and **fracture strength (≈ 6–10 GPa for thin films)**.
   - High **residual stress** — controllable from strongly **tensile** to **compressive** by deposition recipe.

3. **Tunable Intrinsic Stress**
   - LPCVD stoichiometric Si₃N₄: **tensile stress ≈ 1.0 GPa** (very useful for flat, taut membranes).
   - PECVD Si₃N₄: can be made **near-zero or compressive** by adjusting RF power, pressure, and Si/N ratio.

4. **Diffusion Barrier**
   - Excellent barrier against **Na⁺, H₂O, O₂** — used as a **final passivation layer** on integrated circuits.

5. **Thermal & Electrical Stability**
   - Withstands temperatures up to **≈ 1000 °C** in inert atmospheres.
   - High dielectric strength — used as a **capacitor dielectric** in early DRAM and MIM structures.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas in the table below are **directly applicable** to KTU 2024 Scheme numerical and derivation questions. The pipe (absolute value) symbol is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning of Each Term | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{film} = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}\,\varepsilon_{in}$ | $E_f$ = Young's modulus, $\nu_f$ = Poisson's ratio, $\varepsilon_{in}$ = intrinsic strain | Predicts thin-film stress from deposition-induced strain |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{in} = \dfrac{a_{sub} - a_{film}}{a_{film}}$ | $a_{sub}, a_{film}$ = lattice/film constants | Thermal-mismatch strain estimate |
| 3 | $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{thermal} + \sigma_{intrinsic}$ | $\sigma_{thermal} = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}\,(\alpha_f - \alpha_{sub})\,\Delta T$ | Total stress after cool-down |
| 4 | $R_{deflection} = \dfrac{E_f\,t_f^2}{6\,\sigma_f\,(1-\nu_f)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L^2}$ | $R$ = radius of curvature (Stoney), $t_f$ = film thickness, $L$ = scan length | Stoney equation — measures film stress via wafer bow |
| 5 | $t_{Si_3N_4\,\,mask} \geq \dfrac{d_{Si}}{\text{Selectivity}_{KOH:Si_3N_4}}$ | $d_{Si}$ = Si depth etched, Selectivity typically $\ge$ 1000:1 | Minimum mask thickness for KOH/TMAH etching |
| 6 | $k_{Si_3N_4} \approx 30\,\text{W/(m·K)}$ (LPCVD); $\approx 1{-}3$ W/(m·K) (PECVD) | Thermal conductivity | Thermal design of MEMS membranes |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_r^{Si_3N_4} \approx 6{-}7$ | Relative permittivity | Capacitor & parasitic capacitance estimation |
| 8 | $\rho_{Si_3N_4} \approx 2.8{-}3.1\,\text{g/cm}^3$ | Density | Mass / inertia calculation of membranes |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Stoney's Equation (Most-Tested Concept)

When a stressed film is deposited on a substrate, the substrate bends into a spherical cap of radius $R$. **Stoney's equation** quantifies the average film stress purely from the measured bow:

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s\,t_s^2}{6\,R\,t_f}$$

where $E_s$ = biaxial modulus of substrate, $t_s$ = substrate thickness, $t_f$ = film thickness, and $R$ = radius of curvature.

> **Why is this useful in KTU exams?** It lets a fab engineer *characterise* the stress of any newly deposited Si₃N₄ film using just a **profilometer or laser-based wafer-bow tool** — no nano-indenter required.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure sensor diaphragm** (LPCVD tensile Si₃N₄) — taut, fracture-resistant.
- **Silicon-on-Nitride (SON) MEMS resonators** — low acoustic loss, high Q.
- **Dielectric spacer in X-ray / EUV lithography masks.**
- **Passivation overcoat** on biomedical implants — biocompatible and hermetic.
- **Gate dielectric in early MOSFETs** before high-k dielectrics replaced it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Across Deposition Methods (KTU Most-Asked)

| Parameter | LPCVD Si₃N₄ | PECVD Si₃N₄ | Sputtered Si₃N₄ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deposition T (°C) | **700 – 850** | **200 – 400** | Room – 300 |
| Gases | SiH₂Cl₂ + NH₃ (or SiH₄ + NH₃) | SiH₄ + NH₃ + N₂ (RF plasma) | Si target in Ar + N₂ |
| Conformality | **Excellent** (step coverage ≈ 100 %) | Moderate – Good | Poor (line-of-sight) |
| Residual Stress | **High tensile (≈ 1 GPa)** | Tunable: tensile → compressive | Variable |
| H-content | Low | **High (up to 20 at. %)** | Low |
| Etch Rate in HF | Very low | Higher (H-rich etches faster) | Low |
| Pinhole Density | Very low | Higher | Highest |
| MEMS Role | Structural layer, mask | Passivation, low-T sacrificial | Optical coatings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** If the KTU question asks *"Why is LPCVD preferred for bulk-micromachined pressure sensors?"* — the model answer is the **combination of conformal step coverage + high tensile stress for taut membranes + low pinhole density + thermal stability during subsequent high-T anneals.**

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #1 — Stoney's Equation for Thin-Film Stress

**Problem context:** A silicon wafer of thickness $t_s$ is coated on one side with a thin film of thickness $t_f$ ($t_f \ll t_s$). The film exerts a biaxial stress $\sigma_f$ on the substrate, bending the wafer into a spherical cap of radius $R$. Derive the relation between $\sigma_f$ and $R$.

**Step 1 — Force and moment equilibrium on the neutral plane.**

The film pulls (or pushes) the substrate with force per unit width $F = \sigma_f \cdot t_f$. To keep the substrate flat, an equal and opposite moment $M$ must act through the substrate's cross-section about the neutral axis at mid-thickness.

$$M = \sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{t_s}{2} + \dfrac{t_f}{2}\right) \approx \sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot \dfrac{t_s}{2} \quad (t_f \ll t_s)$$

**Step 2 — Express $M$ in terms of curvature using beam-bending theory.**

For a plate of biaxial modulus $E_s / (1 - \nu_s)$, the bending moment per unit width is

$$M = \dfrac{E_s}{1 - \nu_s} \cdot \dfrac{t_s^3}{12} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $M$.**

$$\sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot \dfrac{t_s}{2} = \dfrac{E_s}{1 - \nu_s} \cdot \dfrac{t_s^3}{12} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $\sigma_f$.**

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s}{1 - \nu_s} \cdot \dfrac{t_s^2}{6\,R\,t_f}$$

**Final Result — Stoney's Equation:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s\,t_s^2}{6\,R\,t_f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU 2024 ESE Module 3. Memorise Steps 1–4 verbatim; examiners look for the moment-equilibrium argument in Step 1 and the $t_f \ll t_s$ approximation in Step 3.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #2 — Minimum Si₃N₄ Mask Thickness for KOH Etching

**Problem context:** You need to etch **$d = 400\ \mu m$** of silicon in 30 % KOH at 80 °C to form a diaphragm. Given the KOH : Si₃N₄ etch selectivity $S = 1000 : 1$, what is the minimum required Si₃N₄ mask thickness $t_{min}$?

**Step 1 — Define selectivity mathematically.**

$$S = \dfrac{\text{Etch rate of Si}}{\text{Etch rate of Si}_3\text{N}_4} = \dfrac{R_{Si}}{R_{Si_3N_4}}$$

**Step 2 — Express time to etch Si.**

$$t_{etch} = \dfrac{d}{R_{Si}}$$

**Step 3 — In that same time, the Si₃N₄ consumed is**

$$\Delta t_{Si_3N_4} = R_{Si_3N_4} \cdot t_{etch} = \dfrac{R_{Si}}{S} \cdot \dfrac{d}{R_{Si}} = \dfrac{d}{S}$$

**Step 4 — Insert the numbers.**

$$\Delta t_{Si_3N_4} = \dfrac{400\ \mu m}{1000} = 0.4\ \mu m = 400\ \text{nm}$$

**Final Result — Minimum Mask Thickness:**

$$\boxed{t_{min} \ge \dfrac{d_{Si}}{S} = 400\ \text{nm}}$$

A **deposition thickness of 500 – 600 nm LPCVD Si₃N₄** is standard in MEMS fabs to provide a safety margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the answer NOT depend on KOH concentration or temperature?** Because *selectivity* already absorbs the temperature/concentration dependence — both rates scale together. KTU examiners love this trick.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation #3 — Deflection of a Square Si₃N₄ Diaphragm Under Pressure

**Problem context:** A square LPCVD Si₃N₄ diaphragm of side $a = 1\ \text{mm}$, thickness $t = 1\ \mu\text{m}$, Young's modulus $E = 250\ \text{GPa}$, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.25$, is clamped on all four edges and subjected to a differential pressure $P = 10\ \text{kPa}$. Find the centre deflection $w_0$.

**Step 1 — Small-deflection theory for a clamped square plate under uniform pressure.**

$$w_0 = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{P\,a^4}{E\,t^3}$$

where the coefficient $\alpha$ depends on Poisson's ratio; for $\nu = 0.25$, $\alpha \approx 0.0138$ (standard reference value).

**Step 2 — Compute the bending stiffness denominator.**

$$E\,t^3 = 250 \times 10^9 \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 250 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-18} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$P\,a^4 = 10 \times 10^3 \times (1 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 10^4 \times 10^{-12} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{N·m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Ratio.**

$$\dfrac{P\,a^4}{E\,t^3} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-8}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 40\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Centre deflection.**

$$w_0 = 0.0138 \times 40\ \mu\text{m} = 0.552\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Final Result — Centre Deflection:**

$$\boxed{w_0 \approx 0.55\ \mu\text{m}}$$

> **Sanity check:** $w_0 / t = 0.55 < 0.3$, so the **small-deflection assumption is valid** (no need for large-deflection Von-Kármán plate theory). KTU examiners often award a bonus mark for this self-check.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — LPCVD Si₃N₄ Deposition & MEMS Role

<!-- Mermaid Safeguard: all node IDs are alphanumeric (a, b, c, ...); no reserved keywords used as nodes. -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bare Silicon Wafer 100 orientation] --> B[RCA Clean to Remove Native Oxide and Organics]
    B --> C[Load into LPCVD Furnace Tube at 700 to 850 C]
    C --> D[Introduce SiH2Cl2 and NH3 Gases at Low Pressure about 200 to 400 mTorr]
    D --> E[Surface Reaction SiH2Cl2 plus NH3 yields Si3N4 plus HCl plus H2]
    E --> F[Amorphous Si3N4 Film Nucleates Conformally on Wafer]
    F --> G{Application Decision}
    G -- Yes: Bulk Micromachining --> H[Si3N4 acts as Hard Mask in KOH or TMAH]
    G -- Yes: Surface Micromachining --> I[Si3N4 acts as Structural Beam or Membrane]
    G -- Yes: Passivation --> J[Si3N4 as Final Hermetic and Ion Barrier Layer]
    H --> K[Pattern with Photolithography and RIE using CF4 or SF6]
    I --> L[Release Underlying SiO2 in HF to Free the Si3N4 Structure]
    J --> M[Open Bond Pads by RIE Etching of Si3N4]
    K --> N[End: MEMS Device Ready]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Si₃N₄ in a MEMS Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Pressure P] --> B[Si3N4 Diaphragm 1 um thick]
    B --> C[Piezoresistive Bridge on Backside of Diaphragm]
    C --> D[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    D --> E[Signal Amplifier and Temperature Compensation]
    E --> F[Analog or Digital Voltage Output]
    P2[Anchored Si3N4 Rim on Pyrex or Si Substrate] -.clamps.-> B
    P3[Hermetic Vacuum Reference Cavity] -.sealed under.-> B
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **Si₃N₄ diaphragm** is the central mechanical transducer. Pressure deflects it; the piezoresistors detect strain; the bridge outputs a voltage. The **anchored Si₃N₄ rim** is the same film that was *bonded* to the substrate during fabrication — illustrating how one material can be the *structural element, the mask, and the bond frame* simultaneously.

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture — When to Use Si₃N₄ vs SiO₂

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Process Temperature Constraint?}
    Q -- High above 800 C --> R[Use LPCVD Si3N4 - Stoichiometric and Stable]
    Q -- Low below 400 C --> S[Use PECVD Si3N4 or SiO2]
    Q2{Need for Selective HF Release?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> T[Use SiO2 as Sacrificial Layer with Si3N4 Structural Layer]
    Q2 -- No --> U[Use Si3N4 as Mask Against KOH Etching of Si]
    Q3{Diffusion Barrier Required?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> V[Final Passivation Layer is Si3N4]
    Q3 -- No --> W[SiO2 is Sufficient]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** What is silicon nitride? Why is it preferred over silicon dioxide as a mask in anisotropic wet etching of silicon?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Silicon nitride (Si₃N₄) is a covalently bonded, chemically inert ceramic thin-film material deposited by LPCVD or PECVD, used in MEMS as a structural, masking, and passivation layer.
- **High Etch Selectivity in KOH/TMAH (1 mark):** The etch rate of Si₃N₄ in 30 % KOH at 80 °C is ≈ 1 – 10 nm/min, while the etch rate of Si along ⟨100⟩ is ≈ 1 µm/min — giving a selectivity of **1000 : 1 or higher**, which is far better than SiO₂ (selectivity ≈ 100 : 1 in TMAH, much worse in KOH).
- **Mechanical Strength & Pinhole Density (1 mark):** LPCVD Si₃N₄ is dense, has very low pinhole density, and high tensile stress, making it a reliable mask for the long etch times required to release thick membranes or deep cavities.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Compare LPCVD and PECVD silicon nitride in terms of deposition temperature, stress, and step coverage.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | LPCVD Si₃N₄ | PECVD Si₃N₄ |
|---|---|---|
| Deposition Temperature | **700 – 850 °C** (1 mark partial) | **200 – 400 °C** |
| Residual Stress | **High tensile (≈ 1 GPa)** | **Tunable: tensile → near-zero → compressive** |
| Step Coverage | **Excellent (≈ 100 %)** | **Moderate – Good (depends on aspect ratio)** |

> **One-sentence examiner-style conclusion:** "LPCVD Si₃N₄ is the *high-quality, high-temperature, high-stress* workhorse; PECVD is the *low-temperature, stress-tunable, conformality-limited* variant for post-metal and post-CMOS processing."

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. You must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive Stoney's equation relating thin-film stress to substrate curvature. List **two** assumptions made in the derivation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stating the setup (1 mark):** A thin film of thickness $t_f$ is deposited on a thick substrate of thickness $t_s$ ($t_f \ll t_s$). The intrinsic film stress $\sigma_f$ causes the substrate to bow into a spherical cap of radius $R$.

**Step 2 — Force-moment equilibrium (2 marks):** The biaxial force per unit width from the film is $F = \sigma_f t_f$. This must be balanced by an internal moment $M$ in the substrate about the neutral plane:

$$M = \sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot \dfrac{t_s}{2}$$

(approximation: neutral plane at mid-thickness, valid when $t_f \ll t_s$.)

**Step 3 — Plate-bending moment (2 marks):** From elastic plate theory, the bending moment per unit width is

$$M = \dfrac{E_s}{1 - \nu_s} \cdot \dfrac{t_s^3}{12} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Equating and solving (1 mark):**

$$\sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot \dfrac{t_s}{2} = \dfrac{E_s}{1 - \nu_s} \cdot \dfrac{t_s^3}{12} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s\,t_s^2}{6\,R\,t_f}$$

**Step 5 — Stating the two assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The film is much thinner than the substrate ($t_f \ll t_s$).
2. The substrate is isotropic and remains in the elastic regime.
3. (Bonus, optional) Uniform film thickness and stress across the wafer.

**Final boxed answer (already credited in step 4).**

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A **500 nm** LPCVD Si₃N₄ film is deposited on a **500 µm** silicon wafer. The wafer is measured to bow with a radius of curvature of **20 m**. Using $E_s = 180\ \text{GPa}$ and $\nu_s = 0.28$, calculate the residual stress in the Si₃N₄ film. Comment on whether the film is tensile or compressive.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plug values into Stoney's equation.**

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s\,t_s^2}{6\,R\,t_f}$$

$$E_s = 180 \times 10^9\ \text{Pa},\quad t_s = 500 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m},\quad t_f = 500 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m},\quad R = 20\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator.**

$$E_s\,t_s^2 = 180 \times 10^9 \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 180 \times 10^9 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 4.5 \times 10^4\ \text{N/m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator.**

$$6\,R\,t_f = 6 \times 20 \times 500 \times 10^{-9} = 6 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Divide.**

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^4}{6 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.5 \times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa} = 750\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Sign and physical interpretation (1 mark).** The standard sign convention in Stoney's equation gives a **positive stress** when the film is **tensile** (wafer bows *concave-up* on the film side). Therefore the LPCVD Si₃N₄ film is **highly tensile at ≈ 750 MPa**, which is typical for stoichiometric low-temperature LPCVD nitride.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\sigma_f \approx 750\ \text{MPa (tensile)}}$

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **LPCVD process** for depositing Si₃N₄ using **dichlorosilane (SiH₂Cl₂) and ammonia (NH₃)**. Write the chemical reaction, list the typical deposition parameters, and state two advantages of LPCVD Si₃N₄ over PECVD Si₃N₄.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Chemical Reaction (2 marks).**

$$3\,\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 4\,\text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{\,750{-}850\,^\circ\text{C}\,} \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4 + 6\,\text{HCl} + 6\,\text{H}_2$$

(Alternative reaction with silane: $3\,\text{SiH}_4 + 4\,\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4 + 12\,\text{H}_2$, but DCS/NH₃ is the more common industrial recipe for MEMS.)

**Step 2 — Typical Deposition Parameters (2 marks).**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Temperature | **750 – 850 °C** |
| Pressure | **200 – 400 mTorr** (low pressure) |
| DCS : NH₃ flow ratio | **1 : 3 to 1 : 6** (Si-rich → tensile) |
| Deposition rate | **≈ 3 – 5 nm/min** |
| Thickness uniformity | **± 1 – 2 % across 100 mm wafer** |

**Step 3 — Two Advantages of LPCVD over PECVD (2 marks).**

1. **Excellent step coverage / conformality** — surface-reaction-limited kinetics produce uniform films over high-aspect-ratio trenches, essential for bulk and surface micromachining.
2. **High film quality** — low H content, low pinhole density, stable dielectric properties, and consistent stoichiometry.
3. *(Bonus)* **High throughput** — batch processing of 50 – 100 wafers per furnace tube.

**Step 4 — Closing remark (1 mark):** The trade-off is the **high deposition temperature (800 °C)**, which prohibits LPCVD Si₃N₄ deposition on aluminium-metallised wafers — that is where PECVD is used.

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A MEMS pressure sensor uses an LPCVD Si₃N₄ diaphragm of dimensions $1\ \text{mm} \times 1\ \text{mm} \times 1\ \mu\text{m}$. Compute the **minimum mask thickness** required to protect the nitride during a 5-hour, 30 % KOH etch at 80 °C, where the silicon etch rate is $1\ \mu\text{m/min}$ and the selectivity (Si : Si₃N₄) is 1000 : 1. Justify why Si₃N₄ is the chosen mask over SiO₂.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total silicon depth etched.**

$$d_{Si} = R_{Si} \times t_{etch} = 1\ \mu\text{m/min} \times 5 \times 60\ \text{min} = 300\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply selectivity formula (1 mark).**

$$t_{min} = \dfrac{d_{Si}}{S} = \dfrac{300\ \mu\text{m}}{1000} = 0.3\ \mu\text{m} = 300\ \text{nm}$$

**Step 3 — Add safety factor (1 mark).** Practically, a **500 – 600 nm** LPCVD Si₃N₄ mask is deposited to provide a 2× safety margin against pinholes and process non-uniformity.

**Step 4 — Why Si₃N₄ over SiO₂ (3 marks)?**

| Criterion | Si₃N₄ | SiO₂ |
|---|---|---|
| Selectivity in KOH | **≈ 1000 : 1** (or higher) | ≈ 100 : 1 (poor in KOH; better in TMAH) |
| Pinhole density | **Very low** | Higher |
| Mechanical strength | **High (tensile) — resists blistering** | Lower; may crack under long etches |
| Hermeticity | **Excellent Na⁺ and H₂O barrier** | Good but porous to Na⁺ over time |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Si₃N₄ provides **higher selectivity, better mechanical integrity, and superior hermeticity** during prolonged KOH etching, making it the preferred hard mask for bulk-micromachined MEMS structures.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{t_{min} = 300\ \text{nm (theoretical);\ 500{-}600\ \text{nm (practical)}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $t_f \ll t_s$ assumption in Stoney's derivation** — costing 1 mark in Part-(a).
> 2. **Sign of stress** — examiners specifically want the word **"tensile"** or **"compressive"**, not just a number. A 750 MPa answer *without* the word "tensile" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Stating the KOH selectivity of SiO₂ as "high"** — it is in **TMAH**, *not* in KOH. Many students write the wrong etchant.
> 4. **Plugging wrong powers of 10** — $500\ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, not $5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$. Always double-check the prefix.
> 5. **In Q1(b), omitting the safety-factor discussion** — KTU's marking scheme reserves at least 1 mark for the practical engineering judgement.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bulletins — Read this section 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Si₃N₄ formula:** Si₃N₄ — *3 silicons, 4 nitrogens* (octet-rule satisfied).
- ✅ **Amorphous Si₃N₄** is the *thin-film* form; α- and β- phases are crystalline bulk ceramics.
- ✅ **Dielectric constant:** ≈ 6 – 7 (higher than SiO₂ ≈ 3.9).
- ✅ **Refractive index:** ≈ 2.0 — used as a quick ellipsometric thickness check.
- ✅ **LPCVD precursors:** SiH₂Cl₂ + NH₃ at 750 – 850 °C, 200 – 400 mTorr.
- ✅ **PECVD precursors:** SiH₄ + NH₃ in RF plasma at 200 – 400 °C.
- ✅ **LPCVD stress:** *intrinsically tensile* (≈ 1 GPa) → taut membranes; **PECVD stress:** *tunable* by recipe.
- ✅ **Stoney's equation:** $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s\,t_s^2}{6\,R\,t_f}$ — *memorise the numerator and denominator separately*.
- ✅ **KOH selectivity:** Si : Si₃N₄ ≈ 1000 : 1; Si : SiO₂ ≈ 100 : 1 (much worse).
- ✅ **Minimum mask thickness formula:** $t_{min} = d_{Si}/S$ — multiply result by 2 for safety in practice.
- ✅ **Uses:** Bulk-micromachining mask, structural layer, sacrificial-etch barrier, passivation, sodium barrier, capacitor dielectric.
- ✅ **Failure modes to mention in exams:** Cracking (high tensile stress), buckling (high compressive stress), pinhole-induced etching, H outgassing in PECVD films.
- ✅ **Stoney's equation limitation:** Valid only for *thin films* ($t_f \ll t_s$); fails for very thick films and multilayer stacks.
- ✅ **For numericals,** always write **units** (Pa or MPa) and **state sign** (tensile + / compressive −).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicon Dioxide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Silicon Dioxide # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)** is the most prevalent, thermodynamically stable native oxide of silicon, formed naturally or grown artificially on a silicon substrate. In the context of **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, SiO₂ is the undisputed workhorse dielectric and structural material, used as a mask, an insulator, a sacrificial layer, and a mechanical film. It is an **amorphous** (non-crystalline) glass-like material when grown thermally below 1200 °C, exhibiting a density of approximately **2.27 g/cm³**, a dielectric constant of **3.9**, a Young's modulus of **≈ 73 GPa**, and a refractive index of **1.46** at 633 nm. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In MEMS, SiO₂ is most often discussed in the context of **Surface Micromachining** as a **sacrificial layer** (sacrificial oxide) and as an **insulating/passivating layer**. Its growth via thermal oxidation is governed by the **Deal-Grove Model**, which is mandatory for KTU university examinations. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners) Imagine you are baking a chocolate cake. When the cake comes out of the oven, a thin, slightly dry, brown crust forms on the surface automatically. This crust is **not** something you added separately — it *grew* from the cake itself by reacting with the hot air. **Silicon Dioxide behaves almost identically:** - The **silicon wafer** is your "cake" (the substrate). - The **furnace** is your "oven". - **Oxygen gas** (or water vapor) flowing in the furnace is the "hot air". - The **SiO₂ layer** is the "crust" that grows *out of* the silicon itself. Just like the cake crust consumes some of the cake, growing SiO₂ **consumes the underlying silicon**. The amazing part is that this oxide layer is so versatile that engineers use it as a temporary scaffold (sacrificial), a permanent wall (insulation), a stencil (mask), or even a moving beam (structural layer in MEMS). It is, in a sense, the "Lego brick" of silicon chip manufacturing. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** When silicon is oxidized, roughly **44 % of the total oxide thickness grows *above* the original silicon surface**, and **56 % grows *into* the silicon** (consuming the Si substrate). This volumetric expansion is critical for understanding MEMS step coverage and stress. | Property | Value (Typical) | Engineering Significance | |----------|----------------|--------------------------| | Density | **2.27 g/cm³** | Lower than Si (2.33 g/cm³) — causes built-in stress | | Dielectric Constant (k) | **3.9** | Used in capacitive MEMS sensors | | Young's Modulus (E) | **73 GPa** | Mechanical stiffness of SiO₂ beams | | Breakdown Field | **≈ 10 MV/cm** | Electrical insulation capability | | Refractive Index | **1.46** | Optical MEMS / waveguide applications | | Poisson's Ratio | **0.17** | Used in stress-strain calculations | | Thermal Conductivity | **1.4 W/m·K** | Important for thermal MEMS / bolometers | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Formation Methods of SiO₂ There are **two primary routes** to form SiO₂ in a cleanroom: 1. **Thermal Oxidation** (High Quality — used in MEMS/MOS) 2. **Deposition Methods** (Lower Quality — CVD, Sputtering, Evaporation) ### 2.1.1 Thermal Oxidation The silicon wafer is placed in a high-temperature (800 – 1200 °C) furnace and exposed to an oxidizing ambient. There are two flavors: - **Dry Oxidation (O₂ ambient):** $$Si_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \longrightarrow SiO_{2(s)}$$ - **Wet Oxidation (H₂O vapor ambient):** $$Si_{(s)} + 2H_{2}O_{(g)} \longrightarrow SiO_{2(s)} + 2H_{2(g)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why two methods?** > - **Dry Oxidation** → Slow, dense, high-quality oxide (used for **gate oxides**, **tunnel oxides**, and precision MEMS structures). > - **Wet Oxidation** → Fast, less dense, contains hydroxyl groups (used for **field oxides**, **thick sacrificial layers** in MEMS). ### 2.1.2 Deposited Oxides (LTO, TEOS, PECVD Oxide) Used when thermal oxidation is **not possible** (e.g., metallized wafers, low thermal budget). These do **not** consume silicon and have inferior electrical quality, but are conformal and lower temperature. ## 2.2 The Deal-Grove Model (1965) — Foundation of Oxidation Theory This is the **single most important theoretical framework** for SiO₂ growth and is a frequent question in KTU exams. ### 2.2.1 The Three-Step Physical Picture When O₂ or H₂O molecules reach the Si surface, three sequential events occur: 1. **Transport of Oxidant:** Oxidant species diffuse from the gas bulk through the boundary layer to the **outer oxide surface**. 2. **Diffusion Through the Existing Oxide:** The species diffuse through the already-grown SiO₂ layer toward the **Si/SiO₂ interface**. 3. **Chemical Reaction at the Interface:** The species react with silicon at the Si/SiO₂ interface, forming **new SiO₂** and releasing byproducts (which must diffuse outward). > [!IMPORTANT] > The **slowest** of these three steps controls the oxidation rate. For **thin oxides (< ~200 nm)**, the **interface reaction** dominates (linear regime). For **thick oxides**, **diffusion through oxide** dominates (parabolic regime). ### 2.2.2 The Universal Deal-Grove Equation The thickness of oxide grown, $x_0$, as a function of time, $t$, is governed by: $$x_0^2 + A \cdot x_0 = B \cdot (t + \tau)$$ Where: - $x_0$ = oxide thickness (in µm or Å) - $A$ = characteristic length constant (µm) — depends on the linear rate constant - $B$ = parabolic rate constant (µm²/hr or Å²/hr) - $t$ = oxidation time (hours) - $\tau$ = time shift to account for any initial oxide thickness $x_i$ (where $\tau = \frac{x_i^2 + A \cdot x_i}{B}$) ### 2.2.3 Two Limiting Cases (High-Yield for KTU) **Case 1: Long Oxidation Time (Parabolic Regime — Diffusion Limited)** When $x_0 \gg A$, the $A \cdot x_0$ term becomes negligible, and: $$x_0^2 \approx B \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_0 \approx \sqrt{B \cdot t}$$ The growth is **parabolic in time** — the oxide grows slowly and is limited by how fast O₂/H₂O can diffuse through it. **Case 2: Short Oxidation Time (Linear Regime — Reaction-Rate Limited)** When $x_0 \ll A$, the $x_0^2$ term is negligible: $$A \cdot x_0 \approx B \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_0 \approx \frac{B}{A} \cdot t$$ Growth is **linear in time**, dominated by the chemical reaction at the Si/SiO₂ interface. The ratio $\frac{B}{A}$ is the **linear rate constant** $\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)$ — actually defined as $k_s$ (surface reaction rate constant). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **permanently valuable** for KTU calculations. Memorize the symbols and units. | Symbol | Name | Definition | Typical Units | |--------|------|------------|---------------| | $x_0$ | Oxide thickness | Final SiO₂ thickness | µm (or Å) | | $t$ | Oxidation time | Furnace exposure time | minutes or hours | | $B$ | Parabolic rate constant | Diffusion-limited growth rate | µm²/hr | | $B/A$ | Linear rate constant | Reaction-limited growth rate | µm/hr | | $A$ | Deal-Grove constant | Characteristic length | µm | | $N_1$ | Oxidant solubility | Molecules dissolved in oxide per cm³ | cm⁻³ | | $D$ | Diffusivity of oxidant in SiO₂ | cm²/s | cm²/s | | $k_s$ | Surface reaction rate constant | cm/s | cm/s | | $C^*$ | Equilibrium concentration of oxidant in oxide | cm⁻³ | cm⁻³ | | $h$ | Gas-phase mass-transfer coefficient | cm/s | cm/s | | $x_i$ | Initial oxide thickness | µm | µm | | $\tau$ | Time shift parameter | hr | hr | **Key Engineering Relations:** $$A = 2 \cdot D \cdot \left(\frac{1}{k_s} + \frac{1}{h}\right)$$ $$B = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot C^*}{N_1}$$ $$\tau = \frac{x_i^2 + A \cdot x_i}{B}$$ **Real-World Engineering Utility:** - **Semiconductor Industry:** Used to grow **gate dielectrics** (1 – 10 nm) in CMOS transistors. - **MEMS:** Used to grow **sacrificial layers** (0.5 – 2 µm) which are later etched away in HF to release suspended micro-structures (beams, membranes, cantilevers). - **Solar Cells:** Anti-reflection coatings and surface passivation. - **Biomedical MEMS:** Biocompatible coatings on neural probes. ## 2.4 Oxidation Kinetics Summary > [!NOTE] > **Activation Energy Insight (Frequent KTU Question):** > Both $B$ and $B/A$ follow an **Arrhenius relationship** with temperature $T$ (in Kelvin): > > $$B = B_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a^B}{k_B T}\right)$$ > > $$\frac{B}{A} = \left(\frac{B}{A}\right)_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a^{B/A}}{k_B T}\right)$$ > > Where typical activation energies are: > - $E_a^B \approx 1.24$ eV (Dry O₂) and $\approx 0.71$ eV (Wet H₂O) > - $E_a^{B/A} \approx 2.0$ eV (Dry O₂) and $\approx 1.96$ eV (Wet H₂O) > > **Higher temperature → faster growth. Wet oxidation is ~10× faster than dry oxidation at the same temperature.** # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Deal-Grove Equation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step) We consider a silicon wafer of area $A_{area}$ (cm²) and a growing oxide layer of thickness $x_0$ on top. ### Step 1: Define Flux of Oxidant Molecules The flux $F_1$ of oxidant reaching the **outer oxide surface** (from the gas) is driven by the concentration difference between the bulk gas and the oxide surface: $$F_1 = h \cdot (C_g - C_s)$$ Where: - $C_g$ = oxidant concentration in the bulk gas (cm⁻³) - $C_s$ = oxidant concentration at the outer oxide surface (cm⁻³) - $h$ = gas-phase mass transfer coefficient (cm/s) **Logic:** The molecules must first **diffuse across a thin boundary layer** of stagnant gas adjacent to the wafer. The higher the bulk concentration and the thinner the boundary layer, the higher the flux. ### Step 2: Fick's First Law — Diffusion Through the Oxide The flux $F_2$ of oxidant diffusing **through the existing oxide** of thickness $x_0$ is given by Fick's Law (assuming steady state, linear concentration profile): $$F_2 = D \cdot \frac{(C_s - C_i)}{x_0}$$ Where: - $C_i$ = oxidant concentration at the Si/SiO₂ interface (cm⁻³) - $D$ = diffusivity of the oxidant in SiO₂ (cm²/s) **Logic:** The concentration drops linearly from $C_s$ at the outer surface to $C_i$ at the inner interface. The thicker the oxide, the **smaller** the flux — this is the **diffusion bottleneck**. ### Step 3: Surface Reaction at the Si/SiO₂ Interface The flux $F_3$ consumed at the interface by the chemical reaction is: $$F_3 = k_s \cdot C_i$$ Where $k_s$ is the **surface reaction rate constant** (cm/s). **Logic:** The chemical reaction (Si + O₂ → SiO₂ or Si + 2H₂O → SiO₂ + 2H₂) follows first-order kinetics, consuming the arriving oxidant at a rate proportional to the local concentration $C_i$. ### Step 4: Apply Steady-State Condition At steady state, the fluxes are equal (no accumulation): $$F_1 = F_2 = F_3 = F$$ From $F_1 = F_3$: $$h \cdot (C_g - C_s) = k_s \cdot C_i \quad \cdots (i)$$ From $F_2 = F_3$: $$D \cdot \frac{(C_s - C_i)}{x_0} = k_s \cdot C_i \quad \cdots (ii)$$ **Logic:** In steady state, every molecule that arrives is consumed, so the three fluxes must be identical. ### Step 5: Solve for the Interface Flux $F$ We can rearrange (ii) to express $C_s$: $$C_s - C_i = \frac{k_s \cdot C_i \cdot x_0}{D}$$ $$C_s = C_i \cdot \left(1 + \frac{k_s \cdot x_0}{D}\right)$$ From (i): $$C_s = C_g - \frac{k_s \cdot C_i}{h}$$ Equating the two expressions for $C_s$: $$C_i \cdot \left(1 + \frac{k_s \cdot x_0}{D}\right) = C_g - \frac{k_s \cdot C_i}{h}$$ Rearranging: $$C_i \cdot \left(1 + \frac{k_s \cdot x_0}{D} + \frac{k_s}{h}\right) = C_g$$ $$C_i = \frac{C_g}{1 + \frac{k_s \cdot x_0}{D} + \frac{k_s}{h}}$$ Therefore, the interface flux is: $$F = k_s \cdot C_i = \frac{k_s \cdot C_g}{1 + \frac{k_s \cdot x_0}{D} + \frac{k_s}{h}}$$ ### Step 6: Relate Flux to Oxide Growth Rate The growth rate of the oxide layer is related to the flux by: $$\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1}$$ Where $N_1$ is the **number of oxidant molecules incorporated per unit volume of SiO₂** (cm⁻³). **Logic:** Each SiO₂ unit volume requires $N_1$ oxidant molecules. The faster the molecules arrive, the faster the oxide grows. The equilibrium concentration $C^*$ is defined as the value of $C_i$ when the system is in true equilibrium (when $C_s \to C^*$ and $k_s$ becomes irrelevant at the interface). At normal high temperatures, $h \gg k_s$, so $C^* \approx C_g \cdot N_1$ (for dilute limits). A more precise form: $$C^* = H \cdot P$$ where $H$ is Henry's law constant and $P$ is the partial pressure of the oxidant. Substituting $C_g$ in terms of $C^*$ (and absorbing constants), the growth equation becomes: $$\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{B}{2 x_0 + A}$$ Where: $$A = 2 D \left(\frac{1}{k_s} + \frac{1}{h}\right)$$ $$B = \frac{2 D C^*}{N_1}$$ ### Step 7: Integrate to Obtain the Universal Deal-Grove Equation Multiply both sides by $(2x_0 + A)$ and integrate from $0$ to $x_0$ and $0$ to $t$ (with initial oxide $x_i$): $$\int_{x_i}^{x_0} (2x_0 + A) \, dx_0 = \int_{0}^{t} B \, dt$$ $$[x_0^2 + A \cdot x_0]_{x_i}^{x_0} = B \cdot t$$ $$(x_0^2 + A x_0) - (x_i^2 + A x_i) = B \cdot t$$ $$\boxed{x_0^2 + A \cdot x_0 = B \cdot (t + \tau) \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{x_i^2 + A x_i}{B}}$$ This is the **canonical Deal-Grove equation** that defines the field of thermal oxidation. Every KTU numerical problem is solved from this equation. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem:** A silicon wafer is oxidized in **dry O₂** at **1100 °C** for **2 hours**. The parabolic rate constant is $B = 0.045$ µm²/hr and the linear rate constant is $B/A = 0.045$ µm/hr. Calculate: > > (a) The Deal-Grove constant $A$. > (b) The final oxide thickness $x_0$, assuming no initial oxide ($x_i = 0$). ### Solution (a): Compute $A$ $$A = \frac{B}{(B/A)} = \frac{0.045 \; \mu m^2/hr}{0.045 \; \mu m/hr} = 1.0 \; \mu m$$ ### Solution (b): Solve the Deal-Grove Quadratic Substitute into: $$x_0^2 + (1.0) x_0 = (0.045)(2)$$ $$x_0^2 + x_0 - 0.09 = 0$$ Apply the quadratic formula: $$x_0 = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1^2 - 4(1)(-0.09)}}{2} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1.36}}{2}$$ $$\sqrt{1.36} \approx 1.166$$ $$x_0 = \frac{-1 + 1.166}{2} = \frac{0.166}{2} \approx 0.083 \; \mu m = 83 \; nm$$ **Validation using limiting case (since $x_0 = 0.083 \ll A = 1.0$):** $$x_0 \approx \frac{B}{A} \cdot t = 0.045 \times 2 = 0.09 \; \mu m$$ The slight difference confirms the linear-regime approximation is valid. **This thickness (≈ 83 nm) is typical for a thin gate oxide in a CMOS process.** ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Dry vs Wet Oxidation Comparison | Parameter | Dry Oxidation (O₂) | Wet Oxidation (H₂O) | MEMS Implication | |-----------|--------------------|----------------------|-------------------| | Growth Rate (1000 °C) | **Slow** (~ 30 nm/hr) | **Fast** (~ 300 nm/hr) | Wet for thick sacrificial layers | | Oxide Quality | Excellent (dense) | Lower (porous, OH⁻ rich) | Dry for thin, precise structures | | Density (g/cm³) | 2.27 | ~ 2.18 – 2.21 | Wet oxide is less dense | | Refractive Index | 1.46 | 1.45 – 1.47 | Used for thickness measurement | | Dielectric Strength | High | Slightly lower | Dry for high-voltage MEMS | | Typical Use | Gate oxide, tunnel oxide | Field oxide, sacrificial layer | Choice depends on MEMS application | | Breakdown Field | ~ 10 MV/cm | ~ 8 MV/cm | Dry for capacitors | ## 3.4 Silicon Consumption During Oxidation When SiO₂ grows, it **consumes** silicon from the substrate. The volumetric ratio of SiO₂ (density 2.27) to Si (density 2.33) is approximately **2.2 : 1**. This means: - For every **1 µm of SiO₂ grown**, **≈ 0.44 µm of silicon is consumed**. - The SiO₂ surface is therefore **above** the original Si surface by: $$x_{above} = 0.44 \cdot x_0 \quad ; \quad x_{below} = 0.56 \cdot x_0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **MEMS Consequence:** In **sacrificial oxidation**, when you grow 1 µm of SiO₂ and later etch it in HF, you have effectively **carved a 0.56 µm deep cavity** into the silicon — this is the basis of techniques like **SIMOX** (Separation by IMplantation of OXygen) and **LOCOS** (Local Oxidation of Silicon). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Three-Step Deal-Grove Physical Model (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart LR G1[Gas Bulk
O2 or H2O] -->|Step 1 Transport| nodeA[Outer Oxide Surface
