Cs] nodeA -->|Step 2 Diffusion through SiO2| nodeB[Si / SiO2 Interface
Ci] nodeB -->|Step 3 Surface Reaction| nodeC[Silicon Substrate
Si consumed] nodeC -->|New SiO2 Growth| nodeA style G1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style nodeA fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style nodeB fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style nodeC fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` **Caption:** The three sequential steps of thermal oxidation: (1) gas-phase transport, (2) diffusion through oxide, (3) chemical reaction at the Si/SiO₂ interface. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SiO₂ in MEMS ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[Material Deposition Layer] oxide[SiO2 Source
Thermal or CVD] end subgraph B[Functional Roles in MEMS] role1[Masking Layer
Blocks etch or implant] role2[Insulator
Dielectric isolation] role3[Sacrificial Layer
Removed in HF] role4[Structural Layer
Beam or membrane] role5[Passivation
Surface protection] end subgraph C[Downstream MEMS Devices] dev1[Suspended Cantilever] dev2[Capacitive Pressure Sensor] dev3[RF MEMS Switch] dev4[Biosensor with Functionalized Surface] end oxide --> role1 oxide --> role2 oxide --> role3 oxide --> role4 oxide --> role5 role1 --> dev1 role3 --> dev1 role2 --> dev2 role4 --> dev2 role3 --> dev3 role5 --> dev4 style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` **Caption:** A single SiO₂ film can simultaneously serve five different functions in a MEMS chip — masking, insulation, sacrificial release, structural beam, or passivation. ## 4.3 Concentration Profile During Thermal Oxidation ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Bulk Gas
Concentration Cg] -->|Boundary Layer| P2[Oxide Surface
Concentration Cs] P2 -->|Linear Drop| P3[Si / SiO2 Interface
Concentration Ci] P3 -->|Reaction| P4[Silicon Substrate
Reacted] style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style P2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style P3 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style P4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` **Caption:** Concentration gradient of oxidant across the growing oxide — drives the diffusion flux $F_2 = D(C_s - C_i)/x_0$. ## 4.4 Sacrificial Oxide Process Flow in Surface Micromachining ```mermaid flowchart LR step1[Step 1: Start with Si Wafer] --> step2[Step 2: Thermally Grow SiO2 Sacrificial Layer of 1 to 2 um] step2 --> step3[Step 3: Deposit Structural SiO2 on Top of Sacrificial Layer] step3 --> step4[Step 4: Photolithography and Patterning of Structural Layer] step4 --> step5[Step 5: HF Vapor or Liquid Etch Removes Sacrificial SiO2] step5 --> step6[Step 6: Released MEMS Structure Cantilever or Beam] style step1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style step2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style step3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style step4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 step5 -->|Critical Release Step| step6 style step5 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style step6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` **Caption:** A typical surface micromachining process using SiO₂ as the sacrificial layer — etched in HF to release the suspended mechanical structure. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the Deal-Grove Model of Thermal Oxidation. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** **Model Answer:** The Deal-Grove Model (1965) is a mathematical model that describes the kinetics of thermal oxidation of silicon. It states that the growth of SiO₂ on a silicon wafer proceeds through three sequential steps: 1. **Transport of oxidant** (O₂ or H₂O) from the bulk gas to the outer oxide surface. 2. **Diffusion of oxidant** through the existing oxide layer to the Si/SiO₂ interface. 3. **Chemical reaction** of the oxidant with silicon at the interface to form new SiO₂. The mathematical form of the model is: $$x_0^2 + A \cdot x_0 = B \cdot (t + \tau)$$ where $x_0$ is oxide thickness, $A$ and $B$ are the Deal-Grove and parabolic rate constants, $t$ is time, and $\tau$ is a time shift to account for any pre-existing oxide. ### Question 2: Differentiate between Dry and Wet Thermal Oxidation of Silicon. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Dry Oxidation (O₂) | Wet Oxidation (H₂O) | |-----------|--------------------|----------------------| | Chemical Reaction | Si + O₂ → SiO₂ | Si + 2H₂O → SiO₂ + 2H₂ | | Growth Rate | Slow (e.g., 30 nm/hr at 1000 °C) | Fast (~ 10× faster) | | Oxide Density | 2.27 g/cm³ (dense) | ~ 2.18 g/cm³ (porous) | | Quality | Excellent (used for gate oxides) | Good (used for field oxides) | | Typical Application | Thin, precise dielectrics | Thick sacrificial layers in MEMS | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick:** *Wet = quick but rough; Dry = slow but clean.* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A: [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 3 Model Paper]** **(a) Derive the Deal-Grove equation for thermal oxidation of silicon, clearly explaining the three flux steps involved. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Three Flux Model Setup** The thermal oxidation of Si is described by three sequential fluxes at steady state: - $F_1$ = flux from bulk gas to the outer oxide surface (gas-phase transport) - $F_2$ = flux through the existing oxide (Fickian diffusion) - $F_3$ = flux consumed at the Si/SiO₂ interface (chemical reaction) **Step 2: Governing Equations** $$F_1 = h (C_g - C_s)$$ $$F_2 = D \cdot \frac{(C_s - C_i)}{x_0}$$ $$F_3 = k_s \cdot C_i$$ **Step 3: Steady-State Equality** At steady state, $F_1 = F_2 = F_3 = F$. **Step 4: Solve for Flux F** Eliminating $C_s$ and $C_i$ between the three equations: $$F = \frac{k_s C_g}{1 + \frac{k_s x_0}{D} + \frac{k_s}{h}}$$ **Step 5: Convert Flux to Growth Rate** $$\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1} = \frac{B}{2x_0 + A}$$ where $A = 2D\left(\frac{1}{k_s} + \frac{1}{h}\right)$ and $B = \frac{2DC^*}{N_1}$. **Step 6: Integration** Integrating from initial thickness $x_i$ to final thickness $x_0$ over time $t$: $$\int_{x_i}^{x_0} (2x_0 + A) \, dx_0 = \int_0^t B \, dt$$ $$x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau) \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{x_i^2 + A x_i}{B}$$ This is the **Deal-Grove equation**. --- **(b) A silicon wafer is oxidized in wet O₂ at 1000 °C for 4 hours. Given $B = 0.30$ µm²/hr and $B/A = 0.12$ µm/hr, with an initial oxide of 0.05 µm, calculate the final oxide thickness. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute A** $$A = \frac{B}{(B/A)} = \frac{0.30}{0.12} = 2.5 \; \mu m$$ **Step 2: Compute Time Shift $\tau$** $$\tau = \frac{x_i^2 + A x_i}{B} = \frac{(0.05)^2 + (2.5)(0.05)}{0.30}$$ $$\tau = \frac{0.0025 + 0.125}{0.30} = \frac{0.1275}{0.30} = 0.425 \; \text{hr}$$ **Step 3: Compute Effective Time** $$t + \tau = 4 + 0.425 = 4.425 \; \text{hr}$$ **Step 4: Solve the Deal-Grove Quadratic** $$x_0^2 + 2.5 x_0 = 0.30 \times 4.425 = 1.3275$$ $$x_0^2 + 2.5 x_0 - 1.3275 = 0$$ Apply quadratic formula: $$x_0 = \frac{-2.5 \pm \sqrt{(2.5)^2 + 4(1.3275)}}{2} = \frac{-2.5 + \sqrt{6.25 + 5.31}}{2}$$ $$x_0 = \frac{-2.5 + \sqrt{11.56}}{2} = \frac{-2.5 + 3.4}{2} = \frac{0.9}{2} = 0.45 \; \mu m$$ **Final Answer:** The final oxide thickness is **≈ 0.45 µm (450 nm)**. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice for Internal Option): [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) Explain with neat sketches the role of SiO₂ as (i) a sacrificial layer, (ii) a mask, and (iii) an insulator in MEMS fabrication. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) SiO₂ as a Sacrificial Layer (Surface Micromachining):** SiO₂ is grown thermally (1 – 2 µm thick) on a Si wafer. A structural material (polysilicon, SiO₂, or Si₃N₄) is deposited on top and patterned. The wafer is then immersed in **concentrated or vapor HF**, which selectively etches the underlying SiO₂ without attacking the structural layer. This releases the suspended MEMS structure (cantilever, bridge, or membrane). The "air gap" left behind defines the mechanical behavior of the device. > **Application:** Accelerometers (Analog Devices ADXL), gyroscopes, RF switches. **(ii) SiO₂ as a Mask:** SiO₂ is impervious to many dopants (boron, phosphorus, arsenic) and to certain etchants (KOH, TMAH for Si etching). By patterning the oxide using photolithography and HF etching, engineers can selectively expose regions of the silicon for **doping** or **bulk micromachining**. The unexposed regions are protected. > **Application:** Selective KOH etching of Si to form V-grooves, pyramidal pits, or membranes. **(iii) SiO₂ as an Insulator:** With a dielectric constant of **3.9** and breakdown field of **≈ 10 MV/cm**, thermally grown SiO₂ is an excellent electrical insulator. It is used to isolate conductive layers in multi-level MEMS structures, isolate capacitive sense electrodes, and passivate surfaces. > **Application:** Capacitive pressure sensors, gyroscopes, isolation in CMOS-MEMS integration. --- **(b) A 0.2 µm SiO₂ layer is to be grown on a bare silicon wafer using dry oxidation at 1100 °C. The rate constants are $B = 0.045$ µm²/hr and $B/A = 0.045$ µm/hr. Estimate the oxidation time required. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute A** $$A = \frac{B}{(B/A)} = \frac{0.045}{0.045} = 1.0 \; \mu m$$ **Step 2: Apply the Deal-Grove Equation with $x_i = 0$** $$x_0^2 + A x_0 = B t$$ $$(0.2)^2 + (1.0)(0.2) = (0.045) t$$ $$0.04 + 0.2 = 0.045 t$$ $$0.24 = 0.045 t$$ $$t = \frac{0.24}{0.045} \approx 5.33 \; \text{hr}$$ **Final Answer:** The oxidation time required is approximately **5.33 hours** (≈ 5 hours 20 minutes). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often mix up units (Å vs nm vs µm). Always convert to a **single unit** before solving. 1 µm = 10,000 Å = 1,000 nm. > 2. **Forgetting $\tau$:** When an initial oxide $x_i$ is given, the time shift $\tau$ **must be included**. Many students omit it and lose 2 – 3 marks. > 3. **Wrong Sign in Quadratic:** $x_0$ must be **positive** — always take the **positive root** in the quadratic formula. > 4. **Confusing B with B/A:** $B$ has units of **µm²/hr** (parabolic), $B/A$ has units of **µm/hr** (linear). Memorize the units. > 5. **Linear vs Parabolic:** If $x_0 \ll A$ (e.g., thin oxide), use the linear approximation $x_0 \approx (B/A) t$. If $x_0 \gg A$, use $x_0 \approx \sqrt{B t}$. KTU examiners love testing which regime applies. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before KTU Exam:** - [x] **SiO₂** is amorphous when grown thermally below **1200 °C**. - [x] **Density = 2.27 g/cm³**, **Dielectric Constant = 3.9**, **Young's Modulus ≈ 73 GPa**. - [x] Growing SiO₂ **consumes silicon** — 1 µm of oxide consumes **0.56 µm of Si**. - [x] **Dry Oxidation:** Si + O₂ → SiO₂ — slow, high quality, used for thin oxides. - [x] **Wet Oxidation:** Si + 2H₂O → SiO₂ + 2H₂ — fast, used for thick sacrificial layers. - [x] **Deal-Grove Equation (Universal Law):** $x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$. - [x] **Linear regime (thin oxide, $x_0 \ll A$):** $x_0 \approx (B/A) t$ — reaction-rate limited. - [x] **Parabolic regime (thick oxide, $x_0 \gg A$):** $x_0 \approx \sqrt{B t}$ — diffusion limited. - [x] **MEMS Role 1:** Sacrificial layer (etched in **HF**) → releases suspended structures. - [x] **MEMS Role 2:** Mask for **doping** and **KOH/TMAH etching** of Si. - [x] **MEMS Role 3:** Insulator (dielectric) in **capacitive** MEMS sensors. - [x] **MEMS Role 4:** Passivation layer for **biocompatibility** and **hermetic sealing**. - [x] **Wet oxidation is ~10× faster than dry** at the same temperature. - [x] **Activation Energies (high-yield fact):** $E_a^B \approx 1.24$ eV (dry) / $0.71$ eV (wet). - [x] **Always include $\tau$** when $x_i > 0$. - [x] **Always take the positive root** of the quadratic — oxide thickness cannot be negative. - [x] **Unit conversion:** 1 µm = 1000 nm = 10,000 Å — be consistent. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** > Module 3 (MEMS Materials) questions on **SiO₂** almost always test: > 1. The **Deal-Grove equation** (derivation or numerical). > 2. **Dry vs Wet** oxidation comparison. > 3. **MEMS applications** (sacrificial, mask, insulator). > > Practice at least **2 numerical problems** before the exam and you will confidently secure full marks in this module.

topics.title: Poly Silicon
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Poly Silicon

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polysilicon (polycrystalline silicon, poly-Si)** is a high-purity, polycrystalline form of silicon composed of multiple small silicon crystallites (grains) of varying crystallographic orientations separated by grain boundaries. It is deposited as a thin film onto a substrate (typically a silicon wafer with a thermal SiO₂ layer) and serves as the **primary structural and functional material** in surface-micromachined MEMS devices.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (OEAET722 – MEMS, Module 3: *Materials for MEMS*), polysilicon is treated as the **workhorse structural material** of the surface micromachining process, analogous to steel in conventional mechanical engineering. It is deposited using **Low-Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (LPCVD)** and is selectively patterned and released by sacrificial etching of the underlying oxide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Polysilicon is covered under *Materials for MEMS* alongside single-crystal silicon, silicon dioxide, silicon nitride, and polymers. Students must clearly distinguish between (a) bulk single-crystal Si (substrate) and (b) deposited poly-Si (thin-film structural layer). The 14-mark questions typically focus on deposition kinetics, residual stress control, doping, and sacrificial release.

## 1.2 Physical & Chemical Identity

| Parameter | Value (Typical) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical formula | Si (polycrystalline) | Same as single-crystal Si |
| Atomic number | **14** | Group IV semiconductor |
| Crystal structure | Diamond cubic (within grains) | Random grain orientation |
| Density | **2.32 g/cm³** | Slightly lower than c-Si due to grain boundaries |
| Melting point | **1414 °C** | Determines process thermal budget |
| Bandgap (Eg) | **1.12 eV** | At 300 K, indirect bandgap |
| Refractive index (n) | **3.5 – 4.0** | At 632.8 nm wavelength |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** The density of polysilicon (**2.32 g/cm³**) is *identical* to single-crystal silicon. Students often wrongly assume that the grain boundaries reduce density — in practice, the density difference is less than **1 %**, and is generally neglected in KTU problems.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Bricks Without Mortar"

Imagine building a wall out of tiny, perfectly cut **silicon bricks (grains)** of various sizes and orientations, then gluing them together along their edges (**grain boundaries**). Each individual brick behaves exactly like a perfect single-crystal silicon block — strong, stiff, and predictable. However, the **glue lines (grain boundaries)** are disordered, defect-rich regions that:
- Trap dopant atoms (electrical carriers get "stuck")
- Scatter moving electrons (reducing mobility)
- Act as paths for impurity diffusion
- Are mechanically weaker than the grain interior

This is why the **electrical properties** of polysilicon depend strongly on grain size, while the **mechanical properties** (Young's modulus, hardness) remain remarkably close to those of single-crystal silicon.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a slab of granite (polycrystalline rock) versus a slab of quartz crystal (single crystal). The granite is *almost* as hard as quartz, but it is *electrically* very different from a perfect quartz crystal — exactly analogous to poly-Si vs. c-Si.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deposition of Polysilicon — LPCVD Process

The **dominant** deposition route taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus is **Low-Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (LPCVD)** using silane (SiH₄) as the precursor.

### 2.1.1 Reaction Chemistry

The thermal decomposition of silane on a heated substrate yields polycrystalline silicon:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SiH}_4\text{(g)} &\xrightarrow{\;\Delta\;} \text{Si(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{(g)}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Temperature window for poly-Si:** 580 °C – 650 °C
- **Below 580 °C:** Amorphous silicon (a-Si) is deposited.
- **Above 650 °C:** Epitaxial (single-crystal) growth on c-Si substrates, *not* polysilicon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Point:** If the KTU question asks *"At what temperature is polysilicon deposited by LPCVD?"*, the safe answer is **600 °C ± 20 °C** using **silane at ~25–150 Pa (0.2–1.0 Torr)**. The use of **disilane (Si₂H₆)** allows deposition at **450–550 °C** with finer grain structure.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Gas transport:** SiH₄ molecules diffuse from the gas bulk to the wafer surface through a low-pressure boundary layer.
2. **Adsorption:** SiH₄ molecules adsorb onto the substrate surface (physisorption followed by chemisorption).
3. **Surface reaction:** Si–H bonds break sequentially; H atoms recombine into H₂ and desorb; Si atoms incorporate into the growing film.
4. **Nucleation & grain growth:** At 600 °C, many nuclei form simultaneously, leading to **columnar grain morphology** with grains extending through the full film thickness.
5. **By-product removal:** H₂ gas is pumped away; incomplete removal leads to trapped H, which affects stress.

### 2.1.3 Dopant Incorporation (In-situ Doping)

For conductive polysilicon, dopant gases are mixed with silane during deposition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SiH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2 &\rightarrow \text{Si} + 4\,\text{H}_2 \\
\text{SiH}_4 + \text{PH}_3 &\rightarrow \text{Si:P} + \tfrac{5}{2}\,\text{H}_2 \quad (\text{n-type}) \\
3\,\text{SiH}_4 + 2\,\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 &\rightarrow 3\,\text{Si:B} + 6\,\text{H}_2 \quad (\text{p-type})
\end{aligned}
$$

The two alternative doping routes are **ion implantation** (post-deposition) and **thermal diffusion** from a doped-oxide source. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasises that **in-situ doping gives the most uniform dopant distribution**, especially along grain boundaries.

## 2.2 Microstructure of Polysilicon

Polysilicon microstructure is governed by the **Structure Zone Model (SZM)** originally proposed by Movchan & Demchishin and extended by Thornton.

| Zone | Temperature (T/Tm) | Morphology | Relevant T for poly-Si (Tm ≈ 1687 K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zone 1 | T/Tm < 0.3 | Porous, fibrous | < 506 K (not used) |
| Zone 2 | 0.3 < T/Tm < 0.5 | Columnar grains | 506 K – 843 K (a-Si region) |
| Zone 3 | T/Tm > 0.5 | Equiaxed, dense grains | **> 843 K → ~570 °C+ (poly-Si region)** |

- **Grain size** in as-deposited LPCVD poly-Si at 620 °C: typically **0.1 – 0.5 µm**.
- **Grain boundaries** are typically **0.5 – 1.0 nm** wide and contain disordered Si atoms, dangling bonds, and segregated dopants.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Concept:** Annealing at **1000 – 1100 °C** in N₂ for 1 hour causes **secondary grain growth**, increasing the average grain size to **1 – 3 µm**. This dramatically *lowers* the residual stress and the tensile strength variation — a critical step before MEMS release.

## 2.3 Mechanical Properties of Polysilicon

Polysilicon is used as a **structural layer** in MEMS, so its mechanical behavior is critical.

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value | Single-crystal Si (for comparison) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | E | **160 – 180 GPa** | 168 – 190 GPa |
| Poisson's ratio | ν | **0.22 – 0.28** | 0.27 – 0.28 |
| Yield strength (fracture) | σ_y | **1.0 – 3.0 GPa** | ~7 GPa (ideal) |
| Density | ρ | **2.32 g/cm³** | 2.328 g/cm³ |
| Residual stress (as-deposited) | σ_r | **−300 to +500 MPa** (compressive → tensile) | — |
| Fracture toughness | K_IC | **1.0 – 1.5 MPa·m^{1/2}** | 0.9 – 1.1 MPa·m^{1/2} |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU emphasize Young's modulus ≈ 160 GPa?** Because the most common KTU problem type is: *"A polysilicon cantilever of length L, width w, and thickness t is loaded by a transverse force F. Find the tip deflection."* The answer requires **E = 160 GPa** (or 169 GPa, depending on the textbook adopted by the university). For board-answer safety, write **E_poly-Si ≈ 160 GPa**.

## 2.4 Electrical Properties of Polysilicon

The resistivity of polysilicon is **orders of magnitude higher** than that of single-crystal silicon at the same dopant concentration, because grain boundaries act as potential barriers to carrier transport.

The **grain-boundary trapping model** (Seto's model) gives the effective resistivity as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho \;=\; \left(\dfrac{L_g}{q\,\mu\,n\,A_g}\right) \,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q\,V_b}{kT}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $L_g$ = average grain size (m)
- $A_g$ = grain cross-sectional area (m²)
- $q$ = electronic charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- $\mu$ = carrier mobility (m²/V·s)
- $n$ = free carrier concentration (m⁻³)
- $V_b$ = grain-boundary potential barrier (V)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### Typical Resistivity Values (at 300 K)

| Dopant level (cm⁻³) | Resistivity (undoped) | Heavily doped (n⁺ / p⁺) |
|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic | **10⁶ – 10⁸ Ω·cm** | — |
| Lightly doped (10¹⁵) | **10 – 100 Ω·cm** | — |
| Heavily doped (10¹⁹ – 10²⁰) | — | **0.001 – 0.01 Ω·cm** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Un-doped poly-Si is *almost an insulator* (resistivity ≈ 10⁶ Ω·cm, comparable to undoped glass), while *in-situ P-doped* poly-Si at 10²⁰ cm⁻³ has resistivity comparable to a metal (≈ 0.001 Ω·cm). This dual personality is exactly why poly-Si is used as **both structural material AND electrical interconnect** in MEMS.

## 2.5 Residual Stress in Polysilicon

This is a **14-mark favourite** in KTU examinations.

### 2.5.1 Origin of Stress

Residual stress in LPCVD polysilicon originates from two sources:

1. **Intrinsic (growth) stress $\sigma_{\text{int}}$** — caused by incomplete H₂ desorption, defect incorporation, and grain-boundary rearrangement during film growth.
2. **Thermal (extrinsic) stress $\sigma_{\text{th}}$** — caused by mismatch between the **thermal expansion coefficients (CTE)** of the polysilicon film and the silicon substrate upon cooling from deposition temperature $T_d$ to room temperature $T_r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{th}} \;=\; \dfrac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}\,\bigl(\alpha_f - \alpha_s\bigr)\,\bigl(T_d - T_r\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

For poly-Si on c-Si substrate, $\alpha_f \approx \alpha_s$ (both ~2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K), so $\sigma_{\text{th}}$ is small. The dominant term is **intrinsic stress**.

### 2.5.2 Sign Convention in KTU Problems

| Stress sign | Effect on released structure | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Tensile (+)** | Structure remains flat, taut | High T_d, low deposition pressure, annealed |
| **Compressive (−)** | Structure buckles, wrinkles, or curls | Low T_d, high pressure, contaminants |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often write compressive stress as "negative" without stating the sign convention. The KTU 2024 evaluator awards the mark only if the *direction* (tensile vs compressive) and the *mechanical consequence* (buckling vs flatness) are both stated.

## 2.6 Stress Gradient — Why Released Beams Curl Up

Real released polysilicon beams exhibit a **gradient of stress through the film thickness**, not just uniform stress. This causes released cantilevers to curl upward (or downward) with a radius of curvature $R$ given by **Stoney's equation** (modified for thin films):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{6\,\sigma_{\text{grad}}\,t_f}{E_s\,t_s^{\,2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $t_f$ = film (polysilicon) thickness
- $t_s$ = substrate thickness
- $E_s$ = substrate Young's modulus
- $\sigma_{\text{grad}}$ = difference in stress between the top and bottom of the poly-Si film

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Consolidated)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SiH}_4 \rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\,\text{H}_2$ | LPCVD deposition chemistry | — |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}\,(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\,\Delta T$ | Thermal stress in thin film | Pa |
| 3 | $E_{\text{poly-Si}} \approx 160 \text{ GPa}$ | Young's modulus (K TU default) | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_y \approx 1\text{–}3 \text{ GPa}$ | Fracture strength | Pa |
| 5 | $\rho = \dfrac{L_g}{q\,\mu\,n\,A_g}\exp(qV_b/kT)$ | Seto's grain-boundary resistivity | Ω·m |
| 6 | $1/R = 6\,\sigma_{\text{grad}}\,t_f / (E_s\,t_s^2)$ | Stoney's curvature formula | m⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\delta = \dfrac{4\,F\,L^3}{E\,w\,t^3}$ | Cantilever tip deflection (uniform load) | m |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{E\,w\,t^3}{4\,L^3}$ | Cantilever spring constant | N/m |
| 9 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Resonant frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $\alpha_{\text{Si}} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \,/\text{K}$ | CTE of silicon | K⁻¹ |
| 11 | $\rho_{\text{poly}} = 2.32 \text{ g/cm}^3$ | Density | kg/m³ |
| 12 | $\omega_{\text{deposition}} = 580\text{–}650\,°\text{C}$ | LPCVD poly-Si process window | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For all KTU 2024 numerical answers:** Always quote Young's modulus as **E = 160 GPa** unless the question explicitly states otherwise, and **always express answers in SI units (Pa, m, N)**. Convert µm and GPa carefully: $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cantilever Deflection of a Polysilicon Beam (Classic KTU Problem)

### Problem Setup

A polysilicon cantilever beam has:
- Length $L = 200 \;\mu\text{m}$
- Width $w = 20 \;\mu\text{m}$
- Thickness $t = 2 \;\mu\text{m}$
- Point load $F$ at the free end (transverse to the beam axis)
- Young's modulus $E = 160$ GPa
- Density $\rho = 2320$ kg/m³

**Find:** (a) Spring constant $k$ of the cantilever. (b) Resonant frequency $f_0$ of the fundamental mode. (c) Maximum allowable load $F_{\max}$ if fracture strength $\sigma_y = 1.5$ GPa.

### Part (a) — Spring Constant

The spring constant of an end-loaded cantilever (Euler–Bernoulli beam theory) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \;=\; \dfrac{3\,E\,I}{L^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the second moment of area for a rectangular cross-section is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I \;=\; \dfrac{w\,t^3}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I \;=\; \dfrac{(20 \times 10^{-6})\,(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{(20 \times 10^{-6})\,(8 \times 10^{-18})}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-22}}{12} \;=\; 1.333 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \;=\; \dfrac{3 \times (160 \times 10^{9}) \times (1.333 \times 10^{-23})}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^3} \;=\; \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{-12}}{8 \times 10^{-15}} \;=\; 800 \;\text{N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{k = 0.80 \;\text{N/m (or } 800 \;\text{mN/m)}}$

### Part (b) — Resonant Frequency

The effective mass of a cantilever undergoing its first bending mode is $m_{\text{eff}} = 0.24\,\rho\,w\,t\,L$ (a standard result from Rayleigh's method).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 0.24 \times 2320 \times (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \;=\; 0.24 \times 2320 \times 8 \times 10^{-15}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 0.24 \times 1.856 \times 10^{-11} \;=\; 4.454 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

The fundamental resonant frequency is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{\text{eff}}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{0.80}{4.454 \times 10^{-12}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{k}{m_{\text{eff}}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.80}{4.454 \times 10^{-12}} \;=\; 1.796 \times 10^{11}\;\text{s}^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1.796 \times 10^{11}} \;=\; 4.238 \times 10^{5}\;\text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 \;=\; \dfrac{4.238 \times 10^{5}}{2\pi} \;=\; \dfrac{4.238 \times 10^{5}}{6.283} \;\approx\; 6.745 \times 10^{4}\;\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{f_0 \approx 67.5 \;\text{kHz}}$

### Part (c) — Maximum Allowable Load

Maximum bending stress at the fixed end of an end-loaded cantilever:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{6\,F\,L}{w\,t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_y$ and solving for $F_{\max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_y\,w\,t^2}{6\,L} \;=\; \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10^{9}) \times (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{9} \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{-12}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-7}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{F_{\max} = 100 \;\mu\text{N}}$

## 3.2 Derivation: Stress-Gradient Curling of a Released Beam (Stoney's Equation)

Consider a polysilicon film of thickness $t_f$ deposited on a thick silicon substrate of thickness $t_s$. Suppose the stress at the top of the poly-Si film is $\sigma_1$ and at the bottom (film–substrate interface) is $\sigma_2$, with $\sigma_2 > \sigma_1$. After release, the beam curls into an arc of radius $R$.

**Step 1 — Net biaxial stress:** The average stress is $\bar{\sigma} = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)/2$. The *gradient* is $\sigma_{\text{grad}} = \sigma_2 - \sigma_1$.

**Step 2 — Bending moment per unit width:** The differential stress creates a moment per unit width $M$ that bends the released beam:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \;=\; \int_0^{t_f} \sigma(z)\,z\,dz \;\approx\; \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{grad}}\,t_f^{\,2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate to bending stiffness (per unit width):** The curvature $1/R$ of a beam under moment $M$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{M}{E_f\,I_f} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{grad}}\,t_f^{\,2}\,/\,2}{E_f \cdot t_f^{\,3}\,/\,12} \;=\; \dfrac{6\,\sigma_{\text{grad}}}{E_f\,t_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Correction for substrate constraint (Stoney's full form):** When the substrate is *not* removed, the film–substrate couple bends the substrate as well, and the curvature is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{6\,\sigma_{\text{grad}}\,t_f}{E_s\,t_s^{\,2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **released case** (substrate etched away underneath the beam), use the simpler equation from Step 3.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Poly-Si vs. c-Si vs. a-Si

| Property | Single-crystal Si (c-Si) | Polysilicon (poly-Si) | Amorphous Si (a-Si) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atomic order | Long-range | Long-range *within* grains only | Short-range only (≤ 1 nm) |
| Grain size | ∞ (single crystal) | 0.1 – 3 µm | N/A |
| Young's modulus (GPa) | 168 – 190 | **160 – 180** | 80 – 130 (lower) |
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.328 | **2.32** | ~2.20 |
| Resistivity, undoped (Ω·cm) | ~10³ | **10⁶ – 10⁸** | 10⁹ – 10¹² |
| Resistivity, n⁺ doped (Ω·cm) | 0.001 | **0.001 – 0.01** | 0.01 – 0.1 |
| Deposition T (LPCVD) | 900 – 1100 °C (epi) | **580 – 650 °C** | 400 – 550 °C |
| Surface roughness | < 0.1 nm | 5 – 50 nm | < 1 nm |
| MEMS role | Substrate, bulk microred. | **Structural layer, sacrificial alt.** | Solar cells, TFTs |

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Surface Micromachining Process Flow Using Polysilicon

    A5[Step 5: Reactive Ion Etching RIE of poly-Si using SF6 or Cl2 plasma]
    A6[Step 6: Photoresist strip using acetone or oxygen plasma]
    A7[Step 7: Sacrificial etch in HF to release poly-Si structure]
    A8[Step 8: Critical point drying to prevent stiction]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is polysilicon? Why is it preferred as a structural material in surface micromachining?**

**Model Answer:**
Polysilicon is a polycrystalline form of silicon consisting of small crystallites (grains) separated by grain boundaries, typically deposited by LPCVD at 580 – 650 °C using silane (SiH₄). It is preferred in surface micromachining because:
1. It has high Young's modulus (**E ≈ 160 GPa**) — comparable to single-crystal Si.
2. It is **CMOS-compatible**, enabling monolithic integration of MEMS + circuitry.
3. Its electrical resistivity is **tunable over 9 orders of magnitude** (10⁶ → 10⁻³ Ω·cm) by doping.
4. It can be deposited in thicknesses from 0.1 µm to > 10 µm with **good uniformity**.
5. It can be selectively etched using **RIE (SF₆ or Cl₂)** and released by **HF sacrificial etch** of underlying SiO₂.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between in-situ doping and ion-implantation doping of polysilicon.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | In-situ Doping | Ion Implantation |
|---|---|---|
| **When** | During deposition | After deposition |
| **Method** | Mix dopant gas (PH₃, B₂H₆) with SiH₄ | Accelerate dopant ions into film |
| **Uniformity** | Excellent — uniform along grain boundaries | Non-uniform; needs annealing to redistribute |
| **Damage** | None | Ion-bombardment damage, must be annealed |
| **Dopant concentration** | 10¹⁷ – 10²⁰ cm⁻³ (easily) | 10¹⁴ – 10²¹ cm⁻³ (very flexible) |
| **Process step** | Single step | Two steps (implant + anneal) |
| **Used for** | Heavily doped Si, heaters, interconnects | Precise shallow junctions, piezoresistors |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — 14-Mark Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the LPCVD process for the deposition of polysilicon. Discuss the effect of deposition temperature on the microstructure and properties of the deposited film.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polysilicon cantilever beam of length 400 µm, width 30 µm, and thickness 3 µm is fabricated by surface micromachining. The Young's modulus is 160 GPa and the density is 2320 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the spring constant, (ii) the resonant frequency, and (iii) the tip deflection when a 50 µN load is applied at the free end.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**LPCVD Process:**
- LPCVD stands for **Low-Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition**, performed at pressures of **25 – 150 Pa** (0.2 – 1.0 Torr) in a hot-wall furnace.
- **Precursor:** Silane (SiH₄), often diluted in N₂ or H₂ carrier gas.
- **Reaction:** $\text{SiH}_4 \rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\,\text{H}_2$
- **Temperature window:** **580 – 650 °C** yields polycrystalline silicon.

**Effect of deposition temperature:**

| Temperature | Resulting film | Grain structure | Stress |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 580 °C | Amorphous silicon (a-Si) | No grains | High H content, unstable |
| 580 – 620 °C | Fine-grained poly-Si | 0.1 – 0.3 µm columnar | Compressive to slightly tensile |
| 620 – 650 °C | Coarse-grained poly-Si | 0.3 – 1 µm columnar | Tensile |
| > 650 °C | Epitaxial single crystal on c-Si | — | — |

**Key points for full marks (7):**
1. Identify the process as LPCVD with silane. (1 mark)
2. Write the deposition chemistry. (1 mark)
3. State the temperature window. (1 mark)
4. Explain amorphous vs polycrystalline transition temperature (~580 °C). (1 mark)
5. Discuss grain size variation with temperature. (1 mark)
6. Discuss stress variation (compressive → tensile). (1 mark)
7. Mention effect of H₂ desorption and annealing. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- L = 400 µm = 400 × 10⁻⁶ m
- w = 30 µm = 30 × 10⁻⁶ m
- t = 3 µm = 3 × 10⁻⁶ m
- E = 160 GPa = 160 × 10⁹ Pa
- ρ = 2320 kg/m³
- F = 50 µN = 50 × 10⁻⁶ N

**Step 1 — Second moment of area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I \;=\; \dfrac{w\,t^3}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{(30 \times 10^{-6})\,(3 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-6} \times 27 \times 10^{-18}}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I \;=\; \dfrac{8.1 \times 10^{-22}}{12} \;=\; 6.75 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Spring constant (i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \;=\; \dfrac{3\,E\,I}{L^3} \;=\; \dfrac{3 \times (160 \times 10^{9}) \times (6.75 \times 10^{-23})}{(400 \times 10^{-6})^3} \;=\; \dfrac{3.24 \times 10^{-11}}{6.4 \times 10^{-11}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \;=\; 0.506\;\text{N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective mass (ii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 0.24\,\rho\,w\,t\,L \;=\; 0.24 \times 2320 \times (30 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3 \times 10^{-6}) \times (400 \times 10^{-6})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 0.24 \times 2320 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-14} \;=\; 0.24 \times 8.352 \times 10^{-11} \;=\; 2.004 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resonant frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{\text{eff}}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{0.506}{2.004 \times 10^{-11}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{2.525 \times 10^{10}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \times 1.589 \times 10^{5} \;=\; 2.529 \times 10^{4}\;\text{Hz} \;\approx\; 25.3\;\text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Tip deflection under F = 50 µN (iii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta \;=\; \dfrac{F}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.506} \;=\; 9.88 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m} \;\approx\; 98.8\;\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **k = 0.506 N/m**
- (ii) **f₀ ≈ 25.3 kHz**
- (iii) **δ ≈ 98.8 µm**

---

### Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the mechanical properties of polysilicon used in MEMS. Explain residual stress, its origin, types, and methods to control it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polysilicon film of thickness 2 µm is deposited by LPCVD at 620 °C on a (100) silicon substrate. After cooling to 25 °C, the residual stress measured by wafer-curvature is **200 MPa (tensile)**. Estimate: (i) the thermal component of stress, and (ii) the intrinsic stress. Given: $\alpha_{\text{poly}} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K, $\alpha_{\text{Si}} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K, E = 160 GPa, ν = 0.22.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Mechanical Properties of Polysilicon:**
1. **Young's modulus:** E = 160 – 180 GPa (≈ 169 GPa along ⟨110⟩)
2. **Poisson's ratio:** ν = 0.22 – 0.28
3. **Fracture strength:** σ_y = 1 – 3 GPa (highly dependent on surface flaws)
4. **Density:** ρ = 2.32 g/cm³
5. **Fatigue behaviour:** Polysilicon shows **no cyclic fatigue** in ambient conditions — a major advantage over metals.
6. **Hardness:** 8 – 13 GPa (Vickers)
7. **Fracture toughness:** K_IC = 1.0 – 1.5 MPa·m^{1/2}

**Residual Stress:**
- **Definition:** The internal mechanical stress that remains in a thin film *after* deposition and cooling, without any external load.
- **Types:**
  1. **Intrinsic stress** ($\sigma_{\text{int}}$): generated *during* film growth due to grain boundary formation, defect incorporation, incomplete H₂ desorption.
  2. **Thermal stress** ($\sigma_{\text{th}}$): generated *after* deposition when film cools from T_dep to T_room, due to CTE mismatch between film and substrate.
- **Controlling stress:**
  1. **Annealing** at 1000 – 1100 °C in N₂ reduces intrinsic stress dramatically.
  2. **Doping** (especially heavy in-situ P-doping) shifts stress from tensile to compressive.
  3. **Deposition pressure control** in LPCVD.
  4. **Deposition temperature optimization** at ~620 °C for low stress.
  5. **Multi-layer poly-Si sandwich** (e.g., poly-Si / SiO₂ / poly-Si) to balance stresses.
  6. **Post-release annealing** to flatten released structures.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Thermal stress calculation (i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{th}} \;=\; \dfrac{E}{1-\nu}\,(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\,\Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

With $\alpha_f = \alpha_s = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{th}} \;=\; \dfrac{160 \times 10^{9}}{1 - 0.22} \times (2.6 \times 10^{-6} - 2.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times (620 - 25)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{th}} \;=\; \dfrac{160 \times 10^{9}}{0.78} \times 0 \times 595 \;=\; 0\;\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (i):** $\boxed{\sigma_{\text{th}} = 0\;\text{Pa}}$ — because the CTE values are *identical*, the thermal contribution is exactly zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** This is *exactly* why polysilicon is preferred on a c-Si substrate — the matched CTE eliminates thermal stress, and all the residual stress is then *intrinsic*, which can be controlled by deposition parameters and annealing.

**Step 2 — Intrinsic stress (ii):**

The total residual stress is the sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{residual}} \;=\; \sigma_{\text{th}} + \sigma_{\text{int}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{int}} \;=\; \sigma_{\text{residual}} - \sigma_{\text{th}} \;=\; 200 \times 10^{6} - 0 \;=\; 200\;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (ii):** $\boxed{\sigma_{\text{int}} = 200\;\text{MPa (tensile)}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Polysilicon Questions):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in thermal stress:** Students write $\sigma_{\text{th}} = -200$ MPa by blindly plugging the formula. Always check: if $\alpha_f > \alpha_s$, the film wants to shrink more than the substrate on cooling → film goes into **tension** (positive).
> 2. **Forgetting the biaxial modulus factor $(1 - \nu)$:** The biaxial modulus $E/(1-\nu) = 160/0.78 \approx 205$ GPa. Using $E$ directly under-estimates the stress by ~22%.
> 3. **Confusing intrinsic and extrinsic stress:** Intrinsic = growth-related; extrinsic = thermal mismatch. Total = sum of both.
> 4. **Mixing up deposition vs annealing temperatures:** Deposition at 620 °C → poly-Si; annealing at 1000 °C → grain growth and stress relief.
> 5. **Units mistake:** MPa vs Pa, µm vs m — the KTU evaluator *will* deduct 1 mark for inconsistent units.
> 6. **Not stating the sign convention:** Always write "(tensile)" or "(compressive)" alongside the numerical value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — Polysilicon**

- ✅ **Definition:** Polycrystalline Si — many small Si grains separated by grain boundaries, deposited by **LPCVD at 580 – 650 °C** using **silane (SiH₄)**.
- ✅ **Deposition chemistry:** $\text{SiH}_4 \rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\,\text{H}_2$ (or with disilane at lower T).
- ✅ **Young's modulus (K TU default):** **E = 160 GPa**; Poisson's ratio ν = 0.22; Density ρ = **2.32 g/cm³**.
- ✅ **Fracture strength:** **1 – 3 GPa**; no fatigue (cyclic).
- ✅ **Resistivity range:** Undoped **10⁶ – 10⁸ Ω·cm**; in-situ P-doped → **0.001 – 0.01 Ω·cm**.
- ✅ **Residual stress** = Intrinsic + Thermal. For poly-Si on c-Si, **thermal ≈ 0** (matched CTE ~2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K).
- ✅ **Annealing** at 1000 – 1100 °C in N₂ for 1 h → **grain growth** + **stress relief**.
- ✅ **Piezoresistivity** of poly-Si: gauge factor **~20 – 40** (n-type ⟨100⟩), enables integrated strain gauges.
- ✅ **Cantilever spring constant:** $k = 3EI/L^3$ (point load) or $k = EI/L^3$ × load configuration factor.
- ✅ **Cantilever tip deflection (point load at tip):** $\delta = 4FL^3 / (Ew t^3)$.
- ✅ **Stoney's curvature formula:** $1/R = 6\sigma_{\text{grad}}\,t_f / (E_s\,t_s^{\,2})$.
- ✅ **Resonant frequency (1st mode):** $f_0 = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m_{\text{eff}}}$, with $m_{\text{eff}} = 0.24\,\rho\,w\,t\,L$ for cantilever.
- ✅ **Doping methods:** In-situ (uniform, clean) vs. Ion implantation (precise, needs anneal) vs. Thermal diffusion (older, less common).
- ✅ **Sacrificial release:** Poly-Si on SiO₂ → HF etch of oxide → released structure → critical-point drying to prevent stiction.
- ✅ **Applications:** Pressure sensor diaphragm, accelerometer proof mass, gyroscope rotor, resonator, optical mirror, micro-heater, RF switch, inkjet nozzle wall.
- ✅ **Advantages:** CMOS-compatible, tunable electrical conductivity, high strength-to-weight ratio, precise thickness control, batch fabrication.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Grain-boundary scattering (lower mobility), residual stress and stress gradient, requires HF for release (CMOS-incompatible, special packaging needed), high deposition T (energy cost).
- ✅ **One-line exam fact:** "Polysilicon is the **steel of MEMS** — high strength, tunable conductivity, and CMOS-compatible — making it the most widely used structural material in surface micromachining."

topics.title: Silicon Piezo Resistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Silicon Piezo Resistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Piezoresistivity** is the phenomenon by which the electrical resistivity of a material changes when it is subjected to an applied mechanical stress. In semiconductors such as monocrystalline **Silicon (Si)**, this effect arises primarily from a change in the **mobility of charge carriers** and the **modification of the energy band structure** (intervalley and intravalley scattering shifts) caused by strain, rather than purely from dimensional changes as in metallic strain gauges.

A **Silicon Piezoresistor** is therefore a doped silicon region (p-type or n-type) that has been deliberately integrated into a MEMS structure (typically a thin diaphragm, cantilever, or bridge) so that when the host structure deforms under an external stimulus (pressure, force, acceleration), the resulting stress alters the silicon's resistivity. This change in resistance can be measured using a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit and correlated back to the applied stimulus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Silicon piezoresistors form the sensing core of the most commercially successful MEMS devices — pressure sensors, accelerometers, gyroscopes, and flow sensors. The discovery by **Charles Smith (1954)** of the giant piezoresistive effect in silicon launched the modern MEMS industry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **crowded hallway** (the silicon crystal lattice) filled with people (electrons/holes) walking at a normal pace. Now, if you **squeeze the walls** of the hallway from the sides (apply mechanical stress), the people get pushed against each other — they bump into one another more frequently and slow down. Their "flow" (current) decreases, meaning the **resistance goes up**. Release the squeeze, and they move freely again. 

In a real silicon crystal, mechanical stress doesn't just change the physical dimensions of the conductor (as in a metal wire) — it actually **reshapes the electronic pathways** inside the semiconductor, dramatically affecting how easily charges can move. That is why silicon's piezoresistive effect is roughly **50 to 100 times stronger** than that of a metal strain gauge.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gauge Factor of Silicon:** $GF_{Si} \approx 100$ to $200$ (highly orientation dependent)
- **Gauge Factor of Metal Foil:** $GF_{metal} \approx 2$
- **First-order piezoresistive coefficients of silicon (p-type, $\rho \approx 0.01\ \Omega \cdot cm$):**
  - $\pi_{11} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-11}\ Pa^{-1}$
  - $\pi_{12} \approx -1.1 \times 10^{-11}\ Pa^{-1}$
  - $\pi_{44} \approx 138.1 \times 10^{-11}\ Pa^{-1}$ (dominant in p-type Si)
- **Young's Modulus of Silicon (along $\langle 100 \rangle$):** $E_{\langle 100 \rangle} \approx 130\ GPa$
- **Poisson's Ratio of Silicon:** $\nu \approx 0.28$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is silicon special?** Although metals also change resistance under stress, the dominant mechanism in metals is the **geometric effect** (change in length and cross-section). In silicon, the dominant mechanism is the **resistivity change itself** ($\Delta \rho / \rho$), which is governed by the piezoresistive tensor $\pi_{ijk\ell}$. This makes silicon an extraordinary strain-sensitive material.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Piezoresistive Effect: The Physics Behind It

The fractional change in resistivity of a semiconductor under applied stress is given by a **fourth-rank tensor equation**:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho_i}{\rho} = \sum_{j} \pi_{ij} \sigma_j$$

where $\pi_{ij}$ are the **piezoresistive coefficients** and $\sigma_j$ are the components of applied stress.

Because silicon has a **cubic crystal structure (diamond lattice)**, the tensor reduces to **only three independent components**: $\pi_{11}$, $\pi_{12}$, and $\pi_{44}$.

**Why does stress change resistivity in silicon?**

1. **Valley Repopulation:** Stress shifts the energy of the six equivalent conduction-band valleys in silicon. Electrons redistribute into lower-energy valleys, changing the effective mass tensor and thus mobility.
2. **Intervalley Scattering:** Strain modifies the phonon population, altering the scattering rates of carriers.
3. **Band Warping:** The constant-energy surfaces in k-space become ellipsoidal under strain, leading to anisotropic effective mass.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis of a Piezoresistor in Operation

1. An external stimulus (e.g., pressure) causes the host MEMS structure (diaphragm) to deform.
2. The deformation produces **mechanical stress** $\sigma$ within the diaphragm.
3. The integrated silicon piezoresistor experiences this stress and changes its **resistivity** $\rho$.
4. The change in resistivity produces a change in **resistance** $R$.
5. The resistor is connected in a **Wheatstone bridge**; the imbalance is converted to an output voltage.
6. The output voltage is calibrated to infer the magnitude of the original stimulus.

### 2.3 Directional Piezoresistive Coefficients

When current flows in direction with direction cosines $(l_1, l_2, l_3)$ and stress is applied in direction $(m_1, m_2, m_3)$, the effective piezoresistive coefficient is:

$$\pi = \pi_{11}(l_1^2 m_1^2 + l_2^2 m_2^2 + l_3^2 m_3^2) + \pi_{12}(l_1^2 m_2^2 + l_2^2 m_1^2 + l_2^2 m_3^2 + l_3^2 m_2^2 + l_3^2 m_1^2 + l_1^2 m_3^2) + 2\pi_{44}(l_1 l_2 m_1 m_2 + l_2 l_3 m_2 m_3 + l_3 l_1 m_3 m_1)$$

**Special Cases (KTU-High-Yield):**

- **Longitudinal** (current parallel to stress): $\pi_l = \pi_{11} - 2(\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \pi_{44})$ — maximum sensitivity in p-type Si when current and stress are along $\langle 110 \rangle$.
- **Transverse** (current perpendicular to stress): $\pi_t = \pi_{11} - 2(\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \pi_{44}/2) + \pi_{44} \cdot \text{(orientation factor)}$.

### 2.4 The Gauge Factor Equation

The **Gauge Factor (GF)** is the key figure of merit, defined as the fractional change in resistance per unit strain:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

For a **metal strain gauge**, $GF = 1 + 2\nu$ (geometric only, $\approx 2$).

For a **semiconductor piezoresistor**, the resistivity term dominates:

$$GF = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus in the current direction. For silicon, $\pi_l E \gg (1 + 2\nu)$, so $GF \approx \pi_l E$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta R / R = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | Total fractional resistance change | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge factor definition | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $GF \approx \pi_l E$ | Semiconductor gauge factor (dominant term) | $\approx 100$ for Si |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$ | Wheatstone bridge output (small signal) | Volts |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ | Hooke's law (uniaxial) | Dimensionless strain |
| 6 | $\pi_{l}^{\langle 110 \rangle} = \frac{1}{2}(\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44})$ | P-type Si, current $\vert \vert$ stress along $\langle 110 \rangle$ | $Pa^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $\pi_{t}^{\langle 110 \rangle} = \frac{1}{2}(\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{44})$ | P-type Si, current $\perp$ stress, both in $\langle 110 \rangle$ | $Pa^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 P a^2}{8 h^2}(1 - \nu)$ | Max stress at edge of square diaphragm (radius $a$, thickness $h$, pressure $P$) | $Pa$ |
| 9 | $S = \frac{\Delta R / R}{P}$ | Pressure sensitivity of piezoresistive sensor | $Pa^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $TCGF = \frac{1}{GF} \frac{\partial GF}{\partial T}$ | Temperature coefficient of gauge factor | $K^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Silicon piezoresistive pressure sensors are used in **automotive TPMS (Tyre Pressure Monitoring Systems)**, **medical blood pressure transducers**, **altimeters in smartphones**, and **industrial process control** because of their high sensitivity, small size, IC compatibility, and low cost when batch-fabricated using CMOS+MEMS processes.

### 2.6 Why Piezoresistors are the Backbone of MEMS Sensing

- **High Sensitivity:** $GF \approx 100$ to $200$ vs $\approx 2$ for metals → **50–100x larger signal**.
- **CMOS Compatibility:** Doped silicon regions can be fabricated using the **same ion-implantation and diffusion steps** used in standard CMOS IC technology.
- **Small Footprint:** Active length of 10–100 $\mu m$ is sufficient.
- **Low Power:** Compatible with battery-powered IoT sensor nodes.
- **Self-Generating (passive):** No external excitation current source needed for the resistor (only for the bridge readout).
- **Drawback:** **Strong temperature dependence** — requires on-chip compensation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Stress to Resistance Change

We will derive the master equation of a silicon piezoresistor from fundamental principles.

**Step 1 — Define resistance in terms of material and geometry:**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is resistivity, $L$ is length, and $A$ is cross-sectional area.

**Step 2 — Take a small variation in $R$, $\rho$, $L$, and $A$:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the strain components:**

By definition, longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_l = \Delta L / L$.

For a circular cross-section of diameter $d$: $A = \pi d^2 / 4$, so

$$\frac{\Delta A}{A} = 2 \frac{\Delta d}{d} = -2 \nu \varepsilon_l$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the resistance equation:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon_l + 2 \nu \varepsilon_l = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \varepsilon_l$$

**Step 5 — Define the piezoresistive term:**

In a semiconductor, the change in resistivity arises from the strain-induced mobility and band-structure changes:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

**Step 6 — Combine all terms (Master Equation):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu) \varepsilon_l + \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t}$$

For silicon, $\pi_l \sigma_l \gg (1+2\nu)\varepsilon_l$, so the dominant term is the piezoresistive one.

**Step 7 — Convert strain to stress using Hooke's Law ($\varepsilon = \sigma / E$):**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E) \varepsilon_l = GF \cdot \varepsilon_l$$

This recovers the gauge factor definition derived in Section 2.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

> **Problem:** A silicon piezoresistor of gauge factor $GF = 120$ is bonded on a steel cantilever (which we approximate as having the strain transferred fully). The cantilever is loaded such that the surface strain is $\varepsilon = 500\ \mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain). If the piezoresistor is connected in a quarter-active Wheatstone bridge (only one arm active, the other three are fixed at $R_0 = 5\ k\Omega$), find the output voltage for a bridge excitation of $V_{ex} = 5\ V$. Assume the three fixed resistors are exactly equal to the unstrained resistance of the piezoresistor.

**Step 1 — Compute the fractional change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 0.06 = 6\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute the change in resistance:**

$$\Delta R = 0.06 \times 5000 = 300\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Write the Wheatstone bridge output equation for one active arm:**

The bridge has $R_1 = R_0 + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_0$. Standard bridge transfer function:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values:**

$$V_{out} = 5 \left( \frac{5300}{5300 + 5000} - \frac{5000}{5000 + 5000} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term numerically:**

$$\frac{5300}{10300} = 0.5146 \quad ; \quad \frac{5000}{10000} = 0.5000$$

**Step 6 — Subtract and multiply:**

$$V_{out} = 5 \times (0.5146 - 0.5000) = 5 \times 0.0146 = 0.0731\ V = 73.1\ mV$$

**Step 7 — Verify using small-signal approximation:**

For small $\Delta R / R$, the linearized bridge equation gives:

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} = 5 \times \frac{0.06}{4} = 0.075\ V = 75\ mV$$

The exact result $73.1\ mV$ matches the small-signal approximation $75\ mV$ within $\approx 2.5\%$, confirming the calculation is consistent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $V_{out} \approx 73.1\ mV$ (exact) or $\approx 75\ mV$ (small-signal). In KTU exams, students should use the small-signal formula unless otherwise directed.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Stress on a Square Diaphragm

A square diaphragm of side $2a$, thickness $h$, with applied pressure $P$ on one side, has its **maximum stress at the midpoint of the edges**, given by:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2} (1 - \nu)$$

**Derivation outline:**

1. Apply small-deflection plate theory: $w(x,y)$ is the deflection, governed by the biharmonic equation $\nabla^4 w = P / D$, where $D = E h^3 / [12(1 - \nu^2)]$ is the flexural rigidity.
2. For a clamped square plate, the solution is a double Fourier sine series.
3. The bending moments are obtained from $M_x = -D(\partial^2 w / \partial x^2 + \nu \partial^2 w / \partial y^2)$ and similarly for $M_y$.
4. The maximum bending stress is $\sigma = 6M / h^2$.
5. Substituting the series solution and evaluating at the edge midpoints ($x = \pm a, y = 0$) gives the closed-form expression above.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Piezoresistive MEMS Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Pressure P] --> B[Silicon Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Mechanical Stress sigma]
    C --> D[Diffused Piezoresistors R1 and R2 and R3 and R4]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    E --> F[Differential Amplifier]
    F --> G[Temperature Compensation Block]
    G --> H[Calibrated Output Voltage Vout]
    I[Bandgap Reference] --> E
    J[On chip EEPROM] --> G
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style E fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style H fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
```

### 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Layout on a Square Diaphragm (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Diaphragm [Square Diaphragm top view]
        R1((R1 Longitudinal)) --- NodeA((Node A))
        NodeA --- R2((R2 Transverse))
        R2 --- NodeB((Node B))
        R3((R3 Transverse)) --- NodeB
        NodeB --- R4((R4 Longitudinal))
        R4 --- NodeA
    end
    Vex[Excitation Vex] --- NodeA
    NodeB --- GND[GND]
    Vout[Output Vout] --- Diaphragm
    style R1 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#bf9000
    style R4 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#bf9000
    style R2 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d
    style R3 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** The four piezoresistors are placed at the **edges of the diaphragm** (where stress is maximum). Two are oriented **longitudinally** (resistance increases under tensile stress) and two **transversely** (resistance decreases under the same stress). This **differential configuration** doubles the sensitivity and provides **first-order temperature compensation** because all four resistors experience identical thermal drift.

### 4.3 Orientation of Piezoresistors on $\langle 100 \rangle$ Silicon Wafer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Wafer100 [100 Silicon Wafer Plane]
        Dir110[Current and Stress along 110 direction] --- HighPi[Maximum piezoresistive coefficient pi_l high]
        Dir100[Current and Stress along 100 direction] --- LowPi[Low piezoresistive coefficient]
        Dir111[Current and Stress along 111 direction] --- ZeroPi[Near zero piezoresistive response]
    end
    style HighPi fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#741b47
    style LowPi fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000
    style ZeroPi fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#595959
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** When designing piezoresistors, the orientation of the resistor on the silicon wafer **drastically** changes the sensitivity. For maximum response in p-type silicon, align the current flow along the **$\langle 110 \rangle$ crystallographic direction** — this gives the largest $\pi_{44}$ contribution. Always check the wafer flat/notch orientation before laying out the mask.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define piezoresistivity. Why is the piezoresistive effect in silicon much larger than in metals?**

**Model Answer:**

Piezoresistivity is the change in electrical resistivity of a material when subjected to mechanical stress. In silicon, applying stress changes the energy band structure (shifts the conduction band valleys and modifies effective mass), which in turn alters the carrier mobility and hence resistivity.

In metals, the change in resistance arises almost entirely from **geometric effects** (change in length and cross-section), giving a gauge factor of only $\approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2$. In silicon, the dominant mechanism is the **resistivity change** itself, with $\Delta \rho / \rho$ being directly proportional to the applied stress via the piezoresistive coefficient $\pi$. This makes silicon's gauge factor (100–200) **50 to 100 times greater** than that of metals, making it far more sensitive for strain and pressure sensing applications. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any three key advantages of silicon piezoresistors over conventional metal foil strain gauges.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High Sensitivity:** Gauge factor of 100–200 versus $\approx 2$ for metals, giving much larger output signals.
2. **CMOS/IC Compatibility:** Piezoresistors can be fabricated using standard silicon IC processes (ion implantation, diffusion), enabling **on-chip integration** of sensor and signal-conditioning circuitry.
3. **Small Size:** Active lengths of 10–100 $\mu m$ allow dense arrays and integration into micro-scale structures, ideal for MEMS diaphragms and cantilevers. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for fractional change in resistance of a semiconductor piezoresistor in terms of gauge factor and strain. List the three independent piezoresistive coefficients of silicon and explain which one dominates in p-type silicon. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the resistance definition:**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate logarithmically:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Delta L / L = \varepsilon$ and $\Delta A / A = -2 \nu \varepsilon$:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Express resistivity change via piezoresistive coefficient and stress:**

$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t = \pi_l E \varepsilon + \pi_t E' \varepsilon'$$

**Step 5 — Combine and use Hooke's law $\sigma = E \varepsilon$:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E) \varepsilon = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

Therefore, $GF = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$, and for silicon $\pi_l E \gg (1 + 2\nu)$, so $GF \approx \pi_l E$.

**Three independent piezoresistive coefficients of silicon:**

1. $\pi_{11}$
2. $\pi_{12}$
3. $\pi_{44}$

In **p-type silicon**, $\pi_{44}$ is the dominant coefficient (typically $\approx 138 \times 10^{-11}\ Pa^{-1}$), which is why piezoresistors are aligned along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction to maximize the contribution of $\pi_{44}$ to $\pi_l$. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A silicon piezoresistive pressure sensor has a square diaphragm of side $4\ mm$ and thickness $0.4\ mm$. When a pressure of $100\ kPa$ is applied, the maximum stress at the diaphragm edge is $50\ MPa$. The piezoresistor has a longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_l = 50 \times 10^{-11}\ Pa^{-1}$ and negligible transverse coefficient. The unstrained resistance is $R_0 = 3\ k\Omega$. The resistor is used in a quarter-bridge configuration with $V_{ex} = 5\ V$.

Compute:
(i) The fractional change in resistance.
(ii) The change in resistance in ohms.
(iii) The output voltage using the linearized bridge equation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Fractional change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = \pi_l \sigma_l = 50 \times 10^{-11} \times 50 \times 10^{6} = 0.025 = 2.5\%$$

**(ii) Change in resistance:**

$$\Delta R = 0.025 \times 3000 = 75\ \Omega$$

**(iii) Output voltage (linearized quarter-bridge):**

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4 R_0} = 5 \times \frac{0.025}{4} = 5 \times 0.00625 = 0.03125\ V = 31.25\ mV$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $2.5\%$, (ii) $75\ \Omega$, (iii) $31.25\ mV$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neatly labeled diagram, explain the working of a piezoresistive MEMS pressure sensor. Describe the role of the Wheatstone bridge in signal conditioning. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

A piezoresistive MEMS pressure sensor consists of a thin silicon diaphragm etched from a silicon wafer. Four doped silicon piezoresistors are diffused or ion-implanted near the edges of the diaphragm, where mechanical stress is maximum when pressure is applied to one side of the diaphragm.

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. **Applied pressure $P$** deflects the diaphragm by a small amount $w(x,y)$.
2. The deflection produces **in-plane stresses** $\sigma_l$ (longitudinal) and $\sigma_t$ (transverse) at the locations of the piezoresistors.
3. These stresses change the **resistances** of the piezoresistors: two resistors (placed along the diaphragm edge experiencing tensile stress) increase in resistance, while the other two (on perpendicular edges) decrease.
4. The four resistors are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.
5. The bridge is excited by a stable reference voltage $V_{ex}$.
6. The differential output voltage $V_{out}$ is proportional to the applied pressure.

**Role of the Wheatstone Bridge:**

- Converts small resistance changes ($\Delta R \sim$ tens of ohms) into a measurable voltage signal.
- Provides **common-mode rejection** of temperature variations, since all four resistors drift together.
- The **fully active bridge** (all four arms are piezoresistors) provides **4× higher sensitivity** than a quarter-bridge, and **first-order temperature compensation** because the TC of all four resistors cancels out.
- Output is linear with $\Delta R / R$ for small signals, simplifying calibration.

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 — Wheatstone bridge layout on a square diaphragm with two longitudinal and two transverse piezoresistors at the edges.) **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A silicon cantilever beam of length $L = 500\ \mu m$, width $w = 100\ \mu m$, and thickness $t = 5\ \mu m$ is used as a piezoresistive accelerometer. A piezoresistor of gauge factor $GF = 100$ is diffused at the clamped-end surface of the beam. The beam has Young's modulus $E = 170\ GPa$. A proof mass at the free end creates a surface strain of $\varepsilon = 0.002$ when subjected to an acceleration of $10g$.

Calculate:
(i) The stress at the resistor location.
(ii) The fractional change in resistance of the piezoresistor.
(iii) The sensitivity in $V/g$ if the piezoresistor is used in a half-bridge (two active arms) with a $5\ V$ excitation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stress at the resistor:**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 170 \times 10^9 \times 0.002 = 340 \times 10^6\ Pa = 340\ MPa$$

**(ii) Fractional change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 100 \times 0.002 = 0.2 = 20\%$$

**(iii) Sensitivity:**

For a half-bridge (two active arms with equal and opposite $\Delta R$):

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2R}$$

For $10g$ acceleration:

$$V_{out} = 5 \times \frac{0.2}{2} = 0.5\ V$$

Sensitivity:

$$S = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = \frac{0.5\ V}{10g} = 0.05\ V/g = 50\ mV/g$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $340\ MPa$, (ii) $20\%$, (iii) $50\ mV/g$. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign Convention Trap:** Many students forget that transverse stress contributes a **separate term** with the *opposite sign* effect on resistance. In a properly designed bridge, two resistors *increase* and two *decrease* — the bridge output is therefore *twice* what it would be for a single active arm. Forgetting this **doubles the error**.
> - **Unit Conversion Trap:** Piezoresistive coefficients are given in $Pa^{-1}$ (e.g., $10^{-11}\ Pa^{-1}$). Stress in **MPa** must be converted to **Pa** before multiplication. Mixing units gives answers off by factors of $10^6$.
> - **Orientation Trap:** Drawing the piezoresistor along $\langle 100 \rangle$ instead of $\langle 110 \rangle$ on a p-type wafer can reduce sensitivity by **up to 75%** — a major design mistake in viva and design questions.
> - **Formula Trap:** Do **NOT** use $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$ for a half-bridge — the correct coefficient is $1/2$, not $1/4$. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Piezoresistivity is the stress-induced change in resistivity. In silicon, it is dominated by **mobility and band-structure changes**, not geometry.
- **Gauge Factor Hierarchy:** $GF_{semiconductor} \approx 100$–$200 \gg GF_{metal} \approx 2$.
- **Three Independent Coefficients:** $\pi_{11}$, $\pi_{12}$, $\pi_{44}$ — for p-type Si, $\pi_{44}$ is the **dominant** term.
- **Optimal Orientation:** P-type Si, current along $\langle 110 \rangle$ gives maximum $\pi_l \approx \frac{1}{2}(\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44})$.
- **Master Equation:** $\Delta R / R = (1 + 2\nu) \varepsilon + \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t \approx \pi_l E \varepsilon$ for Si.
- **Wheatstone Bridge Output:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$ (quarter-bridge) and $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (2R)$ (half-bridge).
- **Diaphragm Stress:** Maximum at the **midpoint of the edges** of a square diaphragm, given by $\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 P a^2}{4 h^2}(1 - \nu)$.
- **Sensitivity:** $S = (\Delta R / R) / P$ — high for thin diaphragms and large gauge factor.
- **Drawbacks to Remember:** (1) **Strong temperature dependence** of $GF$ and $R$, (2) **Nonlinearity at high strain**, (3) **Long-term drift**, (4) **Lower bandwidth** than piezoelectric sensors.
- **Fabrication:** Piezoresistors are typically formed by **boron ion implantation** (p-type) into an n-type epitaxial layer, with **LOCOS or shallow-trench isolation** for electrical separation.
- **KTU 2024 Focus Areas:** Derivation of $\Delta R / R$, gauge factor calculation, Wheatstone bridge analysis, and orientation-dependent coefficients of silicon.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymers in MEMS - SU-8
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polymers in MEMS - SU-8

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of SU-8

**SU-8** is a high-contrast, **epoxy-based, negative-tone photoresist** that is the industrial workhorse polymer for fabricating high-aspect-ratio (HAR) microstructures in Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS). Chemically, SU-8 is the **glycidyl-ether of bisphenol-A novolac (EPON SU-8 resin)** dissolved in an organic solvent (typically *gamma-butyrolactone* or *cyclopentanone*) along with a **photoacid generator (PAG)** — most commonly a triarylsulfonium hexafluoroantimonate salt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SU-8 Designation Decoded:**
> - "SU" stands for **S**eries of resists developed originally at **IBM** by **Lober and Suleski** (originally Shell Chemical EPON resin).
> - "8" refers to the number of **epoxy repeat groups** in the monomer chain, which determines the polymer's molecular weight and mechanical robustness.
> - It is a **negative photoresist**: regions exposed to UV light become **cross-linked and insoluble**, while unexposed regions dissolve away in the developer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SU-8 is positioned in Module 3 under *Materials for MEMS* and is the *only* polymer explicitly named in the syllabus. Expect direct questions on its chemistry, processing steps, properties, and applications in **KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of SU-8 like a **photographic "solid-concrete-in-a-can" spray for silicon chips**:

1. Imagine you have a flat silicon wafer (a microscopic kitchen tile).
2. You pour a **honey-like liquid** (SU-8) onto the tile and spin it — centrifugal force spreads it into a uniform thin film (like pizza dough on a spinning plate).
3. You bake it gently so it solidifies slightly (soft bake).
4. You place a **stencil (photomask)** over the wafer and blast it with **UV light** (like a tanning bed for chips). The exposed regions undergo a chemical "welding" reaction and become rock-hard, while covered regions remain soft.
5. You bake it again to complete the welding (post-exposure bake).
6. You dip the wafer in a chemical "soap" (developer) — the soft parts wash away, leaving behind **tower-like 3D microstructures** sculpted exactly as the mask dictated.
7. You now have mechanical micro-pillars, gears, or channels carved from a tough polymer that can stand **20 times taller than they are wide** — an aspect ratio impossible with conventional resists.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SU-8 matters:** Before SU-8 (introduced commercially in the late 1990s), MEMS engineers could only build planar or shallow 3D structures. SU-8 enabled **truly three-dimensional, high-aspect-ratio polymer MEMS**, revolutionizing microfluidics, biomedical devices, and microsystems packaging.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics for SU-8

| Property | Typical Value | Units |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| Refractive Index (cured, 365 nm) | **1.67 – 1.68** | dimensionless |
| Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$) | **~200** | °C (fully cross-linked) |
| Young's Modulus (cured) | **2.0 – 4.0** | **GPa** |
| Tensile Strength | **~30 – 60** | MPa |
| Density | **~1.2** | g/cm³ |
| Optical Transparency | > **90%** above **360 nm** | — |
| Maximum Achievable Aspect Ratio | **> 20:1** (up to **50:1** reported) | — |
| Thermal Stability (decomposition) | **> 380** | °C |
| Linear Shrinkage on Curing | **~7 – 10%** | — |
