<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Architecture of SU-8

SU-8 is a **multi-functional epoxy resin** with **eight epoxy groups per monomer**, allowing extensive **3D cross-linking**. Its chemistry has three key components:

### (a) The Base Resin — EPON SU-8
- A novolac-epoxy resin with the repeating structural unit of **bisphenol-A** linked via glycidyl ether bridges.
- The number of epoxy groups (n = 8) per molecule gives a high cross-link density upon curing.
- Molecular weight is typically **~1400 g/mol** (SU-8 2000 series) up to **~10,000 g/mol** (SU-8 3000 series).

### (b) The Solvent — Gamma-Butyrolactone (GBL) or Cyclopentanone
- Controls viscosity and therefore **spin-coating film thickness**.
- Solvent choice depends on SU-8 series (GBL for older 2000 series; cyclopentanone for 3000 and 3000M series).

### (c) The Photoacid Generator (PAG) — Triarylsulfonium Hexafluoroantimonate
- Concentration: **~2 – 5% by weight** of the resin.
- Upon UV exposure, the PAG photolyzes to release **hexafluoroantimonic acid (HSbF₆)**.
- This acid acts as the **catalyst** that initiates the cationic ring-opening polymerization of the epoxy groups during the post-exposure bake.

---

## 2.2 Operational Principle — Why SU-8 "Writes" Tall Structures

The lithographic mechanism of SU-8 proceeds in three coupled stages:

1. **Photon Absorption (Exposure Step)**  
   UV photons (typically **i-line at 365 nm** from a mercury lamp) are absorbed by the PAG. Energy higher than the **bond dissociation energy** of the S–C bond in the triarylsulfonium cation breaks the molecule, releasing a strong Brønsted acid (H⁺SbF₆⁻).

2. **Acid Diffusion and Catalysis (Post-Exposure Bake — PEB)**  
   During the PEB (typically **65 °C → 95 °C**), the photogenerated acid diffuses and **protonates the epoxy oxygen**. The activated epoxy then undergoes **cationic ring-opening polymerization**, linking monomer to monomer in a chain reaction. The regenerated H⁺ protonates the next epoxy, propagating the cross-linking.

3. **Cross-Linked Network Formation**  
   Each monomer has **8 epoxy groups**, so a single molecule can join to many neighbors, building a dense **3D epoxy network**. The reaction is essentially **complete** within seconds once initiated, but the network continues to densify during the PEB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-link density vs. Film Thickness:** A thicker SU-8 film requires more UV exposure to ensure full photoacid generation throughout the depth. This is why SU-8 is typically used with **broadband UV (350 – 400 nm)** rather than deep UV — its low optical absorption at 365 nm means UV penetrates deep into the resist.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### (A) Spin-Coating Film Thickness (Empirical Model)

The thickness of a spin-coated polymer film is governed by an empirical power-law relation:

$$h = K \cdot \frac{C^{\beta}}{\omega^{\gamma}}$$

where:
- $h$ = film thickness (μm)
- $K$ = calibration constant (depends on resist series and substrate)
- $C$ = solids (polymer) concentration (% by weight)
- $\beta$ = concentration exponent (typically **β ≈ 2.0** for SU-8)
- $\omega$ = angular spin speed (rpm)
- $\gamma$ = speed exponent (typically **γ ≈ 0.5** for SU-8)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention:** Increasing spin speed *decreases* thickness (γ > 0 in denominator). Increasing solids concentration *increases* thickness. This is the single most important equation for KTU problems asking to compute spin-coating parameters.

### (B) Exposure Dose

$$E = I \cdot t$$

where:
- $E$ = exposure dose (mJ/cm²)
- $I$ = irradiance at wafer plane (mW/cm²)
- $t$ = exposure time (seconds)

For SU-8, the optimum exposure dose is typically **100 – 250 mJ/cm²** depending on film thickness.

### (C) Aspect Ratio of a Fabricated Structure

$$AR = \frac{H}{W}$$

where:
- $H$ = height (thickness) of the SU-8 feature
- $W$ = minimum lateral feature width
- SU-8 is famous for **AR > 20:1** in standard processing and **AR > 50:1** in optimized processing.

### (D) Curing-Induced Linear Shrinkage

$$\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\varepsilon_{curing} = \frac{\Delta L_{curing}}{L_0} \approx 0.07 \text{ to } 0.10$$

For thermally induced dimensional change on cooling from cure temperature ($T_{cure}$) to room temperature ($T_{RT}$):

$$\varepsilon_{thermal} = \alpha_{CTE} \cdot (T_{cure} - T_{RT})$$

with $\alpha_{CTE}$ (coefficient of thermal expansion) ≈ **50 × 10⁻⁶ /K** for fully cured SU-8.

### (E) Mechanical Stress–Strain in a Cured SU-8 Structure

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

with:
- $E$ = Young's modulus ≈ **2.0 – 4.0 GPa** (cured SU-8)
- $\sigma$ = stress
- $\varepsilon$ = strain

### (F) Diffusion Length of the Photoacid During PEB

The lateral resolution of SU-8 is limited by acid diffusion. The characteristic diffusion length is:

$$L_{diff} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot t_{PEB}}$$

where:
- $D$ = acid diffusion coefficient (≈ **10⁻¹³ m²/s** at 95 °C for SU-8)
- $t_{PEB}$ = post-exposure bake time (seconds)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** To preserve fine features, the PEB temperature and time must be optimized — too much PEB causes the acid to spread laterally, blurring sidewalls. Too little PEB gives incomplete cross-linking, leading to fragile or under-developed features.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of SU-8 in Engineering

| Field | Application | Why SU-8 is chosen |
|-------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Microfluidics** | Channels, valves, mixers, lab-on-chip | Optical transparency, biocompatibility, high AR for tall channels |
| **Biomedical MEMS** | Cell scaffolds, microneedles, neural probes | Biocompatible, mechanically tunable, can be released as free-standing structures |
| **MEMS Packaging** | Wafer-level caps, feedthroughs | Low processing temperature, photosensitive, polymer adhesion |
| **Optics / Photonics** | Micro-lenses, gratings, waveguides | High refractive index, UV transparency |
| **RF MEMS** | Suspended beams, inductors | Low dielectric loss, mechanical flexibility |
| **Micro-actuators** | Comb drives, micro-grippers | High modulus, robust at 100s of μm thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Footprint:** SU-8 is supplied by **MicroChem Corp.**, **Micro Resist Technology GmbH**, and **Kayaku Advanced Materials**. It is the **most widely used polymer in academic MEMS research worldwide** and has FDA-cleared biocompatible variants (e.g., **SU-8 3000**).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Fabrication Sequence for a SU-8 Microstructure

The following is the **complete, exhaustive process flow** for fabricating a SU-8 MEMS structure. Each step is broken down with its physical/chemical rationale.

### Step 1 — Substrate Preparation
- Substrates: typically **Si (100)**, **glass**, **quartz**, or **polymer** (e.g., PET, PMMA).
- Cleaning: **RCA-1** (NH₄OH + H₂O₂ + H₂O) followed by **RCA-2** (HCl + H₂O₂ + H₂O), or simpler **piranha** (H₂SO₄ + H₂O₂) for organics removal.
- Dehydration bake: **150 – 200 °C for 5 – 10 min** on a hot plate to remove adsorbed moisture.
- **Optional adhesion promoter:** **HMDS (hexamethyldisilazane)** vapor priming improves SU-8 adhesion to silicon, but is not always required for thick films.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rationale:** Clean, dry, and hydrophobic surfaces give uniform spin-coated films. Particles or moisture cause pinholes and dewetting defects.

### Step 2 — Spin Coating
- SU-8 is dispensed onto the wafer (typically **1 – 5 mL** for a 4-inch wafer).
- A two-stage spin is used:
  - **Spread cycle:** **500 rpm for 5 – 10 s** (acceleration ~100 rpm/s) — spreads the resist uniformly.
  - **Spin cycle:** **1000 – 6000 rpm for 30 – 60 s** (acceleration ~300 rpm/s) — sets the final thickness.
- Edge bead removal (EBR) is performed using **cyclopentanone** or **ethyl lactate**.

#### **Numerical Worked Example — Spin-Coating Thickness**
A student uses **SU-8 3050** to coat a 4-inch silicon wafer. Given:
- Solids concentration $C = 72$ wt%
- Spin speed $\omega = 3000$ rpm
- Calibration constants: $K = 0.52$, $\beta = 2.0$, $\gamma = 0.5$

Compute the expected film thickness.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Given the empirical model:

$$h = K \cdot \frac{C^{\beta}}{\omega^{\gamma}}$$

Substitute the values:

$$h = 0.52 \cdot \frac{(72)^{2.0}}{(3000)^{0.5}}$$

Evaluate the numerator:

$$(72)^{2.0} = 5184$$

Evaluate the denominator:

$$(3000)^{0.5} = \sqrt{3000} \approx 54.772$$

Substitute back:

$$h = 0.52 \cdot \frac{5184}{54.772}$$

$$h = 0.52 \cdot 94.638$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 49.2 \ \mu m}$$

This is consistent with the **SU-8 3050** nominal thickness of **~50 μm at 3000 rpm**, validating the model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU problems frequently ask students to *back-solve* for required spin speed given a target film thickness. You must rearrange the formula algebraically — practice isolating $\omega$.

### Step 3 — Soft Bake (Pre-Exposure Bake)
- Purpose: **evaporate residual solvent** so the film is dry and dimensionally stable.
- Hot plate baking is preferred over convection ovens (avoids skin formation).
- **Temperature profile (for SU-8 3050, ~50 μm film):**
  - Ramp from 25 °C → 65 °C at **2 °C/min**, hold **5 min** (low-temp step evaporates bulk solvent).
  - Ramp from 65 °C → 95 °C at **2 °C/min**, hold **15 – 20 min** (high-temp step removes residual solvent).
  - Cool to room temperature slowly (avoid thermal shock).
- **Final solvent content target:** < **1%** by weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequence of poor soft bake:** Trapped solvent causes **poor adhesion, void formation, T-topping (swelling of sidewalls), and under-cut development.**

### Step 4 — Exposure
- Light source: **mask aligner** with **mercury (Hg) i-line (365 nm)** or **broadband UV (350 – 400 nm)**.
- Dose: **100 – 250 mJ/cm²** (increases with film thickness).
- Mode: **contact, proximity, or projection lithography.**
- For thick films (>100 μm), a **long-pass filter** that cuts off below 350 nm is used to reduce standing-wave effects.
- Vacuum contact mode is preferred to avoid the **air gap** between mask and wafer, which causes diffraction-induced sidewall taper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Chemistry Insight:** The PAG absorbs UV photons and dissociates. Each photon generates **one molecule of strong acid**. Higher dose → higher acid concentration → faster and more complete cross-linking.

### Step 5 — Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)
- The PEB is the **most critical thermal step** in SU-8 processing.
- Temperature profile (for SU-8 3050):
  - Ramp from 25 °C → 65 °C at **2 °C/min**, hold **1 – 2 min**.
  - Ramp from 65 °C → 95 °C at **2 °C/min**, hold **5 – 10 min**.
- The acid generated in Step 4 catalyzes the **cationic ring-opening polymerization** of the epoxy groups, forming the cross-linked network.

> [!NOTE]
> **PEB is irreversible** — once cross-linking is complete, the film is essentially permanent. Under-bake → underdeveloped, fragile features. Over-bake → lateral acid diffusion, blurred or T-topped sidewalls.

### Step 6 — Development
- Developer: **SU-8 developer (1-methoxy-2-propyl acetate, also called PGMEA)**.
- Process: **immersion with gentle agitation** for **5 – 20 min** depending on thickness.
- The unexposed (uncross-linked) SU-8 dissolves; the exposed (cross-linked) regions remain.
- Rinse: **isopropanol (IPA)** or fresh developer, then **DI water**, then **N₂ blow-dry**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** Aggressive ultrasonication or over-development can **undercut** or **lift off** fine features, especially if the soft-bake was insufficient. Use **mild agitation**.

### Step 7 — Hard Bake (Optional)
- A final high-temperature bake (150 – 200 °C for 30 min) **further cross-links** the polymer and improves:
  - Mechanical strength
  - Chemical resistance
  - Thermal stability
- It is **not always performed** if the SU-8 structure is to be released (e.g., for free-standing microstructures).

### Step 8 — Release (for free-standing structures)
- For suspended SU-8 beams/membranes, the underlying **sacrificial layer** (e.g., SiO₂, photoresist, or metal) is etched away.
- Wet release uses **HF, KOH, or acetone** depending on the sacrificial material.
- Critical point drying is used to **prevent stiction** (surface-tension-induced collapse) of released structures.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes and Their Causes

| Failure Mode | Likely Cause | Mitigation |
|--------------|--------------|------------|
| **Undercut at base** | Over-development | Reduce development time; improve PEB |
| **T-topping (swollen sidewall)** | Excess acid diffusion during PEB | Lower PEB temperature; shorter PEB time |
| **Cracking** | High internal stress from over-bake or thick film | Use hard bake < 200 °C; ramp slowly |
| **Poor adhesion** | Moisture on substrate or insufficient dehydration bake | Dehydrate at 200 °C; use HMDS |
| **Pin-holes** | Particles or solvent bubbles | Filter resist; vacuum degas before spin |
| **Sidewall taper** | Diffraction at mask-wafer gap | Use vacuum contact mode |
| **Stiction after release** | Capillary forces during drying | Use critical point drying or freeze-drying |
| **Incomplete cross-linking** | Underexposure or insufficient PEB | Increase dose and PEB time |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Acid Diffusion Length in SU-8

The acid (HSbF₆) generated during exposure diffuses during the PEB. The mean-square displacement follows the random-walk diffusion law in 1D:

$$\langle x^2 \rangle = 2 \cdot D \cdot t_{PEB}$$

where:
- $\langle x^2 \rangle$ = mean-squared displacement (m²)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of the acid in the SU-8 matrix (m²/s)
- $t_{PEB}$ = duration of PEB (s)

Therefore, the characteristic **diffusion length** is:

$$L_{diff} = \sqrt{\langle x^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot t_{PEB}}$$

#### **Numerical Worked Example — Diffusion Length Calculation**

For a PEB at **95 °C** lasting **600 s (10 min)**, with $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ m²/s (literature value for SU-8 PAG at 95 °C), find $L_{diff}$.

**Step 1:** Compute $2 \cdot D \cdot t_{PEB}$:

$$2 \cdot D \cdot t_{PEB} = 2 \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-13}) \cdot (600)$$

$$2 \cdot D \cdot t_{PEB} = 1.2 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Take the square root:

$$L_{diff} = \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$L_{diff} \approx 1.095 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L_{diff} \approx 10.95 \ \mu m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The acid diffuses roughly **11 μm** laterally during a 10-minute PEB at 95 °C. This defines the **sidewall resolution limit** — features smaller than ~22 μm (twice $L_{diff}$) may merge or blur.

---

## 3.4 Aspect Ratio Derivation

The aspect ratio of a SU-8 feature is defined as the ratio of its **height** (the spin-coated film thickness $h$) to its **minimum lateral dimension** $W$ (set by the photomask):

$$AR = \frac{h}{W} = \frac{K \cdot C^{\beta} \cdot \omega^{-\gamma}}{W}$$

This shows that **maximum AR is achieved** by:
- **Maximizing** film thickness (high $C$, low $\omega$)
- **Minimizing** lateral feature width (smallest photolithographic feature on the mask)

For a **100 μm thick film** with a **5 μm minimum feature**, $AR = 20:1$, which is the canonical HAR threshold.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define SU-8. Why is it classified as a negative photoresist? List any two of its key mechanical properties.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** SU-8 is an **epoxy-based, negative-tone photoresist** consisting of the glycidyl-ether of bisphenol-A novolac (EPON SU-8 resin) dissolved in a solvent (gamma-butyrolactone or cyclopentanone) together with a photoacid generator (PAG), typically a triarylsulfonium hexafluoroantimonate salt. *(1 mark)*

**Negative photoresist classification:** SU-8 is called a negative photoresist because the regions **exposed to UV light become cross-linked and insoluble** in the developer, while the unexposed regions **dissolve away** during development. Hence, the final pattern on the wafer is a *negative* of the pattern on the photomask. *(1 mark)*

**Key Mechanical Properties (any two):** *(1 mark)*
1. **Young's Modulus** of fully cross-linked SU-8: **2.0 – 4.0 GPa**
2. **Tensile Strength:** approximately **30 – 60 MPa**
3. **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** approximately **200 °C** (fully cross-linked)
4. **Aspect Ratio Capability:** greater than **20:1**, allowing very tall microstructures.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Briefly explain the role of the photoacid generator (PAG) in SU-8 photolithography. Why is a post-exposure bake (PEB) necessary?*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of the PAG:** *(1.5 marks)*
The photoacid generator (PAG) — typically **triarylsulfonium hexafluoroantimonate** — is the light-sensitive component of SU-8. When exposed to UV light (especially the **i-line at 365 nm**), the PAG undergoes **photolysis**, releasing a strong **Brønsted acid (HSbF₆)**. This acid acts as the **catalyst** for the subsequent cross-linking reaction of the epoxy groups. Without the PAG, the resist would be insensitive to UV light.

**Necessity of the PEB:** *(1.5 marks)*
The post-exposure bake (PEB) is necessary to:
1. **Activate the catalytic cross-linking** — the photogenerated acid must diffuse and protonate the epoxy oxygen before polymerization can occur. This requires thermal energy supplied by the PEB (typically **65 °C then 95 °C**).
2. **Complete the cationic ring-opening polymerization** of the epoxy groups into a **3D cross-linked network**.
3. **Drive off any residual solvent** that may have diffused back into the film.

Without the PEB, the exposed regions would remain largely soluble and would be washed away during development, resulting in **complete pattern loss**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks on SU-8 questions by:
> 1. **Confusing the type of photoresist** (negative vs. positive) — be explicit.
> 2. **Skipping the role of PAG and acid catalysis** — this is the most frequently tested *why* question.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems (always state mJ/cm², μm, GPa).
> 4. **Missing the connection between thickness and exposure dose** for thick films.
> 5. **Writing vague answers** about "soft bake removes solvent" without specifying temperatures and the two-step ramp.
> 6. **Conflating soft bake and post-exposure bake** — remember: PEB enables the *cross-linking*, while soft bake only removes *solvent*.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow, describe the **complete fabrication sequence** for a SU-8 high-aspect-ratio MEMS structure. In your answer, clearly state the purpose of each step. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A researcher uses **SU-8 3050** to spin-coat a 4-inch silicon wafer. The solids concentration is **C = 72%**, the spin speed is **ω = 2500 rpm**, and the empirical constants are **K = 0.52, β = 2.0, γ = 0.5**.  
(i) Calculate the expected film thickness.  
(ii) If the researcher wants a film thickness of **100 μm**, what spin speed should be used (assume C and K remain the same)? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Fabrication Sequence:** *(7 marks — 1 mark for each step's purpose statement)*

1. **Substrate Preparation:** Clean the Si/glass substrate using RCA cleaning or piranha solution. Dehydration bake at **200 °C for 10 min** removes moisture. *Purpose:* Ensure clean, dry, hydrophobic surface for uniform resist adhesion.

2. **Spin Coating:** Dispense ~3 mL of SU-8 onto the wafer. Perform a two-stage spin — spread at 500 rpm for 10 s, then spin at the target rpm for 30 s. *Purpose:* Form a uniform thin film of controlled thickness.

3. **Soft Bake (Pre-Exposure Bake):** Bake on a hot plate at **65 °C for 5 min**, then ramp to **95 °C for 15 – 20 min**. *Purpose:* Evaporate the bulk solvent so the film is dry and dimensionally stable for exposure.

4. **UV Exposure:** Place the photomask on the wafer in **vacuum contact mode**. Expose to **i-line UV (365 nm)** at a dose of **100 – 250 mJ/cm²**. *Purpose:* Photolyze the PAG and generate the strong Brønsted acid in the exposed regions.

5. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB):** Bake at **65 °C for 2 min**, then ramp to **95 °C for 5 – 10 min**. *Purpose:* Activate the cationic ring-opening polymerization of the epoxy groups, forming the cross-linked 3D network.

6. **Development:** Immerse in **SU-8 developer (PGMEA)** for 5 – 20 min with mild agitation, followed by IPA rinse and N₂ drying. *Purpose:* Dissolve the unexposed (uncross-linked) regions, leaving behind the cross-linked pattern.

7. **Hard Bake (Optional):** Bake at **150 – 200 °C for 30 min**. *Purpose:* Maximize cross-link density, improving mechanical strength, chemical resistance, and thermal stability.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:** *(7 marks)*

**(i) Film thickness at ω = 2500 rpm:**

Given:
- $K = 0.52$
- $C = 72$ wt%
- $\beta = 2.0$
- $\omega = 2500$ rpm
- $\gamma = 0.5$

The empirical formula is:

$$h = K \cdot \frac{C^{\beta}}{\omega^{\gamma}}$$

Substituting:

$$h = 0.52 \cdot \frac{(72)^{2.0}}{(2500)^{0.5}}$$

Compute numerator:

$$(72)^{2.0} = 5184$$

Compute denominator:

$$(2500)^{0.5} = 50.0$$

Therefore:

$$h = 0.52 \cdot \frac{5184}{50.0} = 0.52 \cdot 103.68$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 53.9 \ \mu m}$$

**(3.5 marks for the calculation)**

**(ii) Required spin speed for h = 100 μm:**

We need to solve for $\omega$ given:
- $h = 100$ μm
- $K = 0.52$
- $C = 72$, $\beta = 2.0$, $\gamma = 0.5$

Rearrange the formula:

$$\omega^{\gamma} = \frac{K \cdot C^{\beta}}{h}$$

$$\omega^{0.5} = \frac{0.52 \cdot (72)^{2.0}}{100}$$

$$\omega^{0.5} = \frac{0.52 \cdot 5184}{100} = \frac{2695.68}{100} = 26.957$$

Square both sides:

$$\omega = (26.957)^{2}$$

$$\omega \approx 726.7 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{\omega \approx 727 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(3.5 marks for the calculation)**

**Engineering Interpretation:** A very low spin speed of ~727 rpm is required to produce a 100 μm film with SU-8 3050. This is at the lower bound of practical spin-coating; very thick films (>200 μm) typically require **multi-coat processing** or use of the **SU-8 3000 series** (which has higher solids content).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **chemical mechanism** by which SU-8 becomes insoluble after UV exposure. In your answer, describe the role of the photoacid generator, the post-exposure bake, and the cationic ring-opening polymerization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare SU-8 with the conventional positive photoresist **AZ-series (e.g., AZ-4620)** in terms of:
   (i) Tone (positive vs. negative)
   (ii) Typical maximum aspect ratio
   (iii) Optical transparency in the visible/near-UV range
   (iv) Post-processing temperature tolerance
   (v) Typical application areas in MEMS
   *Use a tabular comparison. Also discuss two failure modes specific to SU-8 processing and their remedies.* *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Chemical Mechanism:** *(7 marks)*

**Stage 1 — Photolysis of the PAG (during exposure):**
SU-8 contains a **photoacid generator (PAG)** — typically **triarylsulfonium hexafluoroantimonate** — dissolved uniformly in the epoxy resin. When UV photons (especially the **365 nm i-line**) strike the PAG molecule, they transfer enough energy to **cleave a carbon–sulfur bond** in the sulfonium cation. The products of this photolysis are:
- A neutral triarylsulfide fragment
- A **strong Brønsted acid (HSbF₆)** — hexafluoroantimonic acid

The H⁺ from this acid is the **catalyst** for the next stage. *(2 marks)*

**Stage 2 — Acid Diffusion and Catalysis (during PEB):**
During the post-exposure bake (typically **65 °C → 95 °C**), the photogenerated acid becomes mobile. The acid **protonates the oxygen atom of an epoxy ring** in the SU-8 monomer, activating the ring. The protonated epoxy undergoes **cationic ring-opening polymerization**: the C–O bond in the three-membered epoxy ring breaks, and a new C–O bond forms with the oxygen of an adjacent molecule. *(2 marks)*

**Stage 3 — Cross-Linked Network Formation:**
Because each SU-8 monomer has **8 epoxy groups**, the polymerization can branch in 3D, building a dense cross-linked network. Importantly, **the H⁺ ion is regenerated** at each step (it is a catalyst, not a reactant), so the chain reaction propagates through the entire exposed region. The result is a **3D, mechanically robust epoxy network** that is **insoluble** in the SU-8 developer (PGMEA). *(2 marks)*

**Stage 4 — Selective Dissolution (during development):**
The unexposed regions, which received no acid and therefore did not cross-link, remain **soluble** in PGMEA. They dissolve away during development, leaving behind the cross-linked pattern. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Comparison Table:** *(5 marks)*

| Parameter | SU-8 | AZ-4620 (Positive Photoresist) |
|-----------|------|-------------------------------|
| (i) Tone | **Negative** — exposed regions remain | **Positive** — exposed regions dissolve |
| (ii) Max Aspect Ratio | **> 20:1** (up to 50:1 reported) | Typically **< 5:1** |
| (iii) Optical Transparency (above 360 nm) | **> 90%** — highly transparent | Opaque in UV; poor transparency at 365 nm |
| (iv) Post-Process Temperature Tolerance | Up to **~200 °C** (fully cross-linked $T_g \approx 200$ °C) | Up to **~120 °C** (softens above this) |
| (v) Typical MEMS Applications | High-aspect-ratio microstructures, microfluidics, microneedles, bio-MEMS, optical MEMS | Sacrificial layers, planar features, masks for etching, low-AR structures |

**(2 marks) Two SU-8-Specific Failure Modes and Remedies:**

1. **T-Topping (swollen sidewalls):** Caused by excessive acid diffusion during the PEB. *Remedy:* Reduce PEB temperature and time, or use a lower-diffusion PAG. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Cracking in thick films:** Caused by high internal stress from over-curing or rapid cooling. *Remedy:* Use slow ramp rates (≤2 °C/min) and avoid hard-bake temperatures above 200 °C. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for SU-8:**

- ✅ SU-8 = **E**poxy-based **P**olymer **n**egative photoresist (**EPN** mnemonic).
- ✅ Classified as **NEGATIVE tone** — exposed regions cross-link and **remain**; unexposed regions **dissolve**.
- ✅ Active monomer = **EPON SU-8** (glycidyl-ether of bisphenol-A novolac) with **8 epoxy groups** per molecule.
- ✅ Solvent = **gamma-butyrolactone (GBL)** or **cyclopentanone** (3000 series).
- ✅ Photoacid Generator (PAG) = **triarylsulfonium hexafluoroantimonate** → releases **HSbF₆** on UV exposure.
- ✅ Mechanism = **cationic ring-opening polymerization** (acid-catalyzed).
- ✅ PEB is **MANDATORY** — without PEB, no cross-linking.
- ✅ Optimum exposure dose: **100 – 250 mJ/cm²** (i-line, 365 nm).
- ✅ Soft bake: 65 °C → 95 °C; PEB: 65 °C → 95 °C; Hard bake (optional): 150 – 200 °C.
- ✅ Developer: **PGMEA (1-methoxy-2-propyl acetate)**.
- ✅ Spin-coating relation: **$h = K \cdot C^{\beta} / \omega^{\gamma}$** (β ≈ 2, γ ≈ 0.5).
- ✅ Maximum aspect ratio: **> 20:1** (up to 50:1 optimized).
- ✅ Young's Modulus (cured): **2.0 – 4.0 GPa**.
- ✅ Glass Transition Temperature: **~200 °C** (fully cross-linked).
- ✅ Refractive Index: **1.67 – 1.68** at 365 nm.
- ✅ Optical transparency: **> 90%** above 360 nm.
- ✅ Acid diffusion length: **$L_{diff} = \sqrt{2Dt_{PEB}}$** (≈ 11 μm for 10 min PEB at 95 °C).
- ✅ Two main failure modes to remember: **T-topping** (over-bake) and **cracking** (rapid cool/over-cure).
- ✅ Most common applications: **microfluidics, microneedles, bio-MEMS scaffolds, micro-optics, wafer-level packaging**.
- ✅ SU-8 is the **industry-standard polymer for high-aspect-ratio MEMS**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PMMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PMMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Polymethyl Methacrylate (PMMA)**, commercially marketed under trade names such as **Plexiglas®, Lucite®, Perspex®, and Acrylite®**, is a synthetic amorphous thermoplastic polymer of methyl methacrylate (MMA). It belongs to the family of engineering polymers and is one of the most widely employed *polymeric structural and functional materials* in **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, microfluidics, and lab-on-chip platforms.

The IUPAC nomenclature designates the repeat unit as **poly(methyl 2-methylpropenoate)** with the chemical formula:

$$\text{[CH}_2\text{C(CH}_3\text{)(COOCH}_3\text{)]}_n$$

where **n** is the **degree of polymerization** (number of repeat units in a single chain).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Materials for MEMS):**
> PMMA is classified as a **Polymeric Substrate Material** in MEMS, distinct from inorganic materials (Silicon, SiO₂, Si₃N₄) and metallic thin films. It serves as a *positive-tone electron-beam and deep-UV photoresist* and as a *bulk structural polymer* in polymer MEMS, microfluidics, and BioMEMS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **long freight train**. Each **freight car** is a single molecule of **methyl methacrylate (MMA)** — the monomer. When thousands of these cars link together end-to-end through a chemical coupling process called **addition polymerization**, the resulting train is the **PMMA polymer chain**.

Now imagine stacking many of these parallel trains side-by-side in a depot, but with their cars slightly misaligned (because the side groups —COOCH₃ stick out at random angles). This disordered, glassy "parking lot" of chains is what gives PMMA its defining property: **optical transparency combined with amorphous glassy behavior** (no sharp melting point, but a gradual softening at the **glass transition temperature, T_g ≈ 105 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of PMMA as the *"transparent aluminum"* of the polymer world. It has roughly **half the density of glass (1.18 g/cm³ vs. 2.5 g/cm³)**, is **10× more impact-resistant than glass**, and transmits **~92% of visible light** — almost as clear as optical crown glass.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ | **1.17 – 1.20** | g/cm³ |
| Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g$ | **≈ 105** | °C |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **2.4 – 3.4** | GPa |
| Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{ts}$ | **48 – 76** | MPa |
| Elongation at Break | $\epsilon_b$ | **2 – 10** | % |
| Refractive Index | $n$ | **1.490 – 1.492** | – |
| Optical Transmission | $T$ | **≥ 92** | % (visible) |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | $\alpha$ | **5 – 9 × 10⁻⁵** | /°C |
| Dielectric Constant (1 MHz) | $\varepsilon_r$ | **2.6 – 3.5** | – |
| Decomposition Temperature | $T_d$ | **> 200** | °C |
| Water Absorption (24 h) | – | **0.1 – 0.4** | % |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are typical for **bulk cast PMMA sheets**. *Spin-coated PMMA films* (used in lithography) exhibit different mechanical and rheological properties depending on molecular weight, spin speed, and soft-bake conditions.

## 1.4 Why PMMA in MEMS? — The Engineering Rationale

1. **Low-cost, rapid prototyping** of microfluidic devices (vs. costly silicon/SU-8 molds).
2. **Biocompatibility** (USP Class VI certified) → ideal for BioMEMS, organ-on-chip, point-of-care diagnostics.
3. **Optical transparency** → enables in-situ optical observation in optofluidic and LOC systems.
4. **Easy processability** via **hot embossing, injection molding, laser micromachining, and solvent bonding**.
5. **High-resolution e-beam resist** → defines nanostructures down to **~10 nm** feature size — the *highest resolution* positive-tone organic resist used in research nanofabrication.
6. **Tunable surface chemistry** — easily modified by O₂ plasma, UV-ozone, or chemical functionalization for selective biomolecule immobilization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Molecular Architecture of PMMA

PMMA is synthesized by **free-radical addition polymerization** of the vinyl monomer **methyl methacrylate (CH₂=C(CH₃)COOCH₃)**. The polymerization reaction proceeds in three classical steps:

1. **Initiation:** Thermal or photochemical decomposition of an initiator (e.g., AIBN — azobisisobutyronitrile) generates free radicals R•, which attack the C=C double bond of MMA.
2. **Propagation:** The active chain adds successive monomer units, creating a growing chain radical.
3. **Termination:** Two chain radicals combine (coupling) or disproportionate, ending the chain growth.

The resulting polymer has:
- A **carbon backbone** of saturated C–C single bonds (hence the polymer is *thermoplastic, not crosslinked*).
- Bulky side groups: a **methyl (–CH₃)** and a **methoxycarbonyl (–COOCH₃)** on every alternate carbon.
- These bulky pendant groups **prevent close chain packing** and **suppress crystallization** → PMMA is strictly **amorphous** and optically transparent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tacticity Matters!** The arrangement of side groups along the chain (tacticity) drastically alters properties:
> - **Isotactic PMMA** → regular side-group arrangement → partially crystallizable, higher T_g.
> - **Syndiotactic PMMA** → alternating side groups → highest T_g (~ 130 °C), best optical clarity.
> - **Atactic PMMA** → random arrangement → typical commercial PMMA, T_g ≈ 105 °C.

## 2.2 PMMA in MEMS — Operational Roles

### A. PMMA as a Positive-Tone Electron-Beam Resist

When a PMMA film is exposed to a focused electron beam, **main-chain scission** (cleavage of C–C bonds) occurs preferentially over crosslinking, *reducing* the average molecular weight in the exposed region. Upon development in a solvent (typically **MIBK:IPA 1:3** or **acetone**), the lower-MW fragments dissolve **faster** than the unexposed high-MW regions — hence *"positive-tone"*.

### B. PMMA as a Structural / Substrate Material

For polymer-based MEMS, PMMA sheets/films are patterned by:
- **Hot embossing** (nanoimprint against a silicon or nickel master).
- **Injection molding** (high-volume, sub-µm features).
- **CO₂ laser ablation / excimer laser micromachining.**
- **X-ray lithography (LIGA process) using PMMA as a thick resist.**

### C. PMMA in Microfluidics

PMMA is bonded to itself (or to other polymers) by:
- **Thermal bonding** (80–110 °C, near T_g, applied pressure).
- **Solvent bonding** (ethanol, isopropanol, acetonitrile — partially dissolves surface).
- **UV-assisted bonding** (with photoinitiators).
- **Adhesive bonding** (less common in MEMS).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_n = M_0 \cdot n$ | Number-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 2 | $M_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 3 | $PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Polydispersity Index (≈ 1.5 – 2.0 for free-radical PMMA) | – |
| 4 | $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$ | Hooke's law (linear elastic regime) | Pa |
| 5 | $\epsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal strain | – |
| 6 | $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (constrained) | Pa |
| 7 | $T_g(\text{K}) \approx T_g(\text{°C}) + 273.15$ | Unit conversion | K |
| 8 | $R_q = \frac{R_{max}}{N}$ | Surface roughness of replicated PMMA (R_max of master divided by replication factor N ≈ 2–4) | nm |
| 9 | $R = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}$ | Fresnel reflectance at normal incidence (optical interfaces) | – |
| 10 | $\tau = \frac{\eta \cdot L}{\Delta P \cdot w \cdot h^3}$ | Hydraulic residence time in PMMA microchannel (Hagen–Poiseuille) | s |
| 11 | $D_e = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | Etch/diffusion rate (Arrhenius form) | m/s |
| 12 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot w \cdot h^3}{12 \eta L}\!\left[1 - \sum_{n,\text{odd}}\dfrac{192}{(n\pi)^5}\cdot\dfrac{h}{w}\tanh\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi w}{2h}\right)\right]$ | Volumetric flow rate in rectangular PMMA microchannel | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Memorize equations **(1), (3), (4), (5), (6), and (11)**. The **PDI**, **thermal-stress** and **Arrhenius etch-rate** equations are *frequently tested* in Module 3 numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Specific Use of PMMA |
|---|---|
| **Microfluidics / LOC** | Microchannels for cell sorting, droplet generators, organ-on-chip devices. |
| **BioMEMS** | Substrate for DNA microarrays, immunosensors, microneedle arrays. |
| **Optical MEMS** | Waveguides, microlenses, optofluidic lenses (tunable by fluid injection). |
| **Nanofabrication** | E-beam lithography resist for NEMS, photonic crystals, plasmonic nanostructures. |
| **RF MEMS** | Dielectric layers in capacitive switches; low-loss at GHz frequencies ($\tan\delta \sim 0.014$). |
| **Disposable diagnostics** | Low-cost lateral-flow chips, COVID-19 test cartridges, pregnancy test cassettes. |
| **Structural MEMS** | Microvalves, micropumps, microgrippers (when large forces are not required). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Molecular Weight, Degree of Polymerization & PDI

### Statement
A sample of PMMA is found to contain three chain populations:
- 30 % of chains with $M_1 = 20{,}000$ g/mol
- 50 % of chains with $M_2 = 100{,}000$ g/mol
- 20 % of chains with $M_3 = 250{,}000$ g/mol

Calculate the number-average molecular weight $M_n$, weight-average molecular weight $M_w$, and the **Polydispersity Index (PDI)**. The molar mass of the repeat unit $M_0$ is **100.12 g/mol**.

### Step 1 — Identify the mole fractions $N_i$ (in this case, equal to the given weight fractions *when normalized properly*)

Let us interpret the percentages as **number fractions** (chains per 100 chains):

$$N_1 = 0.30, \quad N_2 = 0.50, \quad N_3 = 0.20$$

$$\sum N_i = 0.30 + 0.50 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the number-average molecular weight $M_n$

$$M_n = \sum N_i M_i = (0.30)(20{,}000) + (0.50)(100{,}000) + (0.20)(250{,}000)$$

$$M_n = 6{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 106{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the weight-average molecular weight $M_w$

$$M_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{(0.30)(20{,}000)^2 + (0.50)(100{,}000)^2 + (0.20)(250{,}000)^2}{106{,}000}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = (0.30)(4 \times 10^{8}) + (0.50)(10^{10}) + (0.20)(6.25 \times 10^{10})$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.2 \times 10^{8} + 5.0 \times 10^{9} + 1.25 \times 10^{10}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.786 \times 10^{10}$$

$$M_w = \frac{1.786 \times 10^{10}}{1.06 \times 10^{5}} = 168{,}490 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the Polydispersity Index

$$PDI = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{168{,}490}{106{,}000} = 1.59$$

### Step 5 — Calculate the degree of polymerization

$$\bar{n} = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{106{,}000}{100.12} \approx 1058 \text{ repeat units per chain}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **PDI = 1.59** is typical of **free-radical polymerized PMMA** ($\text{PDI}_{\text{theoretical}} = 1.5$ for pure termination by disproportionation, 1.0 for "living" anionic polymerization). Commercial *e-beam grade 950 K PMMA* has **M_w ≈ 950 000 g/mol**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Stress in a Constrained PMMA Microcantilever

### Statement
A PMMA microcantilever of length $L = 500\ \mu\text{m}$, width $w = 100\ \mu\text{m}$, and thickness $h = 5\ \mu\text{m}$ is fabricated at room temperature (25 °C) and operated at 60 °C. The cantilever end is fully clamped. Compute the **thermal stress** generated at the fixed end, given:

- Young's modulus: $E = 3.0\ \text{GPa}$
- Coefficient of thermal expansion: $\alpha = 7.0 \times 10^{-5}\ /\text{°C}$

### Step 1 — Temperature change

$$\Delta T = T_{op} - T_{fab} = 60 - 25 = 35\ \text{°C}$$

### Step 2 — Free thermal strain (if unconstrained)

$$\epsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (7.0 \times 10^{-5})(35) = 2.45 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 3 — Constrained thermal stress (Hooke's Law)

For a fully clamped beam, the thermal strain is converted directly to stress:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \epsilon_{th} = (3.0 \times 10^{9})(2.45 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = 7.35 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 7.35\ \text{MPa}}$$

### Step 4 — Bending moment at the fixed end (rectangular cross-section)

$$M = \frac{\sigma_{th} \cdot I}{c}$$

where $I = \dfrac{wh^3}{12}$ and $c = h/2$:

$$I = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = 1.042 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{m}^4$$

$$M = \frac{(7.35 \times 10^{6})(1.042 \times 10^{-19})}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.06 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{N·m}$$

### Step 5 — Deflection at the free end (cantilever tip)

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{\sigma_{th} \cdot L^2}{2 E \cdot c} = \frac{(7.35 \times 10^{6})(500 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2(3.0 \times 10^{9})(2.5 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{1.838 \times 10^{-3}}{15} = 1.225 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 122.5\ \mu\text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A temperature swing of only **35 °C** causes **~122 µm tip deflection** in PMMA cantilevers — a *huge* thermal sensitivity. This is exploited in **PMMA-based thermal actuators and bimorph sensors**, but it is also the *primary failure mode* in packaged PMMA MEMS under thermal cycling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Etch Rate of PMMA in O₂ Plasma

### Statement
The etch rate $R$ of a PMMA film in an O₂ reactive-ion etcher follows Arrhenius behavior:

$$R(T) = R_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

The etch rates measured at two substrate temperatures are:

- $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$, $R_1 = 80\ \text{nm/min}$
- $T_2 = 350\ \text{K}$, $R_2 = 180\ \text{nm/min}$

Find the **activation energy $E_a$** in eV/atom and the **prefactor $R_0$**.

### Step 1 — Form the ratio $R_2 / R_1$

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

### Step 2 — Take natural log on both sides

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the temperature term

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{300} = (2.8571 - 3.3333) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= -4.7619 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Ln ratio

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{180}{80}\right) = \ln(2.25) = 0.8109$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $E_a$

$$0.8109 = -\frac{E_a}{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})}(-4.7619 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$E_a = \frac{0.8109 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23}}{4.7619 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = 2.352 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J/atom}$$

Convert to eV: $E_a = \dfrac{2.352 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.147\ \text{eV/atom}$

### Step 6 — Compute $R_0$

$$R_0 = R_1 \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k_B T_1}\right) = 80 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{2.352 \times 10^{-20}}{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}\right)$$

$$\frac{E_a}{k_B T_1} = \frac{2.352 \times 10^{-20}}{4.143 \times 10^{-21}} = 5.677$$

$$R_0 = 80 \cdot e^{5.677} = 80 \cdot 293.0 = 23{,}440\ \text{nm/min} \approx 23.4\ \mu\text{m/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **low $E_a$ (~ 0.15 eV)** indicates that O₂-plasma etching of PMMA is *reaction-limited by surface oxidation chemistry*, not by diffusion of reactive species — hence the strong temperature dependence.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of PMMA with Other MEMS Materials

| Property | **PMMA** | Silicon (Si) | SU-8 | PDMS (Sylgard 184) | Glass (Pyrex) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Amorphous thermoplastic | Crystalline | Epoxy (negative photoresist) | Elastomer | Amorphous ceramic |
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.18 | 2.33 | 1.07 – 1.20 | 1.05 | 2.23 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | **2.4 – 3.4** | 130 – 170 | 2 – 4 | **0.0005 – 0.003** | 64 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 48 – 76 | 7000 (brittle) | 60 – 80 | 2 – 7 | 28 (flexural) |
| $T_g$ (°C) | **105** | – | > 200 (crosslinked) | – 125 (low $T_g$) | ~ 560 |
| Optical Transparency (%) | **92** | Opaque (native) | Transparent (thin films) | Transparent (UV-blocked) | 92 |
| Biocompatibility | **Excellent (USP VI)** | Variable (coating needed) | Good | Excellent | Good |
| Processing | Embossing, molding, laser | Lithography + etch | Photolithography | Casting, soft lithography | Wet/dry etch, anodic bonding |
| Cost (per wafer / chip) | **Very Low** | High | Moderate | Low | High |
| Best Use Case | Microfluidics, BioMEMS, e-beam resist | Inertial sensors, high-Q resonators | Thick structural layers | Soft microfluidics, stretchable electronics | Optofluidics, packaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** PMMA is *not* a replacement for silicon in high-frequency, high-Q resonators (E ~ GPa vs. 130 GPa → much lower resonance frequency). It is a **complementary material** in *polymer MEMS* and *BioMEMS*, where silicon is overkill or bio-incompatible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PMMA Molecular Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Methyl Methacrylate Monomer CH2=CCH3COOCH3] -->|Initiation with AIBN| B1[Active Radical]
    B1 -->|Propagation Step 1| C1[PMMA Dimer]
    C1 -->|Propagation Step 2| D1[PMMA Trimer]
    D1 -->|Propagation Step n| E1[PMMA Polymer Chain CH2 C CH3 COOCH3 repeated n times]
    E1 -->|Many chains aggregate| F1[Amorphous Bulk PMMA via Van der Waals]
    F1 -->|Heat above Tg 105 C| G1[Rubbery state]
    G1 -->|Heat above Td 200 C| H1[Thermal decomposition]
    F1 -->|Optical photons 400 to 700 nm| I1[Transparent 92 percent transmission]
    F1 -->|Electron beam exposure| J1[Main chain scission lower Mw]
    J1 -->|Develop in MIBK IPA 1:3| K1[Positive tone pattern]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the path *MMA → radical → dimer → trimer → polymer chain → amorphous bulk PMMA*. Then note the three operational outputs of the bulk material: **(a)** thermal transitions (Tg, Td), **(b)** optical transparency, and **(c)** e-beam lithographic response.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — PMMA MEMS Fabrication Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Step 1 PMMA pellet or sheet] --> P2[Step 2 Thermal molding or hot embossing at 120 to 140 C]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Demolding]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Surface treatment O2 plasma or UV ozone]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Bonding thermal or solvent based]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 PMMA MEMS device microfluidic or sensor]
    Q1[Step A Alternative PMMA spin coating] --> Q2[Step B Soft bake 180 C for 2 min]
    Q2 --> Q3[Step C E beam exposure 10 to 100 keV]
    Q3 --> Q4[Step D Development MIBK IPA 1:3]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step E Hard bake 100 C for 30 min]
    Q5 --> P6
    P6 --> R1[Step 7 Packaging and electrical interconnects]
    R1 --> S1[Final device ready for BioMEMS or Microfluidic deployment]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — PMMA in Microfluidic LOC System

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INLET_MODULE
        IN1[Sample Inlet Reservoir] --> IN2[PMMA Microchannel 100 um wide 50 um deep]
    end
    IN2 --> MIX[Micromixer Serpentine PMMA]
    MIX --> REACT[Reaction Chamber with immobilized antibodies]
    REACT --> DET[Optical Detection Window PMMA transparent]
    DET --> OUT[Waste Outlet]
    EXT1[External LED 470 nm] -->|excitation| DET
    DET -->|fluorescence| PD[Photodetector]
    PD --> DAQ[Data acquisition system]
    style IN2 fill:#cde4ff
    style MIX fill:#cde4ff
    style REACT fill:#cde4ff
    style DET fill:#fff2a8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **yellow-highlighted "Optical Detection Window"** is the *key enabling feature* of PMMA — its > 92 % optical transparency. The same window in a silicon chip would require tedious anodic bonding of a separate Pyrex glass layer.

---

## 4.4 Schematic — PMMA as E-beam Resist (Cross-Sectional View)

| Layer (top → bottom) | Material | Typical Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vacuum / e-beam column | – | – | Exposure medium |
| Conductive coating (optional) | Espacer / Au-Pd | 5–10 nm | Charge dissipation |
| **PMMA resist (exposed)** | 950 K A4 PMMA | **~ 200 nm** | *Scissioned* — develops away |
| **PMMA resist (unexposed)** | 950 K A4 PMMA | 200 nm total | High-MW — remains |
| Substrate | Si / SiO₂ / Glass | 500 µm | Mechanical support |

> [!NOTE]
> For sub-100 nm features, *bilayer PMMA* (high-MW 950 K bottom + low-MW 495 K top) is used to create an *undercut profile* for clean lift-off metallization.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up positive vs. negative tone:** Many students write "PMMA crosslinks under e-beam" — *wrong*. PMMA undergoes **chain scission** → it is a **positive-tone** resist. Si-containing resists (HSQ, SAL601) are negative-tone.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Arrhenius problems:** The activation energy $E_a$ must be in **eV/atom** or **J/mol**, not just "energy". KTU specifically deducts 1 mark for missing units.
> 3. **Ignoring T_g in bonding problems:** Thermal bonding of PMMA must occur *just below* T_g (≈ 100 °C), not at room temperature and not above 130 °C. Above T_g → polymer deforms. Below → no bond. Examiners will deduct marks for stating the wrong bonding temperature.
> 4. **Confusing E-beam vs. UV exposure:** PMMA is *not* a practical deep-UV photoresist (sensitivity is very low). It shines in **e-beam and X-ray lithography**.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Briefly explain why PMMA is classified as a positive-tone e-beam resist. Mention its typical developer composition."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PMMA, upon exposure to a focused electron beam, undergoes **main-chain scission** — the high-energy electrons break the C–C bonds of the polymer backbone, *reducing* the average molecular weight of the exposed region. Because the lower-MW fragments have higher solubility in organic developers, the exposed regions **dissolve faster** than the unexposed regions — this is the operational definition of a **positive-tone resist** (contrast to negative-tone where exposure *crosslinks* and hardens the resist).

Typical developer: **MIBK (methyl isobutyl ketone) : IPA (isopropyl alcohol) in 1:3 volume ratio**, at 23 °C for 30–60 s, followed by IPA rinse to stop development. Higher sensitivity can be achieved with **1:1 MIBK:IPA** at the cost of contrast.

*Mark split:* 1.5 marks for mechanism of scission + positive tone; 1 mark for developer; 0.5 mark for rinse step.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List six key properties of PMMA that make it suitable for microfluidic and BioMEMS applications."**

**Model Answer (any 6, 1 mark each → 3 marks visible ones get full credit):**
1. **Optical transparency** (~ 92 %) — enables in-situ optical detection.
2. **Biocompatibility** (USP Class VI certified) — safe for cell/drug studies.
3. **Low cost** — disposable lab-on-chip cartridges.
4. **Easy fabrication** — hot embossing, injection molding, laser cutting.
5. **Low glass transition temperature** (T_g ≈ 105 °C) — facilitates low-temperature thermal bonding.
6. **Chemical resistance** to alcohols, dilute acids, and bases (pH 4 – 9) — compatible with most biological buffers.
7. *Bonus (any one):* Low autofluorescence, high dielectric strength, gas permeability (O₂, CO₂) — useful for cell-culture chips.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically offers **internal choice** between Question A and Question B (each ~ 14 marks, split into part (a) 7 marks + part (b) 7 marks). Select **either** Q-A **or** Q-B and answer *both* sub-parts.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, describe the **hot embossing process** for fabricating PMMA microfluidic devices. What are the critical process parameters and how do they affect replication fidelity?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PMMA microcantilever of dimensions $L = 300\ \mu\text{m}$, $w = 80\ \mu\text{m}$, $h = 4\ \mu\text{m}$ is used as a temperature sensor. The cantilever end is clamped. If the device is calibrated at 25 °C and the readout is taken at 70 °C, compute the **tip deflection** of the cantilever. Use $E = 3.0\ \text{GPa}$, $\alpha = 7.0 \times 10^{-5}\ /\text{°C}$.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — Hot Embossing (7 marks)

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Master fabrication** | A silicon or nickel mold (the "master" or "stamp") is fabricated by deep reactive-ion etching (DRIE) or electroplating. The master has the *negative* of the desired microfluidic pattern. | 1 |
| 2. **PMMA substrate preparation** | A PMMA sheet (typical thickness 1–5 mm) is cleaned (IPA + DI water + N₂ dry) and optionally pre-annealed above T_g to release internal stresses. | 0.5 |
| 3. **Heating** | The PMMA sheet and master are heated in a press to a temperature **30 – 50 °C above T_g** (i.e., **135 – 155 °C**), where the polymer becomes soft and viscoelastic. | 1 |
| 4. **Pressing** | The master is pressed into the PMMA at a controlled force (typically 5 – 50 kN for a 4-inch substrate) for a dwell time of 2 – 10 min. Vacuum is often applied to remove trapped air. | 1 |
| 5. **Cooling** | The assembly is cooled **below T_g (to ~ 90 °C)** *while maintaining pressure* to "freeze in" the replicated pattern and avoid spring-back. | 0.5 |
| 6. **Demolding** | The master is separated from the PMMA. An **anti-sticking layer** (e.g., FDTS — perfluorodecyltrichlorosilane) is often coated on the master to aid demolding. | 0.5 |
| 7. **Process parameters** | (i) Embossing temperature, (ii) embossing pressure, (iii) dwell time, (iv) cooling rate, (v) vacuum level, (vi) master surface energy. Higher T → lower viscosity → better filling but risk of polymer degradation. Higher P → faster filling but may damage master. | 1.5 |
| 8. **Fidelity discussion** | Replication fidelity (R_q of replica) is typically 80 – 95 % of master; high aspect-ratio features (> 5) are difficult; demolding can cause sidewall taper or pattern distortion. | 1 |

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — Tip Deflection Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — ΔT (1 mark)**
$$\Delta T = 70 - 25 = 45\ \text{°C}$$

**Step 2 — Free thermal strain (1 mark)**
$$\epsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (7.0 \times 10^{-5})(45) = 3.15 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent bending stress at fixed end (1 mark)**

For a fully clamped cantilever constrained from expanding, the stress at the outermost fiber equals:
$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (3.0 \times 10^{9})(7.0 \times 10^{-5})(45)$$
$$\sigma_{th} = 9.45 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 9.45\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Tip deflection formula (1 mark)**
For a cantilever with a uniform thermal moment:
$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{\sigma_{th} \cdot L^2}{2 E \cdot c}$$
where $c = h/2 = 2\ \mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$.

**Step 5 — Substitute (1 mark)**
$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{(9.45 \times 10^{6})(300 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2(3.0 \times 10^{9})(2 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{(9.45 \times 10^{6})(9 \times 10^{-8})}{1.2 \times 10^{4}}$$

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{8.505 \times 10^{-1}}{1.2 \times 10^{4}} = 7.09 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Final answer with units (1 mark)**
$$\boxed{\delta_{tip} \approx 70.9\ \mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 7 — Comment (1 mark):** This deflection is easily measurable by optical lever or capacitive read-out, confirming that PMMA cantilevers are excellent *low-cost thermal sensors* for ΔT > 5 °C, but the large thermal expansion coefficient also makes them *highly susceptible to drift* in precision applications.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **molecular structure, tacticity, and glass transition behavior** of PMMA. How does tacticity influence T_g and optical properties?

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a hot-embossing experiment, a 2 mm PMMA sheet is embossed at 140 °C. If the preform has a number-average molecular weight $M_n = 1.5 \times 10^{5}$ g/mol and the weight-average molecular weight $M_w = 3.0 \times 10^{5}$ g/mol, calculate:
- (i) the Polydispersity Index (PDI),
- (ii) the degree of polymerization,
- (iii) the average end-to-end distance of a chain in the melt (using the freely-jointed-chain model with bond length $l_0 = 0.154$ nm and 2 bonds per repeat unit, and 3 backbone bonds per MMA unit).

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — Structure, Tacticity, T_g (7 marks)

| Point | Explanation | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Repeat unit** | PMMA repeat unit: –[CH₂–C(CH₃)(COOCH₃)]–. Methyl (–CH₃) and methoxycarbonyl (–COOCH₃) are the pendant groups on the quaternary backbone carbon. | 1 |
| 2. **Tacticity definition** | Tacticity is the stereochemical arrangement of side groups along the chain. Three types: **isotactic** (all same side), **syndiotactic** (alternating), **atactic** (random). | 1 |
| 3. **Synthesis route → atactic** | Free-radical polymerization of MMA gives predominantly **atactic** PMMA (60–70 % syndiotactic, 30–40 % atactic, very little isotactic). Anionic or Ziegler–Natta catalysts are required to make isotactic or syndiotactic PMMA. | 1 |
| 4. **Effect on T_g** | Atactic PMMA: T_g ≈ 105 °C. Syndiotactic PMMA: T_g ≈ 125 – 130 °C (highest, most regular chain packing → reduced chain mobility). Isotactic PMMA: T_g ≈ 70 °C (looser helical packing). | 1.5 |
| 5. **Effect on crystallinity** | Atactic PMMA is **fully amorphous** → no sharp melting point, optically transparent (no crystallites to scatter light). Isotactic PMMA can be partially crystalline but is rare. | 1 |
| 6. **Optical clarity** | Commercial PMMA is amorphous with a refractive index of 1.49. Light transmission > 92 % in the visible range. UV absorption begins at ~ 360 nm; for UV-transparent grades, additives are used. | 1 |
| 7. **Conclusion** | Tacticity controls *order*, which in turn controls *physical state* (amorphous vs. crystalline) and *thermal transitions* (T_g). For MEMS, **atactic / syndiotactic-rich PMMA** is preferred for transparency and embossability. | 0.5 |

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — Polymer Statistics (7 marks)

**(i) Polydispersity Index (2 marks)**
$$PDI = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{5}}{1.5 \times 10^{5}} = 2.0$$

*Interpretation:* PDI = 2.0 is characteristic of **free-radical polymerization with termination by combination** (theoretical PDI = 1.5) or by disproportionation (theoretical PDI = 2.0). A PDI of 2.0 suggests chain termination mainly by **disproportionation**.

**(ii) Degree of polymerization (2 marks)**
$$\bar{n} = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{g/mol}}{100.12\ \text{g/mol}} \approx 1498 \text{ repeat units}$$

**(iii) End-to-end distance (3 marks)**

**Step 1 — Number of backbone bonds per chain (1 mark)**
Each MMA repeat unit contributes 2 backbone C–C bonds; given the problem statement says 3 bonds per MMA unit (including the bond connecting to the next unit), we take:
$$N_{bonds} = 3 \times \bar{n} = 3 \times 1498 = 4494 \text{ bonds}$$

**Step 2 — Bond length (given)**
$$l_0 = 0.154\ \text{nm} = 1.54 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Freely-jointed-chain mean square end-to-end distance (1 mark)**
$$\langle R^2 \rangle = N_{bonds} \cdot l_0^{\,2} = (4494)(1.54 \times 10^{-10})^2$$
$$\langle R^2 \rangle = (4494)(2.372 \times 10^{-20}) = 1.066 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — RMS end-to-end distance (1 mark)**
$$\sqrt{\langle R^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{1.066 \times 10^{-16}} = 1.033 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m} \approx 10.3\ \text{nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical PMMA chain in the melt occupies a *random-coil* volume of only **~ 10 nm radius**, while a 2 mm sheet contains **~ 10¹⁷ such chains per gram** entangled together. This huge disparity of length-scales (10 nm chain → 2 mm device) is the essence of *polymer physics in MEMS*.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second revision checklist for KTU Module 3 – PMMA:**

- ✅ PMMA = **poly(methyl methacrylate)**, repeat unit [CH₂–C(CH₃)(COOCH₃)]ₙ, $M_0 ≈ 100.12$ g/mol.
- ✅ **Amorphous, atactic, glassy** thermoplastic; **T_g ≈ 105 °C**, T_d > 200 °C, density ≈ 1.18 g/cm³.
- ✅ **Positive-tone e-beam / X-ray resist**; develops in **MIBK:IPA (1:3)**.
- ✅ **Optical transparency ≥ 92 %** at visible wavelengths; refractive index ≈ 1.49.
- ✅ **Biocompatible (USP Class VI)**; widely used in microfluidics, BioMEMS, LOC.
- ✅ **Low Young's modulus (~ 3 GPa)** compared to silicon (~ 170 GPa) — *not* suitable for high-frequency resonators.
- ✅ **High CTE (~ 7 × 10⁻⁵ /°C)** — useful for thermal sensors, problematic for thermal-cycling reliability.
- ✅ **Hot embossing** at **T_emboss = T_g + (30 – 50 °C)**, i.e., **135 – 155 °C**; demold *below* T_g.
- ✅ **Thermal bonding** at **T ≈ 100 °C** (just below T_g).
- ✅ **PDI** of free-radical PMMA ≈ 1.5 – 2.0; commercial e-beam grade 950 K PMMA has $M_w ≈ 9.5 \times 10^5$ g/mol.
- ✅ **Chain scission** (not crosslinking) under e-beam — *this is the positive-tone mechanism.*
- ✅ **Tacticity** controls T_g and crystallinity: syndiotactic > atactic > isotactic in T_g.
- ✅ Master equations: $M_n = M_0 n$, $PDI = M_w/M_n$, $\sigma_{th} = E\alpha\Delta T$, $R = R_0 \exp(-E_a/k_BT)$.
- ✅ *Failure mode:* high thermal expansion → thermal-stress-induced delamination in multi-layer stacks.
- ✅ *Comparison:* PMMA is *cheaper, more transparent, and more biocompatible* than SU-8, but has *lower modulus, lower T_g, and lower chemical resistance to strong solvents* (acetone, THF, chloroform).

> [!WARNING]
> **One-line examiner's mantra:** *"PMMA is the polymer of choice when transparency, biocompatibility, and low cost matter more than high frequency or chemical ruggedness."* Memorize this single sentence — it answers **80 % of conceptual questions** in the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PDMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PDMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of PDMS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)** is a silicon-based organic polymer belonging to the silicone elastomer family, chemically represented by the repeating unit **-[Si(CH₃)₂-O]ₙ-**, where the methyl groups (–CH₃) are laterally attached to the silicon-oxygen (siloxane) backbone. In the context of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) and microfluidic engineering, PDMS is the most widely used **elastomeric material** for rapid prototyping of micro-channels, bio-MEMS chips, lab-on-a-chip (LoC) platforms, soft lithography stamps, and flexible bio-interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Context):**
> *PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane) is a transparent, biocompatible, elastomeric polymer widely used in soft lithography and microfluidic MEMS device fabrication due to its low Young's modulus, optical transparency, gas permeability, and ease of molding.*

The standard commercial formulation used in academic MEMS labs is **Dow Corning SYLGARD 184**, supplied as a two-part kit: a *base resin* (vinyl-terminated siloxane oligomers) and a *curing agent* (hydrosilane crosslinker + platinum catalyst).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine **silicone kitchen tongs** or **soft baking molds** — they are flexible, transparent, heat-resistant, and can be peeled off a master shape without damage. PDMS behaves exactly like this at the microscopic scale, except it is engineered to nanometer precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:**
> - **PDMS ↔ Transparent Silicone Rubber**: Just as a rubber stamp picks up ink from a carved master and transfers patterns to paper, a *cured* PDMS slab can replicate micron-sized channels and patterns from a silicon/SU-8 master. The master is the "carved block"; the cured PDMS is the "rubber copy."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Mandatory Values)

| Property | Typical Value (10:1 mix) | Units |
|----------|------------------------|-------|
| Young's Modulus (E) | **1.5 – 2.0 MPa** | MPa |
| Density (ρ) | **0.965** | g/cm³ |
| Poisson's Ratio (ν) | **0.5** | dimensionless |
| Refractive Index (n) | **1.41** | – |
| Glass Transition (Tg) | **–125** | °C |
| Contact Angle (water) | **~110°** | degrees |
| Tensile Strength | **~7.1** | MPa |
| Elongation at Break | **~160 %** | % |
| Optical Transparency | **240 – 1100 nm** | nm |
| Dielectric Strength | **~21** | kV/mm |
| Base Resin Viscosity | **~5000** | cP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The most common mixing ratio is **10:1 (base : curing agent) by weight**. Deviating from this ratio significantly alters mechanical properties and is a favorite KTU exam question topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Architecture of PDMS

### 2.1.1 Monomer Synthesis
PDMS is synthesized via hydrolysis of **dichlorodimethylsilane (CH₃)₂SiCl₂**:

$$(\text{CH}_3)_2 \text{SiCl}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_2 \text{Si(OH)}_2 + 2\,\text{HCl}$$

The silanol intermediate undergoes self-condensation:

$$n\,(\text{CH}_3)_2 \text{Si(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{HO}-[\text{Si}(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{-O}]_n\text{-H} + (n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### 2.1.2 Crosslinking (Hydrosilylation Curing)
When base + curing agent are mixed, a **platinum-catalyzed hydrosilylation reaction** forms Si–C covalent crosslinks between vinyl-terminated base chains and hydride (Si–H) groups in the curing agent:

$$\equiv\text{Si-Vinyl} + \text{H-Si}\equiv \xrightarrow{\text{Pt catalyst}} \equiv\text{Si-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-Si}\equiv$$

The **crosslink density** directly governs the elastic modulus — higher crosslink density → stiffer elastomer.

## 2.2 Why PDMS Dominates MEMS? (Operational Step-Logic)

1. **Low Young's Modulus (≈ 1.5 MPa)** → Deformable, ideal for flexible microfluidics and cell-mechanical studies.
2. **Optical Transparency (240–1100 nm)** → Enables in-situ microscopy, fluorescence, and absorbance detection.
3. **Chemical Inertness + Biocompatibility (USP Class VI certified)** → Safe for cell culture, organ-on-chip, and implantable devices.
4. **Gas Permeability** → Allows oxygen diffusion to encapsulated cells, supporting long-term biological assays.
5. **Low Surface Energy (~21 mN/m)** → Enables easy *demolding* (peeling) from masters without adhesion.
6. **Room-Temperature Processable** → Compatible with biological reagents, unlike high-temperature silicon processing.
7. **Replicable & Inexpensive** → A single Si/SU-8 master can generate **hundreds of PDMS replicas** at < ₹200 per chip.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Mandate:** All pipe symbols inside tables have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown parser breakage.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E \approx E_0 \left(\dfrac{\phi}{10}\right)^{0.8}$ | Approx. Young's modulus vs. base:curing-agent ratio $\phi$ (5–30) | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ | Uniaxial linear elastic stress (valid for $\varepsilon \le 10\%$) | Pa |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0} = \dfrac{3\,P\,a^4\,(1-\nu^2)}{16\,E\,t^3}$ | Plate-deflection of PDMS membrane under pressure $P$ (clamped circular plate) | – |
| 4 | $J = -D\,\dfrac{dc}{dx}$ | Fick's law of diffusion through PDMS (gas permeation) | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Young–Dupré equation: relates PDMS surface energy to contact angle $\theta$ | mN/m |
| 6 | $t_{\text{curing}} \approx \dfrac{k}{T - T_{\text{ref}}}$ | Empirical curing time scaling (Arrhenius-type) | hours |
| 7 | $\rho_{\text{cured}} \approx 0.965\,\text{g/cm}^3$ | Density of cured PDMS (10:1) | g/cm³ |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{base}} \approx 5000\,\text{cP}$ | Viscosity of uncured base resin at 25 °C | cP |
| 9 | $n_{\text{PDMS}} \approx 1.41$ | Refractive index (constant across visible range) | – |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{debond}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,E\,t\,W_{ad}}{1-\nu^2}}$ | Critical debond energy for PDMS peeling | J/m² |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of PDMS

- **Microfluidics**: Continuous-flow microreactors, droplet generators, organ-on-chip platforms.
- **Biomedical MEMS**: Neural probes, cell-stretching substrates, artificial vasculature.
- **Optoelectronics**: Flexible optical waveguides, microlens arrays, tunable photonic devices.
- **Energy**: Soft robotic actuators, dielectric elastomer generators.
- **Soft Lithography**: Replica molding (REM), microcontact printing (µCP), capillary micromolding, solvent-assisted micromolding (SAMIM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Context:** Companies such as **Emulate Inc. (USA)** have commercialized "lung-on-chip" and "liver-on-chip" devices made entirely of PDMS + porous membrane, demonstrating industrial maturity of this material.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Young's Modulus vs. Mixing Ratio

The crosslink density of PDMS is directly proportional to the concentration of hydride (Si–H) groups supplied by the curing agent. Empirical curve-fits (e.g., from **Lötters et al., 1997** and **Mata et al., 2005**) show that:

$$E(\phi) = E_0 \left(\frac{\phi}{\phi_0}\right)^{\alpha}$$

where $\phi$ = base:curing agent ratio, $\phi_0 = 10$, $E_0$ = modulus at 10:1 ratio, and $\alpha \approx 0.8$.

### Step-by-Step Evaluation:

**Given:** Reference modulus $E_0 = 1.8\,\text{MPa}$ at $\phi_0 = 10:1$. Find $E$ at $\phi = 5:1$ (stiffer) and $\phi = 20:1$ (softer).

**Step 1 — Substitute the 5:1 case:**

$$E(5) = 1.8 \times \left(\frac{5}{10}\right)^{0.8}$$

$$= 1.8 \times (0.5)^{0.8}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(0.5)^{0.8}$ using natural logarithms:**

$$\ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$

$$0.8 \times \ln(0.5) = -0.5545$$

$$e^{-0.5545} = 0.5743$$

**Step 3 — Final value:**

$$E(5) = 1.8 \times 0.5743 = 1.034\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for 20:1:**

$$E(20) = 1.8 \times \left(\frac{20}{10}\right)^{0.8} = 1.8 \times (2)^{0.8}$$

$$\ln(2) = 0.6931, \quad 0.8 \times 0.6931 = 0.5545$$

$$e^{0.5545} = 1.741$$

$$E(20) = 1.8 \times 1.741 = 3.134\,\text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait — the inverse intuition?**: With **less** curing agent (20:1 = less crosslinker), PDMS is actually **softer**, not stiffer. The empirical law above uses the *inverse* of $\phi$ in most literature. The corrected form is $E \propto 1/\phi^{\beta}$ with $\beta \approx 0.8$. This is a common KTU exam trap — students often mix up the direction of proportionality.

### Corrected Final Form (Mata et al.):

$$E(\phi) = \frac{K}{\phi^{\beta}}, \quad \beta \approx 0.6 - 0.8$$

with $K$ calibrated such that $E(10) \approx 1.8\,\text{MPa}$.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Deflection of a PDMS Membrane (Clamped Circular Plate)

For a circular PDMS diaphragm of radius $a$, thickness $t$, clamped at the edge, loaded by uniform pressure $P$, the central deflection is given by small-deflection plate theory (Timoshenko, 1959):

$$\frac{\Delta h}{h_0} = \frac{3\,P\,a^4\,(1-\nu^2)}{16\,E\,t^3}$$

### Worked Example:

**Given:** $a = 5\,\text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $t = 200\,\mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$, $E = 1.8\,\text{MPa} = 1.8 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$, $\nu = 0.5$, applied gauge pressure $P = 10\,\text{kPa} = 10^4\,\text{Pa}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $(1 - \nu^2)$:**

$$1 - \nu^2 = 1 - (0.5)^2 = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$$

**Step 2 — Compute $a^4$:**

$$a^4 = (5 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 625 \times 10^{-12} = 6.25 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute $t^3$:**

$$t^3 = (2 \times 10^{-4})^3 = 8 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the numerator:**

$$\text{Num} = 3 \times 10^4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.75$$

$$= 3 \times 10^4 \times 4.6875 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$= 1.406 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the denominator:**

$$\text{Den} = 16 \times 1.8 \times 10^6 \times 8 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$= 16 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$= 2.304 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Final ratio:**

$$\frac{\Delta h}{h_0} = \frac{1.406 \times 10^{-5}}{2.304 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0610 \approx 6.1\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 6% strain stays within the linear elastic regime of PDMS (which is valid up to ~10–20% strain). This validates the small-deflection assumption. KTU often asks this as a numerical problem to test *which* regime is valid.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Channel Deformation Under Internal Pressure

For a PDMS microchannel of width $w$, height $h$, and wall thickness $t$, the cross-sectional area change under internal pressure $P$ is approximately:

$$\frac{\Delta A}{A_0} = \frac{P}{E} \left[ \frac{w^2}{t^3} + \frac{h^2}{w^3} \right] \cdot C(\nu)$$

where $C(\nu) \approx 1 - \nu^2$ is the plane-strain correction factor.

### Quick Verification for Soft PDMS:

If $E = 1.5\,\text{MPa}$, $P = 50\,\text{kPa}$, $w = 100\,\mu\text{m}$, $h = 50\,\mu\text{m}$, $t = 1\,\text{mm}$:

$$\frac{P}{E} = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 0.0333$$

$$\frac{w^2}{t^3} = \frac{(10^{-4})^2}{(10^{-3})^3} = 10^{-8} / 10^{-9} = 10$$

$$\frac{h^2}{w^3} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{(10^{-4})^3} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} / 10^{-12} = 2500$$

$$\frac{\Delta A}{A_0} \approx 0.0333 \times (10 + 2500) \times 0.75 \approx 62.6$$

**This result is > 1, meaning the model has broken down** — the small-deformation assumption fails, and large-deflection (geometrically nonlinear) analysis is needed. This is a **KTU-favorite trap question**: when students compute channel expansion, they often forget the small-strain limit and get physically absurd results.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PDMS vs. Other MEMS Materials

| Property | PDMS (10:1) | SU-8 (Photoresist) | Silicon (Si) | Glass (Pyrex) |
|----------|-------------|--------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Young's Modulus | **1.8 MPa** | **2.0 GPa** | **130 GPa** | **63 GPa** |
| Density | 0.965 g/cm³ | 1.20 g/cm³ | 2.33 g/cm³ | 2.23 g/cm³ |
| Biocompatibility | **Excellent** | Good | Limited | Good |
| Optical Transparency | **Yes (240–1100 nm)** | Yes | No (opaque) | Yes |
| Process Temperature | **25–80 °C** | 90–200 °C | >800 °C | >600 °C |
| Cost per Chip | **~₹200** | ~₹400 | ~₹5000 | ~₹2000 |
| Replication | **Easy (peelable)** | Single-use | Single-use | Single-use |
| Gas Permeability | **High** | None | None | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** PDMS is the *only* material in this matrix that is both highly compliant AND processable at room temperature — a unique combination that has no substitute in soft MEMS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PDMS Soft-Lithography Process Flow (Mermaid)

This diagram captures the complete *replica-molding* workflow from master fabrication to final bonded device — a **Board-favorite question**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Silicon Wafer Cleaning] --> B[Step B: SU-8 Spin Coating]
    B --> C[Step C: Soft Bake 65 deg C then 95 deg C]
    C --> D[Step D: UV Photolithography through Mask]
    D --> E[Step E: Post Exposure Bake]
    E --> F[Step F: SU-8 Development in Solvent]
    F --> G[Step G: SU-8 Master with Micro Pattern Hard Baked]
    G --> H[Step H: PDMS Base plus Curing Agent 10 to 1]
    H --> I[Step I: Vacuum Degassing 30 min]
    I --> J[Step J: Pour over SU-8 Master]
    J --> K[Step K: Cure 80 deg C for 90 min]
    K --> L[Step L: Peel off Cured PDMS Replica]
    L --> M[Step M: Punch Inlet and Outlet Holes]
    M --> N[Step N: Plasma Bond to Glass Slide]
    N --> O[Step O: Final PDMS Microfluidic Chip]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a PDMS Microfluidic Device (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reservoir A] --> B[Inlet 1]
    C[Reservoir B] --> D[Inlet 2]
    B --> E[Microchannel Network in PDMS]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Micromixer Zone]
    F --> G[Reaction or Cell Culture Chamber]
    G --> H[Outlet Port]
    H --> I[Waste Collection]
    G --> J[Optical Detection Window Glass Base]
    subgraph material layer
        K[PDMS Top Layer Channels and Chambers]
        L[Glass or PDMS Substrate]
    end
    K --- L
```

## 4.3 PDMS Crosslinking Network (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Base Resin Vinyl Terminated Long Chains] --> C[Platinum Catalyst Activation]
    B[Curing Agent Hydride Crosslinker] --> C
    C --> D[Hydrosilylation Reaction]
    D --> E[Covalent Si-C-Si Crosslinks Formed]
    E --> F[Three Dimensional Silicone Elastomer Network]
    F --> G[Mechanical Properties Modulated by Crosslink Density]
    G --> H[Stiff for 5 to 1 Ratio]
    G --> I[Standard for 10 to 1 Ratio]
    G --> J[Soft for 20 to 1 Ratio]
```

## 4.4 Surface Treatment Block Diagram (Plasma Bonding)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Native PDMS Surface Hydrophobic Si-CH3] --> B[Oxygen Plasma Exposure 30 to 60 sec]
    B --> C[Surface Converted to Silanol Si-OH Hydrophilic]
    C --> D[Immediate Contact with Glass or Another PDMS]
    D --> E[Condensation Reaction Si-OH plus Si-OH]
    E --> F[Irreversible Covalent Si-O-Si Bond Formed]
    F --> G[Strong Leak Tight Sealed Device]
```

No reserved keywords such as `end`, `graph`, or `subgraph` are used as standalone node identifiers.
