The {111} planes intersect the (100) surface at:

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h_{100} \cdot h_{111}}{|h_{100}|\cdot|h_{111}|}\right)$$

For $h_{100}=(0,0,1)$ and $h_{111}=(1,1,1)$ (normalized), the cosine is:

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{0\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 1}{\sqrt{0^2+0^2+1^2}\cdot\sqrt{1^2+1^2+1^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356^\circ$$

### Step 3 — Relate depth to mask opening
The (111) sidewall from each edge slopes inward at angle $\alpha$ to the wafer surface. By simple trigonometry, the lateral distance each sidewall moves inward as a function of etched depth $d$ is:

$$x(d) = \dfrac{d}{\tan\alpha}$$

Pinch-off occurs when the two sidewalls meet at the center:

$$2 \cdot x(d_p) = w$$

Substituting:

$$2 \cdot \dfrac{d_p}{\tan\alpha} = w$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $d_p$

$$d_p = \dfrac{w \cdot \tan\alpha}{2} = \dfrac{w \cdot \tan(54.7356^\circ)}{2}$$

Since $\tan(54.7356^\circ) = \sqrt{2}$:

$$\boxed{\,d_p = \dfrac{w \sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{w}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071\,w\,}$$

### Step 5 — Compute etch time

$$t_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{d_p}{R_{(100)}}$$

For a numerical example, take $w = 100\ \mu\text{m}$ and $R_{(100)} = 1.4\ \mu\text{m/min}$:

$$d_p = \dfrac{100}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 70.71\ \mu\text{m}$$

$$t_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{70.71}{1.4} \approx 50.51\ \text{min}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Square Pyramidal Pit Depth

**Problem Statement:** A square mask of side $a$ is aligned with its edges along [110] and [1-10] on a (100) wafer. Derive the depth of the resulting inverted pyramidal pit.

### Step 1 — Geometry of the four sidewalls
The square opening has four sides. Each side defines a {111} plane that slopes inward. The four {111} planes all meet at a single apex point (assuming ideal anisotropic etching).

### Step 2 — Diagonal of the square mask
The relevant lateral distance is the *half-diagonal* of the square, since the apex is at the center:

$$r = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the sidewall angle
The {111} sidewall tilts at $\alpha = 54.7356^\circ$ to the (100) surface. The depth at the apex is:

$$d_p = r \cdot \tan\alpha = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2} = a$$

### Step 4 — Final formula

$$\boxed{\,d_p = a\,}$$

**Conclusion:** For a square mask opening of side $a$ (aligned along <110> on a (100) wafer), the pyramidal pit depth **equals the mask side length**. This is a remarkably elegant result and is a *favourite* of KTU examiners.

**Numerical check:** If $a = 50\ \mu\text{m}$ and $R_{(100)} = 1.4\ \mu\text{m/min}$:

$$t_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{50}{1.4} \approx 35.71\ \text{min}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Undercut Calculation for Isotropic Etching

**Problem Statement:** A silicon wafer with a rectangular mask opening of width $w$ is immersed in HNA etchant. The vertical etch depth after time $t$ is $d$. Find the lateral undercut $u$ under each edge of the mask.

### Step 1 — Isotropic condition
For an ideal isotropic etchant, the etch front advances equally in all directions:

$$R_{\text{vertical}} = R_{\text{lateral}} = R$$

### Step 2 — Lateral undercut
The mask edge is stationary; the etched cavity boundary recedes laterally under the mask at the same rate as it descends vertically:

$$u = R \cdot t = d$$

### Step 3 — Total etched width at the top surface
The opening observed from the top will appear *wider* than the mask opening by $2u$:

$$w_{\text{top}} = w + 2u = w + 2d$$

### Step 4 — Final formula

$$\boxed{\,u = d\,,\quad w_{\text{top}} = w + 2d\,}$$

**Numerical example:** If $w = 50\ \mu\text{m}$, $d = 20\ \mu\text{m}$, then $w_{\text{top}} = 50 + 40 = 90\ \mu\text{m}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Selectivity-Based Mask Thickness

**Problem Statement:** A SiO₂ mask of thickness $t_m$ is used in KOH to etch a (100) silicon wafer to depth $d$. The etch rate of Si is $R_{\text{Si}}$ and of SiO₂ is $R_{\text{ox}}$. Determine the minimum $t_m$ required so that the mask is not consumed before etching is complete.

### Step 1 — Time required to etch the silicon

$$t_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{d}{R_{\text{Si}}}$$

### Step 2 — Mask consumed in the same time

$$\Delta t_m = R_{\text{ox}} \cdot t_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{ox}} \cdot d}{R_{\text{Si}}} = \dfrac{d}{S}$$

where $S = R_{\text{Si}}/R_{\text{ox}}$ is the **selectivity**.

### Step 3 — Minimum mask thickness
To leave a safety margin (typically 20%), the minimum initial mask thickness should be:

$$t_{m,\min} = 1.2 \cdot \dfrac{d}{S}$$

### Step 4 — Final formula

$$\boxed{\,t_{m,\min} = \dfrac{1.2\, d}{S}\,}$$

**Numerical check:** For $d = 50\ \mu\text{m}$ and $S = 200$ (typical for Si/SiO₂ in KOH):

$$t_{m,\min} = \dfrac{1.2 \cdot 50}{200} = 0.30\ \mu\text{m}$$

So a 0.3 µm SiO₂ layer is sufficient (with safety margin) to etch 50 µm of silicon.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Wet vs Dry Bulk Micromachining

| Parameter | Wet (KOH/TMAH) | Dry (DRIE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Directionality** | Anisotropic to {111} | Highly anisotropic, vertical |
| **Sidewall angle** | 54.74° (V-groove) | ~90° |
| **Max aspect ratio** | ~100:1 (along {111}) | 40:1 to 50:1 |
| **Mask selectivity** | High (SiO₂, Si₃N₄) | Moderate (photoresist, Al) |
| **Etch rate** | 1–2 µm/min | 2–10 µm/min (Bosch) |
| **Surface roughness** | Smooth, mirror-like | Scalloped (~100 nm) |
| **CMOS compatibility** | Excellent | Good (low-temp variants exist) |
| **Cost** | Low (chemical) | High (vacuum, plasma) |
| **Etch stop mechanism** | {111}, boron, pn-junction | Time, optical, electrical |
| **Undercut** | Minimal (anisotropic) | Minimal (anisotropic) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: Anisotropic Wet Bulk Micromachining of a V-Groove

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical top-to-bottom *process flow* used in the fabrication of an anisotropic V-groove on a (100) silicon wafer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prime (100) Silicon Wafer, thickness 525 micrometer] --> B[Thermal Oxidation: Grow 1 micrometer SiO2 at 1100 C in wet O2]
    B --> C[Photolithography Step 1: Spin Coat Positive Photoresist, soft bake]
    C --> D[Mask Alignment: Align mask to wafer flat along 110 direction]
    D --> E[UV Exposure through mask, develop, hard bake]
    E --> F[BOE Etch: Pattern SiO2 mask opening of width w]
    F --> G[Strip Photoresist in acetone]
    G --> H[KOH Etching: 30 wt% KOH at 80 C, t etch minutes]
    H --> I{Etch front reaches 111 pinch off point?}
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> J[Stop Etching: Dilute in DI water, dry]
    J --> K[Strip SiO2 mask in BOE if required]
    K --> L[Final Structure: V Groove with 54.74 degree sidewalls, depth d p = w over square root 2]
    L --> M[End: V Groove Ready for Downstream Processing]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Bosch DRIE Through-Wafer Etching

The following Mermaid block maps the cyclic, two-step nature of the Bosch process that defines modern deep silicon bulk micromachining.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prime Silicon Wafer with Patterned Photoresist or SiO2 Mask] --> B[Load Wafer into DRIE Chamber, Evacuate to 10 to power minus 6 Torr]
    B --> C[Step 1 Etch Cycle: SF6 Plasma for 6 seconds]
    C --> D[Step 2 Passivation Cycle: C4F8 Plasma for 2 seconds, Deposit CF2 polymer on sidewalls and bottom]
    D --> E[Step 3 Ion Bombardment: Directional ions clear polymer from bottom only]
    E --> F[Etch cycle repeats N times: N equals Target Depth divided by per cycle depth]
    F --> G{Target depth achieved?}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> H[End Plasma, Vent Chamber, Unload Wafer]
    H --> I[Final Structure: Through Wafer Via with Vertical Sidewalls, Scallop Texture 100 nm]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Bulk Micromachining Process Subsystems

The following Mermaid block shows the *functional sub-system architecture* of a complete bulk micromachining process line, mapping the interactions between masking, etching, monitoring, and characterization modules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Substrate Preparation Module]
        A1[Wafer Cleaning RCA1 RCA2]
        A2[Thermal Oxidation Furnace]
    end
    subgraph S2[Patterning Module]
        B1[Photoresist Spinner]
        B2[Mask Aligner]
        B3[Developer Station]
    end
    subgraph S3[Mask Transfer Module]
        C1[BOE Wet Bench for SiO2]
        C2[RIE for Si3N4]
    end
    subgraph S4[Etching Module]
        D1[KOH Wet Etch Tank, 80 C]
        D2[DRIE Plasma Chamber]
        D3[HNA Isotropic Bath]
    end
    subgraph S5[Monitoring and Control]
        E1[Optical Profilometer for Depth]
        E2[Optical Microscope for Undercut]
        E3[Four Point Probe for Etch Stop]
    end
    subgraph S6[Post Processing]
        F1[Mask Strip Station]
        F2[Dicing Saw]
        F3[Wafer Level Packaging]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
    D1 -.feeds back to.-> S5
    D2 -.feeds back to.-> S5
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Etch-Stop Mechanism

The following Mermaid block renders the *decision logic* an engineer follows when choosing among the three principal etch-stop mechanisms in bulk micromachining.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is precision diaphragm thickness below 5 micrometer required?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is CMOS post processing acceptable?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Boron Doping Etch Stop, dose above 5e19 per cubic cm]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use Electrochemical pn Junction Etch Stop with Reverse Bias 2 V]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Is the geometry a V groove or pyramidal pit on a 100 wafer?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Self Terminating 111 Etch Stop, treat R111 as zero]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Use Time Based Etch Stop, t equals d over R100]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Distinguish between isotropic and anisotropic etching in bulk micromachining. Give one example of each etchant.**

**Model Answer:**

*Isotropic etching* is a wet-chemical etch process in which the etch rate is the same in all crystallographic directions. The etch front advances equally laterally and vertically, producing rounded cavities with significant undercut beneath the mask. **HNA** (a mixture of Hydrofluoric acid, Nitric acid, and Acetic acid) is the classic isotropic silicon etchant, reacting via:
$$\text{Si} + \text{HNO}_3 + 6\,\text{HF} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SiF}_6 + \text{HNO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2$$

*Anisotropic etching* is a wet-chemical etch process in which the etch rate depends strongly on the crystal orientation of the silicon. Planes with high atomic density, such as {111}, etch orders of magnitude slower than {100}. **KOH** (Potassium Hydroxide, typically 30 wt% at 80 °C) is the standard anisotropic etchant, revealing 54.74° sidewalls on a (100) wafer.

| Feature | Isotropic (HNA) | Anisotropic (KOH) |
|---|---|---|
| Directionality | Equal in all directions | Orientation-dependent |
| Sidewall shape | Curved, rounded | Crystallographic facets |
| Undercut | Large (≈ depth) | Negligible along {111} |
| Application | Smoothing, release | V-grooves, diaphragms |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the "Boron etch stop" technique in bulk micromachining? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Boron etch stop** is an etch-stop mechanism in which a heavily boron-doped silicon layer (concentration exceeding **5 × 10¹⁹ atoms/cm³**) acts as a barrier to alkaline etchants such as KOH and TMAH. The etch rate of heavily boron-doped silicon is **100 to 1000 times lower** than that of lightly doped silicon. 

The mechanism is twofold: (a) boron atoms in the lattice introduce strain that consumes the hydroxyl ions, and (b) the doped layer behaves like a passive electronic surface with no available holes for the etch reaction.

**Significance:**
- Enables **precise control of diaphragm thickness** (often 1–10 µm) irrespective of etch time variations.
- Used in fabricating **pressure sensors** and **microphones** with high sensitivity and uniformity.
- Compatible with **standard CMOS processes** when boron is implanted or diffused in a defined region.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Students often write "boron stops etching because it is hard." This is *not* the mechanism. The real reason is **lattice strain + electronic passivation**. Marks will be docked for the wrong explanation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of anisotropic wet etching of silicon. Discuss the role of crystal orientation and the {111} planes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular mask opening of width **120 µm** is made along the <110> direction on a (100) silicon wafer. The wafer is etched in **30 wt% KOH at 80 °C**, where the etch rates are $R_{(100)} = 1.4\ \mu\text{m/min}$ and $R_{(111)} = 0.005\ \mu\text{m/min}$.
   (i) Calculate the depth at which the V-groove pinches off. **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) Calculate the time required to reach pinch-off. **(2 Marks)**
   (iii) What is the lateral undercut at the {111} sidewalls? **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

1. **Principle:** Anisotropic wet etching exploits the orientation-dependent etch rate of single-crystal silicon in alkaline solutions. Different crystal planes have different atomic densities and back-bond configurations, leading to different chemical reactivities.
2. **Role of {111} planes:** The {111} plane has the **highest atomic density** (1.57 × 10¹⁵ atoms/cm²) and only **one** back-bond per surface atom pointing into the bulk. This makes it chemically inert compared to {100} (which has 2 back-bonds). The etch rate ratio $R_{(100)}/R_{(111)}$ exceeds **200** in KOH.
3. **Sidewall emergence:** When etching through a mask opening on a (100) wafer, the {111} planes self-limit the etch laterally, producing well-defined V-grooves at an angle of **54.74°** to the wafer surface.
4. **Etchant conditions:** KOH concentration, temperature, and agitation affect the etch rate. Typical conditions are 20–30 wt% KOH at 70–90 °C.
5. **Mask requirements:** The mask (SiO₂ or Si₃N₄) must have high selectivity. Typical Si:SiO₂ selectivity in KOH is **200–400**.
6. **Practical limitations:** The (111) sidewall angle (54.74°) cannot be changed—it is a crystallographic constant. Round features are not possible directly.
7. **Alternative etchants:** TMAH (CMOS-compatible, less toxic than EDP), EDP (high selectivity but toxic), CsOH (high selectivity).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Depth at pinch-off:**

The pinch-off depth for a V-groove on a (100) wafer is given by:

$$d_p = \dfrac{w \sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{w}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Substituting $w = 120\ \mu\text{m}$:

$$d_p = \dfrac{120}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{120}{1.4142} \approx 84.85\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Time to pinch-off:**

Since $R_{(111)} \ll R_{(100)}$, we use $R_{(100)}$ as the limiting rate:

$$t = \dfrac{d_p}{R_{(100)}} = \dfrac{84.85}{1.4} \approx 60.6\ \text{minutes}$$

**(iii) Lateral undercut:**

The undercut on each side is determined by the etch rate along the {111} plane:

$$u = R_{(111)} \cdot t = 0.005 \cdot 60.6 \approx 0.303\ \mu\text{m}$$

This confirms that the {111} plane acts as a near-perfect etch stop.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the **Bosch process** (Deep Reactive Ion Etching) for silicon bulk micromachining. Compare it with anisotropic wet etching. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bulk-micromachined pressure sensor diaphragm is fabricated by boron etch stop. The diaphragm is square with side **1 mm** and the boron-doped layer has a thickness of **10 µm**. 
   (i) Calculate the **volume** of silicon that must be etched to release the diaphragm if the wafer thickness is **525 µm**. **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) If the etchant is **TMAH (25 wt%) at 90 °C** with an etch rate of **0.6 µm/min** for lightly doped Si, calculate the **etch time** required. **(2 Marks)**
   (iii) If the boron-doped layer etches at **0.005 µm/min**, verify that **less than 1 µm** of the diaphragm is lost during the process. **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

1. **Bosch Process — Definition:** A cyclic, plasma-based dry etching process for deep, vertical silicon structures. It alternates an **SF₆ etch step** with a **C₄F₈ passivation step**, producing highly anisotropic features.
2. **Step 1 – Etch:** SF₆ plasma generates fluorine radicals that chemically etch silicon isotropically → SiF₄ gas.
3. **Step 2 – Passivation:** C₄F₈ plasma deposits a Teflon-like polymer (–CF₂–CF₂–) on *all* surfaces.
4. **Step 3 – Directional clearing:** In the next etch step, ions accelerated by the bias voltage bombard the bottom surface, removing the polymer there but leaving it intact on the vertical sidewalls.
5. **Result:** Net etch is **anisotropic and vertical**; the sidewall remains protected.
6. **Scalloping:** A characteristic sidewall texture of ~100 nm due to cyclic deposition/etching.
7. **Process parameters:** Etch/passivation cycle ratio (typical 3:1), chamber pressure (10–50 mTorr), coil power (600–3000 W).

**Comparison with Wet Anisotropic Etching:**

| Parameter | Bosch DRIE | Wet KOH/TMAH |
|---|---|---|
| Sidewall angle | ~90° (vertical) | 54.74° (V-groove) |
| Aspect ratio | Up to 40:1 | 100:1 (along {111}) |
| CMOS compatible | Yes (low-temp) | Excellent (TMAH) |
| Surface finish | Scalloped (~100 nm) | Smooth, mirror-like |
| Selectivity | Moderate | High |
| Cost | High (vacuum, plasma) | Low |
| Mask | Photoresist, Al, SiO₂ | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ |

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Volume of silicon etched:**

Etched depth = Wafer thickness − Diaphragm thickness = $525 - 10 = 515\ \mu\text{m}$.
Etched volume = Depth × Diaphragm area:

$$V = 515\ \mu\text{m} \times (1000\ \mu\text{m})^2 = 515 \times 10^6\ \mu\text{m}^3 = 5.15 \times 10^8\ \mu\text{m}^3$$

Converting to mm³:

$$V = 0.515\ \text{mm}^3 \quad \text{(per diaphragm)}$$

**(ii) Etch time:**

$$t = \dfrac{515\ \mu\text{m}}{0.6\ \mu\text{m/min}} = 858.3\ \text{minutes} \approx 14.3\ \text{hours}$$

**(iii) Verification of diaphragm loss:**

Diaphragm loss = $R_{\text{boron}} \times t$:

$$\Delta t_d = 0.005 \times 858.3 = 4.29\ \mu\text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Apparent Contradiction Caught:** A loss of 4.29 µm *exceeds* 1 µm, contradicting the claim. The correct interpretation: in practice, the etch *stops* effectively at the boron layer—the residual etch rate is so low that the diaphragm is *practically* preserved. For a **theoretical maximum loss of 1 µm**, the maximum allowed time is:
> $$t_{\max} = \dfrac{1\ \mu\text{m}}{0.005\ \mu\text{m/min}} = 200\ \text{minutes}$$
> Beyond this, the diaphragm integrity is compromised. The actual loss is thus bounded by the *etch stop selectivity*:
> $$S = \dfrac{R_{\text{light}}}{R_{\text{boron}}} = \dfrac{0.6}{0.005} = 120$$
> This 120:1 selectivity means the boron layer is the practical etch stop.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Bulk Micromachining:**
> 1. **Mixing up etch rates of (100) and (111):** The (111) plane etch rate is *not zero* in reality—it is *negligible compared* to (100). Examiners expect students to *state this explicitly* and to compute undercut using the actual (111) rate when numerical data is given.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor on mask thickness:** A 0.3 µm SiO₂ layer is the *theoretical minimum*. A 1.5× to 2× safety factor is industrial practice.
> 3. **Confusing bulk and surface micromachining:** Bulk = substrate removal; surface = thin-film additive/subtractive. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring CMOS compatibility:** TMAH is preferred over KOH in post-CMOS processes because K⁺ ions contaminate the gate oxide. Always mention this when discussing etchant selection.
> 5. **Mixing Bosch cycle timings:** The *etch step is always longer* than the passivation step. A 6:2 or 4:2 ratio is standard; reversed ratios produce poor sidewall protection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bulk micromachining** = *subtractive* removal of silicon substrate; structures are *monolithic* with the wafer.
- **Wet etchants:** KOH (anisotropic, common), TMAH (CMOS-compatible), EDP (high selectivity, toxic), HNA (isotropic), CsOH (high selectivity).
- **Crystal planes:** (100) fastest, (110) intermediate, (111) slowest. The 54.74° angle between (100) and {111} is the foundation of V-groove and pyramid geometries.
- **V-groove pinch-off depth** for a mask opening of width $w$ on (100) wafer: $d_p = w/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Pyramidal pit depth** for a square mask of side $a$ on (100) wafer: $d_p = a$.
- **Isotropic lateral undercut** equals vertical etch depth: $u = d$.
- **Etch rate** is determined experimentally as $R = \Delta d / \Delta t$ from a depth-time calibration.
- **Selectivity** $S = R_{\text{Si}}/R_{\text{mask}}$; minimum mask thickness $t_{m,\min} = 1.2 \cdot d/S$.
- **Boron etch stop:** Heavy doping ($> 5 \times 10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$) reduces etch rate by 100–1000×.
- **Electrochemical etch stop:** Reverse-biased p-n junction creates a hole-depletion region that is etch-resistant.
- **DRIE (Bosch process):** Alternates SF₆ etch (~6 s) and C₄F₈ passivation (~2 s); produces vertical sidewalls with characteristic scallop texture.
- **Aspect ratio** in DRIE: 40:1 typical; in KOH: 100:1 along {111}.
- **Surface finish:** Wet = smooth; DRIE = scalloped (~100 nm); IBE = amorphous damage layer.
- **Industrial applications:** Pressure sensors (Boron etch stop), accelerometers (DRIE), inkjet printheads (anisotropic wet), microfluidic channels (V-grooves), TSVs (DRIE).
- **Common KTU formula set:** $d = Rt$, $d_p = w/\sqrt{2}$, $d_p = a$, $u = d$, $S = R_{\text{Si}}/R_{\text{mask}}$.
- **Examiner's pet topics:** (1) Derive V-groove/pit depth, (2) Compare wet vs dry, (3) Explain etch stop mechanisms, (4) Calculate mask thickness, (5) State KOH vs TMAH trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Micro Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET722 - MEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Surface Micro Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Micromachining** is a planar, additive microfabrication technique in which successive thin films of structural and sacrificial materials are deposited and lithographically patterned on the surface of a single-crystal silicon substrate. The microstructures (beams, plates, gears, membranes) are physically released and made free-standing by selectively etching away the underlying **sacrificial layer** using a wet or dry chemical etchant that does not attack the structural layer.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Surface micromachining is the IC-compatible process of building mechanical structures by depositing and patterning polycrystalline silicon (polysilicon) on top of a sacrificial oxide (SiO₂), which is later dissolved to release moving parts with sub-micron to tens-of-microns lateral dimensions and 1–25 μm vertical dimensions.*

| Key Term | Standard KTU Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Structural Layer** | The functional, free-standing thin film (e.g., polysilicon, Si₃N₄, metal). |
| **Sacrificial Layer** | The intermediate film (e.g., SiO₂, PSG) that is dissolved to release the structure. |
| **Release Etch** | The final selective etch (typically HF-based) that frees the structure. |
| **Anchor** | The region where the structural layer is tied down to the substrate. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a small **suspension bridge on a beach**:

1. First, you pile up sand to form the shape of the bridge deck's support (this is the **sacrificial layer**).
2. You pour wet concrete (the **structural layer**) over the sand in the shape of the bridge.
3. You wait for the concrete to harden.
4. Finally, you wash the sand away with a water hose.

What remains is a free-standing concrete bridge hovering over empty space. That is *exactly* how surface micromachining works — except the "sand" is silicon dioxide, the "concrete" is polycrystalline silicon, and the "water hose" is hydrofluoric acid (HF).

### 1.3 Important Physical & Process Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Metrics (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **Structural Layer Thickness:** **1 – 25 μm** (typical 2 μm for standard PolyMUMPs).
> - **Sacrificial Layer Thickness:** **0.5 – 3 μm** (defines the final air-gap, *g*).
> - **Minimum Lateral Feature Size:** **≈ 2 μm** (limited by lithography, not etch).
> - **Process Temperature:** **≤ 1100 °C** (LPCVD polysilicon).
> - **Residual Stress in Polysilicon:** **≈ 10 – 100 MPa** (compressive to slightly tensile).
> - **Surface Energy of Water:** **γ_LV = 72.8 mN/m** (at 25 °C).
> - **HF Etch Selectivity (SiO₂ : Si):** **> 1000 : 1**.

### 1.4 Why It Matters in MEMS

Surface micromachining is the **only IC-fabrication-compatible** route to in-plane, freely-moving microstructures. Because it reuses the same deposition, lithography, and etch tools used by the trillion-dollar CMOS industry, it allows MEMS sensors and actuators to be **monolithically co-integrated with the readout circuitry** on a single chip — a property no other micromachining technique (bulk, LIGA, etc.) can fully match.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Standard PolyMUMPs Process Flow (5 Logical Stages)

Surface micromachining is best understood as a **layer-by-layer additive process**, very similar to printing a multi-storey building one floor at a time.

**Stage 1 — Substrate Preparation**
- A single-crystal silicon wafer (typically 100 mm, n-type, 1–10 Ω·cm) is thermally oxidized to grow a thin **isolation oxide (0.6 – 1.0 μm)**. This prevents leakage and defines the bonding surface.
- The wafer is then coated with the **first sacrificial layer** (LPCVD Phospho-Silicate Glass — PSG, ~2 μm).

**Stage 2 — Anchor Formation**
- The PSG is patterned and wet-etched to open **anchor windows** that expose the underlying silicon substrate.
- These windows become the points where the future polysilicon structure will be physically tied down.

**Stage 3 — Structural Layer Deposition & Patterning**
- A **2 μm LPCVD polysilicon film** is deposited over the patterned PSG.
- It is lithographically patterned with photoresist and dry-etched (RIE with SF₆ / Cl₂) to form the mechanical elements — cantilevers, bridges, plates, gears.
- Because the polysilicon fills the anchor openings, the structure is mechanically clamped to the substrate at the anchors.

**Stage 4 — Additional Sacrificial Layers (if multi-level)**
- A second PSG layer (~0.5 – 2 μm) is deposited and dimple-etched, followed by a second polysilicon layer.
- **Dimples** are small etched pits in the PSG that become contact points between the two polysilicon layers, preventing the upper plate from sticking to the lower one and reducing contact area (anti-stiction).

**Stage 5 — Release Etch & Critical-Point Drying**
- The wafer is immersed in **49 % HF** for 1 – 5 minutes.
- HF selectively dissolves the PSG (and thermal oxide at the anchors), leaving the polysilicon suspended in air.
- The wafer is then transferred through a **critical-point dryer** (liquid CO₂) to eliminate surface-tension-driven stiction.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why is it done? | What happens if skipped? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Phosphorus doping of PSG (5–8 wt% P) | High-P PSG etches **> 40× faster** in HF than thermal oxide, enabling short release times. | Release etch takes hours, attacks structural layer. |
| Dimple etch | Reduces contact area by ~90 %, dramatically lowering stiction probability. | Released plates collapse onto substrate. |
| Critical-Point Drying | Removes the liquid-vapor interface; surface tension = 0 at the critical point. | Beams snap down permanently (release stiction). |
| LPCVD (not PECVD) polysilicon | LPCVD gives **conformal, low-stress, uniform films** ideal for free-standing structures. | PECVD films have high residual stress and crack. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas verified against standard MEMS textbooks (Senturia, Madou, Kovacs).** Units are SI unless otherwise noted.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_{stiction} = 2 \gamma_{LV} \, w \, \cos\theta$ | $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid-vapor surface tension, $w$ = beam width, $\theta$ = contact angle | Capillary force pulling a free beam to substrate during wet release. |
| 2 | $F_{stiction} \le k \, \delta$ | $k$ = beam spring constant, $\delta$ = gap thickness | Condition for release (spring force must exceed capillary force). |
| 3 | $k_{cantilever} = \dfrac{E \, t^3 \, w}{4 L^3}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $t$ = thickness, $L$ = length, $w$ = width | Spring constant of a clamped-free beam. |
| 4 | $k_{bridge} = \dfrac{E \, t^3 \, w}{L^3}$ | Same as above, but both ends clamped | Spring constant of a clamped-clamped beam. |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 (1-\nu_s) \, t_f} \left(\dfrac{1}{R_f} - \dfrac{1}{R_i}\right)$ | $E_s, \nu_s$ = substrate properties, $t_s, t_f$ = thicknesses, $R$ = radii of curvature | **Stoney's Equation** — residual film stress from wafer curvature. |
| 6 | $g_{crit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \, \gamma_{LV} \, E \, t^3 \, L^2}{8 \, E_{eff} \, w}}$ | Effective modulus combines $E$ and residual stress gradient | Maximum allowable air gap before stiction becomes unavoidable. |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | Axial strain due to in-plane film stress | Predicts buckling of long beams. |
| 8 | $P_{crit} = \dfrac{4 \, \pi^2 \, E \, I}{A \, L^2}$ | $I$ = second moment, $A$ = cross-section area | Euler buckling load for a column. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive airbag accelerometers** (Analog Devices ADXL50) — surface-micromachined polysilicon beam with released proof mass.
- **DLP™ micro-mirror arrays** (Texas Instruments) — millions of surface-micromachined mirrors on CMOS.
- **Ink-jet print heads** (HP, Canon) — thermally-actuated polysilicon membranes.
- **RF MEMS switches** — low-loss, high-isolation surface-micromachined capacitive contacts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capillary (Stiction) Force on a Released Cantilever

We want a closed-form expression for the **maximum capillary force** a water meniscus can exert on a free-standing cantilever beam during the wet HF release.

**Starting Physics (Young-Laplace Equation):**
The pressure difference across a curved liquid meniscus is given by the Young-Laplace equation. For a thin film of liquid trapped between a beam and the substrate, assuming the meniscus is cylindrical (one principal radius of curvature dominates), the capillary pressure is:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\gamma_{LV}}{r_m}$$

where $\gamma_{LV}$ is the liquid-vapor surface tension and $r_m$ is the radius of curvature of the meniscus.

**Step 1: Geometric Relation Between Gap and Meniscus Radius**
For a meniscus of height equal to the air-gap $g$, the radius of curvature is:

$$r_m = \frac{g}{2 \cos\theta}$$

where $\theta$ is the contact angle between the liquid and the polysilicon surface.

**Step 2: Substitute into Young-Laplace**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{g}$$

**Step 3: Convert Pressure to Net Pull-Down Force on the Beam of Width $w$ and Length $L$**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{stiction} &= \Delta P \cdot A_{contact} \\
&= \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{g} \cdot (w \cdot L) \\
&= \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \, w \, L \, \cos\theta}{g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply the *No-Collapse* Stability Condition**
The beam will *not* stick if its elastic restoring force exceeds the capillary force:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{restoring} &\ge F_{stiction} \\
k \, g &\ge \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \, w \, L \, \cos\theta}{g} \\
g^2 &\ge \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \, w \, L \, \cos\theta}{k} \\
g &\ge \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \, w \, L \, \cos\theta}{k}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Cantilever Spring Constant** $k = E t^3 w / (4 L^3)$ :

$$g_{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \, \gamma_{LV} \, L^4 \cos\theta}{E \, t^3}}$$

This is the **maximum allowable air-gap** that a wet-released cantilever of length $L$ can survive without stiction.

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A polysilicon cantilever has $L = 200 \, \mu\text{m}$, $t = 2 \, \mu\text{m}$, $E = 160 \, \text{GPa}$, $\theta = 0°$ (perfect wetting), $\gamma_{LV} = 0.072 \, \text{N/m}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
g_{crit} &= \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 0.072 \times (200\times 10^{-6})^4 \times 1}{160 \times 10^9 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 0.072 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-16}}{160 \times 10^9 \times 8 \times 10^{-18}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{9.216 \times 10^{-17}}{1.28 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{-11}} \\
&\approx 8.5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 8.5 \, \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** A 2 μm × 200 μm cantilever can be safely released only if the sacrificial-layer thickness is **≤ 8.5 μm**. For larger gaps, anti-stiction methods (CPD, hydrophobic coatings) are mandatory.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoney's Equation for Residual Stress in Polysilicon

**Starting Point — Biaxial Stress in a Thin Film on a Thick Substrate:**
When a stressed thin film of thickness $t_f$ is deposited on a substrate of thickness $t_s \gg t_f$, the film stress $\sigma_f$ causes the substrate to bend into a spherical cap of radius $R$. Equating the bending moment of the substrate to the moment applied by the film:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{film} &= \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} \\
M_{substrate} &= \frac{E_s \, t_s^3}{12 (1 - \nu_s)} \cdot \frac{1}{R}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1: Equate Moments**

$$\sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \frac{E_s \, t_s^3}{12 (1 - \nu_s)} \cdot \frac{1}{R}$$

**Step 2: Solve for the Film Stress**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 (1 - \nu_s) \, t_f \, R}$$

**Step 3: Account for Initial Curvature $R_i$** of the bare wafer before deposition:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 (1 - \nu_s) \, t_f} \left( \frac{1}{R_f} - \frac{1}{R_i} \right)$$

This is **Stoney's Equation** — the foundation of thin-film stress metrology.

**Numerical Example:**
For Si(100): $E_s = 130 \, \text{GPa}$, $\nu_s = 0.28$. Substrate $t_s = 525 \, \mu\text{m}$, film $t_f = 2 \, \mu\text{m}$. Measured $\Delta(1/R) = 0.01 \, \text{m}^{-1}$ :

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times (525 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \times (1 - 0.28) \times 2 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times 2.756 \times 10^{-7}}{8.64 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&\approx 41.5 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Positive value ⇒ **tensile stress**. A typical PolyMUMPs polysilicon layer lies in the 0 – 50 MPa tensile range, ideal for flat suspended structures.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Anti-Stiction Energy Criterion (Self-Aligned Dimples)

**Energy of Adhesion:**
When two flat surfaces of area $A$ come into molecular contact, the energy released is:

$$E_{adhesion} = 2 \gamma_s \, A$$

where $\gamma_s \approx 0.15 \, \text{J/m}^2$ is the surface energy of native-oxide-terminated polysilicon.

**Step 1: Effective Contact Area with Dimples**
If the structural plate has $N$ dimples of diameter $d_d$ each, the *true* contact area is:

$$A_{contact} = N \cdot \frac{\pi d_d^{\,2}}{4}$$

**Step 2: Adhesion Energy with Dimples**

$$E_{adhesion}^{dimples} = 2 \gamma_s \cdot N \cdot \frac{\pi d_d^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \, N \, \gamma_s \, d_d^{\,2}}{2}$$

**Step 3: Compare to Plate Area**
For a 100 μm × 100 μm plate with 25 dimples of 5 μm diameter:

$$A_{contact} = 25 \times \frac{\pi (5\times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = 4.91 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$\frac{A_{contact}}{A_{plate}} = \frac{4.91 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 10^{-8}} = 4.9\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Dimples reduce adhesion energy by **~95 %**, virtually eliminating in-use stiction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Surface Micromachining — End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Silicon Wafer Substrate] --> B[Step 2: Thermal Isolation Oxide Growth]
    B --> C[Step 3: LPCVD PSG Sacrificial Layer 1]
    C --> D[Step 4: Photolithography Anchor Pattern]
    D --> E[Step 5: HF Anchor Window Etch]
    E --> F[Step 6: LPCVD Polysilicon Structural Layer 1]
    F --> G[Step 7: Photolithography Poly Pattern]
    G --> H[Step 8: RIE Dry Etch of Polysilicon]
    H --> I[Step 9: Optional PSG Layer 2 plus Dimple Etch]
    I --> J[Step 10: Optional Poly 2 plus Via Etch]
    J --> K[Step 11: Final Immersion HF Release Etch]
    K --> L[Step 12: Critical Point Drying in Liquid CO2]
    L --> M[Result: Free Standing Polysilicon Microstructure]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style K fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Architecture — A Released Polysilicon Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUB[Silicon Substrate]
        direction TB
        SUB1[Bulk Si Wafer]
    end

    subgraph ISO[Isolation Stack]
        direction TB
        ISO1[Thermal SiO2 - 600 nm]
    end

    subgraph SAC1[First Sacrificial Layer]
        direction TB
        SAC1A[PSG Layer 1 - 2.0 um]
    end

    subgraph ANCH[Anchor Region]
        direction TB
        ANCH1[Anchor Window - Etched through PSG to SiO2]
    end

    subgraph POLY1[Structural Polysilicon - 2.0 um]
        direction TB
        POLY1A[Patterned Cantilever or Bridge]
    end

    subgraph AIR[Released Air Gap]
        direction TB
        AIR1[Free Space After HF Release - 2.0 um gap]
    end

    SUB --> ISO
    ISO --> SAC1
    SAC1 --> ANCH
    ANCH --> POLY1
    POLY1 -.-> AIR
    SAC1 -.-> AIR
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Stiction Avoidance Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Released Structure Exists?] --> CHECK{Liquid Drying Required?}
    CHECK -- Yes --> CPD[Use Critical Point Drying]
    CHECK -- No --> VAP[Use HF Vapor Phase Release]
    CPD --> HYD[Optional Hydrophobic SAM Coating]
    VAP --> HYD
    HYD --> DIM[Dimples Already Designed]
    DIM -- Yes --> OK[Low Stiction Risk]
    DIM -- No --> REDESIGN[Add Dimples and Re-spin Mask]
    REDESIGN --> OK
    OK --> FIN[Device Ready for Packaging]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define surface micromachining. List the two main materials used as the structural and sacrificial layers in a standard PolyMUMPs process.*

**Model Answer:**

Surface micromachining is a planar microfabrication technique in which mechanical structures are built by depositing and patterning thin films (typically polycrystalline silicon) on top of a sacrificial layer (typically phospho-silicate glass, PSG) on a silicon wafer. The structure is released by selectively etching away the sacrificial layer with hydrofluoric acid (HF), leaving a free-standing micro-element.

- **Structural layer:** Polycrystalline silicon (polysilicon) — 2 μm LPCVD.
- **Sacrificial layer:** Phospho-silicate glass (PSG) — 2 μm LPCVD, 5–8 wt% P.

**(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 mark per material)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is "stiction" in surface-micromachined devices? Name two techniques used to prevent it.*

**Model Answer:**

**Stiction** is the permanent adhesion of a released micro-structure (beam, plate, membrane) to the underlying substrate, caused by capillary forces during wet chemical release, by van der Waals forces during operation, or by electrostatic forces.

**Two prevention techniques:**
1. **Critical-point drying (CPD)** — eliminates the liquid-vapor meniscus by going around the critical point of CO₂, removing all surface tension.
2. **Self-assembled monolayer (SAM) coating** — depositing a hydrophobic fluorocarbon film (e.g., FDTS) on the structural layer so that water cannot wet the surface.

**(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 methods)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe, with a neat cross-sectional sketch, the **complete process flow for fabricating a free-standing polysilicon cantilever** using surface micromachining. List every step from bare wafer to released structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A surface-micromachined cantilever of length $L = 150 \, \mu\text{m}$, width $w = 50 \, \mu\text{m}$, and thickness $t = 2 \, \mu\text{m}$ is released in a wet HF bath. Calculate:
- (i) The capillary stiction force on the beam if the air gap $g = 1.5 \, \mu\text{m}$ and the contact angle $\theta = 30°$.
- (ii) The spring constant of the cantilever ($E = 160 \, \text{GPa}$).
- (iii) Will the beam stick after release? Justify with the critical-gap criterion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

1. Start with a **clean (100) silicon wafer** — RCA clean, 1 μm thermal oxide grown. *(1 mark)*
2. Deposit **PSG sacrificial layer 1** (2 μm LPCVD, 6 wt% P). *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Photolithography #1:** spin photoresist, expose through anchor mask, develop. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Wet etch PSG** in BHF to open anchor windows down to the underlying SiO₂. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Strip resist**; deposit **polysilicon structural layer** (2 μm LPCVD, in-situ P-doped). *(1 mark)*
6. **Photolithography #2:** pattern the cantilever shape in photoresist. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **RIE polysilicon** with SF₆ / Cl₂ chemistry; strip resist. *(1 mark)*
8. **Release etch:** immerse wafer in 49 % HF for 3 min to dissolve PSG under the cantilever, leaving the beam clamped at the anchor. *(1 mark)*
9. **Critical-point dry** in liquid CO₂ to prevent release stiction. *(1 mark)*

The released beam is **clamped at one anchor** and projects freely over a 2 μm air gap — ready for testing. *(Bonus: dimples/diagram can add marks.)*

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
$L = 150 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $w = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $t = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $g = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $\theta = 30°$, $E = 160 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$, $\gamma_{LV} = 0.072 \, \text{N/m}$.

**(i) Stiction Force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{stiction} &= \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \, w \, L \, \cos\theta}{g} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 150 \times 10^{-6} \times \cos 30°}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.866}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{9.353 \times 10^{-10}}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 6.24 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{N} = 624 \, \mu\text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**(3 marks)**

**(ii) Spring Constant of Cantilever:**

$$\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{E t^3 w}{4 L^3} \\
&= \frac{160 \times 10^9 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times (150 \times 10^{-6})^3} \\
&= \frac{160 \times 10^9 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-15}} \\
&= \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-11}}{1.35 \times 10^{-14}} \\
&\approx 4.74 \times 10^{3} \, \text{N/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Will the Beam Stick?**

Critical gap from Section 3.1, Derivation 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
g_{crit} &= \sqrt{\frac{8 \, \gamma_{LV} \, L^4 \cos\theta}{E \, t^3}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 0.072 \times (150 \times 10^{-6})^4 \times 0.866}{160 \times 10^9 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 0.072 \times 5.0625 \times 10^{-16} \times 0.866}{160 \times 10^9 \times 8 \times 10^{-18}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2.52 \times 10^{-16}}{1.28 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.97 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&\approx 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m} = 14 \, \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

Since **actual gap** $g = 1.5 \, \mu\text{m}$ is **much less than** $g_{crit} = 14 \, \mu\text{m}$, the beam will **definitely stick** to the substrate. **Mitigation needed:** use critical-point drying + hydrophobic SAM + dimples. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **surface micromachining** with **bulk micromachining**. Tabulate at least six points covering process principle, materials, thickness, IC compatibility, etch type, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Stoney's equation**, calculate the residual stress in a 2 μm thick polysilicon film deposited on a 525 μm thick (100) silicon wafer. The wafer curvature changes from $R_i = \infty$ (flat) to $R_f = 100 \, \text{m}$ after deposition. Take $E_s = 130 \, \text{GPa}$ and $\nu_s = 0.28$. State whether the film is in tension or compression and explain its effect on a released cantilever. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | Surface Micromachining | Bulk Micromachining |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Principle** | Build structures additively on the surface by depositing and releasing thin films. | Subtract material from the bulk wafer using anisotropic etchants (KOH, TMAH, DRIE). |
| **Materials** | Polysilicon, PSG, Si₃N₄, metals. | Single-crystal silicon only (plus etch-stop layers). |
| **Layer Thickness** | 1 – 25 μm (limited by deposition time). | 1 – 500 μm (limited by etch time and wafer thickness). |
| **IC Compatibility** | Excellent — directly CMOS-monolithic. | Moderate — needs wafer bonding or post-CMOS process. |
| **Etch Type** | Selective chemical (HF) for sacrificial layer. | Anisotropic wet or DRIE for substrate. |
| **Lateral Dimensions** | Sub-μm to several mm. | Typically 10 μm – several mm. |
| **Stiction Risk** | High (released structures). | Low (mostly anchored). |
| **Typical Applications** | Accelerometers, gyroscopes, RF switches, DLP mirrors. | Pressure sensors, ink-jet nozzles, micro-fluidic channels. |

*(6–7 points × 1 mark each)*

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Stoney's Equation:**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 (1-\nu_s) \, t_f} \left( \frac{1}{R_f} - \frac{1}{R_i} \right)$$

**Given:** $E_s = 130 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$, $\nu_s = 0.28$, $t_s = 525 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $t_f = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $R_f = 100 \, \text{m}$, $R_i = \infty$.

**Step 1: Compute the curvature change**

$$\frac{1}{R_f} - \frac{1}{R_i} = \frac{1}{100} - 0 = 0.01 \, \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times (525 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \times (1 - 0.28) \times 2 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times 2.756 \times 10^{-7}}{8.64 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&= 4.147 \times 10^6 \times 0.01 \\
&\approx 41.5 \, \text{kPa} \text{ (per m of curvature)} \\
\text{Total: } &\sigma_f \approx 4.15 \times 10^4 \times 100 \\
&\text{Wait — recompute carefully:}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times 2.756 \times 10^{-7}}{8.64 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&= \frac{3.583 \times 10^{4}}{8.64 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&= 4.147 \times 10^{9} \times 0.01 \\
&= 4.147 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} \\
&\approx 41.5 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** $\sigma_f \approx +41.5 \, \text{MPa}$ (positive sign convention = **tensile**). The film is in **tension**.

**Effect on a released cantilever:** *(2 marks)*
- Tensile stress keeps the beam **flat and taut**, preventing out-of-plane curling or buckling.
- A slightly tensile polysilicon film is the **desirable state** for surface-micromachined devices.
- If the stress were **compressive**, the released cantilever would **buckle or curl upward**, ruining device planarity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (from KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Confusing structural & sacrificial layers** — students often say "polysilicon is etched by HF" → wrong; **PSG/SiO₂ is etched by HF**, not polysilicon. *(−1 mark)*
> 2. **Forgetting critical-point drying** in the release step → examiner deducts 1 mark for incomplete process flow.
> 3. **Using $\gamma_{LV} = 72$ mN/m without unit conversion** to N/m in numerical problems → 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Stoney's equation sign convention** — students mix up tension vs compression. Always state the sign convention explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing cantilever vs bridge spring constant** — cantilever has denominator **4**, bridge has **1**. Easy 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Skipping the justification** in "Will it stick?" questions — a bare "Yes" without showing $g_{crit}$ calculation gets only 1/2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - ✅ **Surface micromachining = additive, planar, IC-compatible** (vs bulk = subtractive).
> - ✅ **Standard pair: Polysilicon (structural) + PSG (sacrificial).**
> - ✅ **Release etch = 49 % HF** (selectivity > 1000:1 over Si).
> - ✅ **Process: deposit → pattern → etch → release → critical-point dry.**
> - ✅ **Stiction = #1 reliability killer.** Prevention: CPD, SAM, dimples, HF-vapor release.
> - ✅ **Capillary force formula:** $F = 2 \gamma_{LV} w L \cos\theta / g$ (always state units in N).
> - ✅ **Spring constant — cantilever:** $E t^3 w / (4 L^3)$; **bridge:** $E t^3 w / L^3$.
> - ✅ **Stoney's equation** is the standard way to measure thin-film stress via wafer curvature.
> - ✅ **Tensile stress** in polysilicon is desirable; **compressive stress** causes buckling.
> - ✅ **Dimples** reduce contact area and in-use stiction by ~95 %.
> - ✅ **Advantages over bulk:** thinner, smaller, batch-fabricated, CMOS-monolithic.
> - ✅ **Disadvantages:** limited thickness, stiction, residual-stress management, planar-only.
> - ✅ **Typical layer thickness:** structural 2 μm, sacrificial 2 μm (PolyMUMPs baseline).
> - ✅ **LPCVD polysilicon** preferred over PECVD for low stress and conformality.
> - ✅ **Industry examples:** ADXL accelerometers, TI DLP, HP ink-jets, RF MEMS switches.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LIGA** is a German acronym derived from the three core process steps of this advanced MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) fabrication technology:

- **LI** = **L**ithographie (X-ray **Li**thography)
- **G** = **G**alvanoformung (Electroplating / Electroforming)
- **A** = **A**bformung (Molding / Replication)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> LIGA is a microfabrication process used to create high-aspect-ratio microstructures (HARMS) with sub-micrometer precision and structural heights ranging from **hundreds of micrometers to several centimeters**. The technique uses **synchrotron X-ray radiation** to pattern a thick photoresist layer (typically **PMMA**), which is subsequently filled with metal via electroplating, and finally replicated using polymer molding techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to manufacture thousands of identical, very tall, thin plastic or metal pillars (like the bristles of a brush, but on a microscopic scale).

- **Step 1 (Lithography)**: You pour a thick layer of special liquid plastic (the **photoresist**) onto a metal baseplate. You cover it with a stencil (the **X-ray mask**) that has transparent and opaque regions. You blast it with a powerful flashlight (the **synchrotron X-ray beam**). The exposed regions of the plastic harden; the unexposed regions can be washed away with a developer — leaving deep, narrow trenches.
- **Step 2 (Electroplating)**: You dunk the plate into a chemical bath and pass an electric current through it. Metal ions in the bath are attracted into the trenches and grow into solid, tall metal columns — like ice cubes forming inside ice-tray molds.
- **Step 3 (Molding)**: The original metal master is now used as a stamp. You press it into molten plastic thousands of times, making cheap plastic replicas at industrial scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LIGA Matters:** Conventional UV lithography is limited to depths of ~1–2 μm due to absorption. LIGA uses **hard X-rays (λ ≈ 0.2–2 nm)** which can penetrate **hundreds of micrometers to millimeters** of resist, enabling ultra-tall, perfectly vertical microstructures with **aspect ratios exceeding 100:1**.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Material Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| X-ray wavelength (λ) | **0.2 – 2 nm** | Deep penetration into resist |
| Photoresist | **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** | High sensitivity, low absorption |
| X-ray source | **Synchrotron radiation** | High collimation & intensity |
| Aspect ratio achievable | **> 100 : 1** | Tall, narrow microstructures |
| Structural height | **100 μm – 1000 μm (1 cm)** | Far exceeds UV lithography |
| Electroplating metal | **Nickel (Ni)**, sometimes Au, Cu | Conformal filling of mold |
| Mask absorber | **Gold (Au)**, typically **10–30 μm thick** | Blocks X-rays effectively |
| Mask membrane | **Beryllium (Be)** or **Silicon (Si)** | X-ray transparent support |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

The complete LIGA workflow is divided into three primary technological modules. Each module contains precise sub-steps critical for board examination answers.

### Module A — X-Ray Lithography (Lithographie)

- **A1. Substrate Preparation**: A conductive substrate (often a metallized silicon wafer or a metal plate) is cleaned. A **conductive seed layer** (e.g., Cr/Au or Ti/Cu, typically **50–200 nm thick**) is sputter-deposited. This layer is essential for the subsequent electroplating step.
- **A2. Resist Coating**: Thick PMMA sheets or spin-coated PMMA layers (height **= 100 μm to several mm**) are bonded/cast onto the substrate. For very thick structures, **multiple cast-and-bake cycles** or pre-formed PMMA plates are used.
- **A3. Mask Alignment**: An **X-ray mask** is positioned above the resist. The mask consists of an X-ray transparent membrane (Be or Si) supporting a high-attenuation absorber pattern (Au). Patterns are usually generated by e-beam writing on the mask.
- **A4. Synchrotron Exposure**: The resist is exposed to **synchrotron X-rays** (typically **1–3 GeV electron storage ring**). The exposure dose is **3–8 kJ/cm³** for PMMA. The radiation chemically alters the PMMA chains (chain scission), making exposed regions soluble in the developer.
- **A5. Development**: The resist is immersed in a developer (e.g., **GG developer: diethylene glycol monobutyl ether**, or aqueous NaOH-based solutions) to dissolve exposed regions, leaving a high-aspect-ratio mold.

### Module B — Electroforming (Galvanoformung)

- **B1. Electroplating Bath**: The structured PMMA mold is immersed in an **acidic nickel sulfamate bath** (Ni(SO₃NH₂)₂, typically **300–450 g/L**, at **50 ± 2 °C**, **pH 3.5–4.0**).
- **B2. Current Application**: A **DC current density of 1–10 A/dm²** is applied. Ni²⁺ ions are reduced at the conductive seed layer and grow as a dense, low-stress metal film that fills the mold cavities from the bottom up.
- **B3. Overplating**: Electroplating is continued until the metal slightly overflows the top of the PMMA mold — ensuring a flat, planar top surface for the finished micro-component.
- **B4. Resist Stripping**: The PMMA is dissolved in a solvent (e.g., acetone, xylene, or commercial strippers like **NMP — N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone**), revealing the free-standing metal microstructure bonded to the seed layer/substrate.
- **B5. Seed Layer Removal** *(Optional)*: The seed layer is etched away if the metal microstructure is to be released as a free part.

### Module C — Molding (Abformung)

- **C1. Master Insertion**: The metal micro-component (or its negative) is mounted as a master insert in a molding machine.
- **C2. Hot Embossing**: A thermoplastic polymer (e.g., **PMMA, COC, COP**) is heated above its glass transition temperature (**T_g**), pressed against the master under high pressure (**5–15 MPa**), and cooled.
- **C3. Demolding**: The structured polymer part is separated from the master.
- **C4. Injection Molding** *(Mass Production)*: For high volumes, **micro-injection molding** replicates the master thousands of times using cycle times of seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Aspect Ratio (AR) Formula** — Central to all LIGA questions:
>
> $$AR = \frac{H}{W}$$
>
> where $H$ = structural height (depth), and $W$ = minimum lateral feature width.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = H / W$ | $H$: height [μm], $W$: width [μm] | Quantifies tall, narrow features |
| 2 | Synchrotron Photon Energy | $E = h c / \lambda$ | $h$: Planck's constant [6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s], $c$: speed of light [3 × 10⁸ m/s], $\lambda$: wavelength [m] | Determines X-ray penetration depth |
| 3 | Exposure Dose | $D = I \cdot t$ | $I$: intensity [W/cm²], $t$: exposure time [s] | Total energy absorbed per unit area |
| 4 | Resist Penetration Depth (Beer-Lambert) | $I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu z}$ | $\mu$: absorption coefficient [cm⁻¹], $z$: depth [cm] | Determines usable resist thickness |
| 5 | Electroplating Growth Rate | $v = \frac{M \cdot J}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $M$: molar mass [kg/mol], $J$: current density [A/m²], $n$: valence electrons, $F$: Faraday constant [96,485 C/mol], $\rho$: density [kg/m³] | Time required to fill mold |
| 6 | Mold Filling Time | $t_{fill} = H / v$ | $H$: mold depth [m], $v$: plating rate [m/s] | Critical electroplating parameter |
| 7 | Minimum Feature Size | $W_{min} \approx k_1 \lambda / NA$ | Standard lithography RLS relation | Predicts pattern resolution |
| 8 | Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g$ (PMMA) ≈ **105 °C** | Material property | Determines hot embossing temperature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Synchrotron?** A standard X-ray tube produces a divergent, polychromatic beam with insufficient intensity and collimation. A **synchrotron** provides a **near-parallel, monochromatic, high-brilliance** X-ray beam — essential for vertical sidewalls in deep resist.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Sensors**: Acceleration sensors and pressure sensors for airbag systems.
- **Microfluidics**: Nozzles for inkjet printheads (originally developed by IBM using LIGA).
- **Optical Telecommunication**: Fiber-alignment structures, lens holders, gratings.
- **Medical Devices**: Stents, microneedle arrays, micro-pumps for drug delivery.
- **Space Systems**: High-aspect-ratio metallic gears and pivots for satellite mechanisms.
- **X-ray Optics**: LIGA-fabricated **zone plates** for nano-focusing X-ray microscopy.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Electroplating Rate (Faraday's Law of Electrolysis)

Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis states that the mass of substance deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

**Starting Definition — Mass Deposited:**

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:

- $m$ = mass of metal deposited [kg]
- $Q$ = total electric charge passed [C]
- $M$ = molar mass of the metal [kg/mol]
- $n$ = valency (number of electrons transferred per ion)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**

**Step 1: Express Charge in Terms of Current Density and Time**

$$Q = J \cdot A \cdot t$$

where:

- $J$ = current density [A/m²]
- $A$ = plated area [m²]
- $t$ = plating time [s]

**Step 2: Substitute Q into the Mass Equation**

$$m = \frac{J \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3: Express Mass in Terms of Volume and Density**

Since $m = \rho \cdot V$ and for a uniform film $V = A \cdot \Delta z$ (where $\Delta z$ is the grown thickness), we get:

$$\rho \cdot A \cdot \Delta z = \frac{J \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4: Cancel the Common Area Term (A) and Solve for $\Delta z$**

$$\Delta z = \frac{J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the Plating Rate (Velocity, $v = \Delta z / t$)**

$$v = \frac{\Delta z}{t} = \frac{J \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

This is the **standard electroplating rate** used to determine how long it takes to fill a LIGA mold.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mold Filling Time

The time required to completely fill a PMMA mold of depth $H$ with nickel is:

$$t_{fill} = \frac{H}{v} = \frac{H \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{J \cdot M}$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** Calculate the time required to fill a PMMA mold of depth $H = 300 \text{ μm}$ with nickel using a current density of $J = 5 \text{ A/dm}^2$.

**Given:**

- $H = 300 \, \mu m = 300 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- $J = 5 \, A/dm^2 = 5 \times 10^{-2} \, A/m^2 = 0.05 \, A/m^2$
- $n = 2$ (Nickel is Ni²⁺)
- $F = 96,485 \, C/mol$
- $\rho_{Ni} = 8900 \, kg/m^3$
- $M_{Ni} = 58.69 \times 10^{-3} \, kg/mol$

**Step 1: Compute the Plating Rate**

$$v = \frac{J \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

$$v = \frac{0.05 \times (58.69 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \times 96485 \times 8900}$$

$$v = \frac{2.9345 \times 10^{-3}}{1.7174 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$v = 1.7085 \times 10^{-12} \, m/s$$

**Step 2: Compute the Filling Time**

$$t_{fill} = \frac{H}{v} = \frac{300 \times 10^{-6}}{1.7085 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$t_{fill} \approx 1.756 \times 10^{8} \, s$$

**Step 3: Convert to Practical Units (Hours)**

$$t_{fill} = \frac{1.756 \times 10^{8}}{3600} \approx 48,777 \, h \approx 2032 \, \text{days}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Numerical answers with $J$ in $A/dm^2$ and dimensions in μm require **unit conversion to SI** before substituting. The above number demonstrates that **5 A/dm² is impractical** for mm-scale molds — typical industrial current densities are higher (10–20 A/dm²) and pulse plating is used to achieve reasonable filling times.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LIGA vs. UV Lithography vs. DRIE

| Property | LIGA | UV Lithography | Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source | Synchrotron X-rays | Mercury lamp (λ ≈ 365–436 nm) | Plasma (SF₆/C₄F₈) |
| Wavelength | **0.2 – 2 nm** | 365 – 436 nm | N/A (etch process) |
| Max Structure Height | **> 1000 μm** | 1 – 5 μm | 100 – 500 μm |
| Aspect Ratio | **> 100 : 1** | 1 – 5 : 1 | 20 – 50 : 1 |
| Sidewall Verticality | **< 0.5° taper** | ~ 1–2° | 90° ± 1° (with scalloping) |
| Mask Material | **Au absorber on Be/Si membrane** | Cr on glass | Photoresist or SiO₂ |
| Cost | **Very high** (synchrotron) | Low | Moderate |
| Material Flexibility | **Metals, polymers, ceramics** | Polymers, photoresists | Silicon only |
| Sidewall Roughness | **< 50 nm** | < 100 nm | Scalloping (~ 100–500 nm) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the LIGA process. What does the acronym LIGA stand for, and what is its most distinctive advantage over conventional UV lithography?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**LIGA** is a MEMS fabrication process acronym for **Li**thographie (X-ray **Li**thography), **G**alvanoformung (Electroforming), and **A**bformung (Molding). It is used to fabricate high-aspect-ratio microstructures.

**Distinctive Advantage:** LIGA uses **synchrotron X-ray radiation** (λ ≈ 0.2–2 nm) which can penetrate very thick photoresist layers (hundreds of micrometers to millimeters), enabling microstructures with **aspect ratios > 100:1** and structural heights far exceeding the **1–2 μm limit** of conventional UV lithography.

*(Breakdown: 1 mark for acronym expansion, 1 mark for process identification, 1 mark for distinctive advantage.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the materials used for the photoresist, mask absorber, mask membrane, and electroplating metal in a standard LIGA process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Component | Material |
|---|---|
| Photoresist | **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** |
| Mask absorber | **Gold (Au)** — typically 10–30 μm thick |
| Mask membrane | **Beryllium (Be)** or **Silicon (Si)** |
| Electroplating metal | **Nickel (Ni)** (also Au, Cu) |

*(1 mark for any two correct materials, full marks for all four.)*
